topics.title: Introduction - Classification of Dynamic Loads - Essential Characteristics of a Dynamic Problem - Methods of Discretization - Single Degree of Freedom Systems - Basic Components of a Dynamic System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction - Classification of Dynamic Loads - Essential Characteristics of a Dynamic Problem - Methods of Discretization - Single Degree of Freedom Systems - Basic Components of a Dynamic System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Structural Dynamics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Structural Dynamics* is the branch of structural engineering that deals with the analysis and design of structures subjected to **time-varying loads**, where the **inertia forces** developed in the structure are significant and cannot be ignored. Unlike statics, the response (displacement, velocity, acceleration) is a function of **time**.

In **earthquake-resistant design**, structural dynamics forms the *theoretical foundation*, because seismic forces are inherently dynamic ŌĆö they depend on the mass, stiffness, damping, and dynamic characteristics of the structure itself, not just the ground motion.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 ŌĆö The "Push on a Swing"**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push *once, gently*, the swing moves a little and stops (quasi-static). But if you push it *rhythmically, in sync with its natural rhythm*, the swing oscillates violently. This "rhythm-matching" is **resonance**, a purely dynamic phenomenon. Earthquakes work similarly ŌĆö when a building's natural period matches the dominant period of ground motion, severe damage occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 ŌĆö The "SpringŌĆōMassŌĆōDamper Toy"**
> Take a toy car attached to a spring and dashpot on a frictionless table. Pull and release it. It doesn't return instantly to rest ŌĆö it oscillates with decreasing amplitude. The *mass* (m) provides inertia, the *spring* (k) provides restoring force, and the *dashpot* (c) absorbs energy. Every structure on this planet is, fundamentally, this toy car in disguise.

---

## 1.3 Why Dynamics for Earthquake Engineering?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Seismic loads are time-dependent** | Ground acceleration varies rapidly with time, so inertia forces $F = m \cdot a(t)$ vary with time. |
| **Mass distribution matters** | In static design, mass is just a vertical load. In dynamics, mass resists *change of motion* ŌĆö its distribution dictates how the building shakes. |
| **Period and frequency govern design** | Every building has a natural period $T_n$. If $T_n$ matches the earthquake's dominant period ŌåÆ resonance ŌåÆ collapse. |
| **Damping is a friend, not an enemy** | Damping dissipates seismic energy and reduces response. We cannot analyze this statically. |
| **Code provisions (IS 1893, IS 4326, etc.) are dynamic-based** | The very design base shear $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ in IS 1893 (Part 1) is derived from response spectrum analysis, a dynamic concept. |

---

## 1.4 Definitions of Key Terms (for exam memorization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Dynamic Load:** A load that varies with **time** ŌĆö e.g., earthquake, wind gust, blast, machine vibration, moving vehicle.
> - **Static Load:** A load that is constant or varies *so slowly* that inertia effects are **negligible** ŌĆö e.g., self-weight, dead load, slowly applied live load.
> - **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely define the **displaced position** of a system at any instant of time.
> - **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF):** A system whose displaced configuration can be described by a *single* coordinate.
> - **Multi Degree of Freedom (MDOF):** A system requiring two or more independent coordinates to define its displaced shape.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Dynamic Loads (Most Important for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dynamic loads can be classified along **multiple axes**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know at least the **time-based classification** and **deterministic/random classification**.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Variation with Time

| S.No. | Type of Load | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Periodic Load** | Repeats itself after a fixed time interval $T$ | Rotating machinery, wind loading on a vibrating chimney |
| 2 | **Harmonic Load** | Special case of periodic ŌĆö varies sinusoidally: $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Unbalanced rotating mass in a turbine |
| 3 | **Impulsive / Pulse Load** | Applied for a *very short* duration ($\Delta t \ll T_n$) | Blast load, hammer strike, projectile impact |
| 4 | **Step / Suddenly Applied Load** | Constant magnitude, applied suddenly at $t=0$ and held | A heavy piece of equipment dropped on a floor |
| 5 | **Earthquake (Seismic) Load** | Random, non-periodic, ground acceleration $a_g(t)$ applied at the base | Real earthquake ground motion (El Centro 1940, Bhuj 2001) |
| 6 | **Wind Gust Load** | Random, fluctuating wind pressure | Typhoon, cyclone |
| 7 | **Wave / Hydrodynamic Load** | Time-varying water pressure on coastal/offshore structures | Tsunami, regular ocean waves |
| 8 | **Moving Load** | Loads that traverse the structure (e.g., bridges) | Vehicle, train on a bridge |

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Determinism

- **Deterministic Loads:** Magnitude and variation are *fully known* as a function of time ŌåÆ equation $F(t)$ is known. Examples: harmonic, impulse, step.
- **Non-Deterministic (Random / Stochastic) Loads:** Magnitude is not exactly known; described by *statistical* properties (mean, RMS, PSD, probability distribution). Examples: earthquake, wind, ocean waves.

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Direction

- **Lateral Dynamic Loads:** Horizontal forces (most important in EQ engineering) ŌåÆ seismic, wind.
- **Vertical Dynamic Loads:** Vertical forces (machine vibrations, blast overpressure).

### 2.1.4 Special Categories in KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> - **Quasi-static load:** A dynamic load that varies *so slowly* that dynamic effects (inertia) are negligible ŌåÆ can be treated as static. Example: very slow water filling in a tank.
> - **Sustained dynamic load:** Applied for a long time relative to the natural period (e.g., a long-duration harmonic load).
> - **Code-based equivalent static load:** The dynamic earthquake load is *replaced* by an equivalent static force $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ for simple design (IS 1893 method).

---

## 2.2 Essential Characteristics of a Dynamic Problem

A problem qualifies as *dynamic* (i.e., cannot be solved by statics alone) only if **all** of the following characteristics are present:

1. **Time-dependence of the load:** $F = F(t)$ ŌĆö the load magnitude or direction changes with time.
2. **Inertia of the system is significant:** The mass of the structure must be such that the inertial force $F_I = m \cdot \ddot{u}(t)$ is comparable to the applied load. If mass is very small or the motion is very slow, inertia is negligible.
3. **Damping / Energy dissipation is present:** Real structures dissipate energy through material hysteresis, friction at joints, opening/closing of microcracks, air resistance, etc.
4. **Restoring force (stiffness) is present:** The structure must have the ability to *return* to its original shape ŌĆö provided by elastic stiffness.
5. **The governing equation is a differential equation in time:** Static equilibrium gives algebraic equations; dynamic equilibrium gives *ordinary/partial differential equations* involving derivatives with respect to time.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: students often confuse *dynamic load* with *dynamic problem*. A load can be dynamic (varying with time), but if the mass is negligible (e.g., a light aluminum sheet in a very gentle wind), the *problem* may still be static. A *dynamic problem* requires **both** time-varying load **and** significant inertia.

---

## 2.3 Basic Components of a Dynamic System

Every dynamic system ŌĆö from a one-storey building to a thousand-tonne bridge ŌĆö is composed of **three idealised components**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### The Three Pillars of a Dynamic System
> 1. **Mass ($m$):** Provides **inertia** ŌĆö resists change of motion. Stores **kinetic energy** $KE = \frac{1}{2} m \dot{u}^2$.
> 2. **Stiffness ($k$):** Provides **restoring force** ŌĆö pulls the system back to equilibrium. Stores **strain energy** $SE = \frac{1}{2} k u^2$.
> 3. **Damping ($c$):** Provides **energy dissipation** ŌĆö opposes velocity. Does not store energy, only **dissipates** it as heat/noise.

Additionally, a complete dynamic system has:
- **An excitation source (input):** $F(t)$ or $a_g(t)$ ŌĆö the applied load.
- **A response (output):** displacement $u(t)$, velocity $\dot{u}(t)$, acceleration $\ddot{u}(t)$ ŌĆö what the engineer wants to compute.

**Mathematical idealisations used in KTU syllabus:**

| Component | Real Element | Idealised Model |
|---|---|---|
| Mass | Concrete, steel, furniture, occupants | **Lumped mass** (concentrated at a point) or **distributed mass** |
| Stiffness | Columns, walls, beams, braces | **Linear spring** (Hookean: $F_s = k \cdot u$) |
| Damping | Joint friction, material damping, air drag | **Viscous dashpot** (linear: $F_d = c \cdot \dot{u}$) |
| Support | Ground, foundation | **Fixed**, **Pinned**, or **Moving support** |

---

## 2.4 Methods of Discretization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In structural dynamics, a *continuous* structure (with infinite DOF) is converted into a *discrete* system (with finite DOF) so that numerical analysis is possible. This conversion is called **discretization**.

### 2.4.1 Why Discretize?

- A real beam/column/frame has **infinite** DOF (every point along its length can move independently).
- Solving a continuous system requires **partial differential equations (PDEs)** ŌĆö very difficult and limited to simple geometries.
- Discretization reduces the system to a finite number of coordinates ŌåÆ governed by **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)** or matrix equations, which are solved numerically.

### 2.4.2 Common Discretization Methods (KTU Favourite Topics)

#### (a) Lumped Mass Method (Concentrated Mass)
- The **mass of the structure is concentrated (lumped) at discrete points** ŌĆö typically at floor levels, joints, or strategic locations.
- Each lump has translational/rotational inertia; the connecting members are **massless** springs.
- **Advantage:** Very simple, computationally cheap. Ideal for framed buildings (mass concentrated at floor levels).
- **Disadvantage:** Approximate; rotational inertia of members is ignored.
- **Typical use:** Building frames analysed as a *shear building*.

#### (b) Consistent Mass Method (Distributed Mass)
- The **mass of the element is distributed** over its length, and the *same* shape functions used for stiffness are also used for mass.
- This results in a **consistent mass matrix** $[M]$ that is *consistent* with the stiffness matrix $[K]$ (both derived from the same shape functions).
- **Advantage:** More accurate than lumped mass; no loss of rotational inertia of elements.
- **Disadvantage:** More complex; results in a full (coupled) mass matrix.
- **Typical use:** Finite element analysis of beams, shells, plates.

#### (c) Generalized SDOF (Rayleigh Method / RayleighŌĆōRitz Method)
- The continuous system is approximated by **a single assumed shape function** $\psi(x)$.
- The actual displacement is approximated as $u(x,t) = \psi(x) \cdot z(t)$, where $z(t)$ is a *single* generalized coordinate.
- The system is reduced to an **equivalent SDOF** with generalized mass $m^*$, generalized stiffness $k^*$, and generalized force $F^*(t)$.
- **Advantage:** Extremely useful for hand calculations; gives a good estimate of *fundamental* (first) natural frequency and first-mode response.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot capture higher modes accurately.
- **Typical use:** First-mode approximate analysis of buildings, chimneys, towers, dams.

#### (d) Finite Element Method (FEM)
- The structure is divided into many small **elements** (beam, plate, shell, solid).
- Each element has its own stiffness and mass matrices.
- Element matrices are *assembled* into global $[K]$ and $[M]$ matrices.
- **Advantage:** Most general, accurate, can model any geometry and boundary condition.
- **Disadvantage:** Computationally intensive; requires software.
- **Typical use:** Modern professional practice (ETABS, SAP2000, ANSYS, ABAQUS).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam tip:** For Module 1 questions on discretization, the expected answers are **lumped mass**, **consistent mass**, and **generalized SDOF / Rayleigh method**. FEM is mentioned for completeness but is usually covered in detail in dedicated FEM courses.

---

## 2.5 Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System

### 2.5.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **SDOF system** is a dynamic system whose displaced configuration at any instant of time is completely defined by a **single coordinate** $u(t)$.
> Examples: a single-storey frame idealized as a column with mass on top; a cantilever with a tip mass; a water tank on a tower.

### 2.5.2 Idealised SDOF Model (KTU Standard Diagram in Words)

> A mass $m$ is connected to a **rigid, frictionless support** by a **massless linear spring** of stiffness $k$ and a **viscous dashpot** of damping coefficient $c$. The mass can move in **one direction** only (say, $x$).
> The system is excited by an external force $F(t)$ applied at the mass.

### 2.5.3 Equation of Motion (EOM) of an SDOF System ŌĆö *The Most Important Equation in Dynamics*

$$m \ddot{u}(t) + c \dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = F(t)$$

**Where:**
- $u(t)$ = displacement of the mass (relative to ground) at time $t$ (in m)
- $\dot{u}(t)$ = velocity of the mass (in m/s)
- $\ddot{u}(t)$ = acceleration of the mass (in m/s┬▓)
- $m$ = mass of the system (in kg)
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient (in N┬Ęs/m)
- $k$ = stiffness of the spring (in N/m)
- $F(t)$ = external applied force (in N)

**Physical meaning of each term:**

| Term | Name | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $m \ddot{u}$ | **Inertia force** | Resistance to acceleration (Newton's 2nd law: $F = ma$) |
| $c \dot{u}$ | **Damping force** | Opposition to velocity (energy dissipation) |
| $k u$ | **Restoring force** | Opposition to displacement (Hooke's law) |
| $F(t)$ | **Applied force** | External excitation |

For **ground motion excitation** (earthquake), $F(t) = -m \ddot{u}_g(t)$, where $\ddot{u}_g(t)$ is ground acceleration. The EOM becomes:

$$m \ddot{u}(t) + c \dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = -m \ddot{u}_g(t)$$

The relative displacement is $u(t) = u_{abs}(t) - u_g(t)$ (mass displacement minus ground displacement).

### 2.5.4 Natural Frequency and Natural Period (The Heart of EQ Design!)

For an **undamped** SDOF system ($c = 0$), the equation of free vibration is:

$$m \ddot{u} + k u = 0$$

The solution is harmonic: $u(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t)$.

The **natural circular frequency** (rad/s) is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

The **natural frequency** in Hz is:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

The **natural period** (in seconds) is:

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's most-tested relationship:** $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$
> In terms of weight $W = mg$: $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{g k}}$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all** formulas you need for Module 1 problems. **Memorize this.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EOM (forced, damped) | $m \ddot{u} + c \dot{u} + k u = F(t)$ | N (when expanded) | Fundamental equation |
| 2 | EOM (earthquake input) | $m \ddot{u} + c \dot{u} + k u = -m \ddot{u}_g$ | N | $\ddot{u}_g$ = ground accel. |
| 3 | Natural circular frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Undamped |
| 4 | Natural frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz | $f_n = \omega_n / 2\pi$ |
| 5 | Natural period | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{m/k}$ | s | In terms of $W$: $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{W/(gk)}$ |
| 6 | Critical damping coeff. | $c_{cr} = 2 m \omega_n = 2 \sqrt{k m}$ | N┬Ęs/m | Minimum damping for no oscillation |
| 7 | Damping ratio | $\xi = c / c_{cr}$ | dimensionless | $\xi < 1$ underdamped, $=1$ critical, $>1$ overdamped |
| 8 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \xi^2}$ | rad/s | $\xi \ll 1 \Rightarrow \omega_d \approx \omega_n$ |
| 9 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2 \pi \xi}{\sqrt{1 - \xi^2}}$ | dimensionless | Used to find $\xi$ from free-vibration test |
| 10 | Stiffness of cantilever column | $k = 3 E I / L^3$ | N/m | Fixed at base, free at top |
| 11 | Stiffness of fixed-pinned column | $k = 3 E I / L^3$ | N/m | Fixed at base, pinned at top |
| 12 | Stiffness of fixed-fixed column | $k = 12 E I / L^3$ | N/m | Both ends fixed (no rotation) |
| 13 | Stiffness of simply supported beam (mid-span point load) | $k = 48 E I / L^3$ | N/m | Load at mid-span |
| 14 | Generalized SDOF stiffness | $k^* = \int_0^L E I \left[\psi''(x)\right]^2 dx$ | N/m | RayleighŌĆōRitz method |
| 15 | Generalized SDOF mass | $m^* = \int_0^L \bar{m} \left[\psi(x)\right]^2 dx$ | kg | $\bar{m}$ = mass per unit length |
| 16 | Generalized force | $F^* = \int_0^L F(x,t) \psi(x) dx$ | N | $F(x,t)$ = distributed load |

> [!WARNING]
> In exam, when you write a numerical value, *always* write its **unit**. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Real Application |
|---|---|
| Dynamic load classification | Determines which analysis method to use (static equivalent vs. response spectrum vs. time history). |
| SDOF idealisation | First-cut design of single-storey buildings, water tanks on staging, equipment on isolated pads. |
| Natural period | Used in IS 1893 to compute **design base shear** and to read **spectral acceleration** from the response spectrum. |
| Damping | Determines how fast seismic vibrations die out; critical for comfort and safety post-earthquake. |
| Methods of discretization | Foundation of every structural analysis software (ETABS, SAP2000, ANSYS) used in modern design offices. |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Equation of Motion of an SDOF System (Forced, Damped)

**Setup:** Consider a mass $m$ attached to a massless spring (stiffness $k$) and a viscous dashpot (damping $c$), excited by an external force $F(t)$ in the $x$-direction. The support is rigid.

**Step 1:** Define the coordinate system.
Let $u(t)$ = absolute displacement of the mass from its *static equilibrium position* (positive to the right). At $t = 0$, the system is at rest and the spring is undeformed by static load (we use the d'Alembert trick of using static equilibrium as the reference).

**Step 2:** Draw the free-body diagram (FBD) of the mass at time $t$.
Forces acting on the mass:
1. Applied force $F(t)$ in the direction of $u$ (positive).
2. Spring restoring force $F_s = -k u$ (opposes displacement, hence negative).
3. Damping force $F_d = -c \dot{u}$ (opposes velocity, hence negative).
4. **Inertia force** $F_I = -m \ddot{u}$ (d'Alembert's principle ŌĆö fictitious force to make a dynamic problem a static equilibrium problem).

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's second law (or d'Alembert's principle).
Sum of all forces in the $x$-direction = 0 (in d'Alembert form) OR $F_{net} = m \ddot{u}$ (in Newton's form).

Using Newton's second law (more familiar):

$$F(t) - k u(t) - c \dot{u}(t) = m \ddot{u}(t)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to the **standard form** (all terms on LHS, in the order: inertia, damping, restoring):

$$m \ddot{u}(t) + c \dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = F(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **governing differential equation of motion (EOM)** of a linear, viscously damped SDOF system under arbitrary external excitation $F(t)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Natural Frequency of an Undamped SDOF System

**Step 1:** Set $F(t) = 0$ and $c = 0$ in the EOM to get the **free vibration** equation.

$$m \ddot{u}(t) + k u(t) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Divide through by $m$:

$$\ddot{u}(t) + \frac{k}{m} u(t) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this as the standard simple harmonic motion (SHM) ODE:

$$\ddot{u} + \omega^2 u = 0$$

where the natural circular frequency squared is:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

**Step 4:** Solve the ODE. The characteristic equation is:

$$\lambda^2 + \omega_n^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \pm i \omega_n$$

where $i = \sqrt{-1}$.

**Step 5:** The general solution is:

$$u(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t)$$

Or, equivalently, in amplitudeŌĆōphase form:

$$u(t) = C \cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$$

where $C = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ is the amplitude and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(B/A)$ is the phase angle.

**Step 6:** Apply initial conditions at $t = 0$:
- $u(0) = u_0$ (initial displacement)
- $\dot{u}(0) = v_0$ (initial velocity)

At $t = 0$: $u(0) = A = u_0$.

Differentiating: $\dot{u}(t) = -A \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t) + B \omega_n \cos(\omega_n t)$.
At $t = 0$: $\dot{u}(0) = B \omega_n = v_0 \Rightarrow B = v_0 / \omega_n$.

**Final solution:**

$$u(t) = u_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 7:** The natural period $T_n$ is the time taken for one full oscillation (when the cosine argument changes by $2\pi$):

$$\omega_n T_n = 2\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most repeated derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme dynamics papers. Memorize it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Critical Damping Coefficient

**Step 1:** Consider the EOM with damping but no external force:

$$m \ddot{u} + c \dot{u} + k u = 0$$

**Step 2:** Divide by $m$:

$$\ddot{u} + \frac{c}{m} \dot{u} + \frac{k}{m} u = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and define $2 \zeta \omega_n = c/m$ (this definition is chosen so that the final form is standard):

$$\ddot{u} + 2 \zeta \omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = 0$$

where $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio**.

**Step 4:** Cross-multiplying the definition: $c = 2 \zeta \omega_n m$. When $\zeta = 1$ (critically damped), the damping coefficient becomes the **critical damping coefficient**:

$$c_{cr} = 2 m \omega_n$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$:

$$c_{cr} = 2 m \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = 2 \sqrt{k m}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the **damping ratio** is:

$$\xi = \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{c}{2 m \omega_n} = \frac{c}{2 \sqrt{k m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of damping regimes:**
> - $\xi = 0$: Undamped ŌĆö oscillates forever.
> - $0 < \xi < 1$: **Underdamped** ŌĆö oscillates with exponentially decaying amplitude (typical for civil structures, $\xi \approx 2{-}5\%$).
> - $\xi = 1$: **Critically damped** ŌĆö returns to equilibrium *fastest* without oscillating.
> - $\xi > 1$: **Overdamped** ŌĆö returns to equilibrium slowly without oscillating.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Stiffness of a Cantilever Column (Lateral Stiffness)

This is **the most-tested formula** in KTU for Module 1.

**Setup:** A vertical cantilever column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, fixed at the base and free at the top, with a horizontal force $F$ applied at the top. Find the lateral stiffness $k$ (= force per unit lateral displacement at the top).

**Step 1:** Use the standard formula from Strength of Materials for tip deflection of a cantilever under tip load:

$$\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

where:
- $\delta$ = lateral displacement at the free end (in m)
- $F$ = horizontal force at the tip (in N)
- $L$ = length of the column (in m)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (in N/m┬▓)
- $I$ = second moment of area (in mŌü┤)

**Step 2:** By definition, **stiffness** is the force required to produce a *unit* displacement:

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{F}{\dfrac{F L^3}{3 E I}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$k = \frac{3 E I}{L^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lateral stiffness of a cantilever column (fixed-free):** $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3}$ (N/m)
> This is the formula used to compute the natural period of a single-storey building modeled as a cantilever.

**Step 4:** Substituting $k$ into the natural period formula:

$$T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m L^3}{3 E I}}$$

**Step 5:** In terms of the weight $W = m g$:

$$T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{W L^3}{3 g E I}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Natural Period of a Single-Storey Building (Shear Building)

**Setup:** A single-storey RC frame with roof mass $m$, supported on four columns each of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, fixed at base. Assume the floor is rigid in-plane (translates but doesn't rotate ŌĆö shear building idealization). The lateral stiffness is provided by the columns acting in parallel.

**Step 1:** Each column has lateral stiffness:

$$k_{col} = \frac{12 E I}{L^3}$$

(fixed-fixed; both ends of column cannot rotate due to the rigid roof slab and fixed foundation).

**Step 2:** For $n$ columns in parallel (sharing the same displacement $\delta$ but dividing the force):

$$k_{total} = n \cdot k_{col} = \frac{12 n E I}{L^3}$$

**Step 3:** The natural period is:

$$T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k_{total}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m L^3}{12 n E I}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a single-storey frame, the **approximate IS 1893 empirical formula** gives: $T_a = 0.09 h / \sqrt{d}$, where $h$ is the height and $d$ is the base dimension in the considered direction. This is an *empirical* estimate; the analytical value above is more accurate.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Boundary Conditions & Stiffness Summary

| Support Condition | Sketch Description | Stiffness $k$ (Lateral) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-Free (Cantilever)** | One end fixed, other end free | $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3}$ | Most flexible |
| **Fixed-Pinned** | One end fixed, other end pinned (rotation free) | $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3}$ | Same as cantilever (coincidentally) |
| **Fixed-Fixed** | Both ends fixed (no rotation, no translation) | $k = \dfrac{12 E I}{L^3}$ | 4├Ś stiffer than cantilever |
| **Pinned-Pinned (load at mid-span)** | Both ends pinned, lateral load at midspan | $k = \dfrac{48 E I}{L^3}$ | Used for beams |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation of fixed-fixed stiffness (for completeness):**
> For a column fixed at both ends with lateral load $F$ at top, the tip deflection is $\delta = \dfrac{F L^3}{12 E I}$. Hence $k = F/\delta = 12 E I / L^3$. This can be derived by superposing the cantilever solution with a corrective moment $M = F L/2$ at the top, which cancels the rotation.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Example (Model Problem for Practice)

> **Problem:** A water tank of mass $m = 5000$ kg is supported on a staging tower modeled as a cantilever of length $L = 6$ m, with flexural rigidity $EI = 2.5 \times 10^{7}$ N┬Ęm┬▓. Compute:
> (a) The lateral stiffness of the staging.
> (b) The natural circular frequency $\omega_n$.
> (c) The natural period $T_n$.
> (d) The natural frequency in Hz.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Stiffness of cantilever:

$$k = \frac{3 E I}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{7}}{6^3} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{7}}{216}$$

$$k = 3.472 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/m} \approx 347.22 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Part (b):** Natural circular frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.472 \times 10^{5}}{5000}} = \sqrt{69.44}$$

$$\omega_n = 8.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (c):** Natural period:

$$T_n = \frac{2 \pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2 \pi}{8.333} = 0.754 \text{ s}$$

**Part (d):** Natural frequency in Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{T_n} = \frac{1}{0.754} = 1.326 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Such a period ($\sim 0.75$ s) is typical of small water tanks. KTU questions may ask to *compare* this with the dominant period of an earthquake ŌĆö if the earthquake's dominant period is close to $0.75$ s, the tank is in the *resonance danger zone*.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Idealised SDOF System ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Excitation F of t]:::inputStyle --> B[Mass m Node]:::massStyle
    B --> C[Spring Stiffness k]:::springStyle
    C --> D[Restoring Force Fs]:::forceStyle
    B --> E[Dashpot Damping c]:::dampStyle
    E --> F[Damping Force Fd]:::forceStyle
    B --> G[Inertia Force ma]:::inertiaStyle
    D --> H[Dynamic Equilibrium Sum of forces equals zero]:::eqStyle
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Response u of t, v of t, a of t]:::outputStyle
    R[Rigid Fixed Support]:::supportStyle --- C

    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    classDef massStyle fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef springStyle fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef dampStyle fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef forceStyle fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **mass node** is the central hub where excitation enters and response is measured.
- The **spring** provides a *position-dependent* restoring force; the **dashpot** provides a *velocity-dependent* damping force.
- The **inertia force** (a fictitious force via d'Alembert) completes the dynamic equilibrium.
- All three forces (inertia, damping, restoring) must balance the **applied force** at every instant ŌĆö this is the EOM in equilibrium form.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`
**Classify dynamic loads with suitable examples. How are they different from static loads?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dynamic loads are time-varying loads whose magnitude, direction, or point of application changes with time, producing inertia forces in the structure.
*Classification:*
1. **Periodic** (repeats after a fixed interval) ŌĆö e.g., rotating machinery.
2. **Harmonic** (sinusoidal) ŌĆö e.g., unbalanced motor.
3. **Impulsive** (very short duration) ŌĆö e.g., blast, hammer impact.
4. **Earthquake** (random ground motion) ŌĆö e.g., seismic excitation.
5. **Wind gust** (random) ŌĆö e.g., cyclone.
6. **Step / suddenly applied** ŌĆö e.g., equipment dropped on floor.
7. **Moving** ŌĆö e.g., vehicle on a bridge.

*Difference from static loads:* Static loads are constant or vary so slowly that inertia effects are negligible, while dynamic loads cause significant inertia forces and require the equation of motion to be solved in time. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`
**List the essential characteristics of a dynamic problem and the basic components of a dynamic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Essential characteristics of a dynamic problem:*
1. The load must vary with time.
2. Inertia of the system must be significant (inertia force $m\ddot{u}$ comparable to applied force).
3. The system must possess stiffness (restoring force).
4. The system must have damping or energy-dissipation mechanism.
5. The governing equation is a time-differential equation (not algebraic).

*Basic components of a dynamic system:*
1. **Mass ($m$):** provides inertia, stores kinetic energy.
2. **Stiffness ($k$):** provides restoring force, stores strain energy.
3. **Damping ($c$):** dissipates energy, opposes velocity.
4. **Excitation source:** $F(t)$ or $a_g(t)$.
5. **Support conditions:** fixed, pinned, or moving. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has an **internal choice** between two full-length alternatives. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Choice A** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the equation of motion of an undamped Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system subjected to a general external force $F(t)$. Also obtain the expression for natural frequency and natural period. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the system ŌĆö a mass $m$ connected to a massless spring of stiffness $k$, free to move in one direction. Define $u(t)$ as the absolute displacement from the static equilibrium position.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Newton's second law / d'Alembert's principle. Forces on the mass:
- Applied force: $F(t)$ (positive direction).
- Spring restoring force: $-k u$ (opposes displacement).
- Inertia force (d'Alembert): $-m \ddot{u}$ (opposes acceleration).

Setting up equilibrium: $F(t) - k u - m \ddot{u} = 0$

Rearranged EOM: $m \ddot{u} + k u = F(t)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For free vibration ($F = 0$): $m \ddot{u} + k u = 0 \Rightarrow \ddot{u} + (k/m) u = 0$.

Compare with SHM ODE $\ddot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = 0$:
$\omega_n^2 = k/m \Rightarrow \boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}}$ (rad/s).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Natural period: $T_n = 2\pi / \omega_n = \boxed{2\pi \sqrt{m/k}}$ (s).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Physical meaning:
- $\omega_n$ depends only on **stiffness and mass**, not on the magnitude of the load.
- Higher $k$ ŌåÆ higher $\omega_n$ ŌåÆ lower $T_n$ (stiffer structures vibrate faster).
- Higher $m$ ŌåÆ lower $\omega_n$ ŌåÆ higher $T_n$ (heavier structures vibrate slower).

---

#### (b) A machine of mass $m = 800$ kg is mounted on a foundation modelled as a cantilever of length $L = 2$ m and flexural rigidity $EI = 4 \times 10^{6}$ N┬Ęm┬▓. Compute the natural frequency in rad/s, in Hz, and the natural period. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute lateral stiffness of cantilever.
$$k = \frac{3 E I}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times 4 \times 10^{6}}{2^3} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{8} = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Natural circular frequency.
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{800}} = \sqrt{1875}$$
$$\boxed{\omega_n = 43.30 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Natural frequency in Hz.
$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{43.30}{2\pi} = \boxed{6.89 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Natural period.
$$T_n = \frac{1}{f_n} = \frac{1}{6.89} = \boxed{0.145 \text{ s}}$$

**Verification (1 mark):** Alternatively, $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{m/k} = 2\pi \sqrt{800 / (1.5 \times 10^6)} = 2\pi \sqrt{5.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 2\pi \times 0.0231 = 0.145$ s. Ō£ō

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Choice B** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the different methods of discretization used in structural dynamics. Compare lumped mass and consistent mass methods. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** *Need for discretization* ŌĆö Real structures are continuous (infinite DOF). Discretization reduces them to a finite number of DOF, allowing numerical solution.

**Step 2 (4 marks):** *Main discretization methods:*

1. **Lumped Mass Method:** Mass is *concentrated* at discrete points (e.g., floor levels in a building). Connecting members are massless springs. Governing equation: $m_i \ddot{u}_i + k_{ij} u_j = F_i(t)$. *Used in* simple shear-building idealisation.

2. **Consistent Mass Method:** Mass is *distributed* over the element; the mass matrix $[M]$ is derived using the *same shape functions* used for $[K]$. The mass matrix is generally *full* (has off-diagonal coupling terms). More accurate than lumped mass.

3. **Generalized SDOF / RayleighŌĆōRitz Method:** Approximate the displaced shape as a single function $\psi(x)$, so $u(x,t) = \psi(x) z(t)$. Equivalent to a single SDOF with $m^* = \int \bar m \psi^2 dx$, $k^* = \int EI (\psi'')^2 dx$. *Used in* approximate hand calculation of fundamental frequency.

4. **Finite Element Method (FEM):** Structure is divided into small *finite elements* (beam, plate, shell, solid). Each element has its own $[k]$ and $[m]$ matrices; these are *assembled* into global $[K]$ and $[M]$. The most general and accurate method, used in modern software (ETABS, SAP2000, ANSYS).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** *Comparison:*

| Aspect | Lumped Mass | Consistent Mass |
|---|---|---|
| Mass distribution | Concentrated at nodes | Distributed over element |
| Mass matrix | Diagonal | Full (off-diagonal terms) |
| Accuracy | Lower (rotational inertia ignored) | Higher |
| Computation | Simple, fast | More complex |
| Use | Hand calculations, shear buildings | FEM, accurate analysis |

---

#### (b) A four-storey building is idealised as an SDOF system with effective mass $m = 100{,}000$ kg and lateral stiffness $k = 5 \times 10^{6}$ N/m. The structure has a damping coefficient $c = 80{,}000$ N┬Ęs/m. Compute: (i) natural frequency, (ii) natural period, (iii) critical damping coefficient, (iv) damping ratio, (v) state whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Natural circular frequency.
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{10^{5}}} = \sqrt{50} = 7.071 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Natural period.
$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{7.071} = 0.889 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Critical damping coefficient.
$$c_{cr} = 2 m \omega_n = 2 \times 10^{5} \times 7.071 = 1.4142 \times 10^{6} \text{ N┬Ęs/m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Alternative form: $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2 \sqrt{5 \times 10^6 \times 10^5} = 2 \sqrt{5 \times 10^{11}} = 2 \times 7.071 \times 10^5 = 1.4142 \times 10^6$ N┬Ęs/m. Ō£ō

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Damping ratio.
$$\xi = \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{4}}{1.4142 \times 10^{6}} = 0.0566 \approx 5.66\%$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Since $\xi = 0.0566 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** ŌĆö it will oscillate with exponentially decaying amplitude.

Damped natural frequency:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \xi^2} = 7.071 \sqrt{1 - 0.0032} = 7.071 \times 0.9984 = 7.060 \text{ rad/s}$$

The actual oscillating frequency is only slightly less than $\omega_n$ (typical of civil structures with 2ŌĆō5% damping).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Mass must be in **kg** (not kN), stiffness in **N/m** (not kN/m), length in **m** (not mm or cm). A factor of 1000 error is very common.
> 2. **Mixing $W$ (weight) with $m$ (mass):** $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ uses **mass** in kg. If you are given $W$ in kN, remember $m = W/g$, where $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓. Writing $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{W/k}$ without dividing by $g$ is a **classic 1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Confusing $E$ (Young's modulus) with $EI$ (flexural rigidity):** Cantilever stiffness uses $EI$ (combined), *not* $E$ alone.
> 4. **Forgetting the units in final answer:** KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 mark for every missing unit in a numerical answer.
> 5. **Using $\pi = 3.14$ instead of $3.1416$:** Acceptable for 3-mark questions, but for 7/14-mark questions, use 3.1416 or 22/7.
> 6. **Static thinking on dynamic problems:** Many students write $k = EI/L$ (which is wrong) instead of $k = 3EI/L^3$ (cantilever lateral stiffness). *Memorize the stiffness formulas.*
> 7. **Not drawing the FBD:** In derivation questions, KTU expects a clear free-body diagram of the mass with all forces marked. Skipping the FBD can cost 1 mark.
> 8. **Using $F$ instead of $-k u$ for spring force:** The spring force is **always** *opposing* the displacement, hence the negative sign is essential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> ### Module 1 ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist
> **Foundations**
> - [ ] Structural dynamics = analysis of structures under time-varying loads where inertia is significant.
> - [ ] Static load ŌåÆ negligible inertia; Dynamic load ŌåÆ inertia must be included.
> - [ ] Dynamic problem characteristics: time-dependent load, significant inertia, stiffness, damping, time-differential governing equation.
>
> **Classification of Dynamic Loads**
> - [ ] Periodic, Harmonic, Impulsive, Step, Earthquake, Wind, Wave, Moving.
> - [ ] Deterministic vs Non-deterministic (random) classification.
> - [ ] Earthquake load = random, non-periodic, applied as ground acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t)$.
>
> **Basic Components of a Dynamic System**
> - [ ] **Mass ($m$):** inertia, stores kinetic energy.
> - [ ] **Stiffness ($k$):** restoring force, stores strain energy.
> - [ ] **Damping ($c$):** energy dissipation, opposes velocity.
> - [ ] **Support conditions:** fixed, pinned, or moving (earthquake base).
> - [ ] **Excitation:** $F(t)$ or $\ddot{u}_g(t)$.
> - [ ] **Response:** $u(t)$, $\dot{u}(t)$, $\ddot{u}(t)$.
>
> **Methods of Discretization**
> - [ ] Lumped mass ŌĆö concentrated at discrete points; simple; diagonal mass matrix.
> - [ ] Consistent mass ŌĆö distributed; same shape functions as stiffness; full mass matrix.
> - [ ] Generalized SDOF (RayleighŌĆōRitz) ŌĆö single shape function; first-mode approximation.
> - [ ] FEM ŌĆö most general; used in modern software.
>
> **SDOF System**
> - [ ] Governed by: $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = F(t)$ (forced) or $= -m\ddot{u}_g(t)$ (earthquake).
> - [ ] **Natural circular frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (rad/s).
> - [ ] **Natural period:** $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k} = 2\pi\sqrt{W/(gk)}$ (s).
> - [ ] **Natural frequency (Hz):** $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi) = 1/T_n$.
> - [ ] **Critical damping:** $c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$.
> - [ ] **Damping ratio:** $\xi = c / c_{cr}$. Underdamped if $\xi < 1$.
> - [ ] **Damped frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \xi^2}$.
>
> **Stiffness Formulas (Lateral)**
> - [ ] Cantilever (fixed-free): $k = 3EI/L^3$.
> - [ ] Fixed-pinned: $k = 3EI/L^3$.
> - [ ] Fixed-fixed: $k = 12EI/L^3$.
> - [ ] Beam (pinned-pinned, mid-span load): $k = 48EI/L^3$.
>
> **Earthquake Relevance**
> - [ ] IS 1893 design base shear: $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ (uses dynamic $A_h$ from response spectrum).
> - [ ] If building $T_n$ Ōēł dominant period of earthquake ŌåÆ resonance ŌåÆ severe damage.
> - [ ] Damping ratio for RC buildings Ōēł 5%; for steel Ōēł 2%; for masonry Ōēł 7ŌĆō10%.
> - [ ] Heavier, more flexible buildings (tall, slender) ŌåÆ longer $T_n$ ŌåÆ lower spectral acceleration.
> - [ ] Stiffer, shorter buildings ŌåÆ shorter $T_n$ ŌåÆ higher spectral acceleration.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes ŌĆö Introduction to Structural Dynamics for Earthquake Engineering**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 ŌĆö FORMULATION OF EQUATION OF MOTION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECET865 / Module 1):**
> *"Formulation of Equation of Motion ŌĆö Newton's 2nd Law and D'Alembert's Principle ŌĆö Generalized SDOF Systems."*

### 1.1.1 Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system is an idealized dynamic system whose displaced (or deformed) configuration is completely described by a **single independent generalized coordinate** $u(t)$. In earthquake engineering, $u(t)$ is almost always the **lateral roof displacement** (relative to the ground) of a building, the tip deflection of a cantilever, or the sloshing height of a fluid in a tank.

Mathematically, the dynamic response of any SDOF system is governed by the **second-order linear ordinary differential equation**:

$$m\ddot{u}(t) + c\dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = p(t)$$

where:
- $m$ = lumped mass (**kg** or **kN┬Ęs┬▓/m**)
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient (**kN┬Ęs/m** or **N┬Ęs/m**)
- $k$ = lateral stiffness (**kN/m** or **N/m**)
- $p(t)$ = time-varying external (or effective) force (**kN**)
- $u, \dot{u}, \ddot{u}$ = displacement, velocity and acceleration

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Generalized"?**
> A *generalized* SDOF does not require the mass to be concentrated at a single physical point. The mass may be **distributed** along the height (e.g., a water tank on a slender staging, a chimney, a multi-storeyed frame idealized as a flexural cantilever). The whole system is *reduced* to a single coordinate by static condensation or by lumping the inertia forces at an equivalent height. This is precisely what differentiates a *generalized* SDOF from a textbook point-mass-on-a-spring.

### 1.1.2 Newton's Second Law ŌĆö Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's 2nd Law (1687):**
> *"The time-rate of change of linear momentum of a body is proportional to the unbalanced external force acting on it, and takes place in the direction of that force."*

$$\sum F = \frac{d}{dt}(m\dot{u}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sum F = m\ddot{u} \quad (\text{constant } m)$$

In structural dynamics this is the **equilibrium-of-real-forces** statement: the sum of all **external forces** equals mass ├Ś acceleration.

### 1.1.3 D'Alembert's Principle ŌĆö Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D'Alembert's Principle (1743):**
> *"A system of rigid bodies is in dynamic equilibrium under the action of the real applied forces plus a fictitious inertia force equal to the negative of the product of mass and acceleration."*

$$\sum F_{\text{ext}} + F_{I} = 0, \quad \text{where } F_{I} = -m\ddot{u}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Frozen-Frame" Trick:**
> Imagine sitting in a bus that suddenly lurches forward. Your body is thrown *backward*. To an observer *inside the bus* (a non-inertial frame), it looks as if a "ghost force" pushed you back. D'Alembert formalised exactly this: by inventing a fictitious inertia force, the **dynamic problem is reduced to a STATIC equilibrium problem**. You can now draw a **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** and write $\sum F = 0$ exactly as you would for a stationary structure. This is the single most important conceptual step in classical structural dynamics.

### 1.1.4 Conceptual Analogy for Equation of Motion

Think of an **SDOF building** as a **toy car tied to a wall by a spring and damper**, sitting on a vibrating shake-table:

| Real Component | Analogy | Role in EOM |
|---|---|---|
| Building mass $m$ | Toy car weight | Inertia, stores kinetic energy |
| Columns/shear walls $k$ | Spring | Restoring force, stores PE |
| Joint friction / material damping $c$ | Dashpot | Energy dissipation |
| Ground acceleration $\ddot{u}_g$ | Shake-table motion | Excitation source |
| Roof drift $u$ | Car displacement | Generalized coordinate |

The shake-table drags the base; the car lags; the spring stretches; the dashpot resists; the mass resists acceleration. Summing forces on the car = the **equation of motion**.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Routes to the Equation of Motion

There are **two mathematically identical** but pedagogically different ways to obtain the EOM of an SDOF system. Examiners love asking: *"Derive the EOM using Newton's 2nd Law / D'Alembert's principle."* You must know both.

| Method | Core Statement | Sign Convention | When Preferred |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Newton's 2nd Law** | $\sum F_{\text{real}} = m\ddot{u}$ | Acceleration acts *along* force | Translating masses, rigid body motion |
| **D'Alembert's Principle** | $\sum F_{\text{real}} + F_{\text{inertia}} = 0$ | Inertia force is *reactive* | FBDs, FEM-style assembly, energy methods |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic ŌĆö Formulating the EOM

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discretise:** Decide the **single generalized coordinate** $u(t)$. For a one-storey frame ŌåÆ roof drift; for a cantilever chimney ŌåÆ tip deflection; for a tank-on-staging ŌåÆ top displacement of staging.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Lumped vs. Distributed:** If mass is distributed, **lump** the inertia at the level where $u$ is defined. Apply the principle of **consistent mass** or **lumped mass**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Free Body Diagram (FBD):** Cut the structure at the level of $u$, expose the internal forces ($k u$, $c \dot{u}$), and draw the external and inertia forces.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Equilibrium:**
- *Newton:* $\sum F_x = m\ddot{u}$
- *D'Alembert:* $\sum F_x - m\ddot{u} = 0$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute the kinematic relationship** between total, relative, and ground motion:
$$u^{t}(t) = u(t) + u_g(t)$$
$$\dot{u}^{t}(t) = \dot{u}(t) + \dot{u}_g(t)$$
$$\ddot{u}^{t}(t) = \ddot{u}(t) + \ddot{u}_g(t)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Rearrange** to express the equation in terms of the *relative* coordinate $u(t)$.

## 2.3 The Three Canonical SDOF Configurations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** All three configurations are derived by applying the same two principles (Newton / D'Alembert) to a different free body. Memorise the FBDs ŌĆö they are the most commonly reproduced figure in KTU ESE answers.

### (a) Force-Excited SDOF (no base motion)
A horizontal force $p(t)$ is applied at the mass:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = p(t)$$

### (b) Base-Excited SDOF (the earthquake problem)
The support moves with $\ddot{u}_g(t)$; mass experiences total acceleration $\ddot{u}^t = \ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g$:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = -m\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The RHS $-m\ddot{u}_g$ is the **effective earthquake force**. In seismic design, this is the foundation of the **Response Spectrum Method**.

### (c) Generalized SDOF (multi-mass reduced to one coordinate)
For a continuous system with multiple masses $m_i$ at heights $h_i$, assume a **shape function** $\psi_i(x)$ (typically the first mode shape), and write:

$$\tilde{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i\, \psi_i^2, \quad \tilde{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k_i\, \psi_i^2, \quad \tilde{p} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i\, \psi_i$$

The reduced EOM is:

$$\tilde{m}\ddot{u} + \tilde{c}\dot{u} + \tilde{k}\, u = \tilde{p}(t)$$

This is the **static condensation / Rayleigh-Ritz** route to a Generalized SDOF (GSDoF).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law | $\sum F = m\ddot{u}$ | N, kg, m/s┬▓ | Real forces only |
| 2 | D'Alembert's Eq. | $\sum F - m\ddot{u} = 0$ | N | Fictitious inertia force |
| 3 | Inertia Force | $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$ | N | Opposes acceleration |
| 4 | Spring Restoring Force | $F_S = -k u$ | N | Opposes displacement |
| 5 | Dashpot Damping Force | $F_D = -c \dot{u}$ | N | Opposes velocity |
| 6 | Unforced SDOF | $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = 0$ | ŌĆö | Free vibration |
| 7 | Force-excited SDOF | $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = p(t)$ | ŌĆö | External load |
| 8 | Base-excited SDOF | $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = -m\ddot{u}_g$ | ŌĆö | **Earthquake eqn** |
| 9 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Fundamental property |
| 10 | Damped Frequency | $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$ | rad/s | $\xi = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ |
| 11 | Damping Ratio | $\xi = c/c_{cr}$ | dimensionless | $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km}$ |
| 12 | Generalized Mass | $\tilde{m} = \sum m_i \psi_i^2$ | kg | Lumped or distributed |
| 13 | Generalized Stiffness | $\tilde{k} = \sum k_i \psi_i^2$ | N/m | RayleighŌĆōRitz |
| 14 | Generalized Force | $\tilde{p} = \sum p_i \psi_i$ | N | Modal reduction |
| 15 | Total motion relation | $u^t = u + u_g$ | m | Kinetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Pitfall:** Never confuse **total** displacement $u^t$ with **relative** displacement $u$. In the equation $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = -m\ddot{u}_g$, the LHS contains $u$ (relative) but the inertia term uses total acceleration $\ddot{u}^t = \ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g$. Many students write $-m u$ on the RHS ŌĆö that is a guaranteed **ŌłÆ2 to ŌłÆ3 mark** deduction.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Earthquake Engineering

1. **Seismic Coefficient Method (IS 1893:2016, Cl. 6.4.2):** Design base shear $V_B = A_h W$ is essentially the maximum of the SDOF response $m \cdot \text{Sa}(T,\xi)$.
2. **Response Spectrum Analysis (RSA):** The whole multi-storey building is *modal-superposed* from individual SDOFs. The EOM derived here is the **building block** of RSA.
3. **Capacity Spectrum Method (ATC-40):** Pushover is converted to an equivalent SDOF using a Generalized-SDOF reduction.
4. **Soil-Structure Interaction (SSI):** Foundation springs and dashpots simply *modify* $k$ and $c$ in the EOM.
5. **Equipment Design (IS 1893 Part 4):** Mechanical/electrical equipment on floors is treated as a tuned-mass-damper, a two-DOF generalization of the same equation.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö SDOF under Horizontal Force (Newton's 2nd Law)

**System:** A rigid block of mass $m$ supported by a massless column of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper $c$. A horizontal force $p(t)$ acts at the top.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the coordinate.** Let $u(t)$ be the horizontal displacement of the mass relative to the un-deformed position of the base.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Draw the FBD and identify all real forces acting on the mass.**

| Force | Symbol | Magnitude | Direction (assumed +ve to the right) |
|---|---|---|---|
| External applied force | $p(t)$ | $p(t)$ | + |
| Spring restoring force | $F_S$ | $k u$ | ŌłÆ (opposes displacement) |
| Damping force | $F_D$ | $c \dot{u}$ | ŌłÆ (opposes velocity) |
| Weight & reaction | $W, R$ | ŌĆö | Vertical, do not enter $\sum F_x$ |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Newton's 2nd Law.** Sum of *real* forces = mass ├Ś acceleration:

$$\sum F_x = m\ddot{u}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute forces with their magnitudes and signs.**

$$p(t) - k u - c \dot{u} = m\ddot{u}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange into the canonical form.**

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = p(t) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Same SDOF, via D'Alembert's Principle

**Step 1 ŌĆö Same FBD, but now add the fictitious inertia force** $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$ acting at the mass, **opposite** to the assumed positive direction of acceleration.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write static equilibrium** of all forces (real + fictitious):

$$\sum F_x = 0$$

$$p(t) - k u - c \dot{u} - m\ddot{u} = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rearrange.**

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = p(t) \quad \blacksquare$$

The two methods yield **identical** equations, confirming D'Alembert's principle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö SDOF under Base Excitation (Earthquake EOM)

This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU ESE Module 1.**

**System:** The base of the previous SDOF is now moved by $u_g(t)$, the **ground displacement** during an earthquake.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the *relative* displacement** of the mass w.r.t. the base:

$$u(t) = u^t(t) - u_g(t)$$

where $u^t(t)$ is the absolute (total) displacement of the mass.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate twice** to get kinematic relations:

$$\dot{u}^t = \dot{u} + \dot{u}_g$$
$$\ddot{u}^t = \ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Draw the FBD.** The mass *physically* experiences the total acceleration $\ddot{u}^t$. Therefore the inertia force is $F_I = -m\ddot{u}^t = -m(\ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g)$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply D'Alembert:**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -k u - c \dot{u} - m(\ddot{u}+\ddot{u}_g) = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange:**

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = -m\ddot{u}_g(t) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The earthquake does **not** apply a force on the structure; it imposes a *kinematic constraint* on the base. The mass, by virtue of its own inertia, *resists* the base motion, generating the effective lateral force $-m\ddot{u}_g$. This is the **origin of inertial seismic forces** in IS 1893.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Reduction of a Two-Mass System to a Generalized SDOF

**Problem Setup:** A two-storey building has floor masses $m_1$ and $m_2$, inter-storey stiffnesses $k_1$ (groundŌåÆL1) and $k_2$ (L1ŌåÆL2). Under a static lateral load profile $p_1, p_2$, the first-mode shape is approximated as $\psi_1 = 1.0$ and $\psi_2 = 1.5$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume a shape function** $\psi_i$ (e.g., first mode of undamped free vibration, normalized so that $\psi$ at the reference DOF = 1).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express each floor's displacement** as $u_i(t) = \psi_i \cdot q(t)$, where $q(t)$ is the new **generalized coordinate**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Lagrange's / RayleighŌĆōRitz energy method** gives the reduced coefficients:

$$\tilde{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} m_i \psi_i^2 = m_1(1)^2 + m_2(1.5)^2 = m_1 + 2.25\, m_2$$

$$\tilde{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} k_i (\psi_i - \psi_{i-1})^2 = k_1(1)^2 + k_2(1.5-1)^2 = k_1 + 0.25\, k_2$$

$$\tilde{p}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} p_i(t) \psi_i$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Write the Generalized SDOF EOM:**

$$\tilde{m}\,\ddot{q} + \tilde{c}\,\dot{q} + \tilde{k}\, q = \tilde{p}(t) \quad \blacksquare$$

This 2-DOF problem is now solved exactly as a single SDOF ŌĆö a 90 % reduction in computational effort while preserving first-mode accuracy.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Generalized SDOF for a Uniform Cantilever Chimney

**System:** A chimney of total height $H$, distributed mass per unit length $\bar{m}$, flexural stiffness $EI$. Subjected to uniform lateral acceleration $\ddot{u}_g$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume the deflected shape under first-mode vibration:**

$$\psi(x) = 1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{2H}\right)$$

This satisfies $\psi(0) = 0$ (fixed at base) and $\psi'(0) = 0$ (no slope at base).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Generalized mass** (continuous integration):

$$\tilde{m} = \int_0^H \bar{m}\, \psi^2(x)\, dx = \int_0^H \bar{m}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\tfrac{\pi x}{2H}\right]^2 dx$$

Expand the square:

$$\left[1 - \cos\theta\right]^2 = 1 - 2\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1 - 2\cos\theta + \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$$

Integrate over $0 \le x \le H$ (so $\theta$ goes $0 \to \pi/2$):

$$\tilde{m} = \bar{m} \int_0^H \left[\tfrac{3}{2} - 2\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\cos 2\theta\right] dx$$

$$\tilde{m} = \bar{m} \left[\tfrac{3H}{2} - 2 \cdot \frac{2H}{\pi} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2H}{\pi} \cdot \tfrac{2}{2}\cdot 0\right]$$

After careful evaluation:

$$\tilde{m} = \bar{m} H \left[\tfrac{3}{2} - \frac{4}{\pi} + \frac{1}{\pi}\right] = \bar{m} H \left[\tfrac{3}{2} - \frac{3}{\pi}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{m} \approx 0.545\,\bar{m}H}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Generalized stiffness** (strain energy of the assumed shape):

$$\tilde{k} = \int_0^H EI\, [\psi''(x)]^2\, dx$$

For the chosen $\psi$:

$$\psi''(x) = \left(\frac{\pi}{2H}\right)^2 \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{2H}\right)$$

Substitute and integrate:

$$\tilde{k} = EI \int_0^H \left(\frac{\pi}{2H}\right)^4 \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{2H}\right) dx$$

$$= EI \left(\frac{\pi}{2H}\right)^4 \cdot \frac{H}{2} = \frac{EI \pi^4}{32 H^3}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Generalized force** for seismic excitation:

$$\tilde{p}(t) = -\ddot{u}_g(t) \int_0^H \bar{m}\, \psi(x)\, dx$$

$$\int_0^H \psi(x)\, dx = H\!\left[1 - \frac{2}{\pi}\right] \approx 0.363\, H$$

Therefore:

$$\tilde{p}(t) = -0.363\,\bar{m}H\,\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final GSDoF EOM:**

$$\boxed{0.545\,\bar{m}H\,\ddot{q} + \tilde{c}\dot{q} + \frac{\pi^4 EI}{32 H^3}\,q = -0.363\,\bar{m}H\,\ddot{u}_g(t)}$$

This is the standard form used in **chimney and stack design** (IS 1893 Part 4, ACI 307).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Newton vs. D'Alembert

| Aspect | Newton's 2nd Law | D'Alembert's Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | 1687, *Principia* | 1743, *Trait├® de dynamique* |
| Equation form | $\sum F_{\text{ext}} = m\ddot{u}$ | $\sum F_{\text{ext}} - m\ddot{u} = 0$ |
| Inertia force | Not explicit; appears as $m\ddot{u}$ | Explicit fictitious force $-m\ddot{u}$ |
| Suitable for | Translation, rigid-body motion | FBDs, FEM assembly, energy methods |
| Marks awarded in KTU ESE | 7 marks (often paired sub-part) | 7 marks |
| Common error | Forgetting sign of $c\dot{u}$ | Sign error on $F_I$ |

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Generalized SDOF Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Multi-DOF Structure] --> B[Discretize Masses m1 m2 ... mN]
    B --> C[Assume Shape Function Psi x]
    C --> D[Compute Generalized Mass tilde m]
    B --> E[Compute Generalized Stiffness tilde k]
    A --> F[Apply External Loads p t]
    F --> G[Compute Generalized Force tilde p t]
    D --> H[Assemble GSDoF EOM]
    E --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Solve as Single SDOF]
    I --> J[Recover Physical Response u_i = Psi_i times q t]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid FBD ŌĆö Base-Excited SDOF (D'Alembert's Real + Inertia Forces)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Base] -->|Stiffness k| B[Mass m]
    B -->|Damper c| A
    B -->|Inertia Force -m x u_ddot + u_g_ddot| C[FBD Cut]
    B -->|Restoring Force -k u| C
    B -->|Damping Force -c u_dot| C
    C --> D[Static Equilibrium: Sum F = 0]
    D --> E[EOM: m u_ddot + c u_dot + k u = -m u_g_ddot]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid FBD ŌĆö Newton's 2nd Law SDOF under Horizontal Force

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ground] -->|Spring k, Damper c| B[Mass m]
    B -->|External Force p t rightward| B
    B -->|Inertia m u_ddot rightward| C[Sum of Real Forces]
    B -->|Restoring k u leftward| C
    B -->|Damping c u_dot leftward| C
    C --> D[Newton: Sum F = m u_ddot]
    D --> E[EOM: m u_ddot + c u_dot + k u = p t]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map ŌĆö Methods of EOM Formulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equation of Motion] --> B[Newton Second Law]
    A --> C[D'Alembert Principle]
    A --> D[Lagrange Energy Method]
    A --> E[Hamilton Principle]
    A --> F[Principle of Virtual Work]
    B --> G[Sum of Real Forces = m x acceleration]
    C --> H[Add Fictitious Inertia Force to obtain Static Equilibrium]
    D --> I[d/dt partial T/partial u_dot - partial T/partial u + partial V/partial u = Q]
    E --> J[Variational Statement over Time Interval]
    F --> K[Internal Virtual Work equals External Virtual Work]
    G --> L[Equivalent Canonical SDOF EOM]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Generalized SDOF Reduction of a 3-Storey Building

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3 Storey Shear Building] --> B[Identify Floor Masses m1 m2 m3]
    B --> C[Identify Inter Storey Stiffness k1 k2 k3]
    A --> D[Free Vibration Mode Shape Extraction]
    D --> E[Normalize Mode 1 to Psi_1 = 1 at Roof]
    E --> F[Compute tilde m = sum mi Psi_i squared]
    C --> G[Compute tilde k = sum ki bracket Psi_i minus Psi_i_minus_1 squared]
    F --> H[Assemble GSDoF EOM]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Evaluate tilde omega_n = sqrt tilde k over tilde m]
    I --> J[Compute Seismic Coefficient A_h = Sa tilde T over g]
    J --> K[Base Shear V_B = tilde m x A_h x g]
```
