# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*State D'Alembert's principle. Using it, derive the equation of motion of a single-storey shear building subjected to a horizontal force $p(t)$ at the roof level. The building has roof mass $m$, lateral stiffness $k$ and damping coefficient $c$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D'Alembert's Principle (1 mark):**
> *"A moving system can be reduced to a statically equivalent system by adding to the real forces a fictitious inertia force equal in magnitude to the product of mass and acceleration, and opposite in direction."* Mathematically: $\sum F_{\text{ext}} - m\ddot{u} = 0$.

**FBD and EOM (2 marks):**
The FBD shows (i) applied force $p(t)$ rightward, (ii) spring force $k u$ leftward, (iii) damping force $c\dot{u}$ leftward, (iv) inertia force $-m\ddot{u}$ rightward.

Equilibrium:
$$p(t) - k u - c\dot{u} - m\ddot{u} = 0$$

$$\boxed{m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = p(t)}$$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system and a Generalized SDOF system. Give two real-world examples of a GSDoF from earthquake engineering.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SDOF | Generalized SDOF |
|---|---|---|
| Mass distribution | Lumped at one point | Lumped at multiple points or distributed |
| Coordinate | Physical displacement at one node | A *generalized* coordinate obtained by shape-function reduction |
| Reduction | None required | Static condensation or modal reduction |

**Examples (1 mark each, any two):**
1. An elevated water tank on a slender staging ŌĆö the water (convective + impulsive masses) and staging mass are lumped at multiple levels, reduced to one coordinate at the top of staging.
2. A multi-storey frame idealized as a flexural cantilever ŌĆö distributed mass is reduced via assumed first-mode shape to a single EOM.
3. A chimney under seismic load ŌĆö continuous mass, continuous stiffness, single generalized coordinate = tip displacement.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö *D'Alembert-Based EOM for Base-Excited SDOF*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the equation of motion of a damped SDOF system subjected to base excitation $\ddot{u}_g(t)$ using D'Alembert's principle. Clearly show the FBD and the sign convention.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A single-storey industrial shed has roof mass $m = 2000\ \text{kg}$, lateral stiffness $k = 800\ \text{kN/m}$, and damping ratio $\xi = 5\%$. It is subjected to a ground acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t) = 0.2\,g\,\sin(10\,t)\ \text{m/s}^2$. Compute (i) the natural frequency $\omega_n$, (ii) the steady-state amplitude of the relative roof displacement $u(t)$.*

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define coordinate [1 mark]:** Let $u(t)$ = horizontal displacement of mass *relative* to the moving base. Total absolute displacement: $u^t(t) = u(t) + u_g(t)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö FBD and force identification [2 marks]:** Draw the mass $m$ with:
- Spring force: $F_S = -k u$ (leftward, opposes $u$)
- Damping force: $F_D = -c\dot{u}$ (leftward, opposes $\dot{u}$)
- Inertia force: $F_I = -m\ddot{u}^t = -m(\ddot{u}+\ddot{u}_g)$ (fictitious, leftward opposite to absolute acceleration)

**Step 3 ŌĆö D'Alembert equilibrium [2 marks]:**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -ku - c\dot{u} - m(\ddot{u}+\ddot{u}_g) = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange [2 marks]:**

$$\boxed{m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = -m\ddot{u}_g(t)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation (extra remark for ┬Į mark):** The RHS is the *effective* earthquake force. It depends only on the *mass* and the *ground acceleration*, not on the stiffness of the structure.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $m = 2000\ \text{kg}$, $k = 800{,}000\ \text{N/m}$, $\xi = 0.05$
- $\ddot{u}_g(t) = 0.2\,g\,\sin(10\,t) = 1.962\,\sin(10\,t)\ \text{m/s}^2$ (using $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$)
- Excitation frequency $\omega = 10\ \text{rad/s}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Natural frequency [2 marks]:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{800{,}000}{2000}} = \sqrt{400} = 20\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency ratio and amplification [2 marks]:**

$$\beta = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{10}{20} = 0.5$$

For *base excitation*, the displacement transmissibility for steady-state is:

$$\left\vert \frac{u}{u_g} \right\vert = \frac{\beta^2}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\xi\beta)^2}}$$

Compute:
- $1 - \beta^2 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$
- $(1-\beta^2)^2 = 0.5625$
- $2\xi\beta = 2(0.05)(0.5) = 0.05$
- $(2\xi\beta)^2 = 0.0025$
- Denominator $= \sqrt{0.5625 + 0.0025} = \sqrt{0.5650} = 0.7517$

$$\left\vert \frac{u}{u_g} \right\vert = \frac{0.25}{0.7517} = 0.3326$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Ground displacement amplitude [1 mark]:**

$$u_g = \frac{\ddot{u}_{g,0}}{\omega^2} = \frac{1.962}{(10)^2} = 0.01962\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Steady-state amplitude of $u$ [2 marks]:**

$$u_0 = 0.3326 \times 0.01962 = 6.53 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \approx 6.53\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{u_0 \approx 6.53\ \text{mm}}$$

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Generalized SDOF Reduction*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *A two-storey shear building has floor masses $m_1 = 2000\ \text{kg}$ (L1) and $m_2 = 1500\ \text{kg}$ (roof), and inter-storey stiffnesses $k_1 = 1500\ \text{kN/m}$ and $k_2 = 900\ \text{kN/m}$. Using the assumed first-mode shape $\psi_1 = 1.0$ and $\psi_2 = 2.0$, derive the Generalized SDOF equation of motion when the base is excited by $\ddot{u}_g(t)$. Clearly state the generalized mass, stiffness and effective force.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compute the natural time period of the resulting GSDoF. If the design spectrum ordinate at this period is $S_a/g = 2.5$, compute the design base shear. State two assumptions of the Generalized SDOF formulation.*

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö Shape vector [1 mark]:** $\boldsymbol{\psi} = [\psi_1, \psi_2]^T = [1.0, 2.0]^T$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Generalized mass [2 marks]:**

$$\tilde{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} m_i \psi_i^2 = 2000(1)^2 + 1500(2)^2 = 2000 + 6000 = 8000\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Generalized stiffness [2 marks]:**

$$\tilde{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} k_i (\psi_i - \psi_{i-1})^2$$

where $\psi_0 = 0$. Therefore:

$$\tilde{k} = k_1(1-0)^2 + k_2(2-1)^2 = 1500{,}000(1) + 900{,}000(1) = 2{,}400{,}000\ \text{N/m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Effective earthquake force [2 marks]:**

$$\tilde{p}(t) = -\tilde{L}\,\ddot{u}_g(t), \quad \tilde{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} m_i \psi_i = 2000(1) + 1500(2) = 5000\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö GSDoF EOM:**

$$\boxed{8000\,\ddot{q} + \tilde{c}\dot{q} + 2.4\times 10^6\, q = -5000\,\ddot{u}_g(t)}$$

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö Natural frequency [2 marks]:**

$$\tilde{\omega}_n = \sqrt{\frac{\tilde{k}}{\tilde{m}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}400{,}000}{8000}} = \sqrt{300} = 17.32\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Natural period [1 mark]:**

$$\tilde{T}_n = \frac{2\pi}{\tilde{\omega}_n} = \frac{2\pi}{17.32} = 0.363\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effective modal mass [1 mark]:** $m^* = \tilde{L}^2 / \tilde{m} = (5000)^2/8000 = 3125\ \text{kg}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Design base shear [2 marks]:**

$$V_B = m^* \cdot S_a = 3125 \times (2.5 \times 9.81) = 3125 \times 24.525 = 76{,}640\ \text{N} \approx 76.6\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Two assumptions of GSDoF [1 mark]:**
1. The structure responds predominantly in its fundamental mode.
2. The assumed shape function $\psi_i$ is a valid approximation of the first-mode shape (typically the deflected shape under a static lateral load).

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme patterns:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention on $u_g$:** Many students write $+m\ddot{u}_g$ on the RHS instead of $-m\ddot{u}_g$. The sign is **negative** because the *inertia force opposes* the base acceleration direction. **ŌłÆ2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing Total vs. Relative Motion:** The LHS of the EOM contains $u$ (relative), but the inertia force uses total acceleration $\ddot{u}^t = \ddot{u}+\ddot{u}_g$. Writing $-m u$ on the RHS costs full credit. **ŌłÆ3 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting to State Assumptions in GSDoF:** Always write: (i) first-mode dominance, (ii) shape function validity, (iii) classical damping. **ŌłÆ1 to ŌłÆ2 marks.**
> 4. **Missing Units in Numerical Sub-parts:** ESE Module 1 numericals demand SI units throughout. Carrying $k$ in kN/m while $m$ is in kg without $g$ conversion is a frequent error. **ŌłÆ1 mark.**
> 5. **Skipping the FBD:** The FBD carries **at least 2 marks** in any 7-mark derivation. Skipping it = immediate **ŌłÆ2 marks** deduction.
> 6. **Mixing D'Alembert and Newton:** Don't write both methods in one answer; examiners deduct for *ambiguity*, not for redundancy.

---

## 5.4 ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **SDOF EOM canonical form:** $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = p(t)$ for force excitation; $= -m\ddot{u}_g$ for base excitation.
- [x] **Newton's 2nd Law:** $\sum F_{\text{real}} = m\ddot{u}$. No fictitious force is added.
- [x] **D'Alembert's Principle:** Add inertia force $-m\ddot{u}$ (or $-m\ddot{u}^t$ for base motion), then write $\sum F = 0$.
- [x] **Generalized SDOF:** Reduce any multi-DOF / continuous system using a shape function $\psi_i$; coefficients are weighted sums: $\tilde{m} = \sum m_i\psi_i^2$, $\tilde{k} = \sum k_i(\Delta\psi_i)^2$, $\tilde{p} = \sum p_i\psi_i$.
- [x] **Earthquake EOM signature:** Always RHS = $-m\ddot{u}_g$ (negative sign is non-negotiable in KTU answers).
- [x] **Kinematic relation:** $u^t = u + u_g$ ŌĆö total, relative, ground. Memorise this; it is asked in 80% of KTU derivations.
- [x] **Modal mass ratio** for GSDoF: $m^* = \tilde{L}^2/\tilde{m}$, where $\tilde{L} = \sum m_i \psi_i$.
- [x] **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (lumped) or $\sqrt{\tilde{k}/\tilde{m}}$ (generalized).
- [x] **Damping ratio:** $\xi = c/(2\sqrt{km}) = c/c_{cr}$.
- [x] **Real-world GSDoF examples:** Elevated water tank on staging, multi-storey frame, cantilever chimney, gravity dam.
- [x] **Exam FBD rule:** Always draw the FBD before any equation. Carries 2 marks.
- [x] **Sign discipline:** Spring force $-ku$, damping force $-c\dot{u}$, inertia force $-m\ddot{u}$ ŌĆö all *oppose* the corresponding kinematic variable. Never mix signs.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook (for the night before exam):**
> *"Mass times the SECOND derivative of RELATIVE motion, plus damping times the FIRST derivative, plus stiffness times the displacement, equals the NEGATIVE of mass times the ground acceleration."*
> Symbolically: $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = -m\ddot{u}_g$. This single sentence is the heartbeat of Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution of the Equation of Motion - Undamped Free Vibration - Damped Free Vibration - Critically Damped, Under Damped and Over Damped SDOF Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solution of the Equation of Motion - Undamped Free Vibration - Damped Free Vibration - Critically Damped, Under Damped and Over Damped SDOF Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System ŌĆō Formal Definition

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system** is an idealized dynamic system whose displaced configuration at any instant can be completely defined by a single independent displacement coordinate. In the context of **earthquake resistant design**, a multi-storey building is often approximated as an **equivalent SDOF system** for preliminary dynamic analysis because the first (fundamental) mode of vibration dominates the seismic response.

The three fundamental properties of an SDOF system are:
- **Mass (m)**: A measure of the inertia of the system, expressed in **kg** or **N┬Ęs┬▓/m**.
- **Stiffness (k)**: A measure of the resistance of the system to deformation, expressed in **N/m** or **kN/m**.
- **Damping coefficient (c)**: A measure of the energy dissipation of the system, expressed in **N┬Ęs/m**.

## 1.2 Equation of Motion ŌĆō Formal Definition

The **Equation of Motion (EOM)** of a linear SDOF system subjected to an external time-varying force $p(t)$ is derived by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** (or D'Alembert's principle) to the mass element:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = p(t)$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ = relative displacement of the mass with respect to time $t$ (in **m**)
- $\dot{x}(t)$ = relative velocity (in **m/s**)
- $\ddot{x}(t)$ = relative acceleration (in **m/s┬▓**)
- $p(t)$ = external dynamic force (in **N**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** When the external force $p(t) = 0$, the system undergoes **Free Vibration**. This is the foundation upon which all forced vibration (including earthquake) analyses are built. The student must master free-vibration solutions thoroughly.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆō The "Building on a Shaking Table"

Imagine a **small concrete block (mass m)** sitting on a **flexible rubber column (stiffness k)**. The block is submerged in a **viscous fluid (damper c)**. When the base of the column is suddenly nudged and released, the block will oscillate up and down before coming to rest.

- The **rubber column** stores potential energy (stiffness term $kx$).
- The **block's inertia** makes it want to keep moving (mass term $m\ddot{x}$).
- The **viscous fluid** drags on the block, dissipating its energy as heat (damping term $c\dot{x}$).

For an actual building under an earthquake, the **ground motion $\ddot{x}_g(t)$** acts as the base excitation. The relative displacement of the roof with respect to the ground is the variable of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Earthquake Engineering Context:** In seismic design, $p(t)$ is replaced by $-m\ddot{x}_g(t)$ (the effective earthquake force), making the equation of motion:
> $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{x}_g(t)$$
> The left-hand side is the **free vibration operator**, and the right-hand side is the **seismic forcing function**.

## 1.4 Free Vibration ŌĆō The Starting Point

**Free vibration** is the natural oscillatory response of a system after being displaced from its equilibrium position and released, with no external force acting on it thereafter. The mathematical condition is $p(t) = 0$, which simplifies the equation of motion to a **homogeneous second-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Free Vibration Response

The behaviour of an SDOF system in free vibration is governed by the magnitude of the **damping ratio (╬Č)**. The damping ratio is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of the actual damping coefficient $c$ to the **critical damping coefficient $c_{cr}$**.

The three regimes are:
- **Undamped (c = 0, ╬Č = 0):** No energy dissipation; oscillation continues forever.
- **Underdamped (0 < ╬Č < 1):** Insufficient damping; oscillation decays exponentially.
- **Critically Damped (╬Č = 1):** The threshold case; fastest return to equilibrium without oscillation.
- **Overdamped (╬Č > 1):** Excessive damping; slow, non-oscillatory return to equilibrium.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 ŌĆō The Undamped System (c = 0):**
- The equation reduces to $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$.
- Dividing by $m$: $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$, where $\omega_n^2 = k/m$.
- The auxiliary equation is $s^2 + \omega_n^2 = 0$, giving purely imaginary roots $s = \pm i\omega_n$.
- The general solution is harmonic: $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆō The Damped System (c > 0):**
- The full equation is $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$.
- Dividing by $m$: $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$, where $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$.
- The auxiliary (characteristic) equation is $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$.
- The roots are: $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$.
- The sign of $\zeta^2 - 1$ decides the three sub-cases.

**Step 3 ŌĆō Case Analysis on the Discriminant:**
- If $\zeta^2 - 1 < 0$ (i.e., $\zeta < 1$): **Underdamped** ŌåÆ complex conjugate roots ŌåÆ decaying oscillation.
- If $\zeta^2 - 1 = 0$ (i.e., $\zeta = 1$): **Critically Damped** ŌåÆ repeated real root ŌåÆ no oscillation, fastest decay.
- If $\zeta^2 - 1 > 0$ (i.e., $\zeta > 1$): **Overdamped** ŌåÆ two distinct real roots ŌåÆ no oscillation, slow decay.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **essential cheat-sheet** for solving all free-vibration problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Undamped natural circular frequency | $\omega_n$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | **rad/s** |
| Undamped natural period | $T_n$ | $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ | **s** |
| Natural frequency (cyclic) | $f_n$ | $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi) = 1/T_n$ | **Hz** |
| Critical damping coefficient | $c_{cr}$ | $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | **N┬Ęs/m** |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta$ | $\zeta = c/c_{cr} = c/(2m\omega_n)$ | dimensionless |
| Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d$ | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | **rad/s** |
| Damped natural period | $T_d$ | $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ | **s** |
| Logarithmic decrement | $\delta$ | $\delta = (2\pi\zeta)/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | dimensionless |
| Roots of characteristic equation | $s_{1,2}$ | $s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The **damped natural period $T_d$** is a key input in **IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016** for the design spectrum. Most civil structures have $\zeta$ between **0.02 and 0.05** (2 %ŌĆō5 %), making them **underdamped**. The structural engineer must verify that the building period does not coincide with the dominant period of the ground motion (a phenomenon called **resonance**).

## 2.4 Physical Interpretation of the Three Damping Regimes

- **Underdamped (╬Č < 1):** The system oscillates (crosses equilibrium) with progressively smaller amplitude. This is the most common case for buildings, bridges, and chimneys.
- **Critically Damped (╬Č = 1):** The system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without oscillating. This is the *ideal* design condition for shock absorbers and gun recoil mechanisms.
- **Overdamped (╬Č > 1):** The system returns to equilibrium slowly without oscillating. Found in heavily cushioned equipment mounts and base-isolated structures during extreme events.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equation of Motion of an SDOF System (D'Alembert's Principle)

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a viscous damper (coefficient $c$) and a linear spring (stiffness $k$). The free-body diagram yields three forces:
- Inertia force: $F_I = m\ddot{x}$ (opposing acceleration).
- Damping force: $F_D = c\dot{x}$ (opposing velocity).
- Spring force: $F_S = kx$ (opposing displacement).

By **D'Alembert's principle** (or Newton's second law for a dynamic equilibrium):

$$\sum F = m\ddot{x}$$

Taking forces opposing motion as negative and the restoring forces as positive:

$$-F_I - F_D - F_S = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

This is the **free-vibration equation of motion**. Adding an external force $p(t)$ on the right side gives the **forced-vibration equation**.

## 3.2 Solution of the Undamped Free Vibration (c = 0, ╬Č = 0)

**Starting Equation:**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 1:** Divide throughout by $m$ to normalize the equation:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $k/m = \omega_n^2$ (the square of the undamped natural frequency). Substituting:

$$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 3:** Assume a trial solution of the form $x(t) = e^{st}$. Substituting gives:

$$s^2 e^{st} + \omega_n^2 e^{st} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (s^2 + \omega_n^2) e^{st} = 0$$

Since $e^{st} \neq 0$, the characteristic equation is:

$$s^2 + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $s$:

$$s = \pm \sqrt{-\omega_n^2} = \pm i\omega_n$$

**Step 5:** The general solution is a linear combination of the two independent solutions:

$$x(t) = A e^{i\omega_n t} + B e^{-i\omega_n t}$$

**Step 6:** Apply Euler's identity, $e^{\pm i\theta} = \cos\theta \pm i\sin\theta$, to convert to real form:

$$x(t) = (A + B)\cos(\omega_n t) + i(A - B)\sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 7:** Redefining the arbitrary complex constants as real constants $C_1$ and $C_2$:

$$\boxed{x(t) = C_1 \cos(\omega_n t) + C_2 \sin(\omega_n t)}$$

**Step 8:** The constants are determined by initial conditions. At $t = 0$:
- $x(0) = x_0 = C_1 \cos(0) + C_2 \sin(0) = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = x_0$.
- $\dot{x}(0) = v_0 = -C_1 \omega_n \sin(0) + C_2 \omega_n \cos(0) = C_2 \omega_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = v_0/\omega_n$.

**Final Undamped Solution:**

$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$$

## 3.3 Solution of the Damped Free Vibration (c > 0)

**Starting Equation (general):**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 1:** Divide by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $c/m = 2\zeta\omega_n$ and $k/m = \omega_n^2$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 3:** Assume $x(t) = e^{st}$. Substituting and simplifying:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Apply the quadratic formula to obtain the two roots:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 5:** Define the three regimes based on the sign of $\zeta^2 - 1$.

### 3.3.1 Case 1: Underdamped System (╬Č < 1)

The discriminant is negative, so $\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} = i\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$. Define the **damped natural frequency**:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

The roots become:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$$

**Step A:** The general solution is:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

**Step B:** Apply initial conditions: $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$.
- $x(0) = A = x_0$.
- Differentiating and setting $t = 0$ gives: $B = (v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0)/\omega_d$.

**Final Underdamped Solution:**

$$\boxed{x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[x_0 \cos(\omega_d t) + \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\sin(\omega_d t)\right]}$$

**Step C:** The amplitude decays as $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$. The number of cycles required for the amplitude to reduce to $1/e$ of its initial value is governed by the **logarithmic decrement**:

$$\delta = \ln\frac{x(t)}{x(t + T_d)} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$$

### 3.3.2 Case 2: Critically Damped System (╬Č = 1)

The discriminant is zero, giving a **repeated real root**:

$$s_1 = s_2 = -\omega_n$$

**Step A:** For a repeated root, the general solution takes the form:

$$\boxed{x(t) = (A + Bt)\,e^{-\omega_n t}}$$

**Step B:** Apply initial conditions: $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$.
- $x(0) = A = x_0$.
- Differentiating: $\dot{x}(t) = Be^{-\omega_n t} - \omega_n(A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$.
- $\dot{x}(0) = B - \omega_n A = v_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = v_0 + \omega_n x_0$.

**Final Critically Damped Solution:**

$$x(t) = \left[x_0 + (v_0 + \omega_n x_0)t\right]e^{-\omega_n t}$$

**Step C:** No oscillation occurs. The system returns to equilibrium monotonically in the **shortest possible time**.

### 3.3.3 Case 3: Overdamped System (╬Č > 1)

The discriminant is positive, giving **two distinct negative real roots**:

$$s_1 = -\zeta\omega_n + \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}, \qquad s_2 = -\zeta\omega_n - \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

Both roots are negative (since $\zeta > 1$), so both exponential terms decay.

**Step A:** The general solution is:

$$\boxed{x(t) = A\,e^{s_1 t} + B\,e^{s_2 t}}$$

**Step B:** Apply initial conditions: $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$.
- $x(0) = A + B = x_0$.
- $\dot{x}(0) = As_1 + Bs_2 = v_0$.

Solving simultaneously:

$$A = \frac{v_0 - s_2 x_0}{s_1 - s_2}, \qquad B = \frac{s_1 x_0 - v_0}{s_1 - s_2}$$

**Step C:** No oscillation occurs. The system returns to equilibrium **more slowly** than the critically damped case.

## 3.4 Summary Matrix of Free-Vibration Solutions

| Regime | Damping Ratio | Roots $s_{1,2}$ | Displacement $x(t)$ | Oscillates? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | $\pm i\omega_n$ | $x_0\cos\omega_n t + (v_0/\omega_n)\sin\omega_n t$ | **Yes** (constant amplitude) |
| Underdamped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | $-\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$ | $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}[x_0\cos\omega_d t + (v_0+\zeta\omega_n x_0)/\omega_d \cdot \sin\omega_d t]$ | **Yes** (decaying amplitude) |
| Critically Damped | $\zeta = 1$ | $-\omega_n$ (repeated) | $(A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$ | **No** (fastest non-oscillatory) |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | $-\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2-1}$ | $Ae^{s_1 t} + Be^{s_2 t}$ | **No** (slow non-oscillatory) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Idealized SDOF System Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the functional block-level architecture of a damped SDOF system, showing the three force elements and the energy flow from base excitation to relative displacement.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[External Force p of t] --> nodeB[Mass m]
    nodeC[Inertia Force m x double dot] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[Damping Element c]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Spring Element k]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Fixed Base or Ground]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Relative Displacement x of t]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Relative Velocity x dot of t]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Relative Acceleration x double dot of t]
    nodeI --> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows denote the **flow of mechanical action**: an external force excites the mass, the mass responds with displacement/velocity/acceleration, and the inertia force feeds back into the dynamic equilibrium.

## 4.2 Free Vibration Analysis Flow Chart

The following Mermaid diagram outlines the **decision tree** that a KTU examiner expects the student to follow when classifying the response regime.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start Free Vibration Analysis] --> compA[Compute c_cr as 2 times sqrt of k times m]
    compA --> compB[Compute damping ratio zeta as c divided by c_cr]
    compB --> judge{Is zeta less than 1?}
    judge -->|Yes| underA[Underdamped Regime]
    judge -->|No| judge2{Is zeta exactly 1?}
    judge2 -->|Yes| critA[Critically Damped Regime]
    judge2 -->|No| overA[Overdamped Regime]
    underA --> solA[Use exponential decay times harmonic solution]
    critA --> solB[Use polynomial times exponential solution]
    overA --> solC[Use sum of two real exponentials]
    solA --> endA[Final response x of t]
    solB --> endA
    solC --> endA
```

## 4.3 Damping Regime Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    regA[UNDAMPED zeta = 0] --> descA[Pure Sinusoidal Motion]
    regB[UNDERDAMPED 0 less than zeta less than 1] --> descB[Decaying Oscillation]
    regC[CRITICAL zeta = 1] --> descC[Fastest Non oscillatory Decay]
    regD[OVERDAMPED zeta greater than 1] --> descD[Slow Non oscillatory Decay]
    descA --> timeA[Time Axis]
    descB --> timeA
    descC --> timeA
    descD --> timeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualization Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "sketch the free-vibration response", the student must clearly distinguish the four cases on a displacement-versus-time graph. The undamped case has **constant amplitude**; the underdamped case shows **envelope curves** $\pm e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ enclosing the decaying sinusoid; the critical and overdamped cases are **monotonically decaying curves** with no envelope.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆō Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define damping ratio. What is its significance in the dynamic analysis of structures?**

**Model Answer:**
The **damping ratio ($\zeta$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual damping coefficient $c$ to the critical damping coefficient $c_{cr}$ of the system.

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n}$$

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **regime** of free vibration (underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped).
2. It governs the **rate of decay** of free vibration amplitude in underdamped systems.
3. It directly controls the **resonant amplification factor** in forced vibration; a higher $\zeta$ gives a flatter, less peaked response curve.
4. In seismic design (IS 1893), $\zeta$ is assumed as **5 % for RC buildings** and **2 % for steel buildings**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**State the equation of motion of an undamped SDOF system. Define natural circular frequency.**

**Model Answer:**
The equation of motion of an undamped SDOF system in free vibration is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + kx(t) = 0$$

The **natural circular frequency $\omega_n$** is the angular frequency at which the undamped system vibrates when displaced from equilibrium and released. It is given by:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(in rad/s)}$$

The corresponding natural period is $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ seconds.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆō Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 1]`

**A damped SDOF system has mass $m = 100$ kg, stiffness $k = 4000$ N/m, and damping coefficient $c = 80$ N┬Ęs/m. The system is initially at rest, displaced by $x_0 = 0.05$ m, and released.**

**(a)** Determine the **damping ratio**, the **damped natural frequency**, and the **critical damping coefficient**.

**(b)** Obtain the complete **equation of motion** $x(t)$ of the system. Sketch the response for the first three seconds.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆō Undamped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4000}{100}} = \sqrt{40} = 6.3246 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆō Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2 \times 100 \times 6.3246 = 1264.91 \text{ N┬Ęs/m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆō Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{80}{1264.91} = 0.0632 \approx 6.32\%$$

Since $\zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

**Step 4 ŌĆō Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 6.3246 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.0632^2} = 6.3119 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆō General underdamped solution:**

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[x_0 \cos(\omega_d t) + \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆō Substitute initial conditions $x_0 = 0.05$ m, $v_0 = 0$:**

$$x(t) = e^{-0.4 t}\left[0.05 \cos(6.3119 t) + \frac{0 + (0.0632)(6.3246)(0.05)}{6.3119}\sin(6.3119 t)\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆō Evaluate the sine coefficient:**

$$\frac{(0.0632)(6.3246)(0.05)}{6.3119} = \frac{0.01999}{6.3119} = 0.003167 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆō Final equation of motion:**

$$\boxed{x(t) = e^{-0.4 t}\left[0.05 \cos(6.3119 t) + 0.00317 \sin(6.3119 t)\right] \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆō Sketch:** The response is a **decaying sinusoid** starting at $x = 0.05$ m, oscillating with period $T_d = 2\pi/6.3119 \approx 0.995$ s, and decaying with envelope $\pm 0.05\,e^{-0.4t}$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**An SDOF system has mass $m = 200$ kg, stiffness $k = 3200$ N/m, and damping coefficient $c = 1600$ N┬Ęs/m. The system is initially displaced by $x_0 = 0.02$ m with zero initial velocity.**

**(a)** Identify the **regime of damping** (underdamped/critically damped/overdamped) and justify with calculations.

**(b)** Derive and solve the **equation of motion** for this system. Comment on the nature of the response.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆō Undamped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3200}{200}} = \sqrt{16} = 4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆō Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2 \times 200 \times 4 = 1600 \text{ N┬Ęs/m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆō Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{1600}{1600} = 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆō Justification:**
Since $\zeta = 1$ exactly, the system is **critically damped**. The damping coefficient equals the critical damping coefficient; any further increase would make it overdamped.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ŌĆō 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆō General critically damped solution:**

$$x(t) = (A + Bt)\,e^{-\omega_n t}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆō Apply initial conditions $x_0 = 0.02$ m, $v_0 = 0$:**

At $t = 0$: $x(0) = A = 0.02$ m.

Differentiating:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Be^{-\omega_n t} - \omega_n(A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

At $t = 0$: $\dot{x}(0) = B - \omega_n A = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = \omega_n A = 4 \times 0.02 = 0.08$ m/s.

**Step 3 ŌĆō Final equation of motion:**

$$\boxed{x(t) = (0.02 + 0.08 t)\,e^{-4 t} \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆō Comment on the nature of the response:**
The response is a **non-oscillatory exponential decay**. The mass returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without crossing the equilibrium position. As $t \to \infty$, $x(t) \to 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆō Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Always check that stiffness is in **N/m**, mass in **kg**, and damping in **N┬Ęs/m**. Mixing units is the #1 reason for losing 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ envelope:** In underdamped problems, students often write the solution as a pure sinusoid, forgetting the exponential decay multiplier. This loses 4 marks.
> 3. **Initial-velocity sign:** When the system is "released from rest", $v_0 = 0$ is **explicitly given**; do not assume it.
> 4. **Logarithmic decrement formula confusion:** The formula is $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$, not $\delta = 2\pi\zeta$. For small $\zeta$ (< 0.2), the approximation $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ may be used.
> 5. **Confusing $T_n$ with $T_d$:** $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ is the undamped period; $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ is the slightly larger damped period. Examiners will deduct marks if the wrong period is used in the response.
> 6. **Not classifying the regime first:** Always compute $\zeta$ and classify the system **before** writing the general solution. The KTU marking scheme awards 2 marks specifically for the regime identification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1, SDOF Free Vibration):**

- **SDOF system** has three properties: **mass $m$**, **stiffness $k$**, **damping $c$**.
- The general EOM is $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = p(t)$; free vibration means $p(t) = 0$.
- **Undamped natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ **rad/s** (most important formula in the entire module).
- **Natural period:** $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ **s**; cyclic frequency $f_n = 1/T_n$ **Hz**.
- **Critical damping:** $c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ **N┬Ęs/m**.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/c_{cr}$ ŌĆö a **dimensionless number** that classifies the response.
- **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ (valid only for $\zeta < 1$).
- **Regime classification:** $\zeta = 0$ (undamped), $0 < \zeta < 1$ (underdamped), $\zeta = 1$ (critical), $\zeta > 1$ (overdamped).
- **Undamped solution:** $x(t) = x_0\cos(\omega_n t) + (v_0/\omega_n)\sin(\omega_n t)$.
- **Underdamped solution:** $x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}[x_0\cos(\omega_d t) + (v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0)/\omega_d \cdot \sin(\omega_d t)]$.
- **Critically damped solution:** $x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$.
- **Overdamped solution:** $x(t) = Ae^{s_1 t} + Be^{s_2 t}$ with $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$.
- **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ ŌĆö used to compute $\zeta$ experimentally from a free-decay test.
- **Only underdamped systems oscillate**; the other three regimes show **monotonic decay** to equilibrium.
- **Earthquake context:** Most civil engineering structures have $\zeta$ in the range **2 % to 5 %**, hence they are **underdamped**.
- **Initial conditions** must be applied **after** writing the general solution, not before. Constants $A$ and $B$ depend on $x_0$ and $v_0$.
- **Always carry units** throughout the solution; final answer should be in **metres (m)** for displacement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logarithmic Decrement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logarithmic Decrement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logarithmic Decrement (╬┤)** is formally defined as the *natural logarithm of the ratio of any two successive peak amplitudes* of a damped free-vibration response of a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system operating in the **underdamped regime** (i.e., the damping ratio $\zeta < 1$).

Mathematically, the fundamental relation is expressed as:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_{n}}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$

where:
- $x_n$ = peak (maximum) displacement amplitude at the $n$-th cycle,
- $x_{n+1}$ = peak displacement amplitude at the immediately succeeding $(n+1)$-th cycle,
- $\ln$ = natural logarithm (base $e$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you push a child on a swing and then let it oscillate freely. Because of air resistance and friction at the pivot, the swing does not return to the same height each time ŌĆö it gets progressively smaller. If you measure the highest point of the swing on push #1 ($x_1$) and on push #2 ($x_2$), the ratio of these two heights, when fed into a natural log, gives you the *logarithmic decrement*. It is essentially a **measure of how fast the vibration dies out**.

- A **large $\delta$** ŌåÆ the system loses energy quickly (heavy damping, e.g., a building base-isolated with rubber bearings).
- A **small $\delta$** ŌåÆ the system rings for a long time (light damping, e.g., a tall flexible tower in wind).
- A **zero $\delta$** ŌåÆ no decay (undamped ideal case, never observed in real civil structures).

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric used in earthquake engineering is the **damping ratio ($\zeta$)**, which is a *dimensionless* fraction. For most civil engineering structures: $\zeta \approx 0.02$ (welded steel) to $\zeta \approx 0.05$ (reinforced concrete), and a few percent for base-isolated systems. The logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is the *experimental observable* from which $\zeta$ is computed during ambient or forced-vibration tests.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ in **rad/s**.
- **Damped natural frequency** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ in **rad/s**.
- **Damped time period** $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$ in **seconds (s)**.
- **Logarithmic decrement $\delta$** ŌĆö dimensionless.
- **Number of cycles for amplitude ratio of $R$** : $n = \dfrac{\ln R}{\delta}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The derivation of logarithmic decrement assumes a linear SDOF system undergoing **free vibration** with viscous damping. The governing differential equation is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing through by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^{2} x = 0$$

The **underdamped** solution is of the form:

$$x(t) = X\, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\,\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

The peaks of this oscillation occur when $\cos(\omega_d t + \phi) = \pm 1$, i.e., at times $t_n = n T_d$ (where $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$).

### Logic Sequence

1. **Identify peak times** ŌåÆ $t_n = n T_d$, $t_{n+1} = (n+1)T_d$.
2. **Evaluate amplitudes at peaks** ŌåÆ $x_n = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n n T_d}$ and $x_{n+1} = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n (n+1)T_d}$.
3. **Form amplitude ratio** ŌåÆ eliminates the phase angle $\phi$ and envelope constant $X$.
4. **Take natural logarithm** ŌåÆ converts the exponential decay to a linear, easy-to-use parameter $\delta$.
5. **Solve for damping ratio $\zeta$** in terms of $\delta$ using $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description / Use** | **Units** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|
| 1 | $\delta = \ln(x_n / x_{n+1})$ | Basic definition of logarithmic decrement | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ | Exact relation between $\delta$ and damping ratio $\zeta$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | Linearised approximation for **small damping** ($\zeta < 0.20$, always true for civil structures) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^{2} + \delta^{2}}}$ | Inverse exact relation ŌĆö used in **vibration testing** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\zeta \approx \dfrac{\delta}{2\pi}$ | Inverse linearised relation (small damping) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\delta_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$ | Logarithmic decrement over **$n$ cycles** (improves accuracy) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ | Damped time period of vibration | seconds (s) |
| 8 | $n = \dfrac{\ln R}{\delta}$ | Number of cycles required for amplitude to drop by a factor $R = x_0/x_n$ | cycles |
| 9 | $\omega_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_d}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ | Natural frequency recovered from damped frequency | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the KTU valuation key, for any damping ratio less than 0.20 (which is virtually every civil engineering structure), the examiner will accept **either** the exact expression $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ **or** the approximate $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$. For full marks on a 14-mark problem, however, you must state which form you are using and justify the approximation by computing the percent error if the simpler form is chosen.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dynamic field-testing of structures** (ambient vibration surveys, forced-vibration tests, snap-back tests): engineers record the free-decay response and use $\delta$ to back-calculate $\zeta$. This is the standard in IS 1893, ATC-40, and FEMA 440 procedures.
- **Earthquake engineering design**: design spectra are defined for specific damping ratios (commonly 5%). Knowing $\delta$ lets the designer *verify* whether a constructed building exhibits the assumed damping.
- **Health monitoring of existing structures**: a sudden *increase* in $\delta$ (i.e., the building rings down faster) can indicate new damage (cracking, joint slippage) ŌĆö damage shifts energy dissipation mechanisms.
- **Tuned mass dampers (TMD)** and **base isolators**: their effectiveness is judged by how much they raise $\delta$ of the combined system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Core Relation $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$

**Starting point:** the underdamped free-vibration response of an SDOF system.

$$x(t) = X\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi) \quad\quad \text{(Eq. 1)}$$

The peaks of the cosine envelope occur at times

$$t_n = n T_d = n\cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

The amplitude at the $n$-th peak is obtained by substituting $t_n$ into Eq. 1 (the cosine equals $\pm 1$ at the peaks, so we take its absolute value):

$$x_n = X\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n\, n T_d} \quad\quad \text{(Eq. 2)}$$

At the next cycle:

$$x_{n+1} = X\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n\,(n+1)T_d} \quad\quad \text{(Eq. 3)}$$

Form the ratio $x_n / x_{n+1}$:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = \frac{X\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n\, n T_d}}{X\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n\,(n+1)T_d}}$$

The constants $X$ cancel. Using the law $a^p / a^q = a^{p-q}$:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n\,T_d} \quad\quad \text{(Eq. 4)}$$

Substitute $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d = 2\pi/(\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}})$:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = \exp\!\left[\zeta\omega_n\cdot\frac{2\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\right] = \exp\!\left[\frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\right]$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides (this is the definition of $\delta$):

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \quad\quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Linearised Approximation (Small Damping)

For civil structures, $\zeta \ll 1$, so $\zeta^{2} \ll 1$, hence $\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} \approx 1$. Therefore:

$$\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta \quad\quad \text{(Eq. 5)}$$

The percent error incurred by this approximation is:

$$\text{Error \%} = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} - 1\right]\times 100$$

| $\zeta$ | $\delta$ (exact) | $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | % Error |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0.02 | 0.1257 | 0.1257 | 0.02% |
| 0.05 | 0.3145 | 0.3142 | 0.13% |
| 0.10 | 0.6312 | 0.6283 | 0.50% |
| 0.20 | 1.2793 | 1.2566 | 1.78% |
| 0.50 | 3.6276 | 3.1416 | 15.5% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For any $\zeta \le 0.20$ (essentially all civil structures), the approximation error is below 2% ŌĆö fully acceptable in engineering practice and routinely used in the KTU valuation key.

## 3.3 Generalisation to $n$ Cycles Apart

If amplitudes are measured $n$ cycles apart, then from Eq. 4 extended to $n$ cycles:

$$\frac{x_0}{x_n} = \left(\frac{x_0}{x_1}\right)^{n} = e^{n\zeta\omega_n T_d} = e^{n\delta}$$

Solving for $\delta$:

$$\delta_n = \frac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right) \quad\quad \text{(Eq. 6)}$$

This $n$-cycle formula **reduces measurement error** because small random noise in any single peak is averaged out over many cycles.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A SDOF system has mass $m = 2000$ kg and stiffness $k = 800{,}000$ N/m. The amplitude drops from $x_0 = 10$ mm to $x_5 = 4.2$ mm in 5 successive cycles of free vibration. Compute the logarithmic decrement and the damping ratio.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the $n$-cycle formula (Eq. 6):**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{5}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_5}\right) = \frac{1}{5}\ln\!\left(\frac{10}{4.2}\right)$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{10}{4.2} = 2.381$$

Take the natural log:

$$\ln(2.381) = 0.8675$$

Divide by 5:

$$\delta = \frac{0.8675}{5} = 0.1735$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{800{,}000}{2000}} = \sqrt{400} = 20.00\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for damping ratio using the exact inverse relation:**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^{2} + \delta^{2}}}$$

Compute $\delta^{2}$:

$$\delta^{2} = (0.1735)^{2} = 0.0301$$

Compute $4\pi^{2}$:

$$4\pi^{2} = 39.4784$$

Add:

$$4\pi^{2} + \delta^{2} = 39.4784 + 0.0301 = 39.5085$$

Take square root:

$$\sqrt{39.5085} = 6.2864$$

Divide $\delta$ by this value:

$$\zeta = \frac{0.1735}{6.2864} = 0.0276 \approx 2.76\%$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify using the linearised approximation (Eq. 5 inverted):**

$$\zeta_{\text{approx}} = \frac{\delta}{2\pi} = \frac{0.1735}{6.2832} = 0.0276 \approx 2.76\%$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute damped frequency and time period:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} = 20.00\sqrt{1 - 0.000762} = 20.00\times 0.99962 = 19.992\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_d = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{6.2832}{19.992} = 0.3143\ \text{s}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute how many cycles for amplitude to decay to 1% of initial:**

$$R = \frac{x_0}{x_n} = 100$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(100)}{\delta} = \frac{4.6052}{0.1735} = 26.5 \approx 27\ \text{cycles}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Decay Response Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since logarithmic decrement is a time-domain property of an underdamped oscillation, the most accurate visual representation is a **decaying sinusoid**. Because Mermaid cannot render mathematical function plots natively, we present a **block-level functional flow** of how the decay curve is processed by the engineer to extract $\delta$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Time-History x(t) from Free Vibration Test"]:::input
    B["STEP 1: Identify All Positive Peak Amplitudes x0, x1, x2, ... xn"]:::proc
    C["STEP 2: Form the Ratio x0 to xn for n cycles"]:::proc
    D["STEP 3: Take Natural Logarithm and Divide by n"]:::proc
    E["OUTPUT: Logarithmic Decrement delta value"]:::out
    F["STEP 4: Apply Exact Formula zeta = delta over sqrt(4 pi squared plus delta squared)"]:::proc
    G["OUTPUT: Damping Ratio zeta (percent of critical)"]:::out
    H["STEP 5: Cross-Check with Approximate zeta = delta over 2 pi"]:::proc
    I["STEP 6: Compute Damped Frequency omega_d and Time Period Td"]:::proc
    J["FINAL OUTPUT: System Dynamic Properties m, k, c, zeta, omega_n, omega_d, Td"]:::out

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    E --> F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I --> J

    classDef input fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#1f3864,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Decay-Envelope Diagram (ASCII Schematic)

Below is an ASCII art representation of an underdamped free-vibration response, with peak amplitudes $x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots$ labelled. This is the actual experimental signature from which $\delta$ is extracted.

```
x(t)
  ^
  |          * x0  <-- Initial peak
  |        /   \
  |      /       \         * x1
  |    /           \      /   \
  |  /               \  /       \        * x2
  |/                   X         \      /   \           * x3
  +-------------------|-----------|-----|---------------> t (s)
  0                   Td         2Td   | 3Td           ...
                                          \
                                           \  exponential envelope
                                            \  X exp(-zeta omega_n t)
                                             v
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid curve = actual damped response $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$.
- Dashed envelope = $X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ ŌĆö touches the response at every peak.
- Distance between successive peaks along the time axis = $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight from the diagram:** Although the *envelope* is exponential, the logarithm of the envelope is *linear*. This linearity in $\ln(x)$ vs. $n$ is precisely what makes the log decrement such a powerful and simple experimental tool ŌĆö a least-squares fit of $\ln(x_n)$ vs. $n$ yields a straight line of slope $-\delta$.

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Damping Identification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SDOF["SDOF System under Test"]:::input
    EXC["Initial Displacement x0 given as Impulse or Pull-Back"]:::proc
    REC["Accelerometer / LVDT Records Decay Time History x of t"]:::proc
    PEAKS["Peak-Picking Algorithm Identifies x0, x1, ..., xN"]:::proc
    LSFIT["Least-Squares Linear Fit: ln xn vs n"]:::proc
    SLOPE["Slope of Fit = minus delta"]:::proc
    ZETA["Damping Ratio zeta from Exact or Approximate Formula"]:::out
    REPORT["Final Test Report: delta, zeta, omega_n, Td"]:::out

    SDOF --> EXC --> REC --> PEAKS --> LSFIT --> SLOPE --> ZETA --> REPORT

    classDef input fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#1f3864,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
