# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**Define logarithmic decrement. State its exact relationship with the damping ratio of a vibrating system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Logarithmic Decrement (╬┤)** is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes of a damped free-vibration response of an underdamped SDOF system.
> 
> $$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$
> 
> The **exact** relationship with damping ratio $\zeta$ is:
> 
> $$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$
> 
> For small damping ($\zeta < 0.20$, typical of civil structures), this simplifies to:
> 
> $$\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$$

**Mark-split as per KTU key:** 1 mark for definition ┬Ę 1 mark for exact formula ┬Ę 1 mark for the small-damping approximation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]
**A free-vibration test on a simply-supported bridge girder shows the amplitude reducing from 8 mm to 2 mm in 6 cycles. Estimate the damping ratio of the girder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Using the $n$-cycle formula:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{6}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_6}\right) = \frac{1}{6}\ln\!\left(\frac{8}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{6}\ln(4) = \frac{1.3863}{6} = 0.2311$$

Damping ratio (approximate, small damping):

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{2\pi} = \frac{0.2311}{6.2832} = 0.0368 \approx 3.68\%$$

**Mark-split:** 1 mark for $\delta$ formula & substitution ┬Ę 1 mark for $\delta$ value ┬Ę 1 mark for $\zeta$ value.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the logarithmic decrement in terms of the damping ratio for a viscously damped single-degree-of-freedom system undergoing free vibration. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reinforced-concrete chimney has a mass of $50{,}000$ kg and a lateral stiffness of $2.0 \times 10^{6}$ N/m. During a free-vibration pull-back test, the amplitude reduces from 12 mm to 3 mm in 4 cycles. Compute: (i) the logarithmic decrement, (ii) the damping ratio, (iii) the damped natural frequency and time period, and (iv) the number of cycles required for the amplitude to drop to 1% of its initial value.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Assumptions:** linear elastic SDOF, viscous (linear) damping, underdamped regime ($\zeta < 1$), small displacements, time-invariant coefficients $m, c, k$.

**Step 1:** The equation of motion for an unforced SDOF system is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 2:** Dividing by $m$ and substituting $c/m = 2\zeta\omega_n$, $\omega_n^2 = k/m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 3:** For underdamped case, the general solution is:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\!\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.

**Step 4:** Successive peaks occur at $t = nT_d$ where $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$. Peak amplitudes:

$$x_n = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n n T_d}, \quad x_{n+1} = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n (n+1)T_d}$$

**Step 5:** Ratio:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d} = e^{\frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

**Step 6:** Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\boxed{\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

**Mark split:** 1 mark (eq. of motion) ┬Ę 1 mark (general solution with $\omega_d$) ┬Ę 1 mark (peak identification) ┬Ę 1 mark (ratio formation) ┬Ę 1 mark (substitution of $T_d$) ┬Ę 1 mark (final $\delta$ expression) ┬Ę 1 mark (assumptions and conclusion).

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Problem [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $m = 50{,}000$ kg, $k = 2.0\times 10^6$ N/m, $x_0 = 12$ mm, $x_4 = 3$ mm, $n = 4$ cycles.

**(i) Logarithmic decrement [2 marks]:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{4}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_4}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\ln\!\left(\frac{12}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\ln(4) = \frac{1.3863}{4} = 0.3466$$

**(ii) Damping ratio [2 marks]:**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} = \frac{0.3466}{\sqrt{39.4784 + 0.1201}} = \frac{0.3466}{\sqrt{39.5985}} = \frac{0.3466}{6.2927} = 0.0551$$

$$\zeta \approx 5.51\% \;\; (\text{or equivalently, by linearised form: } \zeta \approx \delta/2\pi = 0.0552)$$

**(iii) Damped natural frequency and period [2 marks]:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.0\times 10^{6}}{50{,}000}} = \sqrt{40} = 6.3246\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 6.3246\sqrt{1 - 0.00303} = 6.3246\times 0.99848 = 6.3150\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_d = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{6.2832}{6.3150} = 0.9950\ \text{s}$$

**(iv) Cycles for amplitude to drop to 1% [1 mark]:**

$$R = \frac{x_0}{x_n} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(100)}{\delta} = \frac{4.6052}{0.3466} = 13.29 \approx 14\ \text{cycles}$$

**Final Result:** $\delta = 0.347$, $\zeta = 5.51\%$, $\omega_d = 6.315$ rad/s, $T_d = 0.995$ s, $n \approx 14$ cycles.

---

### Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Modified] (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of logarithmic decrement in earthquake-resistant design. With the help of neat sketches, describe how it is determined experimentally using a free-vibration test on a structure. Discuss the relative merits of using the single-cycle formula versus the multi-cycle (n-cycle) formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The free-vibration response of a steel water tower shows that the amplitude reduces to 40% of its initial value in 10 successive cycles. If the natural frequency (undamped) of the tower is 1.5 Hz, determine: (i) the logarithmic decrement, (ii) the damping ratio, (iii) the damped natural frequency in Hz, and (iv) the time taken for the amplitude to reduce to 5% of its initial value.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Descriptive Question [7 marks]:**

**Significance [2 marks]:**
- Logarithmic decrement is the **field-experimental observable** that directly yields the **damping ratio** of an as-built structure.
- In earthquake-resistant design per IS 1893 and related codes, the design response spectrum is defined at a *reference* damping (commonly 5%). The actual damping of an existing structure (extracted via $\delta$) governs how the spectral ordinates are scaled: $S_a(\zeta) = S_a(5\%)\cdot\sqrt{\dfrac{0.05}{\zeta}}$ (for the velocity- and displacement-controlled regimes). Underdamped structures with low $\zeta$ amplify earthquake forces more; structures with higher $\zeta$ (e.g., base-isolated) reduce them.
- Used in **vibration-based health monitoring** to detect damage (a sudden rise in $\delta$ implies new energy-dissipation paths ŌĆö cracking, joint slip, etc.).

**Experimental determination [3 marks]:**
1. Apply a sudden release / pull-back to impart an initial displacement $x_0$.
2. Record the free-decay time history $x(t)$ using accelerometers or LVDTs.
3. Identify positive (or all) peak amplitudes $x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
4. Plot $\ln(x_k)$ vs. cycle number $k$ ŌĆö should yield a straight line of slope $-\delta$ (best-fit line through data).
5. The damping ratio is then back-calculated as $\zeta = \delta/\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}$.

**Single-cycle vs. n-cycle formulas [2 marks]:**

| Aspect | Single-cycle $\delta = \ln(x_n/x_{n+1})$ | n-cycle $\delta_n = \frac{1}{n}\ln(x_0/x_n)$ |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Sensitivity to noise | High (each peak affected by noise) | Low (averaged over $n$ cycles) |
| Use case | Strong damping, few cycles available | Light damping, many cycles available |
| Accuracy | Lower for lightly damped systems | Higher for lightly damped systems |
| Computational effort | Minimal | Slightly more (need $n+1$ peaks) |

**Conclusion:** The $n$-cycle formula is **preferred in practice** for civil structures (light damping) because of its superior noise immunity.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Problem [7 marks]:**

**Given:** Amplitude reduces to 40% of initial in $n = 10$ cycles, $f_n = 1.5$ Hz, so $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 9.4248$ rad/s.

**(i) Logarithmic decrement [2 marks]:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{10}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_{10}}\right) = \frac{1}{10}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.40}\right) = \frac{1}{10}\ln(2.5) = \frac{0.9163}{10} = 0.0916$$

**(ii) Damping ratio [2 marks]:**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} = \frac{0.0916}{\sqrt{39.4784 + 0.00839}} = \frac{0.0916}{6.2901} = 0.01457 \approx 1.46\%$$

(This is consistent with a lightly damped welded steel structure.)

**(iii) Damped natural frequency in Hz [2 marks]:**

$$f_d = f_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 1.5\sqrt{1 - 0.000212} = 1.5\times 0.99989 = 1.4998\ \text{Hz} \approx 1.50\ \text{Hz}$$

(The difference is negligible ŌĆö typical of lightly damped systems.)

**(iv) Time for amplitude to reduce to 5% [1 mark]:**

$$n = \frac{\ln(x_0/x_n)}{\delta} = \frac{\ln(1/0.05)}{0.0916} = \frac{\ln(20)}{0.0916} = \frac{2.9957}{0.0916} = 32.71 \approx 33\ \text{cycles}$$

Damped period:

$$T_d = \frac{1}{f_d} \approx 0.667\ \text{s}$$

Total time:

$$t = n \cdot T_d = 32.71 \times 0.667 = 21.82\ \text{s}$$

**Final Result:** $\delta = 0.0916$, $\zeta = 1.46\%$, $f_d \approx 1.50$ Hz, total time $t \approx 21.8$ s.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $n$** when amplitudes are measured $n$ cycles apart ŌĆö single most common error. Use $\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln(x_0/x_n)$ **not** $\delta = \ln(x_0/x_n)$.
> 2. **Confusing natural and damped frequency.** The formula $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n$ refers to the *undamped* natural frequency. Damped frequency is $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
> 3. **Using the linearised approximation $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ when $\zeta$ is given as 30% or higher.** For $\zeta \ge 0.3$, the error exceeds 5% and the *exact* formula is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Not stating units.** Period must be in seconds, frequency in rad/s or Hz, damping ratio in % or pure fraction. Many students lose 1 mark for unit ambiguity.
> 5. **In free-vibration tests, students often mistakenly use $x_0/x_n$ directly without the log and call it "log decrement."** Always include the $\ln(\cdot)$ step.
> 6. **Forgetting to show derivation of $\omega_n$ from $k$ and $m$** ŌĆö at least 1 mark is allotted to this in numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Logarithmic Decrement":**
> 
> - **Definition (must-memorise):** $\delta = \ln(x_n / x_{n+1})$ ŌĆö *dimensionless*.
> - **Exact master formula:** $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
> - **Linearised form (for $\zeta < 0.20$):** $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.
> - **Inverse relations (used in lab tests):** $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} \approx \dfrac{\delta}{2\pi}$.
> - **$n$-cycle formula (preferred in practice):** $\delta_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$.
> - **Cycles for amplitude ratio $R$:** $n = \dfrac{\ln R}{\delta}$.
> - **Decay envelope** is exponential: $X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$; **log of envelope** is linear in $t$ with slope $-\zeta\omega_n$.
> - **Typical $\zeta$ ranges:** welded steel ~2%, RC ~5%, masonry ~7ŌĆō10%, base-isolated ~15ŌĆō30%.
> - **Experimental setup:** pull-back / snap / impulse release ŌåÆ record $x(t)$ ŌåÆ identify peaks ŌåÆ fit $\ln(x_k)$ vs. $k$ ŌåÆ slope = $-\delta$.
> - **Engineering use:** back-calculate $\zeta$, scale response spectra, monitor structural health (damage increases $\delta$).
> - **Valid only for underdamped ($\zeta<1$) linear SDOF systems with classical viscous damping.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response to Harmonic Loading - Steady State and Transient States - Steady State Amplitude
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Response to Harmonic Loading - Steady State and Transient States - Steady State Amplitude # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In structural dynamics, **Harmonic Loading** refers to a time-varying external force (or excitation) whose magnitude varies sinusoidally with time, typically expressed as: $$p(t) = p_0 \sin(\bar{\omega} t) \quad \text{or} \quad p(t) = p_0 \cos(\bar{\omega} t)$$ where: - $p_0$ = **amplitude (peak value) of the applied harmonic force** - $\bar{\omega}$ = **forcing (excitation) frequency in rad/sec** - $t$ = time in seconds When a Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system is subjected to such a load, the **total dynamic response** $u(t)$ is the superposition of two physically distinct components: > [!IMPORTANT] > **TOTAL RESPONSE = TRANSIENT RESPONSE + STEADY-STATE RESPONSE** > $$u(t) = u_{tr}(t) + u_{ss}(t)$$ ### Component 1: Transient State (Transient Response) The **transient response** is the solution of the *homogeneous* (unforced) equation of motion. It represents the system's free vibration initiated by the initial conditions and decays exponentially with time due to the presence of damping. In an **undamped** system, this component would oscillate forever; in a **damped** system, it vanishes after a few cycles. $$\boxed{u_{tr}(t) = e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}\left[A \cos(\omega_D t) + B \sin(\omega_D t)\right]}$$ where $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**. ### Component 2: Steady State (Steady-State Response) The **steady-state response** is the *particular* solution of the equation of motion that persists indefinitely as long as the harmonic load is applied. It oscillates at the **forcing frequency** $\bar{\omega}$ (not at the natural frequency $\omega_n$) and its amplitude remains constant after the transient dies out. > [!NOTE] > **Steady State Amplitude (Definition):** The maximum displacement attained by the system once the transient vibrations have completely decayed, leaving only the forced vibration at the excitation frequency $\bar{\omega}$. It is also known as the *Dynamic Amplitude* or *Dynamic Magnification*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition Imagine you are **pushing a child on a swing** (the SDOF system) with a periodic push (the harmonic force). - **Transient State**: When you first start pushing, the swing is irregular ŌĆö it wobbles at its own natural pace, mixed with your pushes. This initial "messy" motion is the transient. It depends on **how the motion was started** (initial displacement, initial velocity). - **Steady State**: After a few seconds, the swing settles into a smooth, repetitive back-and-forth pattern that perfectly matches the rhythm of your pushes. The swing now moves **only at the frequency you push it**. This clean, perpetual motion is the steady state. - **Damping (friction in the swing's chains, air resistance)**: Without damping, the transient never goes away. With damping, the messy initial wobble fades, leaving only the steady, forced oscillation. > **Key Intuition:** Damping does NOT eliminate the steady-state response ŌĆö it only kills the transient. The steady-state amplitude is governed entirely by the ratio of forcing frequency to natural frequency and the damping ratio. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | |-----------|--------|--------------| | **Mass of the system** | $m$ | **kg** (or $\text{kN┬Ęs}^2/\text{mm}$) | | **Stiffness** | $k$ | **N/m** (or $\text{kN/mm}$) | | **Damping coefficient** | $c$ | **N┬Ęs/m** | | **Natural circular frequency** | $\omega_n$ | **rad/sec** | | **Damping ratio** | $\zeta$ | **dimensionless** (0 to 1) | | **Forcing frequency** | $\bar{\omega}$ | **rad/sec** | | **Static displacement** | $u_{st}$ | **mm or m** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Equation of Motion of SDOF System under Harmonic Load The governing differential equation for a viscously damped SDOF system subjected to harmonic excitation is: $$m\ddot{u}(t) + c\dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = p_0 \sin(\bar{\omega} t)$$ ### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown: 1. **$m\ddot{u}(t)$** ŌĆö Inertia force (resistance to acceleration change) 2. **$c\dot{u}(t)$** ŌĆö Damping force (resistance to velocity, dissipates energy) 3. **$k u(t)$** ŌĆö Stiffness/Restoring force (resistance to displacement) 4. **$p_0 \sin(\bar{\omega} t)$** ŌĆö External harmonic excitation (driving force) ### Reduced (Normalized) Form: Dividing through by $m$ and using $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$: $$\ddot{u}(t) + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{u}(t) + \omega_n^2 u(t) = \frac{p_0}{m} \sin(\bar{\omega} t)$$ ## 2.2 The Complete Solution ŌĆö Why Two Parts Exist? By the theory of linear differential equations, the general solution is: $$u(t) = \underbrace{u_{tr}(t)}_{\text{Complementary Solution}} + \underbrace{u_{ss}(t)}_{\text{Particular Solution}}$$ ### Why this split? > [!IMPORTANT] > - The **complementary function** $u_{tr}(t)$ solves the homogeneous equation and accounts for **initial conditions**. Because the homogeneous equation has no forcing term, the system vibrates *freely* ŌĆö and due to damping, this vibration **decays with time**. > - The **particular integral** $u_{ss}(t)$ is a forced response that oscillates at the **forcing frequency** $\bar{\omega}$ and is independent of initial conditions. In a stable damped system, this is the only long-term surviving motion. ## 2.3 Derivation of the Steady-State Response (Particular Solution) **Assumption (Method of Undamped Coefficients):** Since the forcing is sinusoidal at $\bar{\omega}$, we assume the steady-state response is also sinusoidal at the same frequency, but with a phase lag $\phi$: $$u_{ss}(t) = U \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)$$ where $U$ = **steady-state amplitude** and $\phi$ = **phase angle** (lags the force due to damping). Substituting and matching coefficients leads to: $$U = \frac{p_0}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}}$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta\beta}{1-\beta^2}\right)$$ where $\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n$ is the **frequency ratio**. ## 2.4 Special Case: Undamped Steady-State Amplitude For an undamped system ($\zeta = 0$): $$U = \frac{p_0/k}{1-\beta^2} = \frac{u_{st}}{1-\beta^2}$$ This becomes **infinite when $\beta = 1$** ŌĆö the famous **RESONANCE** condition! ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > This is the master reference for solving 14-mark problems on this topic. Memorize this table. | # | Parameter | Formula | Meaning / Application | |---|-----------|---------|----------------------| | 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Eigen-frequency of SDOF | | 2 | Damped natural freq. | $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Frequency of transient oscillation | | 3 | Frequency ratio | $\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n$ | Dimensionless forcing-to-natural ratio | | 4 | Static displacement | $u_{st} = p_0/k$ | Displacement if load were applied *statically* | | 5 | **Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF)** | $R_d = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}}$ | Ratio of dynamic to static amplitude | | 6 | **Steady-state amplitude (DAMPED)** | $U = u_{st} \cdot R_d$ | Final amplitude after transients die | | 7 | **Steady-state amplitude (UNDAMPED)** | $U = \dfrac{u_{st}}{\vert 1-\beta^2 \vert}$ | Resonance at $\beta=1$ | | 8 | Phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta\beta}{1-\beta^2}\right)$ | 0 to $\pi$ depending on $\beta$ | | 9 | Transmissibility (base motion) | $TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+(2\zeta\beta)^2}{(1-\beta^2)^2+(2\zeta\beta)^2}}$ | For seismic isolation design | | 10 | Maximum DMF (at $\beta = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$) | $R_{d,\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Peak amplitude locus | | 11 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Experimental damping estimation | | 12 | Half-power bandwidth | $\zeta = \dfrac{\Delta\omega}{2\omega_n}$ | Frequency-domain damping | > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of: > - **Earthquake engineering** ŌĆö for designing structures to avoid resonance > - **Machine foundation design** ŌĆö to isolate vibration from rotating equipment > - **Bridge and tall building design** ŌĆö for wind/footfall-induced vibrations > - **Seismic isolation devices** ŌĆö for base isolation system design # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Steady-State Amplitude (Damped System) ### Step 1 ŌĆö Write the equation of motion $$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = p_0 \sin(\bar{\omega} t)$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Assume the form of the particular solution The forcing is sinusoidal at $\bar{\omega}$. Hence we *assume* (this is the standard ansatz): $$u_{ss}(t) = U \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the derivatives $$u_{ss}(t) = U \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)$$ $$\dot{u}_{ss}(t) = U \bar{\omega} \cos(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)$$ $$\ddot{u}_{ss}(t) = -U \bar{\omega}^2 \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into the equation of motion $$m[-U \bar{\omega}^2 \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)] + c[U \bar{\omega} \cos(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)] + k[U \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)] = p_0 \sin(\bar{\omega} t)$$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Expand using $\sin(A-B)$ and $\cos(A-B)$ identities $$-mU\bar{\omega}^2[\sin\bar{\omega}t \cos\phi - \cos\bar{\omega}t \sin\phi]$$ $$+ cU\bar{\omega}[\cos\bar{\omega}t \cos\phi + \sin\bar{\omega}t \sin\phi]$$ $$+ kU[\sin\bar{\omega}t \cos\phi - \cos\bar{\omega}t \sin\phi] = p_0 \sin\bar{\omega}t$$ ### Step 6 ŌĆö Collect coefficients of $\sin(\bar{\omega}t)$ and $\cos(\bar{\omega}t)$ **Coefficient of $\sin(\bar{\omega}t)$:** $$U[(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)\cos\phi + c\bar{\omega}\sin\phi] = p_0$$ **Coefficient of $\cos(\bar{\omega}t)$:** $$U[(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)\sin\phi - c\bar{\omega}\cos\phi] = 0$$ ### Step 7 ŌĆö Solve the second equation for the phase angle $\phi$ From the $\cos(\bar{\omega}t)$ equation: $$(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)\sin\phi = c\bar{\omega}\cos\phi$$ $$\tan\phi = \frac{c\bar{\omega}}{k - m\bar{\omega}^2}$$ ### Step 8 ŌĆö Normalize using $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $c = 2\zeta m \omega_n$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$: $$\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta m\omega_n \bar{\omega}/k}{1 - m\bar{\omega}^2/k} = \frac{2\zeta \bar{\omega}/\omega_n}{1 - (\bar{\omega}/\omega_n)^2}$$ Substituting $\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n$: $$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta\beta}{1 - \beta^2}\right)}$$ ### Step 9 ŌĆö Solve for amplitude $U$ Square both equations of Step 6 and add: $$U^2\left[(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)^2 + (c\bar{\omega})^2\right] = p_0^2$$ Taking the square root: $$U = \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)^2 + (c\bar{\omega})^2}}$$ ### Step 10 ŌĆö Normalize to obtain the dynamic magnification factor Divide numerator and denominator inside the radical by $k^2$: $$U = \frac{p_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - \beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}}$$ Recognizing $u_{st} = p_0/k$: $$\boxed{U = \frac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}} = u_{st} \cdot R_d}$$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Transient Response (Homogeneous Solution) ### Step 1 ŌĆö Set the RHS to zero (homogeneous equation) $$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = 0$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Assume exponential solution $$u_{tr}(t) = A e^{st}$$ This gives the characteristic equation: $$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the quadratic $$s_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö For underdamped systems ($\zeta < 1$) $$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_D$$ where $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. ### Step 5 ŌĆö Write the general transient solution $$u_{tr}(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A_1 \cos(\omega_D t) + A_2 \sin(\omega_D t)\right]$$ ### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply initial conditions $u(0) = u_0$ and $\dot{u}(0) = \dot{u}_0$ $$A_1 = u_0$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\dot{u}_0 + \zeta\omega_n u_0}{\omega_D}$$ ### Step 7 ŌĆö Physical interpretation > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ ensures that the transient **decays exponentially** with time. As $t \to \infty$, $u_{tr}(t) \to 0$, leaving **only** the steady-state response. For a system starting from rest with no initial velocity ($u_0 = \dot{u}_0 = 0$), the transient still exists briefly because the application of the harmonic force itself acts as a disturbance. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A SDOF system has $m = 2000$ kg, $k = 200$ kN/m, and $\zeta = 0.05$. It is subjected to a harmonic force $p(t) = 5000 \sin(20t)$ N. Compute the **steady-state amplitude**. **Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{200{,}000/2000} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency ratio:** $$\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n = 20/10 = 2$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Static displacement:** $$u_{st} = p_0/k = 5000/200{,}000 = 0.025 \text{ m} = 25 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Dynamic magnification factor:** $$R_d = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-2^2)^2 + (2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 2)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{9 + 0.04}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{9.04}}$$ $$R_d = 1/\sqrt{9.04} \approx 0.3327$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Steady-state amplitude:** $$U = u_{st} \cdot R_d = 25 \times 0.3327 \approx 8.32 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Phase angle:** $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 0.05 \times 2}{1-4}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{-3}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.0667)$$ $$\phi \approx 180┬░ - 3.81┬░ = 176.19┬░ \text{ (in 2nd quadrant since } 1-\beta^2 < 0)$$ **Final Answer:** $U \approx 8.32$ mm, with phase lag $\phi \approx 176.2┬░$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SDOF System under Harmonic Loading ŌĆö Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Mass m
(concentrated weight)"] -- "Stiffness k
(spring) attached" --> B["Foundation
(fixed base)"] A -- "Damper c
(dashpot) attached" --> B C["Harmonic Force
p0 sin Žē t
(rotating machine)"] -- "Applied horizontally" --> A D["Displacement u t
(measured response)"] -. "output" .-> A ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Response Generation ```mermaid flowchart TB A0[Input p0 sin omega t] --> B0[Apply to SDOF System] B0 --> C0{Split by Math} C0 -- Homogeneous part --> D0[Transient Response] C0 -- Particular part --> E0[Steady State Response] D0 --> F0[Decays with time] E0 --> G0[Persists at forcing frequency] F0 --> H0[Final Visible Motion] G0 --> H0 H0[I0 Total Displacement u t] D0 -.depends on.-> J0[Initial Conditions] E0 -.depends on.-> K0[Frequency Ratio beta] E0 -.depends on.-> L0[Damping Ratio zeta] ``` ## 4.3 Amplitude vs Frequency Ratio ŌĆö Resonance Behavior ```mermaid flowchart LR X["Frequency Ratio beta"] --> Y["Amplification Factor Rd"] Y --> Z1["beta less than 1
Rd approx 1
(Quasi-static)"] Y --> Z2["beta approx 1
Rd very large
(RESONANCE)"] Y --> Z3["beta greater than 1
Rd decreases
(Vibration isolation)"] Z2 --> W1["Amplitude can be 10x to 100x
the static value"] Z3 --> W2["Amplitude less than static
Useful for isolation"] ``` ## 4.4 Time-Domain Evolution of Total Response ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Time t = 0
Total = Transient + Steady"] --> B["Time t = small
Transient dominates
Irregular pattern"] B --> C["Time t = moderate
Transient decaying
Steady building up"] C --> D["Time t = large
Transient approx 0
Pure Steady State
at forcing frequency"] D --> E["Final State
u t approx U sin omega t minus phi"] ``` ## 4.5 Phase Angle vs Frequency Ratio ŌĆö Behavior Map ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[beta very small] --> Q1[phi approx 0 degrees] P2[beta less than 1] --> Q2[phi between 0 and 90 degrees] P3[beta = 1 resonance] --> Q3[phi = 90 degrees] P4[beta greater than 1] --> Q4[phi between 90 and 180 degrees] P5[beta very large] --> Q5[phi approx 180 degrees] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **Distinguish between transient and steady-state response of a SDOF system subjected to harmonic loading.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Transient Response | Steady-State Response | |--------|-------------------|----------------------| | **Origin** | Solution of homogeneous equation | Particular integral of forced equation | | **Frequency** | Natural frequency $\omega_n$ (or $\omega_D$) | Forcing frequency $\bar{\omega}$ | | **Damping effect** | Decays exponentially with time | Amplitude reduces but does not decay | | **Initial conditions** | Depends on $u_0$ and $\dot{u}_0$ | Independent of initial conditions | | **Time behavior** | Vanishes as $t \to \infty$ | Persists as long as force is applied | **(1 mark per main distinguishing feature, maximum 3)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024] **Define steady-state amplitude. State the expression for the dynamic magnification factor for a damped SDOF system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The steady-state amplitude $U$ is the maximum displacement of a SDOF system, attained after the transient vibrations have completely died out, when the system oscillates purely at the forcing frequency $\bar{\omega}$. **Expression (1.5 marks):** $$R_d = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}}$$ And steady-state amplitude: $U = u_{st} \cdot R_d = \dfrac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}}$ --- ## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] **Q. (a)** Derive the expression for the steady-state amplitude of a viscously damped single degree of freedom system subjected to harmonic excitation $p(t) = p_0 \sin(\bar{\omega} t)$. Show that in the undamped case the amplitude becomes infinite at resonance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(Step 1) Equation of motion (1 mark):** $$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = p_0 \sin(\bar{\omega} t)$$ **(Step 2) Assume solution of the form (1 mark):** $$u_{ss}(t) = U \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)$$ Compute derivatives: $$\dot{u}_{ss} = U\bar{\omega}\cos(\bar{\omega} t - \phi); \quad \ddot{u}_{ss} = -U\bar{\omega}^2 \sin(\bar{\omega} t - \phi)$$ **(Step 3) Substitute and expand (1 mark):** After substituting and using $\sin(A-B)$ identities, we collect coefficients: - $\sin(\bar{\omega}t)$: $\quad U[(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)\cos\phi + c\bar{\omega}\sin\phi] = p_0$ - $\cos(\bar{\omega}t)$: $\quad U[(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)\sin\phi - c\bar{\omega}\cos\phi] = 0$ **(Step 4) Solve for phase (1 mark):** $$\tan\phi = \frac{c\bar{\omega}}{k - m\bar{\omega}^2} = \frac{2\zeta\beta}{1-\beta^2}$$ **(Step 5) Solve for amplitude (1.5 marks):** Squaring and adding both equations: $$U^2[(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)^2 + (c\bar{\omega})^2] = p_0^2$$ $$U = \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\bar{\omega}^2)^2 + (c\bar{\omega})^2}} = \frac{p_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}}$$ **(Step 6) Undamped case and resonance (1.5 marks):** Setting $\zeta = 0$: $$U = \frac{u_{st}}{|1-\beta^2|}$$ At $\beta = 1$ (i.e., $\bar{\omega} = \omega_n$), the denominator becomes zero: $$\lim_{\beta \to 1} U \to \infty$$ This is **resonance** ŌĆö the amplitude grows without bound in an undamped system, demonstrating why damping and frequency-detuning are critical in design. --- **Q. (b)** A SDOF system with $m = 500$ kg, $k = 200$ kN/m, and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.02$ is subjected to a harmonic force of amplitude $p_0 = 10$ kN at frequency $\bar{\omega} = 30$ rad/s. Determine the **steady-state amplitude** and the **phase angle**. Comment on whether the system is operating near resonance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Natural frequency (1 mark):** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{200{,}000/500} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency ratio (0.5 mark):** $$\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n = 30/20 = 1.5$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Static displacement (0.5 mark):** $$u_{st} = p_0/k = 10{,}000/200{,}000 = 0.05 \text{ m} = 50 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute denominator terms (1 mark):** $$1 - \beta^2 = 1 - 2.25 = -1.25$$ $$2\zeta\beta = 2 \times 0.02 \times 1.5 = 0.06$$ $$(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2 = 1.5625 + 0.0036 = 1.5661$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Dynamic magnification factor (1 mark):** $$R_d = 1/\sqrt{1.5661} = 1/1.2516 \approx 0.7989$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Steady-state amplitude (1 mark):** $$U = u_{st} \times R_d = 50 \times 0.7989 \approx 39.95 \text{ mm} \approx 40 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Phase angle (1 mark):** $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.06}{-1.25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.048)$$ Since $1-\beta^2 < 0$ and $2\zeta\beta > 0$, the angle lies in the **second quadrant**: $$\phi \approx 180┬░ - 2.75┬░ = 177.25┬░ \text{ (or in radians: } 3.094 \text{ rad)}$$ **Step 8 ŌĆö Comment on resonance (1 mark):** The frequency ratio $\beta = 1.5$ is **beyond the resonance point** ($\beta = 1$). The system has *passed through* resonance. Since $\zeta$ is very small (0.02), the amplitude amplification would have been severe at $\beta = 1$, but the current $\beta = 1.5$ puts the system in the *post-resonance* region where $R_d < 1$ and amplitude is even smaller than the static displacement. However, the system is still **operating dangerously close to the resonance band** ($0.7 < \beta < 1.4$) ŌĆö a small change in forcing frequency could trigger large vibrations, and **a design review is recommended**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **Q. (a)** Explain the phenomenon of resonance in a SDOF system. With the help of the frequency-response curve, discuss the effect of damping on the steady-state amplitude. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition of Resonance (1 mark):** Resonance is the condition in which the forcing frequency $\bar{\omega}$ equals (or nearly equals) the natural frequency $\omega_n$ of the system, i.e., $\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n \approx 1$. At this condition, the steady-state amplitude reaches a maximum and (in the undamped case) theoretically becomes infinite. **Mathematical condition (1 mark):** For an undamped system: $U = u_{st}/|1-\beta^2| \to \infty$ as $\beta \to 1$. **Resonance frequency for damped system (1.5 marks):** To find the maximum of $R_d$ for a damped system, differentiate $R_d^2$ w.r.t. $\beta$ and set to zero. The condition gives: $$\beta_{resonance} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$ And the **maximum magnification factor**: $$R_{d,\max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$ For small $\zeta$ (say 0.05), $R_{d,\max} \approx 10$. This shows that even a tiny amount of damping **drastically limits** the resonant amplitude ŌĆö from infinity to a finite, manageable value. **Effect of damping on frequency-response curve (2.5 marks):** | $\beta$ region | Low Damping (e.g. $\zeta = 0.02$) | Higher Damping (e.g. $\zeta = 0.20$) | |----------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------| | $\beta \ll 1$ | $R_d \to 1$ (quasi-static) | $R_d \to 1$ (quasi-static) | | $\beta = 1$ | $R_d \approx 25$ (sharp peak) | $R_d \approx 2.5$ (blunted peak) | | $\beta \gg 1$ | $R_d \to 0$ (vibration isolation) | $R_d \to 0$ (slower roll-off) | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Damping is essential to limit the resonant amplitude in real structures. Earthquake-resistant design relies on this principle ŌĆö supplemental dampers are added to buildings to keep $R_d$ bounded even when $\beta$ approaches 1. --- **Q. (b)** A SDOF system has natural period $T_n = 0.5$ sec and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.10$. A harmonic force with amplitude $p_0 = 50$ kN acts at frequency $\bar{\omega} = 10$ rad/s. If the static stiffness of the system is $k = 5 \times 10^6$ N/m, find (i) the mass, (ii) the frequency ratio, and (iii) the steady-state amplitude. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Mass from period (2 marks):** $$T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n \implies \omega_n = 2\pi/T_n = 2\pi/0.5 = 4\pi \approx 12.566 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega_n^2 = k/m \implies m = k/\omega_n^2 = 5\times 10^6/(12.566)^2$$ $$m = 5\times 10^6/157.91 \approx 31{,}663 \text{ kg} \approx 31.66 \text{ tons}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency ratio (1 mark):** $$\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n = 10/12.566 \approx 0.7957$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Static displacement (1 mark):** $$u_{st} = p_0/k = 50{,}000/5\times 10^6 = 0.01 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Magnification factor (2 marks):** $$1 - \beta^2 = 1 - 0.6331 = 0.3669$$ $$2\zeta\beta = 2 \times 0.10 \times 0.7957 = 0.1591$$ $$(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2 = (0.3669)^2 + (0.1591)^2 = 0.1346 + 0.0253 = 0.1599$$ $$R_d = 1/\sqrt{0.1599} \approx 1/0.3999 \approx 2.501$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Steady-state amplitude (1 mark):** $$U = u_{st} \times R_d = 10 \times 2.501 \approx 25.01 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answers:** - $m \approx 31.66 \times 10^3$ kg - $\beta \approx 0.796$ - $U \approx 25.0$ mm > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the phase angle quadrant:** Many students compute $\phi = \tan^{-1}(-0.048)$ and write it as a *negative* angle. But when $1-\beta^2 < 0$, the phase angle is in the **second quadrant** (between 90┬░ and 180┬░). Always check the signs of numerator and denominator before deciding the quadrant. > > 2. **Confusing $\bar{\omega}$ with $\omega_n$:** $\bar{\omega}$ is the *forcing* frequency (given by the loading), $\omega_n$ is the *natural* frequency (depends on $m$ and $k$). Swapping them is a 1-mark loss. > > 3. **Forgetting to include $u_{st}$ in the final amplitude:** $R_d$ alone is *not* the answer. The steady-state amplitude is $U = u_{st} \times R_d$. Marks lost: 1ŌĆō2. > > 4. **Units of $\bar{\omega}$:** Forcing frequency in problems is sometimes given in Hz. Convert: $\bar{\omega}(\text{rad/s}) = 2\pi f(\text{Hz})$. This is a frequent blunder. > > 5. **Resonance infinity for damped case:** Students often say "resonance amplitude is infinite" without specifying it's the **undamped** case. For damped systems, use $R_{d,\max} = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$. > > 6. **Mixing up transient and steady-state frequencies:** Transient decays at $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$, but steady-state oscillates at $\bar{\omega}$. The two are *different*. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-minute revision ŌĆö read this carefully before the exam.** - ŌśÉ **Total response** = Transient + Steady-state. Total = $u_{tr}(t) + u_{ss}(t)$. - ŌśÉ **Transient** decays as $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$; **Steady-state** persists at $\bar{\omega}$. - ŌśÉ **Steady-state amplitude formula:** $U = \dfrac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-\beta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\beta)^2}}$ - ŌśÉ **Frequency ratio:** $\beta = \bar{\omega}/\omega_n$. **Static disp:** $u_{st} = p_0/k$. - ŌśÉ **Phase angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta\beta}{1-\beta^2}\right)$, quadrant must be checked. - ŌśÉ **Resonance (undamped):** $U \to \infty$ at $\beta = 1$. - ŌśÉ **Resonance (damped):** $\beta_{res} = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$, $R_{d,\max} = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$. - ŌśÉ **Three regions of $\beta$:** (a) $\beta \ll 1$ ŌåÆ quasi-static; (b) $\beta \approx 1$ ŌåÆ resonance; (c) $\beta \gg 1$ ŌåÆ vibration isolation. - ŌśÉ Damping **does not eliminate** steady-state response ŌĆö it only kills the transient. - ŌśÉ For seismic design, **avoid** forcing frequencies (machine speeds, vortex shedding) near $\omega_n$ of the structure. - ŌśÉ $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; $\omega_D = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$; $c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n$ (critical damping). - ŌśÉ **Unit checks:** $p_0$ in N or kN; $k$ in N/m or kN/m; $u_{st}$ then in m or mm. - ŌśÉ **Conversion:** $1 \text{ Hz} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s}$. - ŌśÉ In KTU 14-mark questions, **always show** the full derivation with units and intermediate steps. - ŌśÉ A useful mnemonic: **"TRA**nsient = **T**emporary, **R**inging, **A**ttenuating"; **STE**ady = **S**ame frequency, **T**ime-infinite, **E**quilibrium amplitude."

topics.title: Dynamic Magnification Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Magnification Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 ŌĆö Dynamic Magnification Factor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF)**, also known as the **Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF)**, is formally defined as the **ratio of the maximum dynamic amplitude (steady-state response) of a vibrating system to the static deflection that would be produced by the same applied force acting statically on the system.**

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{DMF} \;=\; \frac{(x_{d})_{\max}}{x_{st}}
$$

Where:
- $(x_d)_{\max}$ = **Maximum dynamic displacement amplitude** (in m or mm)
- $x_{st}$ = **Static deflection** under the same peak force $P_0$ (in m or mm)
- For a SDOF system, $x_{st} = \dfrac{P_0}{k}$ where $k$ is the lateral stiffness.

The DMF is a **dimensionless amplification coefficient** that quantifies *how much larger* the dynamic response is compared to the static response.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö Pushing a Child on a Swing:**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push gently and slowly (low frequency forcing), the swing moves roughly the same amount as the static push. But if you push in rhythm with the natural swinging motion of the swing, the swing's amplitude **grows enormously** with each push ŌĆö this is resonance. The DMF is simply the number that tells you "how many times bigger is the swing's motion compared to a single static push?" If the swing goes 10 times higher than the static push would suggest, then DMF = 10. Now imagine you add a small friction damper (like a brake) to the swing. The amplification reduces ŌĆö the swing still amplifies the push, but less dramatically. That is the role of **damping ratio (╬Č)** in earthquake engineering.

In earthquake-resistant design, the ground itself is the "pusher." The DMF tells the engineer how violently the building will sway relative to the deflection it would experience if the same force were applied very slowly.

### 1.3 Physical Significance in Earthquake Engineering

- The DMF directly links **dynamic analysis** to **static equivalent analysis**.
- It is the foundation for the **Response Spectrum** concept (which is essentially a plot of DMF vs. natural period for a specific earthquake).
- IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016 uses the concept of DMF implicitly through the **design response spectrum** where spectral acceleration $S_a$ relates to peak ground acceleration (PGA) through frequency-dependent amplification.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used (Highlighted)

- **Natural frequency** $\omega_n$ (rad/s) ŌĆö intrinsic to the structure
- **Forcing frequency** $\omega$ (rad/s) ŌĆö frequency of the dynamic load (e.g., ground motion)
- **Frequency ratio** $r = \omega / \omega_n$ (dimensionless)
- **Damping ratio** $\zeta = c / c_c$ (dimensionless), where $c_c = 2m\omega_n$

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 emphasises the understanding of DMF as a **gateway concept** to Response Spectrum, Seismic Coefficient, and Base Shear calculations. Mastery of this topic is *non-negotiable* for Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The DMF is derived from the equation of motion of a **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system subjected to a harmonic forcing function $p(t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the SDOF equation of motion:**

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = P_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Non-dimensionalise using standard structural dynamics parameters:**
   - Define $r = \omega / \omega_n$ (frequency ratio)
   - Define $\zeta = c / c_c$ (damping ratio)
   - Define static deflection $x_{st} = P_0 / k$

3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the ODE for the steady-state particular solution:**

$$
x(t) = \frac{P_0}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} \sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the amplitude:**

$$
(x_d)_{\max} = \frac{P_0}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Form the ratio DMF:**

$$
\text{DMF} = \frac{(x_d)_{\max}}{x_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

---

## 2.2 Why Does the DMF Behave This Way?

- **When $r \to 0$ (very low forcing frequency):** DMF $\to 1$. The system has ample time to respond; the motion is quasi-static.
- **When $r \to \infty$ (very high forcing frequency):** DMF $\to 0$. Inertia dominates and the system cannot keep up with the rapidly oscillating force.
- **When $r = 1$ (resonance, undamped):** DMF $\to \infty$. This is the **catastrophic resonance** condition.
- **When damping $\zeta = 0$ (undamped case):** DMF reduces to the classic $\dfrac{1}{1-r^2}$.
- **When damping is finite:** Peak DMF occurs at $r = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ (for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$) and equals $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Special Condition** |
|-------|-------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\text{DMF} = \dfrac{(x_d)_{\max}}{x_{st}}$ | Ratio of dynamic to static displacement | General definition |
| 2 | $\text{DMF} = \dfrac{1}{1-r^2}$ | Undamped SDOF under harmonic load | When $\zeta = 0$ |
| 3 | $\text{DMF} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Damped SDOF under harmonic load | General damped case |
| 4 | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | Frequency ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural circular frequency (rad/s) | SDOF system |
| 6 | $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{T_n}$ | Natural frequency (Hz) | $T_n$ = natural period (s) |
| 7 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n}$ | Damping ratio | $\zeta < 1$ for underdamped |
| 8 | $r_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ | Frequency ratio at peak DMF (damped) | Valid for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| 9 | $\text{DMF}_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Peak DMF (damped) | Occurs at $r_{\text{peak}}$ |
| 10 | Half-power bandwidth: $\dfrac{\Delta \omega}{\omega_n} = 2\zeta$ | Bandwidth method to find $\zeta$ | $\Delta \omega$ measured at $\text{DMF} = \text{DMF}_{\max}/\sqrt{2}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** In Question 2, students often confuse $r^2$ with $r$ in the formula. The undamped DMF is $\dfrac{1}{1-r^2}$, **not** $\dfrac{1}{1-r}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Earthquake Response Spectrum Generation:** The response spectrum is essentially the DMF plotted as a function of natural period for a given ground motion.
- **Seismic Coefficient Method (IS 1893):** Uses the DMF concept implicitly to scale pseudo-static base shear.
- **Machine Foundation Design:** DMF is used to ensure rotating machinery operates away from resonance ($r \ll 1$ or $r \gg 1$).
- **Bridge & Tower Design under Wind/Seismic:** Avoidance of resonance is a primary design consideration.

> [!NOTE]
> In **earthquake engineering**, the "force" is not a harmonic load ŌĆö it is an arbitrary transient ground motion. However, the **DMF theory for harmonic loads is the building block** of Fourier-based spectral analysis and is the conceptual parent of the **Transfer Function** in random vibration theory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of DMF (Damped SDOF System)

### 3.1.1 Starting Point ŌĆö Equation of Motion

Consider a SDOF system with mass $m$, damping coefficient $c$, and stiffness $k$, subjected to a harmonic force:

$$
p(t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

The governing differential equation of motion is:

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = P_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

### 3.1.2 Define Standard Parameters

**Static deflection** under $P_0$:

$$
x_{st} = \frac{P_0}{k}
$$

**Natural circular frequency**:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}
$$

**Damping ratio**:

$$
\zeta = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \frac{c}{c_c}
$$

**Frequency ratio**:

$$
r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}
$$

### 3.1.3 Non-Dimensionalise the Equation

Divide the entire ODE by $k$:

$$
\frac{m}{k}\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{k}\dot{x} + x = \frac{P_0}{k}\sin(\omega t)
$$

Using $\dfrac{m}{k} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}$ and $\dfrac{c}{k} = \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n}$, we get:

$$
\frac{1}{\omega_n^2}\ddot{x} + \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_n}\dot{x} + x = x_{st}\sin(\omega t)
$$

Multiplying through by $\omega_n^2$:

$$
\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = \omega_n^2 x_{st}\sin(\omega t)
$$

### 3.1.4 Assume Steady-State Particular Solution

Let the steady-state solution be:

$$
x_p(t) = A\sin(\omega t) + B\cos(\omega t)
$$

Compute derivatives:

$$
\dot{x}_p(t) = A\omega\cos(\omega t) - B\omega\sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
\ddot{x}_p(t) = -A\omega^2\sin(\omega t) - B\omega^2\cos(\omega t)
$$

### 3.1.5 Substitute into the ODE

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\bigl[-A\omega^2\sin(\omega t) - B\omega^2\cos(\omega t)\bigr] \\
&+ 2\zeta\omega_n\bigl[A\omega\cos(\omega t) - B\omega\sin(\omega t)\bigr] \\
&+ \omega_n^2\bigl[A\sin(\omega t) + B\cos(\omega t)\bigr] = \omega_n^2 x_{st}\sin(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Group Sine and Cosine Terms

**Coefficient of $\sin(\omega t)$:**

$$
-A\omega^2 - 2\zeta\omega_n B\omega + A\omega_n^2 = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

$$
A(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2) - 2\zeta\omega_n B\omega = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

**Coefficient of $\cos(\omega t)$:**

$$
-B\omega^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n A\omega + B\omega_n^2 = 0
$$

$$
B(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2) + 2\zeta\omega_n A\omega = 0
$$

### 3.1.7 Solve the Linear System

From the second equation:

$$
B = -\frac{2\zeta\omega_n \omega A}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}
$$

Substitute into the first equation:

$$
A(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2) - 2\zeta\omega_n \omega \left(-\frac{2\zeta\omega_n \omega A}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}\right) = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

$$
A(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2) + \frac{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 \omega^2 A}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2} = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

Factor out $A$:

$$
A\left[(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2) + \frac{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 \omega^2}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}\right] = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

Common denominator:

$$
A\left[\frac{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 \omega^2}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}\right] = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

### 3.1.8 Express in Frequency Ratio Form

Recall $r = \omega/\omega_n$, so $\omega^2 = r^2\omega_n^2$ and $\omega_n^2 - \omega^2 = \omega_n^2(1 - r^2)$.

Therefore:

$$
A\left[\frac{\omega_n^4(1-r^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2\omega_n^4 r^2}{\omega_n^2(1-r^2)}\right] = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

$$
A\left[\frac{\omega_n^2\left[(1-r^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2 r^2\right]}{(1-r^2)}\right] = \omega_n^2 x_{st}
$$

Cancel $\omega_n^2$:

$$
A\left[\frac{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}{1-r^2}\right] = x_{st}
$$

$$
A = \frac{x_{st}(1-r^2)}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}
$$

### 3.1.9 Compute B Similarly

By symmetry (substituting $r$ in):

$$
B = \frac{-2\zeta r \, x_{st}}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}
$$

### 3.1.10 Compute the Amplitude

The amplitude of the steady-state response is:

$$
(x_d)_{\max} = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}
$$

$$
(x_d)_{\max} = \frac{x_{st}\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}
$$

$$
(x_d)_{\max} = \frac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

### 3.1.11 Final DMF Expression

Dividing by $x_{st}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DMF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For the **undamped** case, set $\zeta = 0$, and the formula elegantly reduces to $\text{DMF} = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1-r^2 \vert} = \dfrac{1}{1-r^2}$ (for $r < 1$, positive; for $r > 1$, the negative sign simply indicates a 180┬░ phase shift).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Peak DMF and Resonant Frequency (Damped)

Differentiate DMF with respect to $r$ and set $\dfrac{d(\text{DMF})}{dr} = 0$.

Let $D(r) = (1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$. Then:

$$
\frac{dD}{dr} = 2(1-r^2)(-2r) + 2(2\zeta r)(2\zeta) = 0
$$

$$
-4r(1-r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0
$$

$$
4r\left[-(1-r^2) + 2\zeta^2\right] = 0
$$

For $r \neq 0$:

$$
-(1-r^2) + 2\zeta^2 = 0
$$

$$
r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\;r_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}\;}
$$

Valid for $\zeta < \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$.

Substituting $r_{\text{peak}}$ into DMF:

$$
\text{DMF}_{\max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-(1-2\zeta^2))^2 + (2\zeta\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2})^2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\zeta^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2(1-2\zeta^2)}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2 - 8\zeta^4}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^2 - 4\zeta^4}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DMF}_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **light damping** ($\zeta \ll 1$): $\text{DMF}_{\max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A SDOF system has $m = 1000$ kg, $k = 40{,}000$ N/m, $\zeta = 0.05$. The system is subjected to a harmonic force of amplitude $P_0 = 5000$ N at $\omega = 5$ rad/s. Compute the DMF.

**Solution:**

Step 1 ŌĆö Natural frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{40000}{1000}} = \sqrt{40} = 6.3246 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency ratio:

$$
r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{5}{6.3246} = 0.7906
$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Static deflection:

$$
x_{st} = \frac{P_0}{k} = \frac{5000}{40000} = 0.125 \text{ m}
$$

Step 4 ŌĆö DMF:

$$
\text{DMF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-0.7906^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 0.7906)^2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-0.6250)^2 + (0.07906)^2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{(0.3750)^2 + (0.006251)}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.1406 + 0.006251}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.1469}} = \frac{1}{0.3833} = 2.609
$$

Step 5 ŌĆö Maximum dynamic displacement:

$$
(x_d)_{\max} = \text{DMF} \cdot x_{st} = 2.609 \times 0.125 = 0.3261 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dynamic deflection is **2.6 times** the static deflection ŌĆö this amplification is what makes earthquake-resistant design a specialised discipline.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö DMF Behaviour Map

The following flowchart maps how DMF evolves as a function of the frequency ratio $r$ and damping ratio $\zeta$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Harmonic Force on SDOF System"] --> B["Define Parameters: omega_n, zeta, r = omega / omega_n"]
    B --> C{"Damping Present?"}
    C -- "No, zeta = 0" --> D["Use DMF = 1 / (1 - r^2)"]
    C -- "Yes, zeta > 0" --> E["Use DMF = 1 / sqrt((1-r^2)^2 + (2 zeta r)^2)"]
    D --> F{"Compare r with 1"}
    E --> G{"Compare r with r_peak = sqrt(1 - 2 zeta^2)"}
    F -- "r much less than 1" --> H["Quasi-Static Region: DMF approaches 1"]
    F -- "r much greater than 1" --> I["Inertia-Dominated Region: DMF approaches 0"]
    F -- "r equals 1" --> J["Pure Resonance: DMF tends to infinity"]
    G -- "r much less than 1" --> K["Quasi-Static Region: DMF approaches 1"]
    G -- "r much greater than 1" --> L["Inertia-Dominated Region: DMF approaches 0"]
    G -- "r equals r_peak" --> M["Damped Resonance: DMF_max = 1 / (2 zeta sqrt(1 - zeta^2))"]
    H --> N["Output DMF and Maximum Dynamic Displacement"]
    I --> N
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O["Design Decision: Avoid Resonance in Real Structures"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö DMF Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Input: Mass m, Stiffness k, Damping c, Force Amplitude P0, Forcing Frequency omega"] --> A2["Compute Natural Frequency omega_n = sqrt(k/m)"]
    A2 --> A3["Compute Frequency Ratio r = omega / omega_n"]
    A3 --> A4["Compute Damping Ratio zeta = c / (2 m omega_n)"]
    A4 --> A5["Compute Static Deflection x_st = P0 / k"]
    A5 --> A6["Compute DMF using Damped Formula"]
    A6 --> A7["Output: Dynamic Max Displacement = DMF * x_st"]
    A7 --> A8["Compare with Allowable Displacement per IS 1893"]
    A8 --> A9{"Safe?"}
    A9 -- "Yes" --> A10["Accept Design"]
    A9 -- "No" --> A11["Revise Stiffness or Add Damping"]
    A11 --> A2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
**Define Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF). Derive the expression for DMF of an undamped SDOF system under harmonic excitation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Dynamic Magnification Factor is the ratio of the maximum dynamic displacement amplitude of a vibrating system to the static deflection produced by the same force applied statically.

$$
\text{DMF} = \frac{(x_d)_{\max}}{x_{st}}
$$

**Derivation for undamped case:**

The equation of motion of an undamped SDOF system under harmonic force $P_0\sin(\omega t)$:

$$
m\ddot{x} + kx = P_0\sin(\omega t)
$$

Assume steady-state solution $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$. Substituting:

$$
-mA\omega^2\sin(\omega t) + kA\sin(\omega t) = P_0\sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
A(k - m\omega^2) = P_0
$$

$$
A = \frac{P_0}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{P_0/k}{1 - \omega^2/\omega_n^2} = \frac{x_{st}}{1 - r^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{DMF} = \frac{A}{x_{st}} = \frac{1}{1 - r^2}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Setting up ODE (1 mark) + Final expression (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
**Explain the phenomenon of resonance in a SDOF system. What is the value of DMF at resonance for undamped and damped (with $\zeta = 0.05$) systems?**

**Model Answer:**

**Resonance:**
Resonance is the condition in which the frequency of the external harmonic excitation matches (or is very close to) the natural frequency of the system. At resonance, even a small force can produce disproportionately large dynamic amplitudes.

**At resonance, $r = 1$ (or $r = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ for damped).**

**Undamped case ($\zeta = 0$):**

$$
\text{DMF}_{\text{resonance}} = \frac{1}{1 - 1^2} \to \infty
$$

**Damped case ($\zeta = 0.05$):**

$$
\text{DMF}_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2(0.05)\sqrt{1-0.0025}} = \frac{1}{0.10 \times 0.9987} = 10.01
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget that for the damped case, the peak occurs at $r = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$, not at $r = 1$. For light damping, the difference is negligible, but the exact formula must be used for $\zeta > 0.1$.

**Marks Distribution:** Concept of resonance (1 mark) + Undamped value (1 mark) + Damped value with calculation (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module-1 questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper Pattern, Module 1 Long Answer]**

**A SDOF system has the following properties:**
- Mass $m = 2000$ kg
- Stiffness $k = 80{,}000$ N/m
- Damping ratio $\zeta = 0.10$
- Subjected to harmonic force $P(t) = 10{,}000 \sin(8t)$ N

**Answer the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine the natural frequency, the frequency ratio, and compute the Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF) using the damped formula. Also find the static deflection and the maximum dynamic displacement.

**(b) [7 marks]** Plot (describe qualitatively) the variation of DMF with frequency ratio for $\zeta = 0$, $\zeta = 0.10$, and $\zeta = 0.20$. Identify and discuss the practical significance of resonance in earthquake engineering.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{80000}{2000}} = \sqrt{40} = 6.3246 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency ratio:**

$$
r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{8}{6.3246} = 1.265
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Static deflection:**

$$
x_{st} = \frac{P_0}{k} = \frac{10000}{80000} = 0.125 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö DMF (damped formula):**

$$
\text{DMF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

Compute $r^2 = 1.265^2 = 1.600$:

$$
1 - r^2 = 1 - 1.600 = -0.600
$$

$$
(1-r^2)^2 = 0.360
$$

$$
2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.10 \times 1.265 = 0.253
$$

$$
(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.0640
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{0.360 + 0.0640} = \sqrt{0.424} = 0.651
$$

$$
\text{DMF} = \frac{1}{0.651} = 1.536
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Maximum dynamic displacement:**

$$
(x_d)_{\max} = \text{DMF} \times x_{st} = 1.536 \times 0.125 = 0.192 \text{ m} = 192 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $r = 1.265 > 1$, the system is past resonance, so the response is actually **smaller** than the static response in some sense ŌĆö but the DMF > 1 still indicates amplification relative to the quasi-static case. The phase lag here is greater than 90┬░.

**Marks Split:** Natural frequency (1) + Frequency ratio (1) + Static deflection (1) + DMF computation (3) + Maximum dynamic displacement (1).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]:**

The DMF-frequency ratio curves for various damping ratios have the following characteristics:

- For $\zeta = 0$ (undamped): DMF starts at 1, rises steeply and tends to **infinity** at $r = 1$, then decreases and asymptotically approaches 1 from above for large $r$ (with negative phase, meaning out-of-phase).
- For $\zeta = 0.10$: Peak DMF is reduced to $\dfrac{1}{2(0.10)\sqrt{1-0.01}} = 5.025$ at $r = \sqrt{1-0.02} = 0.990$.
- For $\zeta = 0.20$: Peak DMF further reduces to $\dfrac{1}{2(0.20)\sqrt{1-0.04}} = 2.551$ at $r = \sqrt{1-0.08} = 0.960$.

**Qualitative Plot Description:**

- X-axis: Frequency ratio $r$ from 0 to 2.
- Y-axis: DMF from 0 to ~10.
- All three curves start at DMF = 1 when $r = 0$.
- As $r$ approaches 1, undamped curve diverges to infinity, while damped curves peak at finite values.
- After $r = 1$, all curves decrease and approach 0 as $r \to \infty$.
- Higher damping = lower peak, slightly shifted to the left.

**Practical Significance in Earthquake Engineering:**

1. **Avoidance of resonance:** Structures should be designed so that their natural period is well-separated from the dominant period of ground motion (typically 0.1ŌĆō0.6 s for most earthquakes).
2. **Damping devices:** Modern earthquake engineering uses base isolators, viscous dampers, and tuned mass dampers to increase $\zeta$ and limit DMF peaks.
3. **Code provisions:** IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016 specifies design response spectra that account for frequency-dependent amplification ŌĆö a direct application of DMF theory.
4. **Tall buildings:** With long natural periods, the DMF in the "plateau region" of the spectrum is small ŌĆö but the displacement is large, demanding ductile detailing.

**Marks Split:** Curve description (2 marks) + Plot explanation (2 marks) + Earthquake significance (3 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper Pattern, Module 1 Long Answer]**

**(a) [7 marks]** For a damped SDOF system, derive the expression for the Dynamic Magnification Factor in terms of the frequency ratio $r$ and damping ratio $\zeta$. Show that the peak DMF occurs at $r = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ and equals $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** A machine of mass 500 kg operates at 600 rpm. It is to be mounted on a foundation that can be modelled as a SDOF system. Design the stiffness such that the operating frequency is at least **2.5 times** the natural frequency of the foundationŌĆōmachine system. Assume $\zeta = 0$. Also calculate the DMF at the operating frequency.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]:**

[Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 and Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete step-by-step working. The final results are:]

$$
\boxed{\text{DMF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}, \quad \text{DMF}_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}
$$

**Marks Split:** Setting up ODE and solving (3 marks) + Differentiation to find peak (2 marks) + Substituting back for DMF_max (2 marks).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Operating (forcing) frequency:**

$$
\omega = 2\pi \times \frac{600}{60} = 2\pi \times 10 = 62.832 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Required frequency ratio:**

We require:

$$
r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = 2.5 \implies \omega_n = \frac{\omega}{2.5} = \frac{62.832}{2.5} = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Required stiffness:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \implies k = m\omega_n^2 = 500 \times (25.133)^2
$$

$$
k = 500 \times 631.65 = 315{,}825 \text{ N/m} \approx 316 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö DMF at operating frequency (undamped):**

$$
\text{DMF} = \frac{1}{1 - r^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 2.5^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 6.25} = \frac{1}{-5.25} = -0.190
$$

The magnitude is:

$$
|\text{DMF}| = 0.190
$$

The negative sign indicates a 180┬░ phase lag ŌĆö the system is **out of phase** with the excitation (post-resonance behaviour).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Interpretation:**

The operating frequency is well above resonance, so the dynamic response is only about 19% of the static deflection in magnitude. This is a **safe design** that avoids resonance amplification.

**Marks Split:** Operating frequency (1) + Required natural frequency (1) + Stiffness (2) + DMF calculation (2) + Interpretation (1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE MARKS in DMF questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value at resonance** ŌĆö DMF ŌåÆ Ōł× at $r = 1$ for undamped, so no finite answer exists. Students write $\dfrac{1}{0}$ or invent a number.
> 2. **Confusing $r$ and $r^2$** ŌĆö The undamped formula is $\dfrac{1}{1-r^2}$, NOT $\dfrac{1}{1-r}$.
> 3. **Sign convention for damped DMF** ŌĆö The DMF is always taken as the **magnitude**. A negative sign only indicates phase; it does not mean "negative amplification."
> 4. **Unit mix-up** ŌĆö Mass in kg, stiffness in N/m, $\omega$ in rad/s. Mixing units (e.g., stiffness in kN/m) gives a wrong answer by a factor of 1000.
> 5. **Ignoring damping in the formula** ŌĆö Some students apply the undamped formula even when $\zeta$ is given. The damped formula **must** be used whenever $\zeta > 0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DMF Definition:** Ratio of maximum dynamic amplitude to static deflection under the same peak force.
- **Undamped DMF:** $\text{DMF} = \dfrac{1}{1-r^2}$ where $r = \omega/\omega_n$.
- **Damped DMF:** $\text{DMF} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$.
- **Resonance condition (undamped):** $r = 1$, DMF $\to \infty$.
- **Resonance condition (damped):** $r = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$, $\text{DMF}_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- **Static deflection:** $x_{st} = P_0 / k$.
- **Natural frequency of SDOF:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c / (2m\omega_n)$. Typical values: 0.02ŌĆō0.05 for steel, 0.05ŌĆō0.10 for concrete, 0.10ŌĆō0.20 for special damped structures.
- **Quasi-static region ($r \ll 1$):** DMF $\approx 1$, system follows the load.
- **Inertia-dominated region ($r \gg 1$):** DMF $\approx 0$, system is too slow to follow rapid load reversals.
- **Half-power bandwidth:** $\Delta\omega / \omega_n = 2\zeta$ ŌĆö experimental method to find damping ratio.
- **KTU relevance:** DMF is the foundational concept behind response spectra, base shear calculations, and the seismic coefficient method.
- **Avoidance strategy in design:** Detune structures away from dominant ground motion frequencies; introduce damping; use base isolation.
- **Dimensionless nature:** DMF has no units ŌĆö always check that the ratio is consistent.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"DMF = Dynamic = Divided by (1-r┬▓) Fully, with 2╬Čr added under the root for damping."* Visualise the formula:
> 
> $$
> \text{DMF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
> $$
> 
> The first term inside the root represents the **stiffness-inertia interaction**, the second represents the **damping effect**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Pseudo Acceleration and Velocity - Response Spectra
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Pseudo Acceleration and Velocity - Response Spectra

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Response Spectrum** is a plot of the peak (maximum) response of a series of Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) oscillators with varying natural periods (or frequencies) and a specified damping ratio, when subjected to a particular ground motion time history. The peak response may be expressed in terms of absolute acceleration, relative velocity, or relative displacement of the mass.

A **Pseudo-Acceleration Spectrum (PSA)** is defined as the response spectrum ordinate that would produce the same peak base shear in a short-period structure as the actual peak relative displacement, mathematically expressed as:

$$PSA = \omega_n^2 \cdot S_d$$

where $\omega_n$ is the natural circular frequency of the SDOF system and $S_d$ is the spectral relative displacement.

A **Pseudo-Velocity Spectrum (PSV)** is defined as:

$$PSV = \omega_n \cdot S_d$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The terms "pseudo" arise because PSA approximates (rather than equals) the peak absolute acceleration, and PSV approximates (rather than equals) the peak relative velocity. For lightly damped systems (╬Š Ōēż 20%), the approximation is excellent and widely used in earthquake engineering practice.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Tuning Fork" of Earthquakes

Imagine you have 50 tuning forks of different lengths (each representing an SDOF oscillator with a different natural period). Now strike them all simultaneously with the same earthquake ground motion.

- **Long forks (long period structures)** ŌåÆ swing slowly, just like tall buildings or long bridges.
- **Short forks (short period structures)** ŌåÆ vibrate quickly, like stiff low-rise buildings.

Each tuning fork will respond with a different maximum swing (displacement), different speed (velocity), and different force (acceleration ├Ś mass). If you now plot the maximum response of each fork against its own natural period, you get a **Response Spectrum**. 

The "pseudo" terms are like *imaginary friends* of velocity and acceleration: they are not the actual velocity/acceleration, but they are mathematically related to the spectral displacement in a way that makes engineering calculations remarkably simple.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Response Spectrum is *ground-motion specific*. Two different earthquakes will produce two different response spectra. A **Design Spectrum** (used by codes like IS 1893) is a smoothed, normalized envelope representing an "ensemble" of expected earthquakes at a site.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Natural Period (T_n):** 0.01 s to 10 s (typical engineering range)
- **Damping Ratio (╬Š):** 0% to 20% (5% is the conventional reference for buildings)
- **Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA):** typically **0.05g to 0.6g** in design
- **Gravitational acceleration: g = 9.81 m/s┬▓**
- **Pseudo acceleration ratio (PSA/PGA):** often reaches **2.0 to 3.0** in the velocity-sensitive region

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö SDOF System Under Ground Motion

Consider a linear SDOF system with mass **m**, stiffness **k**, and viscous damping coefficient **c**, subjected to horizontal ground acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t)$. The total absolute acceleration of the mass is $\ddot{u}_g + \ddot{u}$ where $u$ is the relative displacement with respect to the ground.

The equation of dynamic equilibrium (D'Alembert's principle) is:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = -m\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

Dividing throughout by **m**:

$$\ddot{u} + 2\xi\omega_n\dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = -\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

where:
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ ŌĆö natural circular frequency (rad/s)
- $\xi = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n}$ ŌĆö damping ratio (dimensionless)
- $T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$ ŌĆö natural period (s)

### 2.2 Duhamel's Integral Solution (Forcing Function Response)

The relative displacement response of the SDOF system, for an undamped system starting from rest, is given by the **Duhamel Integral**:

$$u(t) = -\frac{1}{\omega_n}\int_0^t \ddot{u}_g(\tau)\sin[\omega_n(t-\tau)]\,d\tau$$

For a damped system, the solution becomes:

$$u(t) = -\frac{1}{\omega_d}\int_0^t \ddot{u}_g(\tau)\,e^{-\xi\omega_n(t-\tau)}\sin[\omega_d(t-\tau)]\,d\tau$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$ is the *damped* natural frequency.

### 2.3 Three Spectral Ordinates

For a given ground motion and SDOF oscillator (with fixed $T_n$ and $\xi$), the peak responses are:

| Spectral Ordinate | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Spectral Relative Displacement | $S_d$ | $\max_t \vert u(t) \vert$ |
| Spectral Pseudo-Velocity | $PSV$ | $\omega_n \cdot S_d$ |
| Spectral Pseudo-Acceleration | $PSA$ | $\omega_n^2 \cdot S_d$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural circular frequency | rad/s |
| 2 | $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ | Natural period | s |
| 3 | $f_n = 1/T_n$ | Natural frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $\xi = c/(2m\omega_n)$ | Critical damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $PSV = \omega_n S_d$ | Pseudo-velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $PSA = \omega_n^2 S_d$ | Pseudo-acceleration | m/s┬▓ |
| 7 | $PSA = \omega_n \cdot PSV$ | Tripartite relationship | m/s┬▓ |
| 8 | $PSV^2 = S_d \cdot PSA$ | Logarithmic mean property | m┬▓/s┬│ |
| 9 | $S_{a,\text{abs}} \approx PSA$ | Absolute accel Ōēł Pseudo accel (low ╬Š) | m/s┬▓ |
| 10 | $F_{\text{base}} = m \cdot PSA$ | Equivalent static base shear | N |
| 11 | $S_d = (T_n/2\pi)^2 \cdot PSA$ | Spectral displacement from PSA | m |
| 12 | $V_b = A_h \cdot W$ | IS 1893 design base shear | kN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Civil engineers use response spectra to determine design base shear ($V_b$) for buildings per IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016. Architects use them to size structural members, select dampers, and design base isolators. The tripartite plot is widely used in dynamic analysis software (SAP2000, ETABS) for seismic design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pseudo-Acceleration from the Equation of Motion

**Starting point:** The total acceleration of the mass is the sum of ground acceleration and relative acceleration:

$$a_{abs}(t) = \ddot{u}_g(t) + \ddot{u}(t)$$

From the equation of motion:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = -m\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

Rearranging:

$$m(\ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g) = -c\dot{u} - ku$$

The left-hand side is the **inertial force** in the mass, and the right-hand side is the **restoring force** (damping + spring) acting on the structure.

The peak absolute acceleration is:

$$S_{a,abs} = \max_t \vert \ddot{u}(t) + \ddot{u}_g(t) \vert$$

Now, for the spring force, the peak value is:

$$\max_t \vert ku(t) \vert = k \cdot S_d$$

The peak base shear is therefore:

$$V_{b,\text{peak}} = k \cdot S_d = m\omega_n^2 S_d = m \cdot PSA$$

If we *define* the **pseudo-acceleration** as the quantity $PSA$ that, when multiplied by mass $m$, gives the same peak spring force as the actual peak relative displacement would give, then:

$$PSA = \omega_n^2 S_d$$

This is by definition the *pseudo*-acceleration ŌĆö the same numerical value as the spectral displacement times $\omega_n^2$, which is also (for low damping) a very good approximation to the actual peak absolute acceleration.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Tripartite Relationships

We begin with the three definitions:

$$S_d = \text{spectral displacement}$$

$$PSV = \omega_n S_d$$

$$PSA = \omega_n^2 S_d$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express $S_d$ in terms of PSV:**

$$S_d = \frac{PSV}{\omega_n}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express $S_d$ in terms of PSA:**

$$S_d = \frac{PSA}{\omega_n^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Eliminate $S_d$ to get PSVŌĆōPSA relation:**

$$\frac{PSA}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{PSV}{\omega_n}$$

$$\Rightarrow PSA = \omega_n \cdot PSV \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Eliminate $\omega_n$ to get a log-mean relationship:**

From (i): $\omega_n = PSA/PSV$

Substituting into $S_d = PSA/\omega_n^2$:

$$S_d = \frac{PSA}{(PSA/PSV)^2} = \frac{PSV^2}{PSA}$$

Rearranging:

$$PSV^2 = S_d \cdot PSA \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Taking logarithms of (ii):

$$2\log(PSV) = \log(S_d) + \log(PSA)$$

This is the key property: in a **tripartite (four-way log) plot**, where the horizontal axis is $T_n$ and the three vertical axes (rotated 45┬░ to each other) are $S_d$, $PSV$, and $PSA$, all three spectra collapse onto a **single curve**.

### 3.3 Why "Pseudo" and Not "True"?

For a damped SDOF system, the absolute acceleration is:

$$\ddot{u}(t) + \ddot{u}_g(t) = \omega_n^2 u(t) + 2\xi\omega_n\dot{u}(t)$$

At the instant when the relative displacement $u(t)$ is at its peak, the velocity $\dot{u}(t) = 0$, but the damping force $2\xi\omega_n\dot{u}$ is **not zero in general** because $u$ may have been changing rapidly just before. Hence:

$$\max \vert \ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g \vert \neq \omega_n^2 S_d \text{ exactly, unless } \xi \to 0$$

For $\xi \le 20\%$, the error is small (typically < 10ŌĆō15%), justifying the "pseudo" terminology and the engineering practice of using PSA in lieu of $S_{a,abs}$.

### 3.4 Characteristic Regions of a Response Spectrum

A response spectrum plotted on the standard four-way log paper has three distinct regions:

1. **Acceleration-Controlled Region (Short Periods, $T_n < T_c$):** 
   PSA is approximately constant; structure behaves as a rigid body with the ground. $S_d \propto T_n^2$, $PSV \propto T_n$.

2. **Velocity-Controlled Region (Intermediate Periods, $T_c < T_n < T_d$):**
   PSV is approximately constant; $PSA \propto 1/T_n$ and $S_d \propto T_n$.

3. **Displacement-Controlled Region (Long Periods, $T_n > T_d$):**
   $S_d$ is approximately constant; $PSA \propto 1/T_n^2$ and $PSV \propto 1/T_n$.

The corner periods $T_c$ (constant-acceleration to constant-velocity) and $T_d$ (constant-velocity to constant-displacement) are spectrum-dependent; for design spectra, $T_c \approx 0.4$ŌĆō$0.6$ s and $T_d \approx 3$ s for stiff soil sites.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Response Spectrum Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ground Motion Record u_g t] --> B[Select SDOF Oscillator T_n and xi]
    B --> C[Solve Equation of Motion Duhamels Integral]
    C --> D[Compute Time Histories u t, v t, a_abs t]
    D --> E[Extract Peak Values S_d, PSV, PSA]
    E --> F[Repeat for Range of T_n 0.01s to 10s]
    F --> G[Plot Peak vs T_n on Log-Log Axes]
    G --> H[Response Spectrum Curve for given xi]
    H --> I[Construct Tripartite Plot 4-way Log]
    I --> J[Use for Seismic Design Base Shear]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Tripartite Plot Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Spectrum Curve] --> B[Diagonal Axis 1 S_d]
    A --> C[Diagonal Axis 2 PSV]
    A --> D[Diagonal Axis 3 PSA]
    A --> E[Horizontal Axis T_n]
    B -.PSV equals omega_n S_d.-> C
    C -.PSA equals omega_n PSV.-> D
    D -.S_d equals PSV squared over PSA.-> B
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Three Spectral Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Response Spectrum on 4-way Log] --> B[Short Period Region T_n less than T_c]
    A --> C[Intermediate Period Region T_c less than T_n less than T_d]
    A --> D[Long Period Region T_n greater than T_d]
    B --> E[PSA constant or rising]
    C --> F[PSV constant]
    D --> G[S_d constant or rising]
    E --> H[Acceleration Controlled Design]
    F --> I[Velocity Controlled Design]
    G --> J[Displacement Controlled Design]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Table ŌĆö Why Pseudo Spectra Are Used

| Aspect | True Response | Pseudo Response |
|---|---|---|
