| Relative velocity | $S_v = \max \vert \dot{u} \vert$ | $PSV = \omega_n S_d$ |
| Absolute acceleration | $S_{a,abs} = \max \vert \ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g \vert$ | $PSA = \omega_n^2 S_d$ |
| Computation cost | High (Duhamel integration) | Low (use PSA directly) |
| Validity | Exact | Excellent for xi less than 20 percent |
| Code usage | Rare | Universal (IS 1893, IBC, Eurocode 8) |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
**Define response spectrum. Explain how it differs from a design spectrum.**

**Model Answer:**
A **response spectrum** is a plot of the peak response (in terms of displacement, velocity, or acceleration) of a family of SDOF oscillators with varying natural periods and a fixed damping ratio, subjected to a *specific* ground motion time history. It is ground-motion dependent.

A **design spectrum** is a smoothed, normalized, and code-specified envelope response spectrum intended to represent the design earthquake hazard at a site. It is derived by averaging and smoothing many response spectra of recorded ground motions consistent with the design basis earthquake, and it is the spectrum actually used in structural design (e.g., IS 1893 spectra).

**Key differences (3 marks):**
- Response spectrum: specific to one earthquake record; design spectrum: smooth envelope of many.
- Response spectrum has jagged peaks/valleys; design spectrum is smooth and idealized.
- Design spectrum is used directly for code-based seismic design calculations.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is pseudo-acceleration? Why is it used in earthquake engineering instead of true absolute acceleration?**

**Model Answer:**
**Pseudo-acceleration (PSA)** is defined as $PSA = \omega_n^2 \cdot S_d$, where $S_d$ is the peak relative displacement of the SDOF mass and $\omega_n$ is the natural circular frequency. It has units of acceleration (m/s┬▓).

**Why used:**
1. For lightly damped systems (╬Š Ōēż 20%), PSA is an excellent approximation to the true peak absolute acceleration $S_{a,abs}$.
2. PSA multiplied by mass directly gives the peak base shear: $V_b = m \cdot PSA$.
3. Computationally simpler than solving for the absolute acceleration time history.
4. Used universally in modern seismic codes (IS 1893, ASCE 7, Eurocode 8) for design base shear computations.

---

### PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between pseudo-velocity (PSV), pseudo-acceleration (PSA), and spectral displacement ($S_d$). Show that these three spectra collapse onto a single curve in a tripartite plot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction of a tripartite response spectrum. Sketch the characteristic shape and identify the three spectral regions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** For a SDOF oscillator of natural period $T_n$ and damping ratio $\xi$, the peak relative displacement is $S_d$.

**Step 2:** By definition of pseudo quantities:
$$PSV = \omega_n S_d \quad \text{...(i)}$$
$$PSA = \omega_n^2 S_d \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3:** Dividing (ii) by (i):
$$\frac{PSA}{PSV} = \omega_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad PSA = \omega_n \cdot PSV$$

**Step 4:** From (i): $\omega_n = PSV/S_d$. Substituting in (ii):
$$PSA = \left(\frac{PSV}{S_d}\right)^2 S_d = \frac{PSV^2}{S_d}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiplying:
$$PSV^2 = PSA \cdot S_d \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 6:** Taking logarithms:
$$2\log(PSV) = \log(PSA) + \log(S_d)$$

This is the equation of a straight line in logŌĆōlog space. In the tripartite plot, the three vertical axes ($S_d$, PSV, PSA) are mutually inclined at 60┬░ to each other (i.e., 120┬░ apart) so that the same point on the spectrum curve simultaneously reads off the three spectral ordinates using parallel lines through the point. Hence all three spectra collapse onto one curve.

**(b) Tripartite Spectrum Construction (7 marks):**

**Construction steps:**
1. Use four-way logarithmic graph paper. Horizontal axis: $T_n$ (log scale, 0.01 to 10 s).
2. Three vertical axes mutually at 60┬░ to each other, scaled for $S_d$ (m), PSV (m/s), and PSA (m/s┬▓).
3. The axes are positioned so that a horizontal line reads the same PSV on both PSV and PSA axes, a 45┬░ inclined line reads the same $S_d$, and another 45┬░ inclined line reads the same PSA.

**The curve is divided into three regions:**

| Region | Period Range | Dominant Quantity | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration-controlled | $T_n < T_c$ (short) | PSA | $PSA \approx \text{const}$ |
| Velocity-controlled | $T_c < T_n < T_d$ | PSV | $PSV \approx \text{const}$ |
| Displacement-controlled | $T_n > T_d$ (long) | $S_d$ | $S_d \approx \text{const}$ |

The corner periods $T_c \approx 0.4$ s and $T_d \approx 3$ s for typical design spectra. As damping increases, the entire curve shifts downward (lower spectral ordinates).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Choice Question]**

**(a)** An SDOF system has $T_n = 0.5$ s, $\xi = 5\%$. From the response spectrum for the El Centro 1940 NS earthquake, the peak spectral acceleration is read as $PSA = 0.4g$. Compute the spectral displacement $S_d$, pseudo-velocity $PSV$, and the equivalent static base shear if the mass is $m = 50{,}000$ kg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss with a sketch how a smooth design response spectrum (as per IS 1893) is constructed from several real earthquake response spectra. What is the role of site classification in this process? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_n = 0.5$ s, $\xi = 0.05$, $PSA = 0.4g = 0.4 \times 9.81 = 3.924$ m/s┬▓, $m = 50{,}000$ kg.

**Step 1:** Compute $\omega_n$:
$$\omega_n = \frac{2\pi}{T_n} = \frac{2\pi}{0.5} = 4\pi \approx 12.566 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Spectral displacement $S_d$:
$$S_d = \frac{PSA}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{3.924}{(12.566)^2} = \frac{3.924}{157.91} \approx 0.02485 \text{ m} \approx 24.85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Pseudo-velocity $PSV$:
$$PSV = \omega_n S_d = 12.566 \times 0.02485 \approx 0.3123 \text{ m/s}$$

Verification using direct formula: $PSV = PSA/\omega_n = 3.924/12.566 \approx 0.3123$ m/s Ō£ō

**Step 4:** Equivalent static base shear $V_b$:
$$V_b = m \cdot PSA = 50{,}000 \times 3.924 = 196{,}200 \text{ N} = 196.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** $S_d \approx 24.85$ mm, $PSV \approx 0.312$ m/s, $V_b \approx 196.2$ kN.

---

**(b) Construction of Design Spectrum (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Gather Records:** Select a set of strong-motion records (typically 10ŌĆō20) consistent with the design basis earthquake magnitude, source mechanism, and source-to-site distance at the project location.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Site Classification:** Classify the site into one of the categories as per IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016 ŌĆö Type I (Rock/Hard soil), Type II (Medium/Stiff soil), or Type III (Soft soil). Each category has its own amplification characteristics in the response spectrum.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Response Spectra:** For each record, compute the 5%-damped elastic response spectrum. Normalize each spectrum by its PGA to obtain a spectral shape.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Statistical Averaging:** Compute the mean (or mean + 1 standard deviation) of all normalized spectral shapes for the same site class.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Smooth the Curve:** Apply frequency-domain or hand-smoothing to obtain a smooth, idealized shape ŌĆö typically with constant PSA in the short-period plateau, constant PSV in the intermediate region, and constant $S_d$ in the long-period region.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Scale by Design PGA:** Multiply the smooth normalized shape by the design PGA (or $Z \cdot 2/3 \cdot I_g \cdot S_a/g$ factor as per IS 1893) to obtain the final design spectrum.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Apply Importance Factor:** For important structures (hospitals, schools, heritage), multiply by the importance factor (1.2, 1.5, or 2.0 as per code).

**Role of site classification:** Soft soil sites amplify long-period ground motions and have higher corner periods ($T_c$, $T_d$) than rock sites. Thus, the design spectrum shifts and stretches to the right as soil becomes softer, reflecting the resonance of soft soils with long-period structures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Confusing PSV with $S_v$:** Students often use the symbol $S_v$ for "relative velocity spectrum" and PSV interchangeably. They are different for damped systems. Use $S_v$ for true peak relative velocity and PSV for pseudo.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert g to m/s┬▓:** Always multiply $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓ when computing forces in SI units.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in tripartite plot:** The three axes have *different units* but the *same scale markings* (e.g., a single horizontal line gives the same PSV on both PSV and PSA axes).
> 4. **Wrong corner period values:** For IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016, the corner period $T_c$ is typically around 0.4 s for Type II soil; do not assume universal values.
> 5. **Ignoring the 45┬░ inclination in tripartite plot:** The $S_d$ axis is rotated 45┬░ to the right of PSV; the PSA axis is rotated 45┬░ to the left of PSV. Drawing them vertically will lose marks.
> 6. **Mixing up absolute and relative acceleration:** Absolute acceleration $\ddot{u}_g + \ddot{u}$ is what is recorded; the spectrum by default shows relative response. Always clarify which is being used.

---

### ­¤ōī TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- Ō£ģ **Response spectrum** = peak response of many SDOF oscillators vs. their period, for a given ground motion and damping.
- Ō£ģ **Three spectral ordinates:** $S_d$ (displacement), $PSV = \omega_n S_d$ (pseudo-velocity), $PSA = \omega_n^2 S_d$ (pseudo-acceleration).
- Ō£ģ **Tripartite relationship:** $PSV^2 = S_d \cdot PSA$ (log-mean property).
- Ō£ģ **PSA in SI units = base shear / mass;** for low damping, PSA Ōēł peak absolute acceleration.
- Ō£ģ **Four-way log plot** collapses $S_d$, $PSV$, $PSA$ onto a single curve.
- Ō£ģ **Three spectral regions:** acceleration-controlled (short $T_n$), velocity-controlled (intermediate $T_n$), displacement-controlled (long $T_n$).
- Ō£ģ **Design spectrum** is the smoothed, normalized envelope used by IS 1893 for code-based design; it depends on site class and importance factor.
- Ō£ģ **Pseudo ŌēĀ True:** valid approximation for $\xi \leq 20\%$; otherwise compute Duhamel integral.
- Ō£ģ **$V_b = m \cdot PSA$** is the cornerstone of equivalent static seismic design.
- Ō£ģ **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016** spectra: $A_h = (Z/2)(I_g/S_a)$ or $A_h = (Z/2)(I/R) \cdot (S_a/g)$.
- Ō£ģ **Always remember the three corner periods** for design spectra: 0 (origin), $T_c$ (acc-vel transition), $T_d$ (vel-disp transition).
- Ō£ģ **Memorize key conversion:** $S_d$ (mm) = $9760 \cdot PSA/g \cdot (T_n)^2$ (with $T_n$ in seconds, $PSA$ in g).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Four-way Logarithmic Plot - DVA Spectrum (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Four-way Logarithmic Plot - DVA Spectrum (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Four-Way Logarithmic Plot (Quadrilinear Log Plot):**
A four-way logarithmic plot is a specialised graphical representation in earthquake engineering that uses logarithmic scales on **four** axes simultaneously ŌĆö one horizontal axis (Period, $T$) and **three** vertical axes (Spectral Pseudo-Acceleration $S_a$, Spectral Pseudo-Velocity $S_v$, and Spectral Displacement $S_d$). This unique configuration allows the engineer to read all three response spectral ordinates from a single curve at any desired period.

**DVA Spectrum (DisplacementŌĆōVelocityŌĆōAcceleration Spectrum):**
A DVA spectrum is the **combined tripartite spectrum** in which the three independent response spectra ($S_d$, $S_v$, and $S_a$) are superimposed on a single four-way logarithmic paper. It is sometimes called the **Triple-Response Spectrum** or the **Combined Spectrum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme for PECET865 Module 2, this topic is treated as a **conceptual / descriptive topic**. Numerical plotting problems are not expected; however, students must be able to *describe*, *sketch*, and *explain* the construction and utility of the plot.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you go to a weather station and want to see **three different graphs at once** ŌĆö temperature, humidity, and wind speed ŌĆö all plotted against the *same day* on the x-axis. The smart forecaster places **three separate scales** on the left side of the chart: one for temperature (┬░C), one for humidity (%), and one for wind (km/h). All three curves share the same x-axis (day). This saves space and allows instant comparison.

The **four-way logarithmic plot does exactly this** for earthquake response:

- The **x-axis (Period T)** represents the natural period of the building/structure.
- The **left scale** reads Spectral Acceleration ($S_a$) in **g** or m/s┬▓.
- A **second scale** (offset diagonally) reads Spectral Velocity ($S_v$) in m/s.
- A **third scale** (offset the other way) reads Spectral Displacement ($S_d$) in mm or m.

Just as you instantly know the temperature *and* humidity *and* wind on a given day, the engineer instantly knows $S_a$, $S_v$, **and** $S_d$ for a building of period $T$ ŌĆö from **one single point** on the curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The three vertical axes are NOT independent ŌĆö they are geometrically shifted versions of one another, exploiting the mathematical relation $S_a = \omega \cdot S_v = \omega^2 \cdot S_d$. This is why a single "tripartite" curve can serve all three.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

The following standard engineering metrics are highlighted:

- **Pseudo-Spectral Acceleration ($S_a$):** measured in **g** (9.81 m/s┬▓) or **m/s┬▓**
- **Pseudo-Spectral Velocity ($S_v$):** measured in **m/s** or **cm/s**
- **Spectral Displacement ($S_d$):** measured in **mm** or **m**
- **Period ($T$):** measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$ or $f$):** **rad/s** or **Hz**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need a Four-Way Log Plot?

In conventional linear plots, the response spectrum for an earthquake spans an enormous range of values:
- $S_a$ values can range from $0.001\,g$ to $5.0\,g$ (5000├Ś variation)
- Periods from $0.01$ s to $10$ s (1000├Ś variation)

Plotting all this on a linear scale makes small-period portions unreadable. **Logarithmic axes** compress wide ranges and reveal the power-law nature of spectra. The four-way plot goes one step further by combining three spectra into one.

### 2.2 The Governing Physical Relationships

For a Single-Degree-Of-Freedom (SDOF) system undergoing harmonic motion, the relationships are:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

$$S_v = \omega \cdot S_d = \frac{2\pi}{T} \cdot S_d$$

$$S_a = \omega^2 \cdot S_d = \left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 \cdot S_d$$

$$S_a = \omega \cdot S_v = \frac{2\pi}{T} \cdot S_v$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are EXACT relations for **undamped harmonic motion**. For a *real* earthquake-excited SDOF, the spectrum is constructed numerically (Newmark's method), but the tripartite plot still uses these relations to lay out the three axes.

### 2.3 Slopes on Log-Log Paper (Conceptual)

On a four-way log paper, when one variable changes as a power of another, the relationship becomes a **straight line** with a slope equal to the exponent:

| Plot | Relation | Slope on log-log | Physical Meaning |
|------|----------|------------------|------------------|
| $S_d$ vs $T$ (constant $S_a$) | $S_d = \frac{T^2}{4\pi^2} S_a$ | **+2** | Long-period branch |
| $S_v$ vs $T$ (constant $S_a$) | $S_v = \frac{T}{2\pi} S_a$ | **+1** | Intermediate-period branch |
| $S_a$ vs $T$ (constant) | $S_a = $ const | **0** | Plateau region |
| $S_a$ vs $T$ (zero-period limit) | $S_a \propto 1/T$ | **ŌłÆ1** | Short-period branch |

### 2.4 Construction Logic of the DVA Spectrum (Step-Wise)

1. **Start with $S_a$ŌĆō$T$ spectrum** (computed from time-history analysis or code).
2. **Apply the conversion** $S_v = (T/2\pi)\,S_a$ to obtain pseudo-velocity ordinate.
3. **Apply the conversion** $S_d = (T/2\pi)^2\,S_a$ to obtain displacement ordinate.
4. **Superimpose** all three on the same graph, using the geometrically shifted scales.
5. The single curve is read against three scales, giving the **DVA spectrum**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Circular frequency | rad/s |
| 2 | $T = 1/f$ | Period | s |
| 3 | $S_v = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} S_d$ | Velocity-Displacement relation | m/s |
| 4 | $S_a = \left(\dfrac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 S_d$ | Acceleration-Displacement relation | m/s┬▓ |
| 5 | $S_a = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} S_v$ | Acceleration-Velocity relation | m/s┬▓ |
| 6 | $S_d = \dfrac{T^2}{4\pi^2} S_a$ | Inverse: Displacement from $S_a$ | m |
| 7 | $S_v = \dfrac{T}{2\pi} S_a$ | Inverse: Velocity from $S_a$ | m/s |
| 8 | $\log S_d = \log S_a - 2\log(2\pi/T)$ | Log-linear form (slope ŌłÆ2) | ŌĆö |
| 9 | $\log S_v = \log S_a - \log(2\pi/T)$ | Log-linear form (slope ŌłÆ1) | ŌĆö |
| 10 | $\beta = 0.05$ (typical) | Damping ratio for design spectrum | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "describe the four-way log plot", always mention the three scales, the period axis, the slopes (+2, +1, 0, ŌłÆ1), and the interconversion formulas. Examiners reward completeness.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Design:** Engineers directly read the design base shear ($S_a$), inter-storey drift ($S_d$), and input energy ($S_v$) for a building of known $T$.
- **Equipment Qualification:** Nuclear-plant and sub-station equipment are qualified using $S_d$ read off the plot.
- **Code Calibration:** IS 1893, IBC, and Eurocode 8 design spectra are fundamentally tripartite in nature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between $S_a$, $S_v$, and $S_d$

Consider an SDOF system of mass $m$ and stiffness $k$ in **undamped free vibration** (or equivalently, the peak response under harmonic excitation).

The equation of motion in undamped free vibration is:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega t) + B \sin(\omega t)$$

where the **natural circular frequency** is:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate $x(t)$ to obtain velocity:**

$$\dot{x}(t) = -A\omega \sin(\omega t) + B\omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The maximum (peak) velocity amplitude is therefore:

$$S_v = \omega \cdot \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate again to obtain acceleration:**

$$\ddot{x}(t) = -A\omega^2 \cos(\omega t) - B\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$

The maximum (peak) acceleration amplitude is therefore:

$$S_a = \omega^2 \cdot \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express the three spectral ordinates relative to displacement amplitude $S_d = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$:**

$$S_d = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$$

$$S_v = \omega \cdot S_d = \frac{2\pi}{T} S_d$$

$$S_a = \omega^2 \cdot S_d = \left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 S_d$$

**Final consolidated form:**

$$\boxed{S_a = \frac{2\pi}{T}\,S_v = \left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 S_d}$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** that justifies the construction of the four-way plot.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Slopes on the Four-Way Log Paper

Let us take logarithms (base 10) of both sides of the velocity-displacement relation:

$$S_v = \frac{2\pi}{T} S_d$$

Taking $\log_{10}$:

$$\log_{10} S_v = \log_{10}(2\pi) - \log_{10} T + \log_{10} S_d$$

Rearranging to express $S_d$ in terms of $S_v$ and $T$:

$$\log_{10} S_d = \log_{10} S_v - \log_{10}(2\pi) + \log_{10} T$$

Therefore, on a log-log paper plotting $S_d$ vs $T$:

$$\log_{10} S_d = (+1)\log_{10} T + \text{constant}$$

The **slope is +1**.

Now for displacement-acceleration:

$$S_d = \frac{T^2}{4\pi^2} S_a \;\Rightarrow\; S_d = \frac{S_a}{4\pi^2}\cdot T^2$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\log_{10} S_d = \log_{10} S_a - \log_{10}(4\pi^2) + 2\log_{10} T$$

Therefore, on a log-log paper plotting $S_d$ vs $T$:

$$\log_{10} S_d = (+2)\log_{10} T + \text{constant}$$

The **slope is +2**.

For acceleration vs $T$ in the constant-acceleration plateau region:

$$S_a = \text{constant} \;\Rightarrow\; \log_{10} S_a = 0 \cdot \log_{10} T + \text{constant}$$

The **slope is 0**.

For the very-short-period region (zero-period acceleration limit) where $S_d$ is constant:

$$S_d = \text{constant} \;\Rightarrow\; S_a = (2\pi/T)^2 S_d = \text{const}/T^2$$

The slope of $\log S_a$ vs $\log T$ is **ŌłÆ2**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** The slope of $S_a$ vs $T$ in the long-period displacement-controlled region is **ŌłÆ1** (since $S_a \propto 1/T$ when $S_d$ is held constant via the second-power relation inverted; this arises when $S_v$ is the constant governing value). Students should be careful to state the assumption (what is held constant) when stating a slope.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Reading the Four-Way Plot

Suppose the design $S_a$ at a given period $T = 1.0$ s is read as $S_a = 0.4\,g$. Then:

$$S_v = \frac{T}{2\pi} S_a = \frac{1.0}{2\pi} \times 0.4 \times 9.81$$

$$S_v = \frac{1.0}{6.2832} \times 3.924 \approx 0.6245\;\text{m/s}$$

$$S_d = \frac{T^2}{4\pi^2} S_a = \frac{1.0}{39.478} \times 3.924 \approx 0.0994\;\text{m} \approx 99.4\;\text{mm}$$

These three values would be the **three readings** of the same point on the DVA spectrum at $T = 1.0$ s.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Reading a Four-Way Log Plot

Below is a Mermaid block diagram that captures the **functional reading logic** of a four-way logarithmic plot used to derive the DVA spectrum.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Earthquake Time History] --> B[Time-History Analysis of SDOF]
    B --> C[Sa vs T Spectrum - Linear Plot]
    C --> D[Apply Conversion Formula]
    D --> E["Sv = T by 2pi x Sa"]
    D --> F["Sd = T squared by 4 pi squared x Sa"]
    E --> G[Plot Sv on Velocity Axis]
    F --> H[Plot Sd on Displacement Axis]
    G --> I[Four-Way Log Paper Assembly]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Single Tripartite Curve]
    J --> K[Output: DVA Spectrum]
    K --> L[Read Sa for Base Shear]
    K --> M[Read Sd for Drift]
    K --> N[Read Sv for Energy Demand]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Three-Scale Layout

The diagram below illustrates how the **three vertical axes** are spatially laid out on a four-way logarithmic paper. (Note: the labels intentionally avoid any formatting symbols to ensure clean Mermaid rendering.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Horizontal Axis: Period T seconds, log scale] --> S1
    P --> S2
    P --> S3
    S1[Left Vertical Scale 1: Spectral Acceleration Sa, g or m per s squared, log scale]
    S2[Right Diagonal Scale 2: Spectral Velocity Sv, m per s, log scale]
    S3[Right Diagonal Scale 3: Spectral Displacement Sd, mm or m, log scale]
    S1 --> R1[Reading 1: Base Shear Coefficient]
    S2 --> R2[Reading 2: Pseudo Velocity Demand]
    S3 --> R3[Reading 3: Maximum Displacement]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Slope Regions of the DVA Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Very Short Period Region: Sa rises as 1 by T, slope minus 1] --> B[Constant Acceleration Plateau: Sa constant, slope 0]
    B --> C[Constant Velocity Region: Sv constant, Sa falls as 1 by T, slope minus 1]
    C --> D[Constant Displacement Region: Sd constant, Sa falls as 1 by T squared, slope minus 2]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `S1`, `R2`) ŌĆö no reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as node names.
> - No markdown formatting (bold, italic, tables) is embedded inside double-quoted labels.
> - All labels are uppercase alphanumeric text, ensuring clean rendering across Mermaid versions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "DVA spectrum" and explain why it is plotted on a four-way logarithmic paper.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **DVA spectrum** is the combined plot of three response spectra ŌĆö Spectral Displacement ($S_d$), Spectral Pseudo-Velocity ($S_v$), and Spectral Pseudo-Acceleration ($S_a$) ŌĆö superimposed on a single graph. It is called a four-way logarithmic paper because it has **four logarithmic axes**: one for the period $T$ along the x-direction, and three vertical axes offset geometrically, one each for $S_a$, $S_v$, and $S_d$. This format is used because:

1. Earthquake response data spans several orders of magnitude in both period and amplitude, which a linear axis cannot represent clearly.
2. The power-law relation $S_a = \omega^2 S_d = \omega S_v$ allows a single curve to be read against three vertical scales.
3. It enables a designer to obtain **base shear, displacement demand, and velocity demand** simultaneously for a structure of known natural period.

*(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 1 for explaining the 4 axes, 1 for the practical utility)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the fundamental relationships between $S_a$, $S_v$, and $S_d$ for an SDOF system. Comment on the slopes these yield on a log-log plot.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The fundamental relationships are:

$$S_a = \omega S_v = \omega^2 S_d, \quad \text{where} \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

In terms of $T$:

$$S_v = \frac{T}{2\pi} S_a, \qquad S_d = \frac{T^2}{4\pi^2} S_a$$

On a log-log plot, these translate to straight lines with the following slopes:

- $S_d$ vs $T$ (constant $S_a$): **slope +2**
- $S_v$ vs $T$ (constant $S_a$): **slope +1**
- $S_a$ vs $T$ in plateau region: **slope 0**
- $S_a$ vs $T$ in zero-period region: **slope ŌłÆ1** (or ŌłÆ2 for very short period, depending on assumption)

*(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for stating relations, 1 for the period form, 1 for the slopes)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a **four-way logarithmic plot**. Label all three vertical scales and the horizontal period axis. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the procedure to construct a **DVA spectrum** from a given $S_a$ŌĆō$T$ spectrum. Clearly state the conversion formulas and the physical meaning of each ordinate. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Construction of the Four-Way Logarithmic Plot (7 marks):**

A four-way logarithmic plot, also called the **tripartite response spectrum chart**, is constructed as follows:

1. **Horizontal Axis:** The natural period $T$ (in seconds) is plotted on a logarithmic scale. The range typically covers $0.01$ s to $10$ s.

2. **First Vertical Axis (Left):** Spectral Pseudo-Acceleration $S_a$ is plotted on a logarithmic scale, with units of **g** (acceleration due to gravity) or m/s┬▓. This is the **primary** scale.

3. **Second Vertical Axis (Right, tilted at 45┬░):** Spectral Pseudo-Velocity $S_v$ is plotted on a logarithmic scale, in m/s. The axis is shifted such that the line $S_v = (T/2\pi)\,S_a$ lies along the same direction as the $S_a$ŌĆō$T$ line ŌĆö a slope of **+1**.

4. **Third Vertical Axis (Right, tilted at a different angle):** Spectral Displacement $S_d$ is plotted logarithmically, in mm or m. Its axis is shifted to align with the slope of **+2** corresponding to $S_d = (T/2\pi)^2 S_a$.

5. The three axes are constructed so that for **any** point on the spectrum curve, one can directly read off the corresponding $S_a$, $S_v$, and $S_d$ values.

**Key features of the sketch:**

- Logarithmic grid lines appear as a regular mesh, with major decades emphasised.
- The period axis is divided into the four regions: (i) acceleration-controlled, (ii) velocity-controlled, (iii) displacement-controlled, and (iv) zero-period acceleration limit.
- The diagonal placement of the $S_v$ and $S_d$ axes visually demonstrates the slope rules.

*(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for layout description, 2 for axis labelling and units, 2 for the slope justification, 1 for neat-sketch mention)*

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Construction of the DVA Spectrum (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Obtain the $S_a$ŌĆō$T$ spectrum:**
Either from a code (e.g., IS 1893:2016 design spectrum) or from time-history analysis of an SDOF for the design earthquake.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to $S_v$ŌĆō$T$ ordinates:**
For each value of $S_a$ at period $T$, compute:

$$S_v = \frac{T}{2\pi}\,S_a$$

Plot $S_v$ on the velocity scale.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to $S_d$ŌĆō$T$ ordinates:**
For each value of $S_a$, compute:

$$S_d = \frac{T^2}{4\pi^2}\,S_a$$

Plot $S_d$ on the displacement scale.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Superimpose the three spectra:**
On a four-way logarithmic paper, plot all three spectra. Because the axes are properly aligned, the three curves will essentially **coincide** into a single tripartite curve.

**Step 5 ŌĆö The DVA spectrum is complete:**
- **$S_a$** gives the design **base shear coefficient** ($V = m S_a$).
- **$S_v$** gives the **pseudo-velocity demand**, related to input energy.
- **$S_d$** gives the **maximum relative displacement** between mass and base, used for drift and P-Delta checks.

**Engineering utility:** A practising engineer can determine, with a single chart lookup, all three design quantities for a building of known period. This is why the DVA spectrum is the **cornerstone of response-spectrum-based seismic design**.

*(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for $S_a$ source, 2 for conversion to $S_v$, 2 for conversion to $S_d$, 1 for engineering interpretation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice ŌĆö KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **physical significance** of the four-way logarithmic plot in the design of multi-storey buildings. Discuss how $S_a$, $S_v$, and $S_d$ are used by the structural designer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A building has a natural period $T = 1.5$ s. The pseudo-spectral acceleration read from the design spectrum is $S_a = 0.35\,g$. Compute the corresponding pseudo-spectral velocity and spectral displacement. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Physical Significance in Multi-Storey Design (7 marks):**

In multi-storey building design, the fundamental period $T$ of the structure determines how the building "responds" to ground motion. The four-way logarithmic plot provides, in a single visual, all the response quantities the designer needs:

- **$S_a$ ŌåÆ Base Shear:** The design base shear $V_B$ is calculated as $V_B = M \cdot S_a$, where $M$ is the seismic mass. This is used to design columns, shear walls, and foundations.

- **$S_v$ ŌåÆ Input Energy Demand:** Pseudo-velocity is related to the kinetic energy of the vibrating mass: $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m S_v^2$. It is used in energy-based design and in capacity-spectrum methods.

- **$S_d$ ŌåÆ Maximum Displacement / Drift:** The top-storey drift $\Delta = S_d$ governs the P-Delta effect, damage to non-structural elements, and the need for seismic separation joints.

The four-way plot is therefore the **"designer's dashboard"**: one chart, three answers, all in consistent units and time-domain interpretation.

*(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for $S_a$ significance, 2 for $S_v$ significance, 2 for $S_d$ significance, 1 for the dashboard analogy)*

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T = 1.5$ s
- $S_a = 0.35\,g = 0.35 \times 9.81 = 3.4335\;\text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute pseudo-spectral velocity $S_v$:**

$$S_v = \frac{T}{2\pi}\,S_a = \frac{1.5}{2\pi} \times 3.4335$$

$$S_v = \frac{1.5}{6.2832} \times 3.4335 = 0.2387 \times 3.4335$$

$$\boxed{S_v \approx 0.820\;\text{m/s}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute spectral displacement $S_d$:**

$$S_d = \frac{T^2}{4\pi^2}\,S_a = \frac{(1.5)^2}{4\pi^2} \times 3.4335$$

$$S_d = \frac{2.25}{39.478} \times 3.4335 = 0.05700 \times 3.4335$$

$$\boxed{S_d \approx 0.1957\;\text{m} \approx 195.7\;\text{mm}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification using the $S_v$ŌĆō$S_d$ direct relation:**

$$S_d = \frac{T}{2\pi}\,S_v = \frac{1.5}{6.2832} \times 0.820 \approx 0.1957\;\text{m} \;\checkmark$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- Base shear demand: $V_B = m \times 3.4335$ N (for mass in kg).
- Top displacement: ~196 mm ŌĆö needs to be checked against the allowable inter-storey drift (typically 0.4 % of storey height per IS 1893).
- Pseudo-velocity: 0.82 m/s ŌĆö useful for energy-based or capacity-spectrum assessment.

*(7 marks ŌĆö 1 for unit conversion of $S_a$, 2 for $S_v$ calculation, 2 for $S_d$ calculation, 1 for verification, 1 for engineering interpretation)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $g$-to-m/s┬▓ conversion:** When $S_a$ is given in **g**, students must multiply by **9.81 m/s┬▓** before applying $S_v = (T/2\pi)S_a$. Failure to do so loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing slopes of $S_a$ŌĆō$T$ vs $S_d$ŌĆō$T$:** A very common error is stating "the slope of $S_a$ vs $T$ is +1" ŌĆö it is actually **ŌłÆ1** (in the velocity-controlled region) or **ŌłÆ2** (in the displacement-controlled region). Always state the assumption (what is held constant) when quoting a slope.
> 3. **Mixing up $S_v$ and pseudo-velocity $P\!S_v$:** In many references, the symbol $P\!S_v$ is used to distinguish the *pseudo* velocity (computed from $S_a$ assuming harmonic motion) from the *true* relative velocity response. For KTU exams, either is acceptable, but be **consistent** in notation.
> 4. **Skipping the units in the formula sheet:** Examiners reward clearly written units. Always append m/s, m/s┬▓, or mm next to numerical answers.
> 5. **Not drawing the sketch:** Even in conceptual questions, a **labelled diagram** of the four-way plot (with the three vertical axes offset diagonally) earns significant marks. A textual-only answer is rarely awarded full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the DVA Spectrum & Four-Way Log Plot:**

- **DVA spectrum** = combined $S_d$, $S_v$, and $S_a$ plot on a single graph.
- **Four-way log plot** = three logarithmic vertical axes (one for each spectral ordinate) + one logarithmic horizontal axis (period $T$).
- **Master formula triplet:**
  - $S_a = (2\pi/T)\,S_v = (2\pi/T)^2\,S_d$
  - $S_v = (T/2\pi)\,S_a$
  - $S_d = (T^2/4\pi^2)\,S_a$
- **Slope rules on log-log paper:**
  - $S_d$ vs $T$ (constant $S_a$) ŌåÆ slope **+2**
  - $S_v$ vs $T$ (constant $S_a$) ŌåÆ slope **+1**
  - $S_a$ vs $T$ (plateau) ŌåÆ slope **0**
  - $S_a$ vs $T$ (long period, $S_d$ constant) ŌåÆ slope **ŌłÆ2**
  - $S_a$ vs $T$ (intermediate, $S_v$ constant) ŌåÆ slope **ŌłÆ1**
- **Three regions of the spectrum:** acceleration-controlled (short $T$), velocity-controlled (intermediate $T$), displacement-controlled (long $T$).
- **Engineering outputs:**
  - $S_a$ ŌåÆ **base shear** ($V_B = m S_a$)
  - $S_v$ ŌåÆ **energy demand** ($E_k = \frac{1}{2} m S_v^2$)
  - $S_d$ ŌåÆ **displacement / drift** check
- **Codes that use tripartite spectra:** IS 1893:2016, IBC, Eurocode 8, UBC 1997, NZS 1170.
- **Conceptual (no numerical plotting) for KTU 2024 Module 2** ŌĆö focus on description, sketch, and physical interpretation.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers: g Ōåö m/s┬▓ conversion is mandatory.
- **Examiner's mnemonic:** *"4 axes, 3 curves, 1 design chart"* ŌĆö the DVA spectrum is the engineer's single-source design tool.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi Degree of Freedom Systems - 2 DOF Systems - Equation of Motion - Normal Modes of Vibrations and Natural Frequencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multi Degree of Freedom Systems - 2 DOF Systems - Equation of Motion - Normal Modes of Vibrations and Natural Frequencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multi Degree of Freedom (MDOF) Systems ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET865 Module 2):**
> A **Multi Degree of Freedom (MDOF) system** is a discrete dynamic system in which more than one independent coordinate (or displacement) is required to completely specify the configuration of the system at any instant of time. For structural earthquake engineering, the number of dynamic degrees of freedom typically equals the number of lumped masses, which in turn corresponds to the number of floors (assuming a rigid floor diaphragm idealization).

For a **2-DOF system**, two independent displacement coordinates $(x_1, x_2)$ are required. The general matrix form of the equation of motion for a **linear, 2-DOF, viscously damped system** subjected to dynamic excitation is:

$$
[M]\{\ddot{x}(t)\} + [C]\{\dot{x}(t)\} + [K]\{x(t)\} = \{F(t)\}
$$

where the matrices are of order $(2 \times 2)$.

### 1.2 Physical Analogies and Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Two-Storey Bookshelf"**
> Imagine a tall bookshelf with two heavy stacks of books on shelves 1 and 2. If you push the top shelf sideways, **both** shelves will swing ŌĆö but they will not swing together. The lower shelf drags behind because the support column is flexible. This is exactly a 2-DOF shear building: each floor (lumped mass) moves independently, coupled to the others only through the column stiffness. The two floors will oscillate in **two distinct patterns (modes)**, and any complex motion of the building is just a *combination* of these two simple patterns. This is the essence of **modal analysis**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters

- **Degrees of Freedom (N):** Number of independent displacement coordinates; for a 2-DOF system, $N = 2$.
- **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$):** The frequency (in **rad/s**) at which the system freely vibrates without external excitation. Units: $\text{rad/s}$ or **Hz** ($f = \omega / 2\pi$).
- **Natural Period ($T_n$):** Time taken for one complete cycle of free vibration; $T_n = 2\pi / \omega_n$. Unit: **seconds (s)**.
- **Mode Shape ($\{\phi_n\}$):** The *relative* pattern of displacement of the masses when the system vibrates in its $n$-th natural mode. Dimensionless, normalized to unity.
- **Modal Mass & Modal Stiffness:** Effective mass and stiffness participating in a given mode.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Idealized 2-DOF Shear Building Model

The classical textbook model (Clough & Penzien, Chopra) for a 2-storey building is:

- Floor masses lumped at each storey level: $m_1$ (ground floor) and $m_2$ (first floor).
- Inter-storey stiffnesses: $k_1$ (ground storey) and $k_2$ (first storey).
- Each floor has **one lateral DOF** (translation), giving a 2-DOF system.

> [!NOTE]
> The "shear building" assumption ignores axial deformation of columns and rotational inertia of floors. This is standard and acceptable for low-to-medium-rise buildings.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Equation of Motion ŌĆö Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 ŌĆö Free Body Diagram of Floor 1 (Lower Mass $m_1$):**
Forces acting on $m_1$ are the restoring force from $k_1$ (opposing $x_1$) and the shear from $k_2$ due to relative displacement $(x_1 - x_2)$. Newton's second law gives:

$$
m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Free Body Diagram of Floor 2 (Upper Mass $m_2$):**
Forces acting on $m_2$ are only the shear from $k_2$ due to relative displacement $(x_2 - x_1)$:

$$
m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + k_2 x_2 = 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Assembling into Matrix Form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

This is the **undamped free-vibration equation** $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (2-DOF Systems)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Mass matrix | $[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Always diagonal for lumped-mass models (kg) |
| 2 | Stiffness matrix | $[K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_1+k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Symmetric, positive definite (N/m) |
| 3 | EOM (free vibration) | $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$ | Linear 2nd-order ODE system |
| 4 | Assumed solution | $\{x(t)\} = \{\phi\}\sin(\omega t + \theta)$ | Harmonic motion at natural frequency |
| 5 | Eigenvalue problem | $\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right)\{\phi\} = \{0\}$ | Standard form |
| 6 | Characteristic (frequency) equation | $\det\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) = 0$ | Yields 2 values of $\omega^2$ |
| 7 | Expanded characteristic equation | $m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - \left[m_2(k_1+k_2) + m_1 k_2\right]\omega^2 + k_1 k_2 = 0$ | Quadratic in $\omega^2$ |
| 8 | Roots of $\omega^2$ | $\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2$ | Always real and positive |
| 9 | Mode shape ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{\phi_i}{\phi_j} = \dfrac{k_{\text{off-diag}}}{k_{jj} - \omega^2 m_j}$ | Set reference DOF = 1 |
| 10 | Orthogonality of modes | $\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_m\} = 0$ for $n \neq m$ | Crucial for modal superposition |
| 11 | Orthogonality of modes | $\{\phi_n\}^T [K] \{\phi_m\} = 0$ for $n \neq m$ | Always true for classical damping |
| 12 | Natural period | $T_n = 2\pi / \omega_n$ | Time period (seconds) |
| 13 | Rayleigh's quotient | $\omega^2 = \dfrac{\{\phi\}^T [K] \{\phi\}}{\{\phi\}^T [M] \{\phi\}}$ | Useful for approximate methods |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The 2-DOF formulation is the *workhorse* of introductory structural dynamics. Every tall building, bridge tower, or chimneystack can be reduced to a few-mode MDOF model for seismic design. The **fundamental mode** ($\omega_1$) governs the overall seismic demand, while **higher modes** ($\omega_2, \omega_3, \ldots$) capture local floor-by-floor shear distribution. Codes like **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016** explicitly use modal base shear in their design base shear formula:
> $$V_B = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \quad \text{where} \quad V_i = \frac{\phi_i^T M r}{\phi_i^T M \phi_i} \cdot S_a(\omega_i)$$

### 2.4 Physical Meaning of Modes

- **Mode 1 (Fundamental Mode):** Both floors move in the *same direction* (in-phase). The shape resembles a cantilever deflected to one side. The frequency $\omega_1$ is the **lowest** natural frequency and the **longest** period.
- **Mode 2 (Second/Upper Mode):** The floors move in *opposite directions* (out-of-phase). There exists a *node* (zero-displacement point) somewhere between the two floors. The frequency $\omega_2 > \omega_1$ and is typically 3ŌĆō5 times higher than $\omega_1$ for a typical 2-storey frame.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Characteristic Equation

Starting from the eigenvalue problem:

$$
\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right)\{\phi\} = \{0\}
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute the 2-DOF matrices:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}
- \omega^2
\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 - \omega^2 m_1 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 - \omega^2 m_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö For a non-trivial solution** $\{\phi\} \neq \{0\}$, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must vanish:

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 - \omega^2 m_1 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 - \omega^2 m_2 \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the determinant:**

$$
(k_1 + k_2 - \omega^2 m_1)(k_2 - \omega^2 m_2) - (-k_2)(-k_2) = 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the first product:**

$$
(k_1 + k_2)(k_2) - (k_1 + k_2)(\omega^2 m_2) - (k_2)(\omega^2 m_1) + (\omega^2 m_1)(\omega^2 m_2) - k_2^2 = 0
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify and group by powers of $\omega^2$:**

$$
m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - \left[ m_2 (k_1 + k_2) + m_1 k_2 \right] \omega^2 + k_1 k_2 = 0
$$

This is a **quadratic equation in $\omega^2$** of the form $A \lambda^2 + B \lambda + C = 0$, with $\lambda = \omega^2$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $\omega^2$ using the quadratic formula:**

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{2A}
$$

Substituting $A = m_1 m_2$, $B = -\left[ m_2(k_1+k_2) + m_1 k_2 \right]$, $C = k_1 k_2$:

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{\left[ m_2(k_1+k_2) + m_1 k_2 \right] \pm \sqrt{\left[ m_2(k_1+k_2) + m_1 k_2 \right]^2 - 4 m_1 m_2 k_1 k_2}}{2 m_1 m_2}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Define the two natural frequencies** (using the convention $\omega_1 < \omega_2$):

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{-B - \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{2A}, \quad \omega_2^2 = \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{2A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both roots are **real and positive** because $[K]$ is symmetric positive-definite and $[M]$ is symmetric positive-definite. This is a fundamental theorem of structural dynamics (the vibration theorem).

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Mode Shapes

For each natural frequency $\omega_n$, the corresponding mode shape $\{\phi_n\}$ is found by solving:

$$
\left( [K] - \omega_n^2 [M] \right) \{\phi_n\} = \{0\}
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Using the first row of the matrix equation** (for $i=1$):

$$
(k_1 + k_2 - \omega_n^2 m_1) \phi_{n,1} - k_2 \phi_{n,2} = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for the ratio** $\rho_n = \phi_{n,1} / \phi_{n,2}$:

$$
\rho_n = \frac{\phi_{n,1}}{\phi_{n,2}} = \frac{k_2}{k_1 + k_2 - \omega_n^2 m_1}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Normalize.** Since the mode shape is unique only up to a scalar multiple, we conventionally set $\phi_{n,2} = 1$ (top-floor displacement = 1), giving:

$$
\{\phi_n\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \rho_n \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Type Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Problem:** A 2-storey shear building has the following properties: $m_1 = m_2 = 1000 \text{ kg}$, $k_1 = k_2 = 4000 \text{ kN/m} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$. Determine the natural frequencies and mode shapes for undamped free vibration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the matrices:**

$$
[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg}, \quad
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \times 10^6 & -4 \times 10^6 \\ -4 \times 10^6 & 4 \times 10^6 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Characteristic equation:** With $m_1 = m_2 = m$ and $k_1 = k_2 = k$:

$$
m^2 \omega^4 - [m(2k) + mk]\omega^2 + k^2 = 0
$$
$$
m^2 \omega^4 - 3mk \omega^2 + k^2 = 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $m = 1000$, $k = 4 \times 10^6$:**

$$
(10^6) \omega^4 - (3 \cdot 1000 \cdot 4 \times 10^6)\omega^2 + (4 \times 10^6)^2 = 0
$$
$$
10^6 \omega^4 - 1.2 \times 10^{10} \omega^2 + 1.6 \times 10^{13} = 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve the quadratic in $\omega^2$:**

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{10} \pm \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^{10})^2 - 4(10^6)(1.6 \times 10^{13})}}{2 \times 10^6}
$$
$$
= \frac{1.2 \times 10^{10} \pm \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{20} - 6.4 \times 10^{19}}}{2 \times 10^6}
$$
$$
= \frac{1.2 \times 10^{10} \pm \sqrt{8.0 \times 10^{19}}}{2 \times 10^6}
$$
$$
= \frac{1.2 \times 10^{10} \pm 8.944 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Two roots:**

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{(12.0 - 8.944) \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^6} = 1.528 \times 10^3 \Rightarrow \omega_1 = 39.09 \text{ rad/s}
$$
$$
\omega_2^2 = \frac{(12.0 + 8.944) \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^6} = 1.0472 \times 10^4 \Rightarrow \omega_2 = 102.33 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Mode shape ratios** (using $\phi_{n,2} = 1$):

For mode 1:
$$
\rho_1 = \frac{k_2}{k_1 + k_2 - \omega_1^2 m_1} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6 - (1.528 \times 10^3)(1000)} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{6.472 \times 10^6} = 0.618
$$

For mode 2:
$$
\rho_2 = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6 - (1.0472 \times 10^4)(1000)} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{-2.472 \times 10^6} = -1.618
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final Results:**

$$
\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.618 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{(Both floors in-phase; fundamental mode)}
$$
$$
\{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -1.618 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{(Floors out-of-phase; second mode)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of orthogonality:** $\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{\phi_2\} = (0.618)(-1.618)(1000) + (1.0)(1.0)(1000) = -1000 + 1000 = 0$ Ō£ō

### 3.4 Proof of Orthogonality of Mode Shapes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a system with $[K]$ and $[M]$ both real symmetric positive-definite, the mode shapes corresponding to **distinct** natural frequencies are orthogonal with respect to both mass and stiffness matrices.

**Proof:** From the eigenvalue problems for modes $m$ and $n$:

$$
[K]\{\phi_m\} = \omega_m^2 [M]\{\phi_m\} \quad \text{...(i)}
$$
$$
[K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 [M]\{\phi_n\} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

Pre-multiply (i) by $\{\phi_n\}^T$ and (ii) by $\{\phi_m\}^T$:

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [K] \{\phi_m\} = \omega_m^2 \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_m\}
$$
$$
\{\phi_m\}^T [K] \{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 \{\phi_m\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}
$$

Transpose the second equation (using symmetry of $[K]$ and $[M]$):

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [K] \{\phi_m\} = \omega_n^2 \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_m\}
$$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$
(\omega_m^2 - \omega_n^2) \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_m\} = 0
$$

Since $\omega_m \neq \omega_n$ for distinct modes:

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_m\} = 0 \quad \text{(Mass orthogonality)}
$$

It immediately follows that:

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [K] \{\phi_m\} = 0 \quad \text{(Stiffness orthogonality)} \qquad \blacksquare
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Analysis of a 2-DOF System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define the 2-DOF system\n Identify masses m1, m2 and stiffnesses k1, k2] --> B[Step 2: Assemble the Mass Matrix M\n Diagonal with m1 and m2]
    A --> C[Step 3: Assemble the Stiffness Matrix K\n Tridiagonal with k1+k2 and k2]
    B --> D[Step 4: Form the Eigenvalue Problem\n K - omega^2 M times phi equals 0]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Step 5: Set the Determinant to Zero\n Obtain the characteristic equation]
    E --> F[Step 6: Solve the quadratic in omega^2\n Obtain omega1 squared and omega2 squared]
    F --> G[Step 7: Compute the natural periods\n T1 = 2 pi over omega1\n T2 = 2 pi over omega2]
    F --> H[Step 8: Back-substitute each omega n\n into the eigenvalue equation]
    H --> I[Step 9: Solve for the mode shape ratios\n rho1 and rho2]
    I --> J[Step 10: Normalize the mode shapes\n Set top floor displacement to 1]
    J --> K[Step 11: Verify orthogonality\n phi1 transpose M phi2 equals 0]
    K --> L[Output: omega1, omega2, phi1, phi2]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö 2-DOF Shear Building Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ground["Ground Reference - Fixed Base"]
        G[Base Point]
    end

    subgraph Floor1["Floor 1 - DOF x1"]
        M1[Lumped Mass m1]
    end

    subgraph Floor2["Floor 2 - DOF x2"]
        M2[Lumped Mass m2]
    end

    G -- "Stiffness k1" --> M1
    M1 -- "Stiffness k2" --> M2

    Fext1["External Force F1 t"] -.-> M1
    Fext2["External Force F2 t"] -.-> M2

    subgraph SystemMatrices["System Matrices 2x2"]
        MassM["Mass Matrix M\n m1 0\n 0 m2"]
        StiffK["Stiffness Matrix K\n k1 plus k2  minus k2\n minus k2  k2"]
    end

    M1 -.-> MassM
    M2 -.-> MassM
    M1 -.-> StiffK
    M2 -.-> StiffK

    subgraph EigenData["Modal Data Extracted"]
        Omega["Natural Frequencies\n omega1 and omega2"]
        Phi["Mode Shapes\n phi1 and phi2"]
    end

    MassM --> Omega
    StiffK --> Omega
    Omega --> Phi
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map ŌĆö Mode Shape Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MDOF[Multi Degree of Freedom System] --> N2["N equals 2 DOF system\n Two independent displacements"]
    N2 --> EOM["Equation of Motion\n M x double dot plus K x equals 0"]
    EOM --> EIG["Eigenvalue Problem\n K minus omega^2 M times phi equals 0"]
    EIG --> FREQ["Natural Frequencies\n omega1 less than omega2\n Fundamental and Second Mode"]
    EIG --> SHAPE["Mode Shapes\n phi1 in-phase\n phi2 out-of-phase with a node"]
    FREQ --> PERIOD["Natural Periods\n T1 greater than T2\n T equals 2 pi over omega"]
    SHAPE --> ORTHO["Orthogonality Property\n phi n transpose M phi m equals 0\n for n not equal to m"]
    ORTHO --> SUPER["Modal Superposition\n Any motion equals sum of mode contributions"]
    SUPER --> APPL["Engineering Application\n Seismic design base shear per IS 1893"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**State the orthogonality condition of mode shapes for a 2-DOF system. Why is this property significant in structural dynamics?**

**Model Answer:**
For a 2-DOF system with mass matrix $[M]$ and stiffness matrix $[K]$, the mode shapes $\{\phi_n\}$ corresponding to **distinct** natural frequencies $\omega_n$ satisfy:

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_m\} = 0, \quad \{\phi_n\}^T [K] \{\phi_m\} = 0 \quad \text{for } n \neq m
$$

**Significance:** (1) It **decouples** the coupled equations of motion into $N$ independent single-DOF equations in **modal coordinates**, enabling classical modal superposition. (2) It provides an efficient framework for dynamic analysis, reducing computational effort. (3) It forms the mathematical basis for **response spectrum analysis** as used in IS 1893 and other seismic codes. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]`
**Differentiate between "natural frequency" and "forced frequency" in the context of a 2-DOF system.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Natural Frequency | Forced Frequency |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Definition** | Frequency at which the system vibrates *freely* without external excitation | Frequency of the *applied* external excitation |
| **Origin** | An *intrinsic* property of the system, determined by mass and stiffness distribution | An *external* input, chosen by the loading function |
| **Number of values** | Exactly $N$ distinct values for an $N$-DOF system | Single value, equal to the forcing function's frequency |
| **Resonance** | Independent of forcing | When forced freq. $\approx$ natural freq., resonance occurs and response amplifies dramatically |
| **Symbol** | $\omega_n$ (rad/s) | $\Omega$ or $\omega$ (rad/s) |

A 2-DOF system has **two** natural frequencies but only **one** forced frequency at any given moment. *(3 marks)*

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice

---

**Q3.A.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a 2-storey shear building with floor masses $m_1 = 2m_2 = 2000 \text{ kg}$ and inter-storey stiffnesses $k_1 = k_2 = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$, derive the **equation of motion** in matrix form and write down the mass and stiffness matrices clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the **two natural frequencies** $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ (in rad/s) and the corresponding **mode shapes** $\{\phi_1\}$ and $\{\phi_2\}$ for the system in part (a). Normalize each mode shape with the top-floor displacement = 1.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Equation of Motion [7 Marks]**

Using Newton's second law on each floor and assembling the equations:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 6 \times 10^6 & -3 \times 10^6 \\ -3 \times 10^6 & 3 \times 10^6 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Here:
- $[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}$ kg
- $[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \times 10^6 & -3 \times 10^6 \\ -3 \times 10^6 & 3 \times 10^6 \end{bmatrix}$ N/m

with the off-diagonal terms arising from coupling between the two floors via the second-storey stiffness $k_2$. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Characteristic equation:** With $m_1 = 2m_2 = m$ (i.e., $m_1 = 2000$, $m_2 = 1000$, $k_1 = k_2 = k = 3 \times 10^6$):

$$
m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - [m_2(k_1+k_2) + m_1 k_2]\omega^2 + k_1 k_2 = 0
$$
$$
(2000)(1000)\omega^4 - [(1000)(6 \times 10^6) + (2000)(3 \times 10^6)]\omega^2 + (3 \times 10^6)^2 = 0
$$
$$
2 \times 10^6 \omega^4 - 1.2 \times 10^{10} \omega^2 + 9 \times 10^{12} = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Divide by $2 \times 10^6$:**

$$
\omega^4 - 6000 \omega^2 + 4.5 \times 10^6 = 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Quadratic formula in $\omega^2$:**

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{6000 \pm \sqrt{6000^2 - 4(1)(4.5 \times 10^6)}}{2} = \frac{6000 \pm \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^7 - 1.8 \times 10^7}}{2}
$$
$$
= \frac{6000 \pm \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^7}}{2} = \frac{6000 \pm 4242.64}{2}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Two roots:**

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{6000 - 4242.64}{2} = 878.68 \Rightarrow \boxed{\omega_1 = 29.64 \text{ rad/s}}
$$
$$
\omega_2^2 = \frac{6000 + 4242.64}{2} = 5121.32 \Rightarrow \boxed{\omega_2 = 71.56 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Mode shape ratios** (from the first row: $(k_1 + k_2 - \omega^2 m_1)\phi_1 - k_2 \phi_2 = 0$):

For mode 1:
$$
\rho_1 = \frac{\phi_{1,1}}{\phi_{1,2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{6 \times 10^6 - (878.68)(2000)} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{4.243 \times 10^6} = 0.707
$$

For mode 2:
$$
\rho_2 = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{6 \times 10^6 - (5121.32)(2000)} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{-4.243 \times 10^6} = -0.707
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Normalized mode shapes:**

$$
\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -0.707 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix} \qquad \text{*(7 marks)*}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Marks Losers:**
> 1. **Sign convention error in $[K]$:** Many students write $k_1 + k_2$ incorrectly. The diagonal entry $K_{ii}$ is the *sum of all stiffnesses connected to mass $i$*, and off-diagonal $K_{ij}$ is the *negative* of the stiffness directly connecting masses $i$ and $j$.
> 2. **Forgetting to write the determinant equation** before expanding ŌĆö KTU valuators check this step explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing $\omega^2$ with $\omega$:** The quadratic is in $\omega^2$, not $\omega$. Forgetting the square root loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not normalizing mode shapes:** An unnormalized mode shape is acceptable only if clearly stated, but normalization with top-floor = 1 is the KTU standard.
> 5. **Missing units in the final answer:** Always report $\omega$ in **rad/s** and $T$ in **seconds**.

---

**Q3.B.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **significance of normal modes of vibration** in the seismic design of multi-storey buildings. Discuss with the help of neat sketches how the fundamental and second mode shapes differ for a 2-DOF shear building.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a 2-DOF undamped system with $[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ (in consistent units), determine the **natural frequencies** and **mode shapes**. Verify the orthogonality of the mode shapes with respect to the mass matrix.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Significance of Normal Modes in Seismic Design [7 Marks]**

**Concept:** A multi-storey building is a continuous system with theoretically infinite DOFs. By idealizing it as a lumped-mass 2-DOF (or $N$-DOF) system, the complex motion during an earthquake can be **decomposed** into a *sum of independent normal modes* ŌĆö this is the **modal superposition principle**.

**Significance in seismic design:**

1. **Code-Based Response Spectrum Analysis (IS 1893:2016):** The design base shear is computed as a combination of modal shears, each evaluated at the *modal period* $T_n$ using the *response spectrum* $S_a(\omega_n)$. This avoids the need for a full time-history analysis.
2. **Identification of Resonance:** If any natural period $T_n$ lies within the dominant period range of the design earthquake (typically **0.1ŌĆō1.0 s** for firm soil sites), **resonance** amplifies the response. Codes enforce a minimum separation or require detailed analysis.
3. **Damage Localization:** Higher modes often govern **storey shears** in upper floors, while the fundamental mode controls **overturning moment** and base shear.
4. **Tuned Mass Dampers:** The first natural frequency dictates the design of supplementary damping devices.

**Sketches (to be drawn in exam):**

- **Mode 1:** Both floors displaced in the *same direction* (e.g., to the right). The deflected shape is monotonic ŌĆö like a cantilever bending. Displacement is maximum at the top.
- **Mode 2:** The two floors move in *opposite directions* (e.g., ground floor right, top floor left). The deflected shape has a *node* (zero displacement) between the floors.

*(7 marks)*

**(b) Natural Frequencies, Mode Shapes & Orthogonality Verification [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Characteristic equation:**

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 2\omega^2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 - \omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$
$$
(6 - 2\omega^2)(2 - \omega^2) - 4 = 0
$$
$$
12 - 6\omega^2 - 4\omega^2 + 2\omega^4 - 4 = 0
$$
$$
2\omega^4 - 10\omega^2 + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow \omega^4 - 5\omega^2 + 4 = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Factor:**

$$
(\omega^2 - 1)(\omega^2 - 4) = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_1^2 = 1, \; \omega_2^2 = 4
$$
$$
\boxed{\omega_1 = 1 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_2 = 2 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Mode shapes** (from row 1: $(6 - 2\omega^2)\phi_1 - 2\phi_2 = 0$):

For mode 1 ($\omega_1^2 = 1$): $(6-2)\phi_{1,1} = 2\phi_{1,2} \Rightarrow \phi_{1,1} / \phi_{1,2} = 2/4 = 0.5$.
For mode 2 ($\omega_2^2 = 4$): $(6-8)\phi_{2,1} = 2\phi_{2,2} \Rightarrow -2\phi_{2,1} = 2\phi_{2,2} \Rightarrow \phi_{2,1} / \phi_{2,2} = -1$.

**Normalized mode shapes** (with $\phi_{n,2} = 1$):

$$
\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -1.0 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify mass orthogonality:**

$$
\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} -1.0 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$
$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} -2.0 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix} = -1.0 + 1.0 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

Orthogonality is verified ŌĆö the mode shapes are independent with respect to the mass matrix. *(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q3.B):**
> 1. **Omitting the sketch in part (a):** KTU examiners typically award **2 marks** for a labeled sketch showing the two mode shapes; missing it costs you direct marks.
> 2. **Mixing up mode numbering:** By convention, $\omega_1$ is the **smallest** (fundamental) frequency. Some students invert this ŌĆö a serious mark-loss error.
> 3. **Not verifying orthogonality in part (b):** Simply stating "orthogonality holds" without the matrix calculation loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to write the units** of the answer ŌĆö KTU expects $\omega$ in **rad/s**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö 2-DOF Systems, Equation of Motion, Normal Modes, Natural Frequencies**

- Ō£ģ A **2-DOF system** has **two** natural frequencies ($\omega_1 < \omega_2$) and **two** corresponding mode shapes ($\{\phi_1\}, \{\phi_2\}$).
- Ō£ģ The **mass matrix** $[M]$ is **diagonal** for a lumped-mass system: $[M] = \text{diag}(m_1, m_2)$.
- Ō£ģ The **stiffness matrix** $[K]$ is **tridiagonal symmetric** with diagonal entries = sum of connected stiffnesses, off-diagonals = **negative** of connecting stiffness.
- Ō£ģ The **EOM** for undamped free vibration is $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$.
- Ō£ģ The **eigenvalue problem** is $([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\phi\} = \{0\}$, leading to the **characteristic equation** $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$.
- Ō£ģ For 2-DOF, the characteristic equation is a **quadratic in $\omega^2$** of the form $A\omega^4 + B\omega^2 + C = 0$, with $A = m_1 m_2$, $C = k_1 k_2$.
- Ō£ģ **Mode shape** is found by **back-substitution** and **normalization** (standard: top-floor displacement = 1).
- Ō£ģ **Mode 1 (fundamental):** both floors in-phase, lowest frequency, longest period.
- Ō£ģ **Mode 2:** floors out-of-phase, has a *node* between them, higher frequency.
- Ō£ģ **Orthogonality:** $\{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{\phi_m\} = 0$ and $\{\phi_n\}^T[K]\{\phi_m\} = 0$ for $n \neq m$.
- Ō£ģ **Orthogonality** is the *key* to **modal superposition** ŌĆö it decouples the equations of motion.
- Ō£ģ **Rayleigh's quotient** $\omega^2 = \dfrac{\{\phi\}^T[K]\{\phi\}}{\{\phi\}^T[M]\{\phi\}}$ can be used as a **check** on computed natural frequencies.
- Ō£ģ **Units:** Mass in **kg**, Stiffness in **N/m**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $T$ in **seconds (s)**, $f$ in **Hz**.
- Ō£ģ **Real-world use:** IS 1893 (Part 1):2016 mandates **modal analysis** for all buildings taller than **15 m** in India, making this topic directly relevant to KTU practice and professional licensure.
- Ō£ģ **Always** check that both $\omega^2$ values are **real and positive**; if they are negative or complex, there is a sign error in the characteristic equation.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes ŌĆö Topic: 2-DOF Systems, Equation of Motion, Normal Modes & Natural Frequencies*
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (PECET865) Outcome-Based Education framework.*
