topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MDOF Systems - Shear Building Idealization and Equation of Motion - Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes - Orthogonality of Normal Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Multi-Degree-of-Freedom (MDOF) System?

A **Multi-Degree-of-Freedom (MDOF) system** is a structural idealization in which the mass, stiffness, and damping characteristics of a building are represented by a finite number of discrete dynamic coordinates. For earthquake engineering purposes, a typical multi-storey building is represented as an MDOF system where each storey level possesses one lateral translational degree of freedom (neglecting torsional and vertical modes).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> An MDOF system requires as many independent coordinates as the number of displacement components necessary to define the configuration of the system at any instant of time. For a planar shear building with *N* storeys, the system has *N* dynamic degrees of freedom.

### 1.2 Shear Building Idealization

A **Shear Building** is a structural idealization commonly used to model multi-storey framed buildings subjected to lateral (earthquake) loads, in which the following assumptions are adopted:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Assumptions of Shear Building Model (KTU Board Favourite):**
> 1. **Mass Concentration (Lumped Mass):** The entire mass of the structure is lumped at the floor levels. The columns are assumed massless.
> 2. **Axially Rigid Columns:** Floor diaphragms are assumed to be infinitely rigid in their own plane, and columns are axially inextensible, so each floor translates horizontally as a rigid body.
> 3. **Flexural Flexibility:** The lateral flexibility is provided solely by the bending of columns; beams are infinitely stiff relative to columns.
> 4. **Negligible Axial Deformation in Columns:** The shortening/elongation of columns is ignored.
> 5. **No Torsional Coupling:** The center of mass coincides with the center of rigidity, so the building vibrates purely in translation.
> 6. **Proportional Damping:** The damping matrix is assumed to be classical (proportional/rayleigh damping).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Stack of Blocks and Springs"

**Intuition for a First-Time Learner:**

Imagine a tall tower made by stacking rectangular concrete blocks (representing floor masses *mŌéü, mŌéé, mŌéā*ŌĆ” *mŌéÖ*) one above the other, connected by four vertical coil springs at the corners (representing the bending flexibility of columns). The base is fixed to the ground. When you push the top block gently, it drags the middle block, which in turn drags the bottom block, but the base stays put.

- Each block can **only slide sideways** (translation), not rotate.
- The springs get **stretched/compressed** as the blocks displace.
- The **inertia** of each block resists acceleration.
- The **restoring force** comes from the deformed springs.

This is precisely the **shear building model** used in earthquake engineering. The relative displacement between consecutive floors represents the **inter-storey drift**, and the shear force in any column depends on the drift of the storey it belongs to.

### 1.4 Physical Quantities of Interest

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Floor Mass | $m_i$ | **kg** or **kN┬Ęs┬▓/m** |
| Storey Stiffness | $k_i$ | **kN/m** |
| Natural Frequency | $\omega_n$ | **rad/s** |
| Natural Period | $T_n$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Mode Shape | $\phi$ | dimensionless (normalized) |
| Damping Ratio | $\xi$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Watch:** $1 \text{ kN┬Ęs}^2/\text{m} = 1000 \text{ kg}$. Always check units in numerical problems ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct marks for unit inconsistency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lumped Mass Model ŌĆö Deriving Storey Stiffness

For a typical storey *i*, four columns of flexural rigidity *EI* and height *h* (in series arrangement) collectively resist the relative inter-storey drift $(u_i - u_{i-1})$. The **storey stiffness** is given by:

$$
k_i = \frac{12 (EI)_i}{h_i^3} \quad \text{(fixed far ends)}
$$

> For a more general case where the beam-column joints are flexible, the **effective stiffness** of the column is reduced using the **stiffness modification factor** depending on the far-end condition (fixed, hinged, or rotated).

## 2.2 Global System Matrices

Once the floor masses and storey stiffnesses are evaluated, the structure is described by three fundamental matrices:

### (a) Mass Matrix [M] ŌĆö Lumped, Diagonal

$$
[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & m_3 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & m_N \end{bmatrix}
$$

### (b) Stiffness Matrix [K] ŌĆö Symmetric, Tridiagonal (Coupled)

The stiffness matrix is **tridiagonal** because only adjacent floors are dynamically coupled. The diagonal terms equal the sum of stiffnesses of the storey above and below, while the off-diagonal terms equal the negative of the connecting storey stiffness.

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ -k_2 & k_2 + k_3 & -k_3 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & -k_3 & k_3 + k_4 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & -k_N \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & -k_N & k_N \end{bmatrix}
$$

### (c) Damping Matrix [C] ŌĆö Classical (Proportional)

$$
[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K] \quad \text{(Rayleigh Damping)}
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are scalars chosen to match modal damping ratios at two control frequencies.

## 2.3 The Equation of Motion (Matrix Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Equation ŌĆö Most Tested:**
> The general **forced vibration equation of motion** of an MDOF system is:
> $$[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [C]\{\dot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{P(t)\}$$
> For **free vibration** (and undamped modal analysis), this reduces to:
> $$[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{0\}$$

## 2.4 Eigenvalue Problem Formulation

Assuming a **harmonic solution** of the form $\{u(t)\} = \{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta)$, the substitution yields:

$$
\{\ddot{u}(t)\} = -\omega^2 \{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta)
$$

Substituting into the free-vibration equation:

$$
\bigl([K] - \omega^2 [M]\bigr)\,\{\phi\} = \{0\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **generalized eigenvalue problem** of order *N*. Non-trivial solutions exist **only** if the determinant of the coefficient matrix vanishes:
> $$\det\bigl([K] - \omega^2 [M]\bigr) = 0$$
> This is the **frequency equation (characteristic equation)**, whose *N* roots $\omega_1^2 < \omega_2^2 < \cdots < \omega_N^2$ give the **N natural (circular) frequencies** in rad/s.

## 2.5 Mode Shapes (Eigenvectors)

For each $\omega_n^2$, the corresponding vector $\{\phi_n\}$ (non-zero) is the **n-th natural mode shape**. Since the determinant is zero, the solution is obtained up to a multiplicative constant. The mode shapes are conventionally **normalized** so that the largest entry equals unity.

## 2.6 Properties of the Mode Shape Matrix

The complete set of *N* mode shapes can be assembled into a square matrix:

$$
[\Phi] = \bigl[\{\phi_1\}\ \ \{\phi_2\}\ \ \cdots\ \ \{\phi_N\}\bigr]
$$

> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Mode Shape Orthogonality Properties**

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass Orthogonality** | $\{\phi_m\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$ | Mode shapes are dynamically independent in inertia |
| **Stiffness Orthogonality** | $\{\phi_m\}^T [K] \{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$ | Mode shapes are dynamically independent in elastic force |
| **Damping Orthogonality** | $\{\phi_m\}^T [C] \{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$ | True only for classical/proportional damping |
| **Generalized Eigenvalue** | $\{\phi_m\}^T [K] \{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 \{\phi_m\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}$ | Couples both properties into one equation |
| **Mass-Normalization** | $\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\} = 1$ | Allows modal coordinates to have unit mass |
| **Unit Diagonal Form** | $[\Phi]^T [M] [\Phi] = [I]$ and $[\Phi]^T [K] [\Phi] = [\Omega^2]$ | Fully decouples equations in modal space |
| **Period-Frequency** | $T_n = 2\pi / \omega_n$ | Converts rad/s to seconds |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MDOF shear-building model is the **backbone of modern seismic design**. It is used in:

- **Response Spectrum Analysis (RSA)** ŌĆö used in IS 1893:2016 for design.
- **Time History Analysis (THA)** ŌĆö for performance-based design of tall buildings.
- **Pushover Analysis** ŌĆö static non-linear analysis using equivalent MDOF representation.
- **Code-Based Base Shear Distribution** ŌĆö lateral force distribution is directly proportional to mode shapes and mass.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equation of Motion for a 3-Storey Shear Building

Consider a **3-DOF shear building** with floor masses $m_1$, $m_2$, $m_3$ and storey stiffnesses $k_1$, $k_2$, $k_3$. Let the absolute lateral floor displacements relative to the ground be $u_1$, $u_2$, $u_3$.

**Step 1: Identify Forces Acting on Each Floor Mass**

Using **D'Alembert's principle**, dynamic equilibrium is written for each mass by summing the **inertia force**, **damping force**, and **elastic restoring force** (spring forces from adjacent storeys) and equating them to the external force.

**For Floor 1 (Ground floor):**
- Inertia: $m_1 \ddot{u}_1$
- Damping: $c_1 \dot{u}_1$ (relative to ground) plus $c_2(\dot{u}_1 - \dot{u}_2)$
- Spring: $k_1 u_1$ (ground to first floor) plus $k_2(u_1 - u_2)$
- External: $p_1(t)$

**For Floor 2:**
- Inertia: $m_2 \ddot{u}_2$
- Damping: $c_2(\dot{u}_2 - \dot{u}_1)$ plus $c_3(\dot{u}_2 - \dot{u}_3)$
- Spring: $k_2(u_2 - u_1)$ plus $k_3(u_2 - u_3)$
- External: $p_2(t)$

**For Floor 3:**
- Inertia: $m_3 \ddot{u}_3$
- Damping: $c_3(\dot{u}_3 - \dot{u}_2)$
- Spring: $k_3(u_3 - u_2)$
- External: $p_3(t)$

**Step 2: Rearrange Each Equation in Matrix Form**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 \ddot{u}_1 + (c_1 + c_2)\dot{u}_1 - c_2 \dot{u}_2 + (k_1 + k_2)u_1 - k_2 u_2 &= p_1(t) \\
m_2 \ddot{u}_2 - c_2 \dot{u}_1 + (c_2 + c_3)\dot{u}_2 - c_3 \dot{u}_3 - k_2 u_1 + (k_2 + k_3)u_2 - k_3 u_3 &= p_2(t) \\
m_3 \ddot{u}_3 - c_3 \dot{u}_2 + c_3 \dot{u}_3 - k_3 u_2 + k_3 u_3 &= p_3(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Write the Global Matrix Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
m_1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & m_2 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & m_3
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix}
\ddot{u}_1 \\
\ddot{u}_2 \\
\ddot{u}_3
\end{Bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
c_1+c_2 & -c_2 & 0 \\
-c_2 & c_2+c_3 & -c_3 \\
0 & -c_3 & c_3
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix}
\dot{u}_1 \\
\dot{u}_2 \\
\dot{u}_3
\end{Bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
k_1+k_2 & -k_2 & 0 \\
-k_2 & k_2+k_3 & -k_3 \\
0 & -k_3 & k_3
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix}
u_1 \\
u_2 \\
u_3
\end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix}
p_1(t) \\
p_2(t) \\
p_3(t)
\end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **coupled matrix equation of motion**. Each row represents the dynamic equilibrium of one floor.

## 3.2 Free Vibration Solution ŌĆö Deriving the Eigenvalue Problem

Setting $\{P(t)\} = \{0\}$ and considering **undamped free vibration** ($[C] = 0$):

$$
[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{0\}
$$

**Step 1: Assume a Harmonic Solution**

The system is linear, time-invariant, and homogeneous. Therefore, a simple harmonic motion is a valid trial solution:

$$
\{u(t)\} = \{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta)
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate Twice with Respect to Time**

$$
\{\dot{u}(t)\} = \omega \{\phi\} \cos(\omega t + \theta)
$$

$$
\{\ddot{u}(t)\} = -\omega^2 \{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Back into the Equation of Motion**

$$
-\omega^2 [M]\{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta) + [K]\{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta) = \{0\}
$$

Since $\sin(\omega t + \theta) \neq 0$ for general *t*:

$$
\bigl([K] - \omega^2 [M]\bigr) \{\phi\} = \{0\}
$$

> This is the **standard generalized eigenvalue problem** of order *N*. The scalar $\omega^2$ is the eigenvalue and $\{\phi\}$ is the corresponding eigenvector (mode shape).

**Step 4: Condition for Non-Trivial Solution**

For a non-trivial $\{\phi\} \neq \{0\}$, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must vanish:

$$
\det\bigl([K] - \omega^2 [M]\bigr) = 0
$$

Expanding this determinant yields a polynomial of degree *N* in $\omega^2$ ŌĆö the **frequency equation**. Its *N* positive real roots give the natural circular frequencies $\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_N$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Mode Shape Orthogonality (Mass and Stiffness)

This is one of the most **important derivations in KTU exams**. Let $\omega_m$ and $\omega_n$ be two distinct natural frequencies with corresponding mode shapes $\{\phi_m\}$ and $\{\phi_n\}$.

**Step 1: Write the Eigenvalue Equation for Mode *m***

$$
[K]\{\phi_m\} = \omega_m^2 [M]\{\phi_m\}
$$

Pre-multiply both sides by $\{\phi_n\}^T$:

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [K]\{\phi_m\} = \omega_m^2 \{\phi_n\}^T [M]\{\phi_m\}
$$

**Step 2: Write the Eigenvalue Equation for Mode *n***

$$
[K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 [M]\{\phi_n\}
$$

Pre-multiply both sides by $\{\phi_m\}^T$:

$$
\{\phi_m\}^T [K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 \{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\}
$$

**Step 3: Transpose Equation of Mode *m***

Since $[K]$ and $[M]$ are real symmetric matrices, $[K]^T = [K]$ and $[M]^T = [M]$. Transposing the equation of step 1:

$$
\{\phi_m\}^T [K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_m^2 \{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\}
$$

**Step 4: Subtract the Two Equations**

$$
\bigl(\omega_m^2 - \omega_n^2\bigr)\, \{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\} = 0
$$

**Step 5: Conclude the Orthogonality Condition**

Since the natural frequencies are distinct ($\omega_m \neq \omega_n$ for $m \neq n$), and the mass matrix is positive definite:

$$
\{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\} = 0 \quad \text{(Mass Orthogonality)}
$$

Substituting this back into the stiffness equation:

$$
\{\phi_m\}^T [K]\{\phi_n\} = 0 \quad \text{(Stiffness Orthogonality)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Orthogonality Matters (KTU Perspective):**
> The orthogonality property allows the **N coupled equations of motion** to be **decoupled** into *N* independent single-DOF equations through the **modal superposition technique**, making seismic analysis computationally tractable.

## 3.4 Normalization of Mode Shapes (Mass-Normalized Mode Shapes)

The natural mode shape $\{\phi_n\}$ obtained from the eigenvalue problem is only defined up to a multiplicative constant. To make it unique, the mode shape is **normalized** such that:

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\} = 1
$$

If the un-normalized mode shape is $\{\psi_n\}$, the normalized mode shape $\{\phi_n\}$ is given by:

$$
\{\phi_n\} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\{\psi_n\}^T [M]\{\psi_n\}}}\,\{\psi_n\}
$$

This ensures that the **modal mass equals unity** and the **modal stiffness equals $\omega_n^2$**:

$$
[\Phi]^T [M] [\Phi] = [I] \quad ; \quad [\Phi]^T [K] [\Phi] = [\Omega^2]
$$

where $[\Omega^2] = \text{diag}(\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \ldots, \omega_N^2)$.

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example ŌĆö 2-DOF Shear Building

Consider a 2-DOF shear building with $m_1 = m_2 = m$ and $k_1 = k_2 = k$.

**Step 1: Form the Mass and Stiffness Matrices**

$$
[M] = m \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad [K] = k \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Write the Frequency Equation**

$$
\det\bigl([K] - \omega^2 [M]\bigr) = 0
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det \begin{bmatrix} 2k - \omega^2 m & -k \\ -k & k - \omega^2 m \end{bmatrix} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Expand the Determinant**

$$
(2k - \omega^2 m)(k - \omega^2 m) - k^2 = 0
$$

$$
2k^2 - 2k \omega^2 m - k \omega^2 m + \omega^4 m^2 - k^2 = 0
$$

$$
\omega^4 m^2 - 3 k \omega^2 m + k^2 = 0
$$

**Step 4: Solve the Quadratic in $\omega^2$**

Let $\lambda = \omega^2 m / k$. Then the equation becomes $\lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 1 = 0$.

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{9 - 4}}{2} = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Natural Frequencies**

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{k}{m} \cdot \frac{3 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 0.382 \, \frac{k}{m}
$$

$$
\omega_2^2 = \frac{k}{m} \cdot \frac{3 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 2.618 \, \frac{k}{m}
$$

**Step 6: Find the First Mode Shape**

For mode 1, substitute $\omega_1^2$ into the first row of $([K] - \omega_1^2 [M])\{\phi_1\} = 0$:

$$
(2k - 0.382k) \phi_{1,1} - k \phi_{1,2} = 0 \Rightarrow 1.618 \phi_{1,1} = \phi_{1,2}
$$

Normalizing so that $\phi_{1,1} = 1$:

$$
\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1.618 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

> Note: $\phi_{1,2} > \phi_{1,1}$ ŌĆö the top floor moves more than the bottom (typical of first mode).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of a 3-Storey Shear Building Model (Mermaid Representation)

The following Mermaid block represents the **block-level architecture of the shear-building idealization** and the dynamic coupling between floors. Node IDs follow the alpha-rule (letters + digits only), and labels contain only clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GROUNDNODE["GROUND FIXED BASE"]
    STOREY1["STOREY 1  k1  m1"]
    STOREY2["STOREY 2  k2  m2"]
    STOREY3["STOREY 3  k3  m3"]
    SPRINGA["COLUMN SPRING 1"]
    SPRINGB["COLUMN SPRING 2"]
    SPRINGC["COLUMN SPRING 3"]
    DASHBOARD["DYNAMIC DASHBOARD  M x double dot plus C x dot plus K x equals P of t"]
    OUTPUTX["EIGEN SOLVER  omega and phi"]
    ORTHOCHK["ORTHOGONALITY CHECK  phiT M phi equals zero"]

    GROUNDNODE --> SPRINGA
    SPRINGA --> STOREY1
    STOREY1 --> SPRINGB
    SPRINGB --> STOREY2
    STOREY2 --> SPRINGC
    SPRINGC --> STOREY3

    STOREY1 --> DASHBOARD
    STOREY2 --> DASHBOARD
    STOREY3 --> DASHBOARD
    DASHBOARD --> OUTPUTX
    OUTPUTX --> ORTHOCHK
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The fixed ground is the boundary condition.
- Each storey is depicted as a **mass lump** connected to its adjacent storey through a **column spring** of stiffness $k_i$.
- The three floor masses $m_1, m_2, m_3$ feed their displacements, velocities, and accelerations into the **dynamic equilibrium equation**.
- The system matrix equation is then processed by the **eigen-solver** to yield natural frequencies and mode shapes, which are finally subjected to the **orthogonality check**.

## 4.2 Procedural Flow ŌĆö Eigenvalue Analysis Pipeline

The following block-level flowchart summarises the complete procedure for obtaining natural frequencies and mode shapes from an MDOF shear-building model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1 IDEALISE BUILDING AS LUMPED MASS SHEAR MODEL"]
    S2["STEP 2 COMPUTE STOREY STIFFNESS k i"]
    S3["STEP 3 ASSEMBLE GLOBAL M MATRIX AND K MATRIX"]
    S4["STEP 4 FORM EIGEN EQUATION det of K minus omega squared M equals zero"]
    S5["STEP 5 SOLVE CHARACTERISTIC POLYNOMIAL FOR omega squared"]
    S6["STEP 6 OBTAIN MODE SHAPES phi n"]
    S7["STEP 7 NORMALISE MODE SHAPES PHIT M PHI equals I"]
    S8["STEP 8 VERIFY ORTHOGONALITY PHIT M PHI equals zero"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Use in Exam:** In KTU valuation, the **order of these steps** is critical. A 2-mark deduction applies if a student derives mode shapes *before* solving the characteristic equation.

## 4.3 Stiffness Matrix ŌĆö Banded Structure Visualisation

The physical meaning of the **tridiagonal nature** of the stiffness matrix is best understood through the connection pattern:

```mermaid
graph TD
    MASS1["MASS m1"] -- k1 --> BASE["FIXED BASE"]
    MASS1 -- k2 --> MASS2["MASS m2"]
    MASS2 -- k3 --> MASS3["MASS m3"]

    K11["K 1 1 equals k1 plus k2"]
    K12["K 1 2 equals minus k2"]
    K22["K 2 2 equals k2 plus k3"]
    K23["K 2 3 equals minus k3"]
    K33["K 3 3 equals k3"]
```

- **Diagonal term $K_{ii}$** = Sum of stiffnesses of the storeys immediately above and below floor *i*.
- **Off-diagonal term $K_{ij}$** = $-k_j$ where $k_j$ is the stiffness of the storey *j* shared between floors *i* and *j*.
- All other entries are **zero** because the shear building has only nearest-neighbour coupling.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 2]

**Q: Define a shear building and list any four assumptions made in its idealization.**

**Model Answer:**

A **shear building** is a mathematical idealization of a multi-storey framed building in which the mass of the structure is lumped at the floor levels and the lateral flexibility is provided by the bending of columns alone, with floor diaphragms assumed rigid in their own plane.

> The four key assumptions are:
> 1. The total mass of the structure is **lumped at the floor levels**; the columns are considered massless.
> 2. The **floor diaphragms are rigid** in their own plane, ensuring all columns at a given floor undergo the same horizontal displacement.
> 3. The **axial deformation of columns is negligible**; columns only bend in double curvature.
> 4. The **beams are infinitely stiff** compared to the columns, so column joints do not rotate.
>
> *(A fifth commonly cited assumption is that torsional coupling is neglected, i.e., the centre of mass coincides with the centre of rigidity.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 2]

**Q: State and prove the orthogonality property of natural mode shapes with respect to the mass matrix.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For an undamped MDOF system with distinct natural frequencies $\omega_m \neq \omega_n$, the corresponding natural mode shapes $\{\phi_m\}$ and $\{\phi_n\}$ satisfy:

$$
\{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad m \neq n
$$

**Proof:** Starting with the eigenvalue equations for modes *m* and *n*:

$$
[K]\{\phi_m\} = \omega_m^2 [M]\{\phi_m\} \quad \text{and} \quad [K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 [M]\{\phi_n\}
$$

Pre-multiplying the first by $\{\phi_n\}^T$ and the second (transposed) by $\{\phi_m\}^T$, and using the symmetry of $[K]$ and $[M]$:

$$
\{\phi_n\}^T [K]\{\phi_m\} = \omega_m^2 \{\phi_n\}^T [M]\{\phi_m\}
$$

$$
\{\phi_m\}^T [K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 \{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
(\omega_m^2 - \omega_n^2)\, \{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\} = 0
$$

Since $\omega_m \neq \omega_n$, we obtain $\{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\} = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $\omega$** ŌĆö examiners specifically check whether the student writes $\omega$ in rad/s; an answer in Hz will be penalised unless the formula $f = \omega/2\pi$ is explicitly used.
> 2. **Not normalising mode shapes** ŌĆö the mode shape vector is defined only up to a scalar multiple; marks are lost if the student does not specify the normalisation convention.
> 3. **Confusing the sign convention in the stiffness matrix** ŌĆö the off-diagonal terms are **negative**, not positive. A sign error here propagates into all subsequent computations.
> 4. **Mixing up $[K] - \omega^2 [M]$ and $\omega^2 [M] - [K]$** ŌĆö both are mathematically valid, but the determinant expansion and sign handling differ.
> 5. **Writing the wrong order of the matrix** ŌĆö for an *N*-storey building, the order is *N*, not *N-1* or *N+1*.
> 6. **Skipping the orthogonality check** ŌĆö KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests orthogonality verification; missing it costs at least 2 marks.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Module 2] ŌĆö 14 Marks

A **two-storey shear building** has the following properties:

- Floor masses: $m_1 = m_2 = 1000$ kN┬Ęs┬▓/m (= 1000 ├Ś 10┬│ kg)
- Storey stiffnesses: $k_1 = 4000$ kN/m, $k_2 = 3000$ kN/m

**(a)** Develop the **mass matrix** [M] and the **stiffness matrix** [K] of the structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **natural frequencies** $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ (in rad/s) and the corresponding **mode shapes**. Verify the orthogonality of mode shapes with respect to the mass matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a): Formation of [M] and [K]**

For a 2-DOF shear building, the lumped mass matrix is diagonal:

$$
[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kN┬Ęs}^2/\text{m}
$$

The stiffness matrix is tridiagonal, with diagonal terms equal to the sum of adjacent storey stiffnesses and off-diagonal terms equal to the negative of the connecting storey stiffness.

**Diagonal elements:**
- $K_{11} = k_1 + k_2 = 4000 + 3000 = 7000$ kN/m
- $K_{22} = k_2 = 3000$ kN/m

**Off-diagonal element:**
- $K_{12} = K_{21} = -k_2 = -3000$ kN/m

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 7000 & -3000 \\ -3000 & 3000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kN/m}
$$

---

**Part (b): Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes**

The frequency equation is:

$$
\det\bigl([K] - \omega^2 [M]\bigr) = 0
$$

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 7000 - 1000\omega^2 & -3000 \\ -3000 & 3000 - 1000\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$
(7000 - 1000\omega^2)(3000 - 1000\omega^2) - (-3000)^2 = 0
$$

$$
(7000 - 1000\omega^2)(3000 - 1000\omega^2) - 9 \times 10^6 = 0
$$

Expanding the first term:

$$
21 \times 10^6 - 7 \times 10^6 \omega^2 - 3 \times 10^6 \omega^2 + 10^6 \omega^4 - 9 \times 10^6 = 0
$$

$$
10^6 \omega^4 - 10^7 \omega^2 + 12 \times 10^6 = 0
$$

Dividing by $10^6$:

$$
\omega^4 - 10 \omega^2 + 12 = 0
$$

Solving the quadratic in $\omega^2$ using the quadratic formula:

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{10 \pm \sqrt{100 - 48}}{2} = \frac{10 \pm \sqrt{52}}{2} = \frac{10 \pm 7.2111}{2}
$$

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{10 - 7.2111}{2} = 1.3945 \text{ (rad/s)}^2
$$

$$
\omega_2^2 = \frac{10 + 7.2111}{2} = 8.6055 \text{ (rad/s)}^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\omega_1 = 1.181 \text{ rad/s} \quad ; \quad \omega_2 = 2.934 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**First Mode Shape (fundamental mode):**

Substitute $\omega_1^2 = 1.3945$ into the first row of $([K] - \omega_1^2 [M])\{\phi_1\} = 0$:

$$
(7000 - 1000 \times 1.3945) \phi_{1,1} - 3000 \phi_{1,2} = 0
$$

$$
(7000 - 1394.5) \phi_{1,1} = 3000 \phi_{1,2}
$$

$$
5605.5 \phi_{1,1} = 3000 \phi_{1,2}
$$

$$
\phi_{1,2} = 1.8685\, \phi_{1,1}
$$

Normalising with $\phi_{1,1} = 1$:

$$
\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1.000 \\ 1.8685 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Second Mode Shape:**

Substitute $\omega_2^2 = 8.6055$ into the first row:

$$
(7000 - 1000 \times 8.6055) \phi_{2,1} - 3000 \phi_{2,2} = 0
$$

$$
(7000 - 8605.5) \phi_{2,1} = 3000 \phi_{2,2}
$$

$$
-1605.5 \phi_{2,1} = 3000 \phi_{2,2}
$$

$$
\phi_{2,2} = -0.5352\, \phi_{2,1}
$$

Normalising with $\phi_{2,1} = 1$:

$$
\{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1.000 \\ -0.5352 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Verification of Orthogonality with respect to Mass Matrix:**

$$
\{\phi_1\}^T [M]\{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 1.8685 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1.000 \\ -0.5352 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 1868.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1.000 \\ -0.5352 \end{Bmatrix} = 1000 - 1868.5 \times 0.5352 = 1000 - 1000.02 \approx 0
$$

> The small residual $\approx 0$ (due to rounding) confirms the **mass orthogonality** $\{\phi_1\}^T [M]\{\phi_2\} = 0$. Ō£ō

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 2] ŌĆö 14 Marks

A **three-storey shear building** has the following properties:

- Floor masses: $m_1 = 2000$ kN┬Ęs┬▓/m, $m_2 = 1500$ kN┬Ęs┬▓/m, $m_3 = 1000$ kN┬Ęs┬▓/m
- Storey stiffnesses: $k_1 = k_2 = k_3 = 5000$ kN/m

**(a)** Form the **mass matrix** [M] and the **stiffness matrix** [K], and develop the **equation of motion** for free undamped vibration in matrix form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Stodola iteration method** or the **characteristic polynomial approach**, determine the **fundamental natural frequency** $\omega_1$ of the structure. Also sketch the **fundamental mode shape**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a): Formation of [M] and [K]**

**Lumped Mass Matrix:**

$$
[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1500 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kN┬Ęs}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Stiffness Matrix:**

Diagonal elements equal the sum of the storey stiffnesses above and below each floor:
- $K_{11} = k_1 + k_2 = 5000 + 5000 = 10000$ kN/m
- $K_{22} = k_2 + k_3 = 5000 + 5000 = 10000$ kN/m
- $K_{33} = k_3 = 5000$ kN/m

Off-diagonal elements:
- $K_{12} = K_{21} = -k_2 = -5000$ kN/m
- $K_{23} = K_{32} = -k_3 = -5000$ kN/m

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 10000 & -5000 & 0 \\ -5000 & 10000 & -5000 \\ 0 & -5000 & 5000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Equation of Motion (Free Undamped Vibration):**

$$
[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{0\}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 2000 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1500 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{u}_1 \\ \ddot{u}_2 \\ \ddot{u}_3 \end{Bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} 10000 & -5000 & 0 \\ -5000 & 10000 & -5000 \\ 0 & -5000 & 5000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} =
\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b): Fundamental Natural Frequency using Stodola Iteration**

The **Stodola iteration (matrix iteration) method** is an approximate procedure for finding the fundamental (lowest) natural frequency and corresponding mode shape.

**Step 1: Compute the Dynamic Flexibility Matrix [D]**

The dynamic flexibility matrix is the inverse of the stiffness matrix: $[D] = [K]^{-1}$.

For the given tridiagonal [K], we compute the inverse numerically:

$$
[D] = [K]^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0002 & 0.0002 & 0.0002 \\ 0.0002 & 0.0004 & 0.0004 \\ 0.0002 & 0.0004 & 0.0006 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/kN}
$$

(Values in m/kN, computed by direct inversion.)

**Step 2: Initial Trial Vector**

Choose a unit vector for displacements:

$$
\{u\}_0 = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: First Iteration**

$$
\{y\}_1 = [D][M]\{u\}_0
$$

Compute $[M]\{u\}_0$ first:

$$
[M]\{u\}_0 = \begin{Bmatrix} 2000 \\ 1500 \\ 1000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Then:

$$
\{y\}_1 = [D][M]\{u\}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0002 & 0.0002 & 0.0002 \\ 0.0002 & 0.0004 & 0.0004 \\ 0.0002 & 0.0004 & 0.0006 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 2000 \\ 1500 \\ 1000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

$$
\{y\}_1 = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.2 \\ 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.4 \\ 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.6 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 1.4 \\ 1.6 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Normalise with respect to the largest entry (1.6):

$$
\{u\}_1 = \frac{1}{1.6}\begin{Bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 1.4 \\ 1.6 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.5625 \\ 0.8750 \\ 1.0000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Second Iteration**

$$
[M]\{u\}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1500 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 0.5625 \\ 0.8750 \\ 1.0000 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1125 \\ 1312.5 \\ 1000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

$$
\{y\}_2 = [D][M]\{u\}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0002 & 0.0002 & 0.0002 \\ 0.0002 & 0.0004 & 0.0004 \\ 0.0002 & 0.0004 & 0.0006 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1125 \\ 1312.5 \\ 1000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

$$
\{y\}_2 = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.225 + 0.2625 + 0.200 \\ 0.225 + 0.525 + 0.400 \\ 0.225 + 0.525 + 0.600 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.6875 \\ 1.1500 \\ 1.3500 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Normalise with respect to 1.35:

$$
\{u\}_2 = \frac{1}{1.35}\begin{Bmatrix} 0.6875 \\ 1.1500 \\ 1.3500 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.5093 \\ 0.8519 \\ 1.0000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Third Iteration (Convergence Check)**

Repeat the procedure:

$$
[M]\{u\}_2 = \begin{Bmatrix} 1018.5 \\ 1277.8 \\ 1000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

$$
\{y\}_3 = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.2037 + 0.2556 + 0.2000 \\ 0.2037 + 0.5111 + 0.4000 \\ 0.2037 + 0.5111 + 0.6000 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.6593 \\ 1.1148 \\ 1.3148 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Normalise with respect to 1.3148:

$$
\{u\}_3 = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.5015 \\ 0.8480 \\ 1.0000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The mode shape has **converged** (changes are less than 1%):

$$
\{\phi_1\} \approx \begin{Bmatrix} 0.502 \\ 0.848 \\ 1.000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Fundamental Natural Frequency**

The Rayleigh quotient gives:

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{\{u\}_3^T [K]\{u\}_3}{\{u\}_3^T [M]\{u\}_3}
$$

Compute the numerator and denominator:

**Numerator:**

$$
[K]\{u\}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 10000 & -5000 & 0 \\ -5000 & 10000 & -5000 \\ 0 & -5000 & 5000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 0.502 \\ 0.848 \\ 1.000 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 5020 - 4240 \\ -2510 + 8480 - 5000 \\ -4240 + 5000 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 780 \\ 970 \\ 760 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

$$
\{u\}_3^T [K]\{u\}_3 = 0.502(780) + 0.848(970) + 1.000(760) = 391.6 + 822.6 + 760 = 1974.2
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
[M]\{u\}_3 = \begin{Bmatrix} 2000 \times 0.502 \\ 1500 \times 0.848 \\ 1000 \times 1.000 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1004 \\ 1272 \\ 1000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

$$
\{u\}_3^T [M]\{u\}_3 = 0.502(1004) + 0.848(1272) + 1.000(1000) = 504.0 + 1078.7 + 1000 = 2582.7
$$

**Fundamental Natural Frequency:**

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{1974.2}{2582.7} = 0.7645 \text{ (rad/s)}^2
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 0.874 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Sketch of Fundamental Mode Shape:**

The fundamental mode shape is:

$$
\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.502 \\ 0.848 \\ 1.000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The plot is **monotonic and increasing** with elevation ŌĆö every floor moves in the **same direction** with the **roof displacement being the largest**. There are **no interior nodes** (a hallmark of the fundamental mode).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MDOF = N coordinates** for an N-storey shear building (one per floor).
- **Shear building assumptions** (must know for KTU short answers):
  - Lumped mass at floor levels
  - Massless columns
  - Rigid floor diaphragms
  - Axially rigid columns
  - Infinitely stiff beams relative to columns
  - No torsional coupling
- **Mass matrix** [M] is **diagonal** (lumped) ŌĆö elements are floor masses.
- **Stiffness matrix** [K] is **symmetric, tridiagonal** ŌĆö diagonal terms are sum of adjacent storey stiffnesses; off-diagonal terms are negative.
- **Equation of motion:** $[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [C]\{\dot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{P(t)\}$.
- **For free undamped vibration:** $[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{0\}$.
- **Eigenvalue equation:** $\det([K] - \omega^2 [M]) = 0$ ŌĆö yields **N natural frequencies** $\omega_1 < \omega_2 < \cdots < \omega_N$.
- **Mode shapes** $\{\phi_n\}$ are eigenvectors; they are defined only up to a multiplicative constant. Always **normalise** them.
- **Orthogonality properties** (the most tested result in KTU):
  - Mass orthogonality: $\{\phi_m\}^T [M]\{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$.
  - Stiffness orthogonality: $\{\phi_m\}^T [K]\{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$.
- **Mass-normalised mode shapes** satisfy $[\Phi]^T [M] [\Phi] = [I]$ and $[\Phi]^T [K] [\Phi] = \text{diag}(\omega_n^2)$.
- **Period-frequency relation:** $T_n = 2\pi / \omega_n$.
- **Mode shape features to remember:**
  - Fundamental mode (n = 1): all floor displacements in the **same direction**; roof displaces the most.
  - Higher modes have **nodes** (zero displacement points) between floors.
  - Number of internal nodes = (mode number ŌłÆ 1).
- **Units reminder:** $\omega$ in **rad/s**; $T$ in **seconds**; $f$ in **Hz**; $k$ in **kN/m**; $m$ in **kN┬Ęs┬▓/m** (or kg in SI).
- **Sign convention:** Off-diagonal entries of [K] are **negative**; diagonal entries are **positive**.
- **Damping matrix:** For proportional (classical) damping, use Rayleigh form $[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forced Vibration Analysis of MDOF Systems - Modal Expansion of Response - Mode Superposition Method (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forced Vibration Analysis of MDOF Systems - Modal Expansion of Response - Mode Superposition Method (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Forced Vibration Analysis of MDOF Systems** refers to the dynamic response evaluation of a Multi-Degree-of-Freedom (MDOF) structural system when subjected to time-dependent external excitation forces. In earthquake engineering, these "forcing functions" are typically the inertia forces arising from ground acceleration, or applied dynamic loads such as wind, machinery vibration, or blast loads.

The **Modal Expansion of Response** is a mathematical decomposition technique in which the total displacement response vector of a coupled MDOF system is expressed as a linear combination (superposition) of the system's natural mode shapes (eigenvectors), each multiplied by a scalar time-dependent **generalized (or modal) coordinate**. 

The **Mode Superposition Method (MSM)** is the formal solution procedure that leverages modal expansion to transform a set of *N* coupled second-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) of motion into *N* uncoupled (independent) single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) equations. Each independent equation is then solved using standard SDOF techniques (Duhamel's integral for arbitrary forcing), and the final physical response is reconstructed by recombining the modal contributions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The emphasis in this module is *conceptual understanding* of the superposition principle, the role of orthogonality in decoupling, the physical meaning of modal coordinates, and the justification for modal truncation. Full numerical implementation belongs to higher-level structural dynamics courses.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a tall **office building** swaying during an earthquake. The entire 10-story building is a complex, tangled system where every floor pulls and pushes on its neighbors ŌĆö the equations describing its motion are *coupled* (you cannot solve Floor 1 without knowing Floor 2, and so on).

Now, observe the building carefully. It actually prefers to sway in **specific, natural patterns** ŌĆö these are the **mode shapes**. 

- **Mode 1** (Fundamental Mode): The whole building sways in a smooth curve, like an inverted pendulum. Most displacement at the top.
- **Mode 2**: The building forms an "S" shape ŌĆö top half goes one way, bottom half the other.
- **Mode 3**: A more complex "double-S" wave shape, and so on.

**Modal Expansion** is the idea that *any* actual motion of the building during an earthquake can be recreated by **mixing these natural swaying patterns in the right proportions**. It is the same as saying: any complex musical chord is just a combination of pure musical notes played together.

**Mode Superposition** is the procedure of:
1. Figuring out how strongly each natural mode is "excited" by the earthquake.
2. Calculating the response of the building in *each mode independently* (as if it were a simple SDOF system).
3. Adding up all the individual mode responses to get the total motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** Because the earthquake usually excites only the **first few low-frequency modes** significantly. The higher modes contribute very little. So instead of solving a giant *N ├Ś N* coupled matrix problem, we only need to solve 3ŌĆō5 small SDOF problems ŌĆö a massive computational saving in real earthquake engineering software like SAP2000, ETABS, or OpenSees.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Number of Modes to Retain (m):** Typically $m \approx \sqrt{N}$ to $3\sqrt{N}$, where $N$ is the total DOF.
- **Modal Mass Participation Factor (MMPF):** A scalar between 0 and 1; KTU code (IS 1893:2016) requires **Ōēź 90%** cumulative mass participation in each principal direction.
- **Natural Time Period ($T_n$):** The time taken for the $n$-th mode to complete one full cycle of free vibration. Units: **seconds (s)**.
- **Circular Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$):** $\omega_n = 2\pi / T_n$, units: **radians per second (rad/s)**.
- **Critical Damping Ratio ($\xi_n$):** Typically **0.02 to 0.05** (2%ŌĆō5%) for reinforced concrete, and **0.01 to 0.02** (1%ŌĆō2%) for steel buildings.

---

## 1.4 Glossary of KTU 2024 Module 2 Keywords

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **MDOF** | Multi-Degree-of-Freedom system (N > 1). |
| **Modal Coordinate ($q_n(t)$)** | Scalar time function describing motion in the $n$-th mode. |
| **Mode Shape ($\{\phi_n\}$)** | A fixed spatial pattern of relative displacement; dimensionless. |
| **Orthogonality Condition** | $\{\phi_m\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$. |
| **Decoupling** | The process of removing off-diagonal terms via modal transformation. |
| **Truncation Error** | Error introduced by ignoring higher modes. |
| **Static Correction (Base Excitation)** | A technique to compensate for the omitted higher-mode mass. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Modal Analysis? The Logic Behind the Method

The standard matrix equation of motion for a linear, classically damped MDOF system subjected to an external force vector $\{P(t)\}$ is:

$$[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [C]\{\dot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{P(t)\}$$

The fundamental problem is that the matrices $[M]$, $[C]$, and $[K]$ are **coupled** ŌĆö meaning $[K]$ is not diagonal in physical coordinates. Solving *N* coupled ODEs simultaneously is computationally expensive and obscures the physical contribution of each vibration mode.

**The Logic of Mode Superposition (5 Steps):**

1. **Free Vibration First:** Solve the undamped eigenvalue problem $[K]\{\phi\} = \omega^2 [M]\{\phi\}$ to obtain $N$ natural frequencies $\omega_n$ and mode shapes $\{\phi_n\}$.
2. **Orthogonality Exists:** The mode shapes are orthogonal with respect to $[M]$ and $[K]$. This is a property of all conservative (mass-stiffness) systems.
3. **Expand the Displacement:** Express the unknown displacement vector as a linear combination of mode shapes: $\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$.
4. **Pre-Multiply and Decouple:** Substitute the expansion into the equation of motion, pre-multiply by $\{\phi_n\}^T$, and use orthogonality to eliminate all cross-modal terms. The result is *N* independent SDOF equations.
5. **Solve and Superpose:** Solve each SDOF equation (Duhamel's integral for arbitrary loading), then reconstruct: $\{u(t)\} = \sum \{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" in One Sentence:** Because orthogonal modal vectors diagonalize the system matrices, the *N* coupled equations become *N* simple SDOF equations we already know how to solve.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation: Modal Expansion Theorem

Any admissible displacement vector $\{u(t)\}$ in an N-DOF system can be expressed as a unique linear combination of the *N* linearly independent mode shapes $\{\phi_n\}$:

$$\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$$

In compact matrix form, this is:

$$\{u(t)\} = [\Phi]\{q(t)\}$$

where:
- $[\Phi] = \big[\{\phi_1\}\ \{\phi_2\}\ \dots\ \{\phi_N\}\big]$ is the **modal matrix** ($N \times N$).
- $\{q(t)\} = \{q_1(t), q_2(t), \dots, q_N(t)\}^T$ is the **modal coordinate vector**.

The transformation $\{u\} = [\Phi]\{q\}$ is a **change of basis**: from physical nodal coordinates to modal (generalized) coordinates. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of $q_n(t)$:** It is *not* a displacement at a single node. It is the *amplitude* of the $n$-th mode shape at time *t*. Think of it as the volume knob for the $n$-th musical note in our building analogy.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` inside all tables below to preserve Markdown syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$ | Modal expansion of displacement | m |
| 2 | $\{u(t)\} = [\Phi]\{q(t)\}$ | Compact modal matrix form | m |
| 3 | $\{\ddot{u}\} = [\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\}; \{\dot{u}\} = [\Phi]\{\dot{q}\}$ | Time derivatives of expansion | m/s, m/s┬▓ |
| 4 | $[\Phi]^T [M] [\Phi] = [m^*]$ | Diagonal modal mass matrix | kg |
| 5 | $[\Phi]^T [K] [\Phi] = [k^*]$ | Diagonal modal stiffness matrix | N/m |
| 6 | $[\Phi]^T [C] [\Phi] = [c^*]$ | Diagonal modal damping matrix (if Rayleigh) | N┬Ęs/m |
| 7 | $m_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}$ | Generalized (modal) mass for mode *n* | kg |
| 8 | $k_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T [K] \{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 m_n^*$ | Generalized (modal) stiffness | N/m |
| 9 | $\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{k_n^*}{m_n^*} = \dfrac{\{\phi_n\}^T [K] \{\phi_n\}}{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}}$ | Rayleigh's quotient for natural frequency | (rad/s)┬▓ |
| 10 | $M_n^{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\left( \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{r\} \right)^2}{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}}$ | Effective modal mass for base excitation | kg |
| 11 | $\Gamma_n = \dfrac{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{r\}}{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}}$ | Modal participation factor | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\{P_n^*(t)\} = \{\phi_n\}^T \{P(t)\}$ | Generalized (modal) force | N |
| 13 | $m_n^* \ddot{q}_n + c_n^* \dot{q}_n + k_n^* q_n = P_n^*(t)$ | **Uncoupled SDOF equation for mode *n*** | ŌĆö |
| 14 | $q_n(t) = \dfrac{1}{m_n^* \omega_{dn}} \int_0^t P_n^*(\tau) e^{-\xi_n \omega_n (t-\tau)} \sin \omega_{dn}(t-\tau) d\tau$ | Duhamel's integral solution | m |
| 15 | $\sum_{n=1}^{N} M_n^{\text{eff}} = \sum M_{ii}$ (total mass) | Sum of effective modal masses = total physical mass | kg |
| 16 | $\xi_n = \dfrac{c_n^*}{2 m_n^* \omega_n}$ | Critical damping ratio for mode *n* | dimensionless |

---

## 2.4 The Decoupled SDOF Equations: Detailed Structure

After applying the modal transformation to the original equation of motion and pre-multiplying by $[\Phi]^T$:

$$[\Phi]^T [M] [\Phi] \{\ddot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T [C] [\Phi] \{\dot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T [K] [\Phi] \{q\} = [\Phi]^T \{P(t)\}$$

Because of orthogonality, all three coefficient matrices become **diagonal**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1^* & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & m_2^* & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & m_N^* \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \ddot{q}_N \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} c_1^* & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & c_2^* & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & c_N^* \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{q}_N \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1^* & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & k_2^* & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & k_N^* \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \vdots \\ q_N \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} P_1^*(t) \\ P_2^*(t) \\ \vdots \\ P_N^*(t) \end{Bmatrix}$$

Since the matrices are diagonal, the *N*-dimensional vector equation collapses into *N* **independent scalar SDOF equations**:

$$m_n^* \ddot{q}_n(t) + c_n^* \dot{q}_n(t) + k_n^* q_n(t) = P_n^*(t), \quad n = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decoupling means:** The equation for $q_1$ contains *no* $q_2$ or $q_3$. Each mode evolves independently in time, governed solely by its own modal mass, damping, and stiffness, and driven by its own modal force. This is the central miracle of modal analysis.

---

## 2.5 The Complete Mode Superposition Procedure (Conceptual Algorithm)

1. **Formulate** $[M]$, $[K]$, and (optionally) $[C]$ from the structural idealization.
2. **Solve the Eigenvalue Problem:** $[K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 [M]\{\phi_n\}$ to obtain $\{\omega_n^2, \{\phi_n\}\}$ for $n = 1$ to $N$.
3. **Mass-Orthonormalize (optional but recommended):** Normalize mode shapes such that $\{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{\phi_n\} = 1$. This simplifies subsequent equations to $m_n^* = 1$.
4. **Compute Modal Properties:** $m_n^*, k_n^*, \omega_n, \xi_n$ for each retained mode.
5. **Compute Modal Forces:** $P_n^*(t) = \{\phi_n\}^T \{P(t)\}$ for each mode and each time instant.
6. **Solve Each SDOF Equation:** Use Duhamel's integral (numerically or analytically) to obtain $q_n(t)$.
7. **Reconstruct Physical Response:** $\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$.
8. **Compute Derived Quantities:** Member forces, base shear, storey drifts, etc., from $\{u(t)\}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** This algorithm is the heart of every modern earthquake engineering software. ETABS, SAP2000, MIDAS Gen, OpenSees ŌĆö all of them use mode superposition (or its variant, modal time-history analysis) as the default method for dynamic analysis. It is computationally vastly superior to direct integration of the coupled matrix equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Coupled MDOF to Uncoupled SDOF

**Starting Point (Coupled Equation of Motion):**

$$[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [C]\{\dot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{P(t)\} \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the Modal Expansion:**

Substitute $\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t) = [\Phi]\{q(t)\}$ into Equation (1):

$$[M][\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} + [C][\Phi]\{\dot{q}\} + [K][\Phi]\{q\} = \{P(t)\} \quad \cdots (2)$$

*Why this works:* The expansion theorem guarantees that *any* displacement history can be represented this way. The coefficients $q_n(t)$ are the only unknowns that vary with time ŌĆö the spatial patterns $\{\phi_n\}$ are fixed.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Pre-Multiply by $[\Phi]^T$:**

$$[\Phi]^T [M][\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T [C][\Phi]\{\dot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T [K][\Phi]\{q\} = [\Phi]^T \{P(t)\} \quad \cdots (3)$$

*Why pre-multiply?* Because the property of orthogonality (which we are about to invoke) is a *bilinear* operation. The inner product of one mode shape with the equation, taken with respect to the mass (or stiffness) matrix, eliminates all other modes. This is the **Galerkin projection** step.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Invoke the Orthogonality Conditions:**

For a classically damped system, the mode shapes satisfy:

$$\{\phi_m\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\} = 0 \quad \text{for } m \neq n \quad \cdots (4a)$$

$$\{\phi_m\}^T [K] \{\phi_n\} = 0 \quad \text{for } m \neq n \quad \cdots (4b)$$

$$\{\phi_m\}^T [C] \{\phi_n\} = 0 \quad \text{for } m \neq n \quad \cdots (4c)$$

*(Equation 4c holds if the damping matrix is proportional ŌĆö i.e., Rayleigh damping: $[C] = a_0[M] + a_1[K]$.)*

Therefore, the products $[\Phi]^T[M][\Phi]$, $[\Phi]^T[K][\Phi]$, and $[\Phi]^T[C][\Phi]$ reduce to **diagonal matrices**:

$$[\Phi]^T[M][\Phi] = [m^*] = \text{diag}(m_1^*, m_2^*, \dots, m_N^*) \quad \cdots (5)$$

$$[\Phi]^T[K][\Phi] = [k^*] = \text{diag}(k_1^*, k_2^*, \dots, k_N^*) \quad \cdots (6)$$

$$[\Phi]^T[C][\Phi] = [c^*] = \text{diag}(c_1^*, c_2^*, \dots, c_N^*) \quad \cdots (7)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the Decoupled Structure:**

Equation (3), with the diagonal matrices from Steps 3 substituted, becomes:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1^* & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & m_2^* & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & m_N^* \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \ddot{q}_N \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} c_1^* & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & c_2^* & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & c_N^* \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{q}_N \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1^* & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & k_2^* & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & k_N^* \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \vdots \\ q_N \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} P_1^*(t) \\ P_2^*(t) \\ \vdots \\ P_N^*(t) \end{Bmatrix} \quad \cdots (8)$$

Since the off-diagonal terms are *all zero*, each row of the matrix equation is **completely independent** of the others. Extracting the *n*-th row:

$$m_n^* \ddot{q}_n(t) + c_n^* \dot{q}_n(t) + k_n^* q_n(t) = P_n^*(t) \quad \cdots (9)$$

**This is the celebrated uncoupled SDOF equation for the n-th mode.**

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve Each SDOF Equation:**

The general solution is:

$$q_n(t) = \underbrace{q_n^{\text{hom}}(t)}_{\text{free vibration, from IC}} + \underbrace{q_n^{\text{part}}(t)}_{\text{forced vibration}}$$

For an initially at-rest system, the homogeneous part is zero, and the particular solution (for arbitrary forcing $P_n^*(t)$) is given by **Duhamel's integral**:

$$q_n(t) = \frac{1}{m_n^* \omega_{dn}} \int_0^t P_n^*(\tau) \, e^{-\xi_n \omega_n (t - \tau)} \, \sin\left[ \omega_{dn}(t - \tau) \right] d\tau \quad \cdots (10)$$

where $\omega_{dn} = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \xi_n^2}$ is the damped natural frequency.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Reconstruct the Physical Response:**

$$\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t) \quad \cdots (11)$$

This is the **mode superposition** ŌĆö adding the contributions of all modes (or a truncated subset) to obtain the total physical response. Each $\{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$ is a snapshot of the building swaying in its *n*-th natural pattern, scaled by the time-varying modal amplitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** Equation (11) is an *algebraic* sum, not a *temporal* sum. At any given instant *t*, the displacement of each floor equals the algebraic sum of all mode-shape contributions at that instant.

---

## 3.2 Why the Lower Modes Dominate the Earthquake Response

For seismic (base) excitation, the modal force is:

$$P_n^*(t) = \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{r\} \cdot \ddot{u}_g(t) = \Gamma_n m_n^* \ddot{u}_g(t)$$

where $\{r\}$ is the **influence vector** (all ones for horizontal ground motion) and $\Gamma_n$ is the **modal participation factor**:

$$\Gamma_n = \frac{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{r\}}{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}} = \frac{L_n}{m_n^*} \quad \cdots (12)$$

The corresponding SDOF equation becomes:

$$\ddot{q}_n + 2\xi_n \omega_n \dot{q}_n + \omega_n^2 q_n = -\Gamma_n \ddot{u}_g(t) \quad \cdots (13)$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- The participation factor $\Gamma_n$ tells us *how much* the earthquake "activates" mode *n*.
- For typical low- to mid-rise buildings, $\Gamma_1 \approx 1.3$ŌĆō$1.5$ (fundamental mode is strongly excited), while $\Gamma_n$ for $n \geq 3$ is usually small.
- The dynamic amplification factor of the SDOF system decreases as $\omega_n$ increases (above the forcing frequency band), so the higher-mode responses $q_n(t)$ are *small in magnitude* even if excited.
- **Conclusion:** Only the first few modes (typically 3 to 5 for regular buildings) need to be retained for engineering accuracy.

---

## 3.3 Truncation and the Concept of Modal Mass Participation

If only $m < N$ modes are retained, the approximate response is:

$$\{u(t)\} \approx \sum_{n=1}^{m} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t) \quad \cdots (14)$$

The **cumulative mass participation ratio** is:

$$\rho(m) = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{m} M_n^{\text{eff}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} M_{ii}} \quad \cdots (15)$$

where the effective modal mass is:

$$M_n^{\text{eff}} = \frac{\left( \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{r\} \right)^2}{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}} = \Gamma_n^2 m_n^* \quad \cdots (16)$$

**KTU / IS 1893:2016 Requirement:** The number of modes *m* must be chosen such that $\rho(m) \geq 0.90$ (90% mass participation) in each principal horizontal direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 90%?** It is a balance between accuracy and computational cost. The omitted 10% of mass contributes higher-frequency oscillations that are typically small in amplitude for ordinary buildings and rarely control design.

---

## 3.4 Static Correction (Conceptual)

When higher modes are truncated, the elastic **base shear** may be underestimated because the missing mass is "rigid" at high frequencies. The static correction adds back the inertial effect of the omitted mass assuming zero-period (rigid) response:

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{modes retained}} + \omega_{\text{ref}}^2 \cdot \{r\}^T [M] \{r\} \cdot d_{\text{roof}} \cdot (\text{omitted mass fraction}) \quad \cdots (17)$$

This is a **conceptual correction** and is rarely required in KTU Module 2 problems but may appear as a 3-mark conceptual question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

    I --> J[Step 9: Reconstruct Physical Response u of t = sum phi_n q_n of t]
    J --> K[Step 10: Apply Truncation Check 90 percent Mass Participation]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Mode Superposition Method as applied to the dynamic analysis of MDOF systems. List its two main advantages over the direct integration method.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Mode Superposition Method (MSM)** is a dynamic analysis technique in which the displacement response of an MDOF system is expressed as a linear combination of the system's natural mode shapes, and the governing coupled equations of motion are transformed into a set of independent SDOF equations that are solved individually and then superimposed to obtain the total response.

**Two Main Advantages:**
1. **Computational Efficiency:** A system with *N* DOFs is reduced to *N* (or fewer) independent SDOF problems, avoiding the need to integrate the full *N* ├Ś *N* matrix system at every time step.
2. **Physical Insight:** The contribution of each natural mode to the total response is explicitly visible, allowing engineers to understand *which modes dominate* the response and to verify mass participation requirements (Ōēź 90% per IS 1893:2016).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is meant by "decoupling" of equations in the context of modal analysis? Why is it mathematically possible?**

**Model Answer:**

**Decoupling** refers to the process by which the *N* coupled second-order differential equations of motion (in physical coordinates) are transformed via the modal expansion $\{u\} = [\Phi]\{q\}$ and pre-multiplication by $[\Phi]^T$ into *N* **independent** (uncoupled) SDOF equations ŌĆö one for each mode.

**Why it is mathematically possible:** Because the natural mode shapes of a conservative system are **mutually orthogonal** with respect to the mass and stiffness matrices (and, for proportional damping, with respect to the damping matrix). This orthogonality forces all the off-diagonal terms in the transformed system matrices $[\Phi]^T[M][\Phi]$, $[\Phi]^T[K][\Phi]$, and $[\Phi]^T[C][\Phi]$ to vanish, leaving purely diagonal matrices. The *n*-th diagonal equation then contains only the *n*-th modal coordinate and is completely independent of all others.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the uncoupled equation of motion for the *n*-th mode of a classically damped MDOF system, starting from the matrix equation of motion and the modal expansion. Clearly state the role of the orthogonality conditions in your derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a 2-DOF shear building, the mass and stiffness matrices are:

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix}, \quad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} 2k & -k \\ -k & k \end{bmatrix}$$

Given the normalized mode shapes (with respect to mass) as:

$$\{\phi_1\} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2m}} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ \sqrt{2} - 0 \end{Bmatrix} \approx \begin{Bmatrix} 0.653 \\ 0.923 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.923 \\ -0.653 \end{Bmatrix}$$

*(Note: numerical values are illustrative. Use the conceptual procedure for the actual eigenvalue solution.)* Compute the **modal participation factors** $\Gamma_1$ and $\Gamma_2$ for horizontal ground acceleration, the **effective modal masses** $M_1^{\text{eff}}$ and $M_2^{\text{eff}}$, and verify the mass participation sum rule.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Detailed Derivation:**

The matrix equation of motion for a classically damped MDOF system is:

$$[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [C]\{\dot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{P(t)\} \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 1:** Apply modal expansion $\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t) = [\Phi]\{q(t)\}$ and substitute into (1):

$$[M][\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} + [C][\Phi]\{\dot{q}\} + [K][\Phi]\{q\} = \{P(t)\} \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 2:** Pre-multiply both sides by $[\Phi]^T$:

$$[\Phi]^T[M][\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T[C][\Phi]\{\dot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T[K][\Phi]\{q\} = [\Phi]^T\{P(t)\} \quad \cdots (3)$$

**Step 3:** Apply orthogonality. The mode shapes satisfy:

- Mass orthogonality: $\{\phi_m\}^T[M]\{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$
- Stiffness orthogonality: $\{\phi_m\}^T[K]\{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$
- Damping orthogonality (Rayleigh damping): $\{\phi_m\}^T[C]\{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$

Therefore the products become **diagonal matrices**:

$$[\Phi]^T[M][\Phi] = [m^*] = \text{diag}(m_1^*, m_2^*, \dots, m_N^*) \quad \cdots (4a)$$

$$[\Phi]^T[K][\Phi] = [k^*] = \text{diag}(k_1^*, k_2^*, \dots, k_N^*) \quad \cdots (4b)$$

$$[\Phi]^T[C][\Phi] = [c^*] = \text{diag}(c_1^*, c_2^*, \dots, c_N^*) \quad \cdots (4c)$$

**Step 4:** The diagonal system (3) reduces to *N* independent scalar equations. Extracting the *n*-th row:

$$\boxed{m_n^* \ddot{q}_n(t) + c_n^* \dot{q}_n(t) + k_n^* q_n(t) = P_n^*(t)} \quad \cdots (5)$$

where $m_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{\phi_n\}$, $k_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T[K]\{\phi_n\} = \omega_n^2 m_n^*$, and $P_n^*(t) = \{\phi_n\}^T\{P(t)\}$ is the generalized modal force.

**Step 5 (optional extension):** For base excitation, $P_n^*(t) = -\{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{r\} \cdot \ddot{u}_g(t) = -\Gamma_n m_n^* \ddot{u}_g(t)$, yielding:

$$\ddot{q}_n + 2\xi_n\omega_n \dot{q}_n + \omega_n^2 q_n = -\Gamma_n \ddot{u}_g(t) \quad \cdots (6)$$

This is solved via Duhamel's integral and the physical response is recovered from $\{u(t)\} = \sum \{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation:**

For a 2-DOF system, the influence vector for horizontal ground motion is $\{r\} = \{1, 1\}^T$.

**Modal Participation Factor for Mode 1:**

$$\Gamma_1 = \frac{\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{r\}}{\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{\phi_1\}}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{r\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.653 \\ 0.923 \end{Bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = m(0.653 + 0.923) = 1.576\, m$$

Denominator: $\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{\phi_1\} = m(0.653^2 + 0.923^2) = m(0.426 + 0.852) = 1.278\, m$

Therefore:

$$\Gamma_1 = \frac{1.576\, m}{1.278\, m} = 1.233$$

**Modal Participation Factor for Mode 2:**

Numerator: $\{\phi_2\}^T [M] \{r\} = m(0.923 - 0.653) = 0.270\, m$

Denominator: $\{\phi_2\}^T [M] \{\phi_2\} = m(0.923^2 + 0.653^2) = m(0.852 + 0.426) = 1.278\, m$

Therefore:

$$\Gamma_2 = \frac{0.270\, m}{1.278\, m} = 0.211$$

**Effective Modal Mass:**

$$M_1^{\text{eff}} = \Gamma_1^2 m_1^* = (1.233)^2 \times 1.278\, m = 1.943\, m$$

$$M_2^{\text{eff}} = \Gamma_2^2 m_2^* = (0.211)^2 \times 1.278\, m = 0.057\, m$$

**Mass Participation Sum Check:**

$$M_1^{\text{eff}} + M_2^{\text{eff}} = 1.943\, m + 0.057\, m = 2.000\, m = M_{\text{total}} \checkmark$$

**Cumulative Mass Participation:**
- Mode 1 alone: $1.943 / 2.000 = 97.15\%$ Ōēź 90% Ō£ō
- Mode 1 + Mode 2: $100\%$

> **Conclusion:** For this 2-DOF system, retaining **only the first mode** is sufficient per IS 1893:2016, since the fundamental mode alone captures over 97% of the total participating mass.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat mathematical expressions the concept of **modal expansion of response** in an MDOF system. Using the orthogonality of mode shapes, show how the original coupled matrix equation reduces to *N* independent SDOF equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the physical significance of the **modal participation factor** $\Gamma_n$ and the **effective modal mass** $M_n^{\text{eff}}$. State the IS 1893:2016 criterion for the minimum number of modes to be considered in seismic analysis, and explain what is meant by **modal truncation error** and how it is minimised.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Concept and Derivation:**

The **Modal Expansion Theorem** states that any displacement response $\{u(t)\}$ of an N-DOF system can be expressed as:

$$\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} \, q_n(t) = [\Phi]\{q(t)\}$$

where:
- $\{\phi_n\}$ ŌĆö the $n$-th natural mode shape (spatial, time-invariant).
- $q_n(t)$ ŌĆö the *n*-th modal (generalized) coordinate (scalar, time-dependent).

The $\{\phi_n\}$ form a **complete basis** for the N-dimensional displacement space, and the $q_n$ are the corresponding coefficients in this basis ŌĆö analogous to Fourier coefficients in a series expansion.

**Reduction to Uncoupled SDOF Equations:**

Starting from $[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [C]\{\dot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = \{P(t)\}$, substituting the expansion, and pre-multiplying by $\{\phi_n\}^T$:

$$\{\phi_n\}^T[M][\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} + \{\phi_n\}^T[C][\Phi]\{\dot{q}\} + \{\phi_n\}^T[K][\Phi]\{q\} = \{\phi_n\}^T\{P(t)\}$$

Using the orthogonality conditions:

$$m_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{\phi_n\}; \quad c_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T[C]\{\phi_n\}; \quad k_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T[K]\{\phi_n\}$$

and noting that all cross-terms ($m \neq n$) vanish, the matrix-vector product collapses to a single scalar equation:

$$\boxed{m_n^* \ddot{q}_n + c_n^* \dot{q}_n + k_n^* q_n = P_n^*(t)} \quad \text{for each } n = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

The *N* such equations are **completely independent** ŌĆö each one is a standard SDOF problem solvable by Duhamel's integral.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Physical Significance and Code Provisions:**

**Modal Participation Factor $\Gamma_n$:**

$$\Gamma_n = \frac{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{r\}}{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** $\Gamma_n$ measures the *degree to which the $n$-th mode is excited by the ground motion*. It is essentially a "weight" telling us how strongly the earthquake's translational input couples into each natural swaying pattern. For the fundamental mode of typical buildings, $\Gamma_1$ is usually between 1.2 and 1.6 (always greater than 1 for a cantilever-type structure).

**Effective Modal Mass $M_n^{\text{eff}}$:**

$$M_n^{\text{eff}} = \Gamma_n^2 \, m_n^* = \frac{\left( \{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{r\} \right)^2}{\{\phi_n\}^T [M] \{\phi_n\}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** It represents the *portion of the total building mass that effectively participates in the n-th mode during base excitation*. Unlike modal mass $m_n^*$ (which depends on the normalization of $\{\phi_n\}$), $M_n^{\text{eff}}$ is a unique, scale-invariant property of the mode shape.

**IS 1893:2016 Criterion:**

Clause 7.8.4.2 of IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016 requires that *"the number of modes to be used in the analysis shall be such that the sum total of the modal masses considered is at least 90% of the total seismic mass, and missing mass correction shall be included for modes not considered."*

In other words: $\sum_{n=1}^{m} M_n^{\text{eff}} \geq 0.90 \times M_{\text{total}}$.

**Modal Truncation Error:**

When only $m < N$ modes are retained, the reconstructed response is approximate:

$$\{u_{\text{approx}}(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{m} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t) \neq \{u_{\text{exact}}(t)\}$$

The **truncation error** is the omitted part:

$$\{e(t)\} = \sum_{n=m+1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t)$$

**How it is minimised:**
1. Retain enough modes to satisfy the **90% mass participation** criterion.
2. Apply the **Missing Mass (Static) Correction** ŌĆö treat the omitted mass as undergoing zero-period (rigid) acceleration equal to the peak ground acceleration.
3. Use **CQC (Complete Quadratic Combination)** or **SRSS (Square Root of Sum of Squares)** for combining peak modal responses, which statistically accounts for closely spaced modes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Modal Mass Term in the Reconstruction:** When asked to find floor displacements, students often write only $\{u\} = \sum \{\phi_n\} q_n$ *without normalizing properly*. Always check: does $\{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{\phi_n\}$ appear in your expressions?
>
> 2. **Confusing Modal Mass with Effective Modal Mass:** $m_n^*$ depends on the arbitrary scaling of $\{\phi_n\}$, but $M_n^{\text{eff}} = \Gamma_n^2 m_n^*$ is unique. The mass participation sum rule applies to $M_n^{\text{eff}}$, not $m_n^*$.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up Forced vs. Free Vibration Solution:** Duhamel's integral is for the **forced** part; the homogeneous (free vibration) part comes from initial conditions. If the problem says "initially at rest," the homogeneous part is zero ŌĆö students frequently forget this.
>
> 4. **Using SRSS for Closely Spaced Modes:** If $T_i / T_j > 0.9$ (closely spaced), **SRSS is invalid**; KTU (per IS 1893) recommends the **CQC rule** with a damping-dependent correlation coefficient. Writing SRSS for closely spaced modes will lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.
>
> 5. **Neglecting Damping Orthogonality:** Rayleigh damping is required for the damping matrix to diagonalize via the modal transform. Non-proportional damping breaks orthogonality and the simple MSM no longer applies ŌĆö students must then use complex mode shapes or direct integration.
>
> 6. **Sign Error in Base Excitation Equation:** The forcing term for ground acceleration is $-\Gamma_n \ddot{u}_g(t)$ (negative sign), not $+\Gamma_n \ddot{u}_g(t)$. This is a 1-mark killer.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the Influence Vector:** For non-uniform excitation, $\{r\}$ is *not* always $\{1, 1, \dots, 1\}^T$. Multi-support excitation, torsion, or vertical motion all have different $\{r\}$ forms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts (Must-Know)
- [x] Modal expansion: $\{u(t)\} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \{\phi_n\} q_n(t) = [\Phi]\{q(t)\}$
- [x] Modal coordinate $q_n(t)$ is a *scalar* time function ŌĆö *not* a physical displacement
- [x] Mode shape $\{\phi_n\}$ is a *vector* of relative displacements ŌĆö *not* absolute
- [x] MSM transforms *N* coupled equations into *N* independent SDOF equations

### Key Equations (Must-Memorize)
- [x] **Orthogonality:** $\{\phi_m\}^T[M]\{\phi_n\} = 0$ for $m \neq n$
- [x] **Uncoupled SDOF eqn:** $m_n^* \ddot{q}_n + c_n^* \dot{q}_n + k_n^* q_n = P_n^*(t)$
- [x] **Modal properties:** $k_n^* = \omega_n^2 m_n^*$ and $\xi_n = c_n^* / (2 m_n^* \omega_n)$
- [x] **Participation factor:** $\Gamma_n = L_n / m_n^* = \{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{r\} / \{\phi_n\}^T[M]\{\phi_n\}$
- [x] **Effective mass:** $M_n^{\text{eff}} = \Gamma_n^2 m_n^*$
- [x] **Sum rule:** $\sum_{n=1}^{N} M_n^{\text{eff}} = M_{\text{total}}$
- [x] **Duhamel's integral:** the solution for the forced SDOF response

### Code Provisions (KTU/IS 1893 Specific)
- [x] **Minimum 90% cumulative mass participation** in each principal direction
- [x] Typically **3 modes for regular** and **5+ modes for irregular** buildings
- [x] **CQC** rule for closely spaced modes ($T_i/T_j > 0.9$); **SRSS** otherwise
- [x] **Missing mass correction** is required for omitted modes

### Physical Intuitions (Must-Understand)
- [x] Lower modes ŌåÆ larger displacements, longer periods, greater mass participation
- [x] Higher modes ŌåÆ smaller displacements, shorter periods, smaller mass participation
- [x] An earthquake (low-frequency input) excites the first few modes most strongly
- [x] Rayleigh damping ($[C] = a_0[M] + a_1[K]$) preserves orthogonality; non-proportional damping does not

### Common Confusions to Avoid
- [x] Modal mass vs. effective modal mass (the latter is scale-invariant)
- [x] Free vs. forced vibration (Duhamel handles the forced part only)
- [x] Influence vector $\{r\}$ vs. mode shape $\{\phi_n\}$
- [x] Natural frequency $\omega_n$ vs. damped natural frequency $\omega_{dn} = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi_n^2}$

### One-Sentence Summary for Exam
> *Mode Superposition is the transformation of a coupled MDOF system into independent SDOF systems by expressing the displacement as a linear combination of natural mode shapes and exploiting their orthogonality with respect to the mass and stiffness matrices.*

---

## 5.5 Cross-References to Other KTU 2024 Modules

| Module | Connection to Mode Superposition |
|--------|----------------------------------|
| **Module 1** | Single-DOF solution techniques (Duhamel's integral) are directly used in each uncoupled modal equation. |
| **Module 3** | Response spectrum method is *built upon* mode superposition ŌĆö it uses pre-computed SDOF spectral responses for each mode. |
| **Module 4** | Lateral load distribution (Equivalent Static Method) approximates the fundamental mode contribution. |
| **Module 5** | Pushover analysis and capacity spectrum method use a *single-mode* (fundamental) approximation of MSM. |

---

> **END OF KTU 2024 SCHEME NOTES ŌĆö Module 2, Topic: Forced Vibration Analysis of MDOF Systems ŌĆö Modal Expansion & Mode Superposition Method (Concept Only)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plate Tectonics and Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plate Tectonics and Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plate Tectonics ŌĆö Formal Definition

**Plate Tectonics** is the unifying geophysical theory that describes the large-scale movement of the Earth's **lithosphere** (the rigid outer shell composed of the crust and the uppermost solid mantle) which is broken into a series of rigid segments called **tectonic plates**. These plates float and glide over the semi-fluid **asthenosphere** beneath them, and their relative motion is the primary cause of most earthquakes, volcanic activity, and mountain-building processes (orogeny).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The **Theory of Plate Tectonics** is the foundational cause of earthquakes. For a KTU Board examination, students MUST be able to connect plate movement to the release of **seismic energy** ($E_s$) at faults.

The seven (or eight, depending on classification) major tectonic plates are:
- **Pacific Plate** (largest, most seismically active)
- **North American Plate**
- **South American Plate**
- **Eurasian Plate**
- **African Plate**
- **Indo-Australian Plate** (Indian + Australian)
- **Antarctic Plate**
- **Nazca Plate** (minor but highly active)

## 1.2 Faults ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Fault** is a planar fracture or discontinuity in a volume of rock across which there has been measurable displacement due to rock-mass movement. The **Fault Plane** is the surface of rupture, while the **Hanging Wall** is the rock block lying *above* the fault plane and the **Foot Wall** is the rock block lying *below* the fault plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term ŌĆö Stick-Slip Mechanism:** Earthquakes occur when two adjacent rock masses lock together (the "stick" phase) accumulating elastic strain energy. When the accumulated shear stress exceeds the frictional strength of the fault plane, the rocks suddenly slip (the "slip" phase), releasing energy as **seismic waves**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Cracked Egg Shell on Boiling Custard"

Imagine a hard-boiled egg where the shell is gently cracked into 6ŌĆō8 large pieces. The egg white beneath is still soft. Now imagine that the soft white is slowly churning. The cracked shell pieces will slowly drift, collide, and override one another.

- The **egg shell pieces** = the **tectonic plates**.
- The **soft egg white** = the **asthenosphere** (semi-molten mantle).
- The **collisions and drag** = **plate boundaries** and **faults**.
- When a shell piece suddenly slips past another with a *crack*, that snap and vibration is the **earthquake**.

This analogy is particularly relevant for **Kerala's seismic context**: the **Indian Plate** is being pushed northward into the **Eurasian Plate** at roughly **40ŌĆō50 mm/year**, creating the **Himalayan seismic belt** and major fault systems (e.g., the **Main Central Thrust**).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Average crustal thickness: **30ŌĆō50 km** (continental), **5ŌĆō10 km** (oceanic).
- Typical plate velocity: **1ŌĆō10 cm/year** (the Indian Plate moves at ~5 cm/year).
- Seismic wave velocity in crust: **P-wave Ōēł 6 km/s**, **S-wave Ōēł 3.5 km/s**.
- Gutenberg-Richter recurrence interval scaling constant **b Ōēł 1.0**.
