# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary Types of Plate Boundaries

The interaction between plates determines the type of geological feature (and earthquake) produced. There are exactly **three** fundamental boundary types:

### (a) Divergent Boundary (Constructive)
- Two plates move **away** from each other.
- Magma upwells from the asthenosphere, creating new crust.
- Produces **mid-ocean ridges** (e.g., Mid-Atlantic Ridge) and **rift valleys** (e.g., East African Rift).
- Earthquakes here are typically **shallow-focus** (depth < 70 km) and of **moderate magnitude**.

### (b) Convergent Boundary (Destructive)
- Two plates move **toward** each other.
- **Subduction**: The denser oceanic plate sinks beneath the lighter continental plate, producing a deep oceanic trench and a volcanic arc.
- **Collision**: Two continental plates collide, producing mountain ranges (e.g., the **Himalayas** from the Indo-Eurasian collision).
- Generates the **largest and deepest earthquakes** (Wadati-Benioff zone, depths up to 700 km).

### (c) Transform Boundary (Conservative)
- Two plates slide **horizontally** past each other.
- Crust is neither created nor destroyed.
- Classic example: the **San Andreas Fault** in California.
- Produces **shallow, destructive strike-slip earthquakes**.

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Types of Faults

The **type of fault** is determined by the **direction of relative slip** of the hanging wall with respect to the foot wall.

### (a) Normal Fault (Tensional Stress)
- Hanging wall moves **down** relative to the foot wall.
- Caused by **tensional (pulling apart) forces** ŌĆö typical of divergent boundaries.
- Generates **shallow earthquakes**.
- **Dip angle** is typically **60┬░ ┬▒ 15┬░**.

### (b) Reverse Fault (Compressional Stress)
- Hanging wall moves **up** relative to the foot wall.
- Caused by **compressional forces** ŌĆö typical of convergent boundaries.
- Dip angle > 45┬░.

### (c) Thrust Fault (Low-Angle Reverse)
- A special case of reverse fault with **dip angle < 45┬░** (usually < 30┬░).
- Responsible for the largest earthquakes (e.g., 2011 T┼Źhoku, 2004 Sumatra-Andaman).
- **Critical for KTU**: The **Main Himalayan Thrust (MHT)** is a low-angle thrust fault responsible for major Himalayan seismicity.

### (d) Strike-Slip Fault (Shear Stress)
- Movement is **horizontal**, parallel to the strike of the fault.
- Two subtypes:
  - **Right-lateral (dextral)**: The far side appears to move to the right.
  - **Left-lateral (sinistral)**: The far side appears to move to the left.
- The **San Andreas Fault** is right-lateral.
- The **North Anatolian Fault** (Turkey) is right-lateral.
- The **Great Sumatran Fault** is right-lateral.

## 2.3 The Elastic Rebound Theory (Reid, 1910)

Harry Fielding Reid's **Elastic Rebound Theory** is the cornerstone of modern seismology. It states:

> **An earthquake is the sudden release of elastic strain energy stored in the crust due to the slow, continuous deformation of rock across a fault.**

The four-stage cycle is:
1. **Interseismic**: Tectonic stress slowly deforms the rock elastically (strain accumulation phase).
2. **Pre-seismic**: Stress approaches the **frictional strength** of the fault.
3. **Coseismic (Earthquake)**: Sudden slip releases stored energy as seismic waves.
4. **Post-seismic**: Continued adjustment (aftershocks, viscoelastic relaxation).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Moment Magnitude** ($M_w$) | $M_w = \dfrac{2}{3} \log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07$ | $M_0$ in N┬Ęm (dyne┬Ęcm in CGS) |
| 2 | **Seismic Moment** ($M_0$) | $M_0 = \mu \cdot A \cdot \bar{D}$ | $\mu$ = shear modulus (Pa); $A$ = rupture area (m┬▓); $\bar{D}$ = avg. slip (m) |
| 3 | **Energy Released** ($E_s$) | $\log_{10} E_s = 1.5 M_w + 4.8$ | $E_s$ in Joules |
| 4 | **Strain Energy Density** | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \cdot \epsilon = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2E}$ | $\sigma$ in Pa; $E$ in Pa |
| 5 | **Fault Slip Type (Anderson's Theory)** | $\sigma_1$ vs $\sigma_3$ orientation determines fault type | $\sigma_1$ = max principal stress; $\sigma_3$ = min principal stress |
| 6 | **Gutenberg-Richter Law** | $\log_{10} N(M) = a - b M$ | $N(M)$ = number of events $\geq$ magnitude $M$ |
| 7 | **Snell's Law (seismic refraction)** | $\dfrac{\sin i_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{\sin i_2}{V_2}$ | $i$ = incidence angle; $V$ = wave velocity |
| 8 | **P-wave Velocity** | $V_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{\lambda + 2\mu}{\rho}}$ | $\lambda, \mu$ = Lam├® constants (Pa); $\rho$ = density (kg/m┬│) |
| 9 | **S-wave Velocity** | $V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\rho}}$ | $\mu$ = shear modulus; $\rho$ = density |
| 10 | **Focal Depth Classification** | Shallow $<$ 70 km; Intermediate 70ŌĆō300 km; Deep $>$ 300 km | $h$ in km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to identify a fault, always state **(i) the relative motion of the hanging wall**, **(ii) the stress regime (tensional, compressional, shear)**, and **(iii) the typical plate boundary** it is associated with. Missing any one of these three will cost you 2 marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seismic Hazard Zoning**: Identification of active faults is the basis of India's **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016** seismic zone map.
- **Building Code Design**: A site within 5 km of an active fault typically requires **special studies** and may be excluded from construction per **IS 1893**.
- **Critical Infrastructure**: Dams, nuclear plants, and bridges are *always* set back from active fault traces per **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** and **CWC** guidelines.
- **Kerala Context**: Kerala falls in **Seismic Zone III** (IS 1893), with localized Zone IV pockets near the **Wayanad** and **Idukki** regions due to proximity to **rift-related fault systems**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Elastic Rebound Energy Released in an Earthquake

We now rigorously derive the relationship between **fault slip**, **shear modulus**, and the **seismic energy** released ŌĆö the central equation of seismology.

### Setup
Consider a fault segment of length $L$, depth $W$ (down-dip width), and average slip $\bar{D}$. The rock on either side has a shear modulus $\mu$.

### Step 1: Define the Stress Drop
The stress drop $\Delta \sigma$ is the difference between the initial (pre-slip) shear stress $\tau_0$ and the final (post-slip) shear stress $\tau_f$ on the fault plane.

$$
\Delta \sigma = \tau_0 - \tau_f
$$

### Step 2: Strain Energy Density in the Stressed Volume
Before the earthquake, the rock volume adjacent to the fault (a box of dimensions $L \times W \times r$, where $r$ is the characteristic deformation distance) is loaded to shear stress $\tau_0$. The volumetric strain energy density is:

$$
u = \frac{\tau_0^{2}}{2\mu}
$$

### Step 3: Total Elastic Strain Energy Stored
Multiply the strain energy density by the volume over which it is stored:

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = u \cdot V = \frac{\tau_0^{2}}{2\mu} \cdot (L \cdot W \cdot r)
$$

### Step 4: Energy Released During Slip
Not all stored energy is radiated as seismic waves; some is dissipated as frictional heat. Define a **radiation efficiency** $\eta_r$ (typically $\eta_r \approx 0.05$ to $0.15$ for crustal earthquakes). The radiated seismic energy is:

$$
E_s = \eta_r \cdot \Delta \sigma \cdot A \cdot \bar{D}
$$

where $A = L \cdot W$ is the rupture area, and the product $\Delta \sigma \cdot \bar{D}$ is proportional to the available strain energy per unit area.

### Step 5: Substitute the Stress Drop Expression
Using $\Delta \sigma = \mu \cdot \dfrac{\bar{D}}{W}$ (a commonly used scaling from dislocation theory), we obtain:

$$
E_s = \eta_r \cdot \mu \cdot \frac{\bar{D}}{W} \cdot (L \cdot W) \cdot \bar{D}
$$

$$
E_s = \eta_r \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot \bar{D}^{2}
$$

### Step 6: The Seismic Moment Definition
The **seismic moment** $M_0$ is defined as:

$$
M_0 = \mu \cdot A \cdot \bar{D} = \mu \cdot L \cdot W \cdot \bar{D}
$$

### Step 7: Combine Energy and Moment
Dividing the expression for $E_s$ by $M_0$:

$$
\frac{E_s}{M_0} = \frac{\eta_r \cdot \bar{D}}{W}
$$

For typical crustal earthquakes where $\bar{D} \approx 10^{-3} W$, this gives $E_s / M_0 \approx 10^{-5}$, which matches empirical observations used to derive the famous **Kanamori (1977) energy-magnitude relation**:

$$
\log_{10} E_s \;\; (\text{in Joules}) = 1.5 M_w + 4.8
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Fault Type vs. Stress vs. Plate Boundary

The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam summary** that directly links Anderson's Theory of Faulting (which uses the orientations of the three principal stresses $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$) to observed geological features.

| Fault Type | Hanging Wall Motion | Stress Regime | $\sigma_1$ Orientation | $\sigma_3$ Orientation | Typical Plate Boundary | Real-World Example |
|------------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Normal** | Moves **down** | Tensional (extension) | Vertical | Horizontal | **Divergent** | East African Rift; Basin and Range (USA) |
| **Reverse** | Moves **up** (high angle) | Compressional | Horizontal | Horizontal (perpendicular) | **Convergent** | Pacific NW megathrusts |
| **Thrust** | Moves **up** (low angle) | Strongly compressional | Horizontal | Horizontal | **Convergent (collisional)** | Main Himalayan Thrust; 2011 T┼Źhoku |
| **Strike-Slip** | Horizontal shear | Shear | Horizontal | Horizontal (parallel) | **Transform** | San Andreas Fault; North Anatolian Fault |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anderson's Theory of Faulting (1951):** Given that one principal stress must be vertical (since the Earth's surface is a free surface), the other two are horizontal. Their relative magnitudes determine the fault type.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A fault segment ruptures over an area of $A = 50 \text{ km}^2$ with an average slip of $\bar{D} = 2.5 \text{ m}$. The shear modulus of the rock is $\mu = 30 \text{ GPa}$. The radiated seismic energy is $E_s = 1.5 \times 10^{16}$ J. Find the **moment magnitude** $M_w$ and the **radiation efficiency** $\eta_r$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Convert Units**

$$
A = 50 \text{ km}^2 = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\mu = 30 \text{ GPa} = 30 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Seismic Moment $M_0$**

$$
M_0 = \mu \cdot A \cdot \bar{D}
$$

$$
M_0 = (30 \times 10^{9}) \times (5 \times 10^{7}) \times (2.5)
$$

$$
M_0 = 3.75 \times 10^{19} \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Moment Magnitude $M_w$**

$$
M_w = \frac{2}{3} \log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07
$$

$$
M_w = \frac{2}{3} \log_{10}(3.75 \times 10^{19}) - 6.07
$$

$$
\log_{10}(3.75 \times 10^{19}) = \log_{10}(3.75) + 19 = 0.574 + 19 = 19.574
$$

$$
M_w = \frac{2}{3} \times 19.574 - 6.07
$$

$$
M_w = 13.049 - 6.07 = 6.98 \approx 7.0
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Stress Drop $\Delta \sigma$** (using a typical down-dip width $W = 15$ km)

$$
W = 15 \text{ km} = 1.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta \sigma = \mu \cdot \frac{\bar{D}}{W} = (30 \times 10^{9}) \times \frac{2.5}{1.5 \times 10^{4}}
$$

$$
\Delta \sigma = 5.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 5.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Radiation Efficiency $\eta_r$**

$$
E_s = \eta_r \cdot \Delta \sigma \cdot A \cdot \bar{D}
$$

$$
\eta_r = \frac{E_s}{\Delta \sigma \cdot A \cdot \bar{D}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{16}}{(5 \times 10^{6}) \times (5 \times 10^{7}) \times 2.5}
$$

$$
\eta_r = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{16}}{6.25 \times 10^{14}} = 24.0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Failure:** A radiation efficiency greater than 1 is **physically impossible**. This indicates either the assumed value of $W$ is too small, or the energy $E_s$ is unphysically large for the given $M_w$. In KTU exams, you would still be awarded partial credit for the methodology, but should note this discrepancy in your final answer.

A more realistic case: if $W = 30$ km, then $\Delta \sigma = 2.5$ MPa and $\eta_r = 12$ (still $> 1$). The discrepancy in this textbook problem is a reminder that the **constant of proportionality** between $E_s$ and $M_0$ is empirical ŌĆö for crustal earthquakes, $\eta_r \cdot \bar{D} / W \approx 5 \times 10^{-5}$, so $E_s \approx 5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M_0$.

For $M_0 = 3.75 \times 10^{19}$ N┬Ęm:

$$
E_s \approx 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.75 \times 10^{19} = 1.875 \times 10^{15} \text{ J}
$$

This corresponds to $M_w \approx 6.95$ (consistent with the moment magnitude calculation above).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Plate Tectonic Boundaries `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"With neat sketches, explain the three types of plate boundaries and the associated earthquake characteristics."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three types of plate boundaries are:

**(a) Divergent Boundary (1 mark):** Two plates move apart. Magma upwells to form new crust. Example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge. Earthquakes are **shallow-focus** (depth < 70 km) and **moderate magnitude** ($M_w$ typically 5ŌĆō6) due to normal faulting on the ridge flanks.

**(b) Convergent Boundary (1 mark):** Two plates move toward each other. Either subduction (oceanic beneath continental) or collision (continent-continent). Produces the **largest and deepest** earthquakes along the **Wadati-Benioff zone** (up to 700 km depth). Example: the Himalayan collision zone.

**(c) Transform Boundary (1 mark):** Two plates slide horizontally past each other. Crust is conserved (neither created nor destroyed). Produces **shallow, destructive strike-slip earthquakes**. Example: San Andreas Fault (California) and the North Anatolian Fault (Turkey).

---

| Feature | Normal | Reverse | Thrust | Strike-Slip |

| Hanging wall | Down | Up | Up | Horizontal |
| Stress | Tensional | Compressional | Strongly compressional | Shear |

Diagrams should clearly show the **hanging wall**, **foot wall**, **fault plane**, **dip**, and **arrows of relative motion**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Explain in detail the Theory of Plate Tectonics. Discuss its relevance to the seismic zoning of India as per IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016."**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Theory of Plate Tectonics (7 marks)

The **Theory of Plate Tectonics** (proposed by Harry Hess, Robert Dietz, and Tuzo Wilson in the 1960s) posits that the Earth's **lithosphere** is fragmented into about **15 major and minor plates** that move over the semi-fluid **asthenosphere** at rates of **1ŌĆō10 cm/year**.

**The driving forces (1.5 marks):**
- **Slab Pull** (dominant, ~80% of force): The weight of a subducting slab pulls the rest of the plate down.
- **Ridge Push** (~20%): Gravity-driven sliding of newly formed hot lithosphere away from mid-ocean ridges.
- **Basal Drag** (minor): Convection currents in the asthenosphere drag the lithospheric base.

**Three boundary types (3 marks):** Divergent, Convergent, Transform (as detailed in Section 2.1).

**Earthquake generation (1.5 marks):** Plate boundaries are the loci of **strain accumulation** and **sudden release** (Elastic Rebound Theory). 90% of all earthquakes occur at plate boundaries; 10% are **intra-plate** (e.g., the 2001 Bhuj earthquake, $M_w$ 7.7).

**Hotspots** (1 mark): Intra-plate volcanism (e.g., Hawaii, R├®union) is explained by deep mantle plumes and is *not* directly a plate-boundary feature.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Relevance to Indian Seismic Zoning (7 marks)

India's seismic zoning under **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016** divides the country into **four zones**:

| Zone | Effective PGA ($g$) | Max Intensity (MSK) | Tectonic Origin |
|------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Zone II** | 0.10 g | VI (or less) | Stable continental interior (Peninsular India interior) |
| **Zone III** | 0.16 g | VII | Moderate seismicity; **Kerala falls here** |
| **Zone IV** | 0.24 g | VIII | Himalayan foothills, Kutch, NE India, **Kerala's Idukki pocket** |
| **Zone V** | 0.36 g | IX+ | Most severe; Kashmir, Himachal, Uttarakhand, NE |

**Tectonic basis (4 marks):**
- The **Indo-Eurasian collision** (still active for ~50 million years) produces **Zone IV and V** in the Himalayan belt and the Indo-Gangetic plains.
- The **Kachchh rift zone** in Gujarat (intersection of three failed rifts) is **Zone V**, site of the 2001 Bhuj earthquake.
- The **Indo-Burmese arc** and **Shillong Plateau** are **Zone V** due to active subduction and complex faulting.
- **Peninsular India** (including most of Kerala) is **Zone III** as a stable intra-plate region, but with **Zone IV pockets** near **rift-related faults** (e.g., the **Idukki-Wayanad shear zone**).
- The **Narmada-Son-Tapti lineament** is a deep-seated ancient rift with reactivated seismicity.

**Engineering implications (3 marks):**
- **Zone V** structures must be designed for **PGA = 0.36 g** using **Response Spectrum** methods.
- **IS 1893** uses **Importance Factor $I$** (1.0 to 1.5) and **Response Reduction Factor $R$** (3.0 to 5.0).
- Special provisions for **critical facilities** (hospitals, schools, nuclear plants) require **Zone V** design even if the site is in Zone IV.
- **Site-specific response analysis** is required for major projects near active faults.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake in 14-mark questions on Plate Tectonics is **not connecting the theory to the engineering design code**. Examiners explicitly check for at least one mention of **IS 1893**, **PGA**, or **seismic zone classification**. Failure to bridge geology and engineering will cost you 2ŌĆō3 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 ŌĆö Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Define a fault. With the help of neat diagrams, classify faults based on (i) the direction of slip, and (ii) the relative motion of the hanging wall. Explain the Elastic Rebound Theory in detail."**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Fault Definition and Classification (7 marks)

A **fault** is a **planar fracture or discontinuity** in a volume of rock across which there has been measurable displacement of the two sides relative to one another. The principal geometric elements of a fault are: **strike** (the compass direction of the horizontal line on the fault plane), **dip** (the angle the fault plane makes with the horizontal), and **dip direction**.

**Classification by direction of slip (3.5 marks):**
- **Dip-slip faults**: Motion is along the dip direction.
  - **Normal fault**: Hanging wall slides down. Driven by **tensional** stress. Dip 60┬░ ┬▒ 15┬░. Common at divergent boundaries.
  - **Reverse fault**: Hanging wall slides up. Dip > 45┬░. Driven by **compressional** stress. Common at convergent boundaries.
  - **Thrust fault**: Low-angle reverse fault (dip < 30┬░ŌĆō45┬░). Responsible for the largest megathrust earthquakes (e.g., 2011 T┼Źhoku $M_w$ 9.1).
- **Strike-slip faults**: Motion is horizontal, parallel to the fault strike. Divided into:
  - **Right-lateral (dextral)**: The far block moves to the right (e.g., San Andreas).
  - **Left-lateral (sinistral)**: The far block moves to the left (e.g., Dead Sea Transform).
- **Oblique-slip faults**: Combined dip-slip and strike-slip motion.

**Classification by hanging wall motion (3.5 marks):** The hanging wall is the rock *above* the fault plane, and the foot wall is the rock *below*. The relative motion of these two defines whether the fault is normal, reverse, thrust, or strike-slip (as detailed above). Diagrams must clearly show the **hanging wall** and **foot wall** blocks with **arrows of relative motion** and a **dip-angle measurement**.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Elastic Rebound Theory (7 marks)

The **Elastic Rebound Theory** was proposed by **Harry Fielding Reid** in **1910**, following the great **1906 San Francisco earthquake** along the **San Andreas Fault**. The theory states:

**Statement (2 marks):** An earthquake is the **sudden release of elastic strain energy** that has been stored in the Earth's crust due to the **slow, continuous relative motion** of two rock masses across a fault.

**Four-stage cycle (4 marks):**

1. **Stage 1 ŌĆö Interseismic (Strain Accumulation):** Tectonic forces slowly deform the rock on either side of the fault. The rock behaves **elastically** (i.e., deformation is recoverable). Strain energy is stored in the rock volume.

2. **Stage 2 ŌĆö Pre-seismic (Stress Buildup):** Stress on the fault plane approaches the **frictional strength** of the rock. Small, precursory phenomena (foreshocks, radon emission, ground tilt) may occur.

3. **Stage 3 ŌĆö Coseismic (Earthquake):** Stress exceeds frictional strength. The rock **suddenly slips**, releasing stored elastic energy as **seismic waves** (P-waves, S-waves, surface waves). The rocks on either side rebound to a less-strained state.

4. **Stage 4 ŌĆö Post-seismic:** Continued adjustment via **aftershocks** and **viscoelastic relaxation** of the lower crust and upper mantle.

**Mathematical formulation (1 mark):** The radiated energy is given by:

$$
E_s = \eta_r \cdot \Delta \sigma \cdot A \cdot \bar{D}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** The Elastic Rebound Theory is the **foundation of modern seismology** and **seismic hazard assessment**. It underpins the **Gutenberg-Richter recurrence law**, **probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA)**, and the **seismic gap hypothesis** used in long-term earthquake forecasting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common error in answering Elastic Rebound Theory questions is to state that rocks are **plastic** during deformation. They are **elastic** ŌĆö that is precisely why the energy can be stored and then suddenly released. Confusing these two will cost you 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Plate Tectonics** is the unifying theory explaining earthquakes via the **movement of rigid lithospheric plates** over the **asthenosphere**.
- The **three plate boundaries** ŌĆö **divergent**, **convergent**, and **transform** ŌĆö produce distinct earthquake characteristics: **shallow-moderate**, **shallow-deep-large**, and **shallow-destructive** respectively.
- The **three (or four) principal fault types** are: **normal** (tensional), **reverse** (compressional, high angle), **thrust** (compressional, low angle), and **strike-slip** (shear, horizontal). The classification hinges on the **relative motion of the hanging wall**.
- **Anderson's Theory of Faulting** links **principal stress orientations** to the **type of fault** produced. One principal stress is always vertical (due to the free surface).
- The **Elastic Rebound Theory** (Reid, 1910) is the **cornerstone** of seismology: earthquakes are the **sudden release** of stored **elastic strain energy**.
- The **seismic moment** $M_0 = \mu A \bar{D}$ and the **moment magnitude** $M_w = \tfrac{2}{3} \log_{10} M_0 - 6.07$ are the standard modern measures of earthquake size.
- The **Indian Plate** is moving **northward at ~5 cm/year** into the **Eurasian Plate**, producing the **Himalayan seismic belt** and driving India's **Zone V** seismicity in the north and **Zone III** in the south (including most of Kerala).
- **Kerala falls in IS 1893 Seismic Zone III**, with **Zone IV pockets** in **Idukki** and **Wayanad** due to local rift-related faulting.
- For **KTU 2024 Scheme**, you must connect **geological theory** (plate tectonics, faults) to **engineering design** (IS 1893, PGA, response spectrum) to score full marks in **Part B (14-mark)** questions.
- **Highest-yield diagrams** for the exam: **(i) a labeled cross-section of each plate boundary**, **(ii) a labeled block diagram of each fault type showing hanging wall, foot wall, dip, and slip direction**, and **(iii) the four-stage elastic rebound cycle**.
- **Avoid the common pitfalls**: confusing reverse and thrust (difference is **dip angle**), confusing plastic and elastic deformation in Reid's theory, and failing to cite a specific Indian or global fault as an example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earthquake Magnitude and Intensity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Earthquake Magnitude and Intensity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Earthquake Magnitude ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnitude** is a quantitative, instrumentally measured scale that quantifies the **energy released at the seismic source (focus/hypocenter)** of an earthquake. It is a single, unique number for any given earthquake ŌĆö independent of the observation station.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, magnitude is a **source parameter** obtained by processing seismograph records. It does **not** vary with distance, depth of observation, or local site conditions.

## 1.2 Earthquake Intensity ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intensity** is a qualitative (semi-quantitative) measure of the **effects of ground shaking** on structures, people, and the natural environment at a specific **locality on the Earth's surface**. Intensity varies from place to place for the same earthquake.

Intensity is an **observed effect parameter** ŌĆö it decreases with distance from the epicenter and is heavily influenced by local soil and geology.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Lightbulb vs. Brightness" Model

| Analogy Element | Earthquake Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Wattage of a bulb (60 W)** | **Magnitude** ŌĆö fixed, intrinsic energy at the source |
| **Brightness on a wall** | **Intensity** ŌĆö varies at different points; brighter near, dimmer far |
| **Frosted glass vs. clear glass** | **Local soil condition** ŌĆö modifies intensity even at same distance |

> [!NOTE]
> A single earthquake has **ONE magnitude**, but **MANY intensities** (each at a different location). This is the most important conceptual distinction in Module 3.

## 1.4 Why KTU Emphasizes This Distinction

In structural design, **magnitude** dictates the input energy for seismic hazard analysis, while **intensity** correlates with the actual damage a structure may experience. Modern codes (IS 1893:2016, IS 4326) primarily use **Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA)** and **spectral acceleration**, both of which are intensity-related strong-motion parameters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Magnitude Scales

Magnitude scales are broadly classified into:

- **Wave-Amplitude Based Scales** (Empirical, body & surface waves)
- **Seismic Moment Based Scale** (Physical, modern standard)

### 2.1.1 Local Magnitude Scale (Richter, $M_L$)

Proposed by **Charles F. Richter (1935)** for Southern California earthquakes. Defined as:

$$M_L = \log_{10} A - \log_{10} A_0(\Delta)$$

where:
- $A$ = maximum trace amplitude (in mm) on a **standard Wood-Anderson seismograph**
- $\Delta$ = epicentral distance (km)
- $A_0$ = reference amplitude for a "zero-magnitude" quake at distance $\Delta$

> [!NOTE]
> Richter scale is **open-ended** (no upper or lower bound) but **saturates near M Ōēł 7.0** because the Wood-Anderson instrument clips at large amplitudes. It is best suited for **shallow, local earthquakes (╬ö Ōēż 600 km)**.

### 2.1.2 Body Wave Magnitude Scale ($m_b$)

Uses the first few cycles of the **P-wave**:

$$m_b = \log_{10} \left( \frac{A}{T} \right) + Q(\Delta, h)$$

where $A$ = amplitude (╬╝m), $T$ = period (s), $Q$ = empirical attenuation-depth correction.

### 2.1.3 Surface Wave Magnitude Scale ($M_S$)

Uses Rayleigh surface waves of period Ōēł 20 s:

$$M_S = \log_{10} \left( \frac{A}{T} \right) + 1.66 \log_{10} \Delta + 3.30$$

where $\Delta$ is in degrees (1┬░ Ōēł 111 km). Valid for $20┬░ \leq \Delta \leq 160┬░$, $h \leq 50$ km.

### 2.1.4 Moment Magnitude Scale ($M_w$)

The **modern, saturation-free standard** (Hanks & Kanamori, 1979):

$$M_w = \frac{2}{3} \log_{10} M_0 - 6.07$$

where the **seismic moment** $M_0$ (N┬Ęm) is:

$$M_0 = \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{D}$$

with:
- $\mu$ = shear modulus of rock (Pa)
- $A_f$ = fault rupture area (m┬▓)
- $\bar{D}$ = average slip (dislocation) across the fault (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $M_w$ directly relates to **fault physics** (geometry, slip, rigidity), making it the most physically meaningful scale. **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016 implicitly uses $M_w$-based hazard inputs** through the uniform hazard spectrum.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Local Magnitude | $M_L = \log_{10} A - \log_{10} A_0$ | $A$ in mm; $A_0$ = reference | $M_L \leq 7$; ╬ö Ōēż 600 km; Wood-Anderson |
| 2 | Body Wave Magnitude | $m_b = \log_{10}(A/T) + Q(\Delta, h)$ | $A$ in ╬╝m; $T$ in s | P-waves, teleseismic |
| 3 | Surface Wave Magnitude | $M_S = \log_{10}(A/T) + 1.66\log_{10}\Delta + 3.30$ | $\Delta$ in degrees | Rayleigh waves Ōēł 20 s period |
| 4 | Seismic Moment | $M_0 = \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{D}$ | $\mu$ in Pa, $A_f$ in m┬▓, $\bar{D}$ in m | Physical source parameter |
| 5 | Moment Magnitude | $M_w = (2/3)\log_{10} M_0 - 6.07$ | $M_0$ in N┬Ęm | No saturation; global standard |
| 6 | GutenbergŌĆōRichter Energy Law | $\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$ | $E$ in Joules | Approximate; $M$ = $M_S$ or $M_L$ |
| 7 | Energy per Unit Magnitude Step | $\Delta E \approx 31.6 \times$ per unit $M$ | factor 10^{1.5} | **~32├Ś** per step |
| 8 | MMI Approx. from PGA | $I_{MMI} \approx 3.66 \log_{10}(PGA) + 1.66$ | PGA in cm/s┬▓ | Wald (1999) empirical |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seismic Hazard Mapping** uses recurrence relations $\log_{10} N = a - bM$ (GutenbergŌĆōRichter frequency law) to estimate return periods of design earthquakes.
- **Design Basis Earthquake (DBE)** and **Maximum Considered Earthquake (MCE)** in IS 1893:2016 correspond to **475-year and 2475-year return periods**, respectively ŌĆö both ultimately tied to $M_w$ distributions.
- **Liquefaction, landslide, and tsunami triggering** are all correlated with both magnitude thresholds and local intensity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Energy and Magnitude

**GutenbergŌĆōRichter (1956) Energy-Magnitude Relation** is **empirically derived** by correlating radiated seismic energy $E$ (computed from integrated velocity-squared records) with surface-wave magnitude $M_S$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define Radiated Seismic Energy

The radiated energy flux across a spherical surface of radius $r$ surrounding the source is:

$$E = \int_0^{\infty} \oint_S \rho \alpha \left( \dot{u} \right)^2 \, dS \, dt$$

where $\rho$ = density, $\alpha$ = P-wave velocity, $\dot{u}$ = particle velocity time-derivative.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Empirical Calibration

Gutenberg & Richter (1956) correlated $E$ (in ergs, where $1$ erg $= 10^{-7}$ J) with magnitude and fitted a straight line on a semi-log plot, yielding (after unit conversion to Joules):

$$\log_{10} E = 1.5\, M_S + 4.8$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Magnitude Step Energy Ratio

Let $E_1$ correspond to magnitude $M$ and $E_2$ to magnitude $M+1$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} E_2 - \log_{10} E_1 &= 1.5\,(M+1) + 4.8 - \left(1.5\,M + 4.8\right) \\
&= 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{E_2}{E_1} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A magnitude increase of 1.0 corresponds to Ōēł 32 times more radiated energy.** A magnitude-7 quake releases Ōēł 1,000 times the energy of a magnitude-5 quake (since $10^{1.5 \times 2} = 10^3$).

### Step 4 ŌĆö Practical Energy Table

| Magnitude $M_w$ | Approx. Radiated Energy $E$ (J) | TNT Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 2.0 | $6.3 \times 10^{7}$ | Ōēł 15 kg |
| 4.0 | $6.3 \times 10^{10}$ | Ōēł 15 tons |
| 6.0 | $6.3 \times 10^{13}$ | Ōēł 15 kilotons |
| 8.0 | $6.3 \times 10^{16}$ | Ōēł 15 megatons |
| 9.0 | $2.0 \times 10^{18}$ | Ōēł 475 megatons |

## 3.2 Derivation: Magnitude Equivalence Between $M_L$, $m_b$, $M_S$, and $M_w$

For most shallow tectonic earthquakes, the following empirical equivalences hold (used in catalogues like USGS NEIC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_b &\approx 0.85\, M_S \quad \text{(for } M_S \leq 6.5\text{)} \\
M_S &\approx 1.13\, M_w - 1.08 \quad \text{(general)} \\
\log_{10} M_0 &= 1.5\, M_w + 9.1 \quad \text{(N┬Ęm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first relation arises because body waves (short period) **saturate earlier** than surface waves; once fault slip exceeds the source dimension, $m_b$ plateaus while $M_S$ continues to grow.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Magnitude vs. Intensity

| Attribute | **Magnitude** | **Intensity** |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Quantitative (instrumental) | Qualitative / semi-quantitative (observed) |
| Uniqueness | ONE value per earthquake | MANY values (one per locality) |
| Source / Variable | Earthquake source energy | Site-specific ground motion effects |
| Scale Type | Logarithmic (open-ended) | Ordinal (IŌĆōXII) Roman numerals |
| Quantifies | Released energy $E$ | Damage to structures, felt perception |
| Typical Scales | $M_L$, $m_b$, $M_S$, $M_w$ | MMI, MSK-64, JMA, EMS-98 |
| Isoseismal Map? | No (point source) | Yes (contours of equal intensity) |
| Saturation | $M_L$ saturates Ōēł 7; $M_w$ does NOT | Saturates at "Total Destruction" (XII) |
| Typical Use | Hazard recurrence, source modelling | Rapid post-event damage assessment, building code zoning |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö KTU-Style

> **Problem:** A seismograph records a peak ground amplitude of $A = 25$ mm at epicentral distance $\Delta = 100$ km. The Wood-Anderson zero-magnitude reference amplitude is $A_0 = 0.01$ mm. Compute the local magnitude $M_L$. If the surface-wave magnitude is reported as $M_S = 6.5$, estimate the radiated energy $E$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $M_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_L &= \log_{10}(25) - \log_{10}(0.01) \\
&= 1.3979 - (-2) \\
&= 3.3979 \approx 3.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $E$ from GutenbergŌĆōRichter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} E &= 1.5 \times 6.5 + 4.8 \\
&= 9.75 + 4.8 = 14.55 \\
E &= 10^{14.55} \approx 3.55 \times 10^{14} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is equivalent to Ōēł **85 kilotons of TNT** ŌĆö comparable to a moderate-sized nuclear device.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Magnitude & Intensity Pipelines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Seismic Event at Focus / Hypocenter] --> B[Seismic Waves Propagate]
    B --> C[Seismograph Station Network]
    C --> D[Instrumental Records]
    D --> E[Magnitude Scales]
    E --> E1[ML - Local]
    E --> E2[mb - Body Wave]
    E --> E3[MS - Surface Wave]
    E --> E4[Mw - Moment]
    B --> F[Surface Ground Motion]
    F --> G[Local Site Effects]
    G --> H[Observed Damage / Felt Reports]
    H --> I[Intensity Scales]
    I --> I1[MMI - Modified Mercalli]
    I --> I2[MSK-64]
    I --> I3[JMA Scale]
    I --> I4[EMS-98]
    E4 --> J[Seismic Hazard Map]
    I1 --> K[Isoseismal Contours]
    J --> L[Design Basis for Structures]
    K --> L
    L --> M[IS 1893:2016 / IS 4326 Compliant Design]
```

## 4.2 Magnitude Scale Evolution & Saturation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[ML - 1935] -->|Saturates near 7| S2[mb - 1960s]
    S2 -->|Saturates near 7.5| S3[MS - 1960s]
    S3 -->|Saturates near 8.5| S4[Mw - 1979]
    S4 -->|No Saturation| S5[Global Standard for Modern Hazard Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Intensity Attenuation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Epicenter - Highest Intensity] -->|Decreasing with distance| I1[Intensity IX-X - Heavy Damage]
    I1 --> I2[Intensity VII-VIII - Moderate Damage]
    I2 --> I3[Intensity V-VI - Light Damage]
    I3 --> I4[Intensity III-IV - Felt]
    I4 --> I5[Intensity I-II - Not Felt]
    style E fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style I5 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#060,color:#000
```

## 4.4 MagnitudeŌĆōEnergyŌĆōTNT Equivalent Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Mw 2.0] --> E1[Energy 6.3e7 J]
    M2[Mw 4.0] --> E2[Energy 6.3e10 J]
    M3[Mw 6.0] --> E3[Energy 6.3e13 J]
    M4[Mw 8.0] --> E4[Energy 6.3e16 J]
    M5[Mw 9.0] --> E5[Energy 2.0e18 J]
    E1 --> T1[TNT 15 kg]
    E2 --> T2[TNT 15 tons]
    E3 --> T3[TNT 15 kilotons]
    E4 --> T4[TNT 15 megatons]
    E5 --> T5[TNT 475 megatons]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently confuse **magnitude with intensity** in 2-mark/3-mark sub-questions ŌĆö instant **50% mark deduction**.
> - Formula $\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$ uses $E$ in **Joules** (not ergs). Writing ergs without conversion = **wrong numerical answer**.
> - For Wood-Anderson $M_L$ questions, **never** use $m_b$ or $M_S$ corrections ŌĆö instrument-specific bias is penalized.
> - In intensity descriptions (MMI scale), stating only the Roman numeral without descriptive effects fetches **partial marks only** ŌĆö always pair with a damage descriptor (e.g., "Intensity VIII ŌĆö Heavy damage to ordinary masonry, slight damage to specially designed structures").

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Question]
**Differentiate between earthquake magnitude and earthquake intensity. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Magnitude | Intensity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Measure of energy released at the source | Measure of ground-shaking effects at a site |
| Variability | Single value per earthquake | Varies from place to place |
| Basis | Instrumental (seismograph) | Observed damage and felt reports |
| Scale type | Logarithmic, open-ended | Ordinal (IŌĆōXII), e.g., MMI |

Magnitude is a **source parameter**; Intensity is an **effect parameter** at a given location. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Question]
**What is the significance of the Moment Magnitude Scale ($M_w$) over the Richter Scale ($M_L$)? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Moment Magnitude Scale ($M_w$) is preferred over $M_L$ because:
1. **No saturation** ŌĆö $M_L$ saturates at Ōēł 7.0 due to Wood-Anderson instrument clipping; $M_w$ has no upper limit. **(1 mark)**
2. **Physical basis** ŌĆö $M_w$ is derived from seismic moment $M_0 = \mu A_f \bar{D}$, directly linked to fault geometry, rigidity, and slip. **(1 mark)**
3. **Global applicability** ŌĆö $M_w$ is valid for any earthquake size, mechanism, or depth. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Internal choice ŌĆö students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.*

### ŌØæ Question A ŌĆö [14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Local Magnitude Scale ($M_L$)** proposed by Richter. Derive the relationship between seismic energy released and magnitude. State its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition (2 marks):** $M_L$ was defined by Charles F. Richter (1935) as:

$$M_L = \log_{10} A - \log_{10} A_0(\Delta)$$

where $A$ is the maximum trace amplitude in mm on a **standard Wood-Anderson torsion seismograph** (free period 0.8 s, magnification 2800) located at epicentral distance $\Delta$ (km), and $A_0$ is a calibration reference amplitude for a "zero-magnitude" earthquake at the same distance.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Energy Derivation (3 marks):** Radiated seismic energy $E$ correlates empirically with $M_S$ (and approximately $M_L$ for moderate quakes) by:

$$\log_{10} E = 1.5\,M + 4.8 \quad (E \text{ in Joules})$$

A unit increase in magnitude multiplies energy by $10^{1.5} \approx 31.62$. Hence:

$$E_{M+1} = 31.62 \times E_M$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Limitations (2 marks):**
- Saturates at $M_L \approx 7$ (Wood-Anderson instrument clips).
- Valid only for Southern California crustal events.
- Restricted to ╬ö Ōēż 600 km.
- Empirical ŌĆö no direct link to fault physics.

**(b)** A seismograph records maximum amplitude $A = 10$ mm at $\Delta = 100$ km. Given $A_0 = 0.005$ mm at this distance, determine $M_L$. If the earthquake has $M_S = 7.0$, find the radiated energy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $M_L$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_L &= \log_{10}(10) - \log_{10}(0.005) \\
&= 1.0 - (-2.301) \\
&= 3.301 \approx 3.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Energy from $M_S = 7.0$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} E &= 1.5 \times 7.0 + 4.8 \\
&= 10.5 + 4.8 = 15.3 \\
E &= 10^{15.3} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö TNT Equivalent (1 mark):** $1$ ton TNT $\approx 4.184 \times 10^9$ J, so

$$E \approx \frac{2.0 \times 10^{15}}{4.184 \times 10^9} \approx 4.78 \times 10^5 \text{ tons} \approx 478 \text{ kilotons TNT}$$

---

### ŌØæ Question B ŌĆö [14 Marks]

**(a)** Define **Seismic Moment ($M_0$)** and **Moment Magnitude ($M_w$)**. How is $M_w$ superior to surface-wave magnitude $M_S$ for large earthquakes? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Seismic Moment (2 marks):**

$$M_0 = \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{D}$$

where $\mu$ is the shear modulus (Pa), $A_f$ is the fault rupture area (m┬▓), and $\bar{D}$ is the average slip (m). $M_0$ has units of N┬Ęm.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moment Magnitude (2 marks):**

$$M_w = \frac{2}{3}\log_{10} M_0 - 6.07 \quad (M_0 \text{ in N┬Ęm})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Superiority over $M_S$ (3 marks):**
- **No saturation:** $M_S$ saturates near 8.5; $M_w$ is valid for $M > 9$ (e.g., 2011 T┼Źhoku $M_w = 9.1$).
- **Direct fault-physics link** ŌĆö measurable from geodesy (GPS, InSAR) and seismology independently.
- **Unambiguous for tsunami/rupture modelling.**

**(b)** An earthquake has fault area $A_f = 50 \text{ km} \times 20 \text{ km} = 1000 \text{ km}^2$, average slip $\bar{D} = 2$ m, and shear modulus $\mu = 3 \times 10^{10}$ Pa. Compute $M_0$ and $M_w$. Compare with a second event of half the slip and compare radiated energies using the GutenbergŌĆōRichter relation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $M_0$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f &= 1000 \text{ km}^2 = 1000 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 10^9 \text{ m}^2 \\
M_0 &= \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{D} \\
&= 3 \times 10^{10} \times 10^9 \times 2 \\
&= 6 \times 10^{19} \text{ N┬Ęm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $M_w$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= \frac{2}{3}\log_{10}(6 \times 10^{19}) - 6.07 \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \times 19.778 - 6.07 \\
&= 13.185 - 6.07 = 7.115 \approx 7.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Second event with $\bar{D} = 1$ m (1 mark):**

$$M_{0,2} = 3 \times 10^{19} \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

$$M_{w,2} = \frac{2}{3}\log_{10}(3 \times 10^{19}) - 6.07 \approx 7.0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Energy Comparison (3 marks):** Using $\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} E_1 &= 1.5 \times 7.115 + 4.8 = 15.4725 \\
E_1 &= 2.97 \times 10^{15} \text{ J} \\
\log_{10} E_2 &= 1.5 \times 7.0 + 4.8 = 15.30 \\
E_2 &= 2.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ J} \\
\frac{E_1}{E_2} &= 10^{0.1725} \approx 1.488
\end{aligned}
$$

Halving the slip reduces the energy by a factor of Ōēł 1.49 (since energy scales with the square of slip on average).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 ŌĆö Earthquake Magnitude and Intensity**

- ­¤öæ **Magnitude = one number per earthquake; Intensity = many numbers (one per location).** *(most-asked 2-marker)*
- ­¤öæ **Richter $M_L$ saturates at Ōēł 7.0; $M_w$ does not saturate.**
- ­¤öæ **Seismic moment:** $M_0 = \mu A_f \bar{D}$ (units: N┬Ęm).
- ­¤öæ **Moment magnitude:** $M_w = (2/3)\log_{10} M_0 - 6.07$.
- ­¤öæ **Energy-magnitude law:** $\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$ with $E$ in **Joules**.
- ­¤öæ **Each unit of magnitude = Ōēł 32├Ś more energy** (factor $10^{1.5} \approx 31.62$).
- ­¤öæ **Two unit increase in magnitude = Ōēł 1000├Ś more energy.**
- ­¤öæ **Intensity scales:** MMI, MSK-64, JMA, EMS-98 ŌĆö all Roman numeral IŌĆōXII, ordinal.
- ­¤öæ **MMI XII = "Total destruction"**; MMI I = "Not felt except by very few".
- ­¤öæ **Isoseismal map:** contour map connecting points of equal intensity ŌĆö a key deliverable in intensity-based post-earthquake surveys.
- ­¤öæ **Strong-motion parameters (PGA, PGV, PGD) are intensity-correlated**, not magnitude. IS 1893:2016 uses PGA (g) for design.
- ­¤öæ **GutenbergŌĆōRichter recurrence law** $\log_{10} N = a - bM$ ŌĆö used for probabilistic seismic hazard assessment (PSHA).
- ­¤öæ **For KTU Part-B questions:** always show the substitution step explicitly, label all variables, include **units**, and state the validity range of any scale used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Focus and Epicentre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Focus and Epicentre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Focus (Hypocenter)

The **Focus** (also called the **Hypocenter** or **Seismic Focus**) is the precise three-dimensional point located *within* the Earth's crust or upper mantle where the sudden rupture of rock, slippage along a fault plane, or volcanic activity initiates the release of accumulated **strain energy**. This is the **true point of origin** of an earthquake.

Mathematically, the focus is represented by three coordinates:
- Latitude (Žå)
- Longitude (╬╗)  
- Depth (h) below the Earth's surface

$$ \text{Focus} = (\phi_f, \lambda_f, h_f) $$

where $h_f$ is the focal depth measured vertically downward from the mean sea level datum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> The focus or hypocenter is the actual location *below* the Earth's surface where the earthquake originates due to the sudden release of elastic strain energy stored in rocks. The coordinates of the focus are specified by its latitude, longitude, and depth.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Epicentre

The **Epicentre** (or **Epicenter**) is the point on the **ground surface** (i.e., the Earth's free surface) that lies *vertically directly above* the focus/hypocenter. It is the surface projection of the focus onto the ground.

Mathematically:

$$ \text{Epicentre} = (\phi_f, \lambda_f, z = 0) $$

The epicentre is the point on the map that seismologists mark with a star symbol (Ōśģ) in news reports and geological maps after a seismic event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** The FOCUS is an *underground* 3-D point, while the EPICENTRE is a *surface* 2-D point. The vertical line connecting the epicentre to the focus is called the **seismic vertical** or **epicentral vertical**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are standing in a swimming pool holding a rubber ball underwater. When you release the ball (or push it suddenly), it creates ripples that travel outward in all directions on the water surface.

| Pool Analogy Element | Earthquake Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Your hand releasing the ball | Rock rupture at the **Focus** |
| The 3-D point in water where ball moved | **Hypocenter** (3-D underground point) |
| The point on water surface directly above your hand | **Epicentre** (2-D surface point) |
| Ripples traveling outward | **Seismic Waves** (P-waves, S-waves, Surface waves) |

> [!TIP]
> A simpler analogy: Think of the focus as the **location of a firecracker explosion inside a watermelon**, and the epicentre as the **dot you would draw on the outer skin of the watermelon directly above the explosion**.

## 1.4 Classification of Earthquakes Based on Focal Depth

The **focal depth** ($h_f$) is a critical parameter for earthquake engineering, as it directly governs the **intensity of ground shaking** at the surface.

| Classification | Depth Range (km) | Frequency of Occurrence | Damage Potential |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shallow Focus** | 0 ŌĆō 70 | ~ 75% of all earthquakes | **Very High** (most destructive) |
| **Intermediate Focus** | 70 ŌĆō 300 | ~ 22% of all earthquakes | Moderate to High |
| **Deep Focus** | 300 ŌĆō 700 | ~ 3% of all earthquakes | Generally low (energy dissipates) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Shallow-focus earthquakes are responsible for **~90% of all earthquake-related damage worldwide**. The 2001 Bhuj earthquake (Gujarat) was a shallow-focus event with $h_f \approx 16$ km, causing catastrophic damage. The 2023 Turkey-Syria earthquakes were also shallow focus ($h_f \approx 17$ km).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Focus and Epicentre Are Critical in Earthquake Engineering

The location of focus and epicentre directly affects:
1. **Ground motion intensity** at a given site
2. **Arrival time of seismic waves** (P, S, Surface)
3. **Design basis ground acceleration** for structures
4. **Areal extent of damage (felt area)**
5. **Tsunami generation potential** (subduction zone earthquakes)

## 2.2 Seismic Wave Propagation from Focus

When the focus ruptures, two principal body waves emanate:

### 2.2.1 P-Waves (Primary Waves)
- **Type:** Longitudinal / Compressional
- **Particle motion:** Parallel to wave propagation direction
- **Velocity ($V_p$):** Fastest seismic wave
- **Medium:** Travels through solids, liquids, and gases
- **Formula:** 

$$ V_p = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda + 2\mu}{\rho}} $$

where $\lambda$ = Lam├®'s constant, $\mu$ = Shear modulus, $\rho$ = density.

### 2.2.2 S-Waves (Secondary Waves)
- **Type:** Transverse / Shear
- **Particle motion:** Perpendicular to wave propagation direction
- **Velocity ($V_s$):** Slower than P-wave, $V_s \approx \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{3}}$ for Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.25$
- **Formula:**

$$ V_s = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\rho}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $V_p$ is always **greater** than $V_s$. This time lag between P and S arrivals is the **fundamental principle** used to locate the epicentre.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = V_p \cdot (T_p - T_0)$ | Distance to focus using P-wave | Wave travel time |
| 2 | $d = V_s \cdot (T_s - T_0)$ | Distance to focus using S-wave | Wave travel time |
| 3 | $T_s - T_p = d \left( \dfrac{1}{V_s} - \dfrac{1}{V_p} \right)$ | S-P time difference vs distance | Distance calculation |
| 4 | $d = k \cdot (T_s - T_p)$ | Simplified linear relation | Practical use |
| 5 | $\Delta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{h_f}\right)$ | Epicentral distance geometric relation | Geometry |
| 6 | $\overline{OE} = h_f \tan(90┬░ - \Delta)$ | Geometrical relation between O, E, F | Triangulation |
| 7 | $\tau = T_s - T_p$ | Time interval between S and P arrivals | Seismograph reading |
| 8 | $M_0 = \mu \cdot A \cdot D$ | Seismic moment | Source characterization |

Where:
- $d$ = hypocentral distance (km)
- $T_p, T_s$ = arrival times of P and S waves
- $T_0$ = origin time of earthquake
- $V_p, V_s$ = P and S wave velocities
- $h_f$ = focal depth
- $k$ = empirical constant Ōēł 8 km/sec (average crust)
- $\Delta$ = angle subtended at focus between vertical and the seismic station

## 2.4 The S-P Time Method (Wadati Concept)

The **time difference** between S and P wave arrivals at a single seismograph station is the foundation of epicentral location.

**Derivation Logic (Step-by-step thinking):**

1. Both P and S waves originate at the **same time** $T_0$ from the focus.
2. P-wave travels faster, so it arrives **earlier** at the station.
3. Travel time for P-wave: $T_p - T_0 = \dfrac{d}{V_p}$
4. Travel time for S-wave: $T_s - T_0 = \dfrac{d}{V_s}$
5. Subtracting: 

$$ T_s - T_p = d \left( \frac{1}{V_s} - \frac{1}{V_p} \right) $$

6. Since $\dfrac{1}{V_s} - \dfrac{1}{V_p}$ is approximately constant for a given region:

$$ d \approx k \cdot (T_s - T_p) $$

This is the **Wadati's empirical relation**, which gives the **epicentral distance** from a single station!

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Civil engineers use this method to rapidly estimate **how far a strong earthquake is from a dam, nuclear plant, or critical structure** for emergency shutdown protocols.

## 2.5 Triangulation Method for Locating Epicentre

Since one station only gives a *distance* (a circle of possible locations on the map), we need **at least 3 stations** to pinpoint the epicentre.

**Logic:**
1. Each station $S_i$ calculates its epicentral distance $d_i$ from $T_s - T_p$.
2. A circle of radius $d_i$ is drawn around each station.
3. The **intersection point** of these three circles is the **epicentre**.
4. The **origin time** $T_0$ is then back-calculated.

## 2.6 Geometric Relationship: Focus, Epicentre, and Recording Station

Consider the right-angled triangle formed by:
- **O** = Focus (origin point)
- **E** = Epicentre (on ground)
- **S** = Seismograph station (on ground)

The angle at the focus between the vertical OE and the line OS is the **emergence angle** $\Delta$ (also called the *take-off angle*). Since OE is vertical and the surface is horizontal:

$$ \sin(\Delta) = \frac{d_e}{d_h} $$

$$ d_h = \frac{h_f}{\cos(\Delta)} $$

where:
- $d_e$ = epicentral distance (horizontal, on surface) = ES
- $d_h$ = hypocentral distance (3-D, straight line OS)
- $h_f$ = focal depth = OE
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Distance from a Single Station Using S-P Time

**Given:**
- P-wave velocity $V_p$ = 6.0 km/s
- S-wave velocity $V_s$ = 3.5 km/s  
- S-P time difference $\tau = T_s - T_p$ = 12 seconds

**To find:** Epicentral distance $d$

**Step 1:** Express P and S wave travel times.

$$ T_p - T_0 = \frac{d}{V_p} $$

$$ T_s - T_0 = \frac{d}{V_s} $$

**Step 2:** Subtract the first from the second to eliminate the unknown origin time $T_0$.

$$ T_s - T_p = \frac{d}{V_s} - \frac{d}{V_p} $$

**Step 3:** Factor out $d$.

$$ T_s - T_p = d \left( \frac{1}{V_s} - \frac{1}{V_p} \right) $$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values into the bracket.

$$ \frac{1}{V_s} - \frac{1}{V_p} = \frac{1}{3.5} - \frac{1}{6.0} $$

$$ = 0.2857 - 0.1667 = 0.1190 \; \text{s/km} $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $d$.

$$ d = \frac{T_s - T_p}{0.1190} = \frac{12}{0.1190} $$

$$ d = 100.84 \; \text{km} $$

**Conclusion:** The seismograph station is approximately **100.84 km** from the epicentre.

## 3.2 Derivation: Locating Epicentre via Three-Station Triangulation

**Given three stations with measured distances:**

| Station | S-P Time (sec) | Computed Distance $d_i$ (km) |
|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ (Kozhikode) | 8 | 67.2 |
| $S_2$ (Ernakulam) | 14 | 117.6 |
| $S_3$ (Thiruvananthapuram) | 20 | 168.0 |

**To find:** The epicentre coordinates $(x_E, y_E)$ on a 2-D map (treating Earth surface as a flat plane for local events).

**Step 1:** Set up the circle equations.

For each station $S_i$ located at $(x_i, y_i)$, the epicentre $(x_E, y_E)$ must satisfy:

$$ (x_E - x_i)^2 + (y_E - y_i)^2 = d_i^2 $$

**Step 2:** Expand and simplify by subtracting equations to eliminate quadratic terms.

For stations 1 and 2:

$$ (x_E - x_1)^2 + (y_E - y_1)^2 = d_1^2 $$

$$ (x_E - x_2)^2 + (y_E - y_2)^2 = d_2^2 $$

Subtracting (linearization):

$$ 2x_E(x_2 - x_1) + 2y_E(y_2 - y_1) = d_1^2 - d_2^2 - x_1^2 + x_2^2 - y_1^2 + y_2^2 $$

**Step 3:** Repeat for stations 1 and 3 to get a second linear equation.

**Step 4:** Solve the resulting 2├Ś2 linear system for $(x_E, y_E)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} 2x_E(x_2 - x_1) + 2y_E(y_2 - y_1) &= d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\ 2x_E(x_3 - x_1) + 2y_E(y_3 - y_1) &= d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 \end{aligned} $$

**Step 5:** Solve using Cramer's Rule or matrix inversion to obtain $(x_E, y_E)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Finding Focal Depth from Emergence Angle

**Given:**
- Epicentral distance $d_e$ = 200 km
- Emergence angle $\Delta$ = 35┬░ (angle at focus between vertical and the ray to station)

**To find:** Focal depth $h_f$

**Step 1:** From the geometry of the OE-S triangle (right-angled at E):

$$ \tan(\Delta) = \frac{d_e}{h_f} $$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for $h_f$:

$$ h_f = \frac{d_e}{\tan(\Delta)} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$ h_f = \frac{200}{\tan(35┬░)} = \frac{200}{0.7002} $$

$$ h_f = 285.66 \; \text{km} $$

**Step 4:** Verify classification: $h_f = 285.66$ km falls in the **Intermediate Focus** category (70-300 km).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quick-Reference for KTU Problems

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distance from 1 station | $T_s, T_p, V_p, V_s$ | $d$ | $d = \dfrac{\tau}{1/V_s - 1/V_p}$ | 3-7 |
| Origin time | $d, V_p$ | $T_0$ | $T_0 = T_p - d/V_p$ | 3 |
| Triangulation (3 stations) | $S_i(x_i, y_i), d_i$ | $(x_E, y_E)$ | Circle intersection | 14 |
| Focal depth | $d_e, \Delta$ | $h_f$ | $h_f = d_e / \tan(\Delta)$ | 7 |
| Hypocentral distance | $d_e, h_f$ | $d_h$ | $d_h = \sqrt{d_e^2 + h_f^2}$ | 3-7 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hypocentral distance** (straight-line 3-D distance from focus to station):

$$ d_h = \sqrt{d_e^{\,2} + h_f^{\,2}} $$

This is the **Pythagorean theorem** applied to triangle OES where OE = $h_f$ and ES = $d_e$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Focus, Epicentre, and Seismic Wave Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SURF["EARTH SURFACE GROUND LEVEL"]
    EPI["EPICENTRE E - Surface Point x, y, 0"]
    FOC["FOCUS F - Hypocenter 3D Point x, y, depth h"]

    ST2["Seismograph Station S2 at distance d2"]
    ST3["Seismograph Station S3 at distance d3"]
    FAULT["FAULT PLANE - Rock Rupture Surface"]
    PWAVE["P-WAVE Travel Path Compressional Fastest"]
    SWAVE["S-WAVE Travel Path Shear Slower"]
    SURFWAVE["SURFACE WAVES RAYLEIGH and LOVE waves"]
    DEPTH["FOCAL DEPTH hf Categories Shallow 0 to 70 km Intermediate 70 to 300 km Deep 300 to 700 km"]

    FAULT -- initiates rupture at --> FOC
    FOC -- vertical projection upward --> EPI
    EPI -- sits on --> SURF
    FOC -- emits --> PWAVE
    FOC -- emits --> SWAVE
    EPI -- generates --> SURFWAVE
    PWAVE -- arrives first at --> ST1
    PWAVE -- arrives first at --> ST2
    PWAVE -- arrives first at --> ST3
    SWAVE -- arrives later at --> ST1
    SWAVE -- arrives later at --> ST2
    SWAVE -- arrives later at --> ST3
    ST1 -- distance d1 from --> EPI
    ST2 -- distance d2 from --> EPI
    ST3 -- distance d3 from --> EPI
    FOC -- characterized by --> DEPTH
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Epicentre Determination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rupture at Focus Energy Release] --> B[Seismic Waves Generated P-wave and S-wave]
    B --> C[Travel through Earth Crust Velocities Vp and Vs]

    D --> E[Measure P arrival time Tp and S arrival time Ts]
    E --> F[Compute S-P Time Difference tau at each station]
    F --> G[Calculate Epicentral Distance di using tau and velocity model]
    G --> H[Draw Circles of Radius di around each Station Si]
    H --> I[Intersect Three Circles to locate Epicentre E]
    I --> J[Compute Origin Time T0 back from arrival times]
    J --> K[Estimate Focal Depth hf from emergence angle and geometry]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFDAB9
    style K fill:#98FB98
```

## 4.3 Schematic Explanation (Text-Based Rendering)

```

                 Ōśģ E  (EPICENTRE - surface point)
                 Ōöé
                 Ōöé  ŌåÉ vertical seismic line OE
                 Ōöé     h_f (focal depth)
                 Ōöé
                 ŌĆó
                 F  (FOCUS - underground point)
                 Ōöé  ŌĢ▓
                 Ōöé    ŌĢ▓  ŌåÉ P-wave path (straight line)
                 Ōöé      ŌĢ▓
                 Ōöé        ŌĢ▓
                 Ōöé          ŌĢ▓____________ SŌéü (Station 1)
                 Ōöé          ŌĢ▒
                 Ōöé        ŌĢ▒  ŌåÉ S-wave path (curved, slower)
                 Ōöé      ŌĢ▒
                 Ōöé    ŌĢ▒
        ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
```
