Once CM and CR are located, the eccentricity is simply the vector difference:

$$\vec{e} = \vec{r}_{CM} - \vec{r}_{CR}$$

For force in the X-direction, the relevant eccentricity is $e_y$ (perpendicular distance along Y). For force in the Y-direction, the relevant eccentricity is $e_x$ (perpendicular distance along X).

### 2.4 Design Eccentricity (IS 1893:2016, Clause 7.4.3)

The *actual* eccentricity in the structure is not the only consideration. IS 1893:2016 mandates a **Design Eccentricity** that adds an *accidental eccentricity* of $\pm 0.05 \times L$ (where L is the plan dimension perpendicular to the direction of force) to account for:

- Uncertainties in mass distribution.
- Uncertainties in stiffness (cracking, foundation flexibility).
- Rotational component of ground motion not captured in translational spectra.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Eccentricity Formula (IS 1893:2016):**
> $$e_{di} = e_i \pm 0.05 \, L_i$$
> The ┬▒ sign creates two design cases: one where the *additional moment* is *added* to the natural torsion (worst-case) and one where it *subtracts*. The structural designer must verify the building for **both** cases.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass tributary to element | $m_i = w_i \cdot A_i$ | kN┬Ęs┬▓/m | $w_i$ = load intensity, $A_i$ = tributary area |
| X-coordinate of CM | $x_{CM} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | Weighted by gravity load $W_i$ |
| Y-coordinate of CM | $y_{CM} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot y_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | ŌĆö |
| Column lateral stiffness | $k = \frac{12EI}{L^3}$ | kN/m | Far end fixed assumption |
| Shear wall stiffness | $k_w = \frac{EI_w}{L^3}$ | kN/m | Cantilever flexural model |
| X-coordinate of CR | $x_{CR} = \frac{\sum k_{yi} \cdot x_i}{\sum k_{yi}}$ | m | $k_{yi}$ = stiffness in Y-direction |
| Y-coordinate of CR | $y_{CR} = \frac{\sum k_{xi} \cdot y_i}{\sum k_{xi}}$ | m | $k_{xi}$ = stiffness in X-direction |
| Eccentricity (X-dir force) | $e_y = \vert y_{CM} - y_{CR} \vert$ | m | Perpendicular distance |
| Eccentricity (Y-dir force) | $e_x = \vert x_{CM} - x_{CR} \vert$ | m | ŌĆö |
| Design Eccentricity | $e_d = e \pm 0.05 L$ | m | IS 1893:2016, Cl. 7.4.3 |
| Torsional moment at floor | $M_t = V_b \cdot e_d$ | kN┬Ęm | $V_b$ = storey shear |
| Plan aspect ratio check | $L/B \leq 4$ (preferred) | ŌĆö | IS 1893 vertical irregularity limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notations used:** In some textbooks, $k_{xi}$ denotes the lateral stiffness of column $i$ in the X-direction (i.e., bending about Y-axis). The student must clearly distinguish the *direction of bending* from the *direction of force*.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Conceptual Design Stage:** Architects use CM-CR coincidence as a *first-pass design check* to ensure that the structural layout minimises torsion.
- **Code Compliance:** IS 1893:2016 mandates that buildings with torsion eccentricity exceeding 15% of the plan dimension be classified as **Torsionally Irregular** (a Soft/Irregular structure).
- **Retrofit Decisions:** Existing buildings with significant CM-CR offset are candidates for adding shear walls or bracings to *shift the CR back toward the CM*, thereby reducing torsion.
- **3D Modelling Software (ETABS, SAP2000):** Auto-computes CM, CR, and design eccentricities. However, KTU exams expect students to perform the calculation *manually* for simple plans.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coordinate of Centre of Mass

Consider a rigid floor diaphragm with N vertical elements. Let $W_i$ be the gravity load (proportional to mass) at the location of element $i$ with coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$.

By definition, the moment of the entire mass system about the Y-axis must equal the sum of the moments of individual elements:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \cdot x_i = W_{total} \cdot x_{CM}$$

Solving for $x_{CM}$:

$$x_{CM} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i}$$

By the same argument, applying moment equilibrium about the X-axis:

$$y_{CM} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two equations define the *unique* point on the floor where the sum of mass moments vanishes. In structural engineering, $W_i$ is taken as the *tributary gravity load* on each column/wall, not the actual column self-weight, because that is what determines the inertia force distribution under seismic excitation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Coordinate of Centre of Rigidity

For each column $i$, define the *lateral stiffness* in a given direction. For columns with both ends constrained against rotation, the classical stiffness is:

$$k_i = \frac{12 E I_i}{L^3}$$

For a force applied at the floor level in the X-direction, each column $i$ develops a shear $V_i$ proportional to its stiffness and the relative displacement. If the floor is rigid and translates by $\Delta$ and rotates by $\theta$ about the Y-axis, the displacement of column $i$ is:

$$\Delta_i = \Delta - y_i \cdot \theta$$

The shear in column $i$ is:

$$V_i = k_{xi} \cdot \Delta_i = k_{xi} (\Delta - y_i \cdot \theta)$$

For **pure translation** (no rotation, $\theta = 0$), the resultant of the column shears must pass through the CR. The point of application of the resultant is found by taking moments about an arbitrary point (say, the origin).

The moment of the column shears about the origin must equal the moment of the resultant $V_{total}$ applied at the CR:

$$V_{total} \cdot y_{CR} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \cdot y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k_{xi} \cdot y_i \cdot \Delta$$

Since $V_{total} = \sum k_{xi} \cdot \Delta$ for pure translation, this simplifies to:

$$y_{CR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} k_{xi} \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} k_{xi}}$$

A symmetric argument in the X-direction yields:

$$x_{CR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} k_{yi} \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} k_{yi}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of These Formulas:**
> The CR is the *first moment of stiffness* divided by *total stiffness*. It is mathematically analogous to the CM, but with **stiffness** as the weighting factor instead of **mass/weight**. This is the *stiffness-weighted centroid* of the lateral-load-resisting system.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Torsion on Element Forces

When a building with torsional eccentricity is subjected to a lateral seismic force $V_b$ at floor level:

1. **Direct shear** on each column = $\dfrac{k_i}{\sum k_i} \cdot V_b$ (from pure translation).
2. **Torsional moment** induced at the floor = $M_t = V_b \cdot e_d$ (using design eccentricity).
3. **Rotation of floor** about the vertical axis = $\theta = \dfrac{M_t}{\sum k_i \cdot r_i^2}$, where $r_i$ is the distance from CR to column $i$.
4. **Additional shear on column i** due to torsion = $k_i \cdot r_i \cdot \theta$ (sign depends on direction of eccentricity).
5. **Total design shear** on column $i$ = Direct shear + Torsional shear.

> [!WARNING]
> The torsional shear *adds* to the direct shear on one side of the CR and *subtracts* on the other. The most-loaded columns are typically the *corner and edge elements* farthest from the CR. **Edge elements always govern the design** in buildings with significant torsion.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration

Consider a single-storey building with 4 columns located at the corners of a $6\,m \times 4\,m$ plan, plus a shear wall at midspan of one edge. Column properties: $E = 25 \times 10^6$ kN/m┬▓, square section $0.4\,m \times 0.4\,m$, height = 3 m.

| Element | Location (x, y) | $I$ (mŌü┤) | $k_x = 12EI/L^3$ (kN/m) | $k_y = 12EI/L^3$ (kN/m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | (0, 0) | 0.4Ōü┤/12 = 0.002133 | 23,703 | 23,703 |
| C2 | (6, 0) | 0.002133 | 23,703 | 23,703 |
| C3 | (6, 4) | 0.002133 | 23,703 | 23,703 |
| C4 | (0, 4) | 0.002133 | 23,703 | 23,703 |
| W1 | (3, 0) | 0.05 (wall equiv.) | 555,556 | ŌĆö |

**Computation of CR (assuming uniform mass, CM at centre of plan = (3, 2)):**

X-coordinate of CR (using $k_y$ for force in Y-direction):

$$x_{CR} = \frac{(23703)(0) + (23703)(6) + (23703)(6) + (23703)(0) + 0}{4 \times 23703} = 3.0\,m$$

Y-coordinate of CR (using $k_x$, with wall W1 at y = 0):

$$y_{CR} = \frac{(23703)(0) + (23703)(0) + (23703)(4) + (23703)(4) + (555556)(0)}{4 \times 23703 + 555556}$$

$$y_{CR} = \frac{0 + 0 + 94912 + 94912 + 0}{94812 + 555556} = \frac{189824}{650368} \approx 0.292\,m$$

**Eccentricity for force in X-direction:**

$$e_y = \vert y_{CM} - y_{CR} \vert = \vert 2.0 - 0.292 \vert = 1.708\,m$$

**Design eccentricity:**

$$e_{dy} = 1.708 + 0.05 \times 4 = 1.708 + 0.20 = 1.908\,m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows the dramatic effect of a single shear wall on one side: it pulls the CR toward itself, creating a large eccentricity with the centrally located CM. The resulting torsional moment is significant and must be accounted for in the design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CM, CR, and Torsion Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FloorMass[Floor Mass and Gravity Load] --> CM[Centre of Mass CM]
    LateralElements[Columns Shear Walls Braces] --> CR[Centre of Rigidity CR]
    CM --> OffsetCalc[Compute Eccentricity e]
    CR --> OffsetCalc
    OffsetCalc --> AddAccidental[Add Accidental Eccentricity 0.05L]
    AddAccidental --> DesignE[Design Eccentricity ed]
    SeismicForce[Base Shear Vb] --> DirectShear[Direct Translation Shear on Each Element]
    DesignE --> TorsionalMoment[Torsional Moment Mt equals Vb times ed]
    TorsionalMoment --> FloorRotation[Floor Rotation theta about vertical axis]
    FloorRotation --> TorsionalShear[Torsional Shear on Each Element]
    DirectShear --> TotalShear[Total Design Shear on Element]
    TorsionalShear --> TotalShear
    TotalShear --> EdgeDemand[Critical Demand on Edge Elements]
```

### 4.2 Plan Layout Schematic: CM-CR Offset Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FloorPlan[Rectangular Floor Plan 6m by 4m]
        direction LR
        C1[Column C1 at 0,0]
        C2[Column C2 at 6,0]
        C3[Column C3 at 6,4]
        C4[Column C4 at 0,4]
        SW[Shear Wall at 3,0]
        CMp[CM Centroid at 3,2]
        CRp[CR Shifted to 3, 0.29 due to Shear Wall]
        Ecc[Eccentricity e equals 1.71m]
    end
    CMp --- Ecc
    CRp --- Ecc
```

### 4.3 Logical Flow: How a Seismic Force Generates Torsion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Earthquake Ground Motion] --> B[Lateral Inertia Force acts at CM]
    B --> C[Resisting Force Resultant acts at CR]
    C --> D{CM equals CR?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Pure Translation No Torsion]
    D -- No --> F[Couple Forms between CM and CR]
    F --> G[Torsional Moment Mt]
    G --> H[Floor Rotates about vertical axis]
    H --> I[Additional Shear on Edge Elements]
    I --> J[Design Shear increases on one side]
    J --> K[Governs column and wall design]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Matrix: CM vs CR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CMBlock[Centre of Mass]
        CMDef[Definition: Mass weighted centroid of floor]
        CMDep[Depends on: Gravity loads and their distribution]
        CMLoc[Location: Coincides with geometric centre for regular plans]
    end
    subgraph CRBlock[Centre of Rigidity]
        CRDef[Definition: Stiffness weighted centroid of lateral system]
        CRDep[Depends on: Column sizes, wall locations, bracing layout]
        CRLoc[Location: Shifts toward stiffer elements]
    end
    CMBlock <--> Comparison[CM minus CR gives Eccentricity]
    CRBlock <--> Comparison
    Comparison --> Result[Torsional Demand on Structure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> These flowcharts are functional representations ŌĆö not physical drawings ŌĆö that capture the cause-and-effect relationships of seismic torsion. In an actual building plan, CM is a *point* and CR is a *point*; their separation is what matters.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define **Centre of Mass (CM)** and **Centre of Rigidity (CR)** of a building floor diaphragm. How is torsional eccentricity computed from these two quantities?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Centre of Mass (CM)** of a floor is the point through which the resultant of the seismic inertia forces (proportional to the floor's mass distribution) is assumed to act. It is the mass-weighted centroid of all gravity loads tributary to the floor.

The **Centre of Rigidity (CR)** is the point on the floor through which the resultant of the lateral restoring forces of all vertical lateral-load-resisting elements (columns, shear walls) passes when the floor undergoes pure translation without rotation. It is the stiffness-weighted centroid of the lateral system.

The **torsional eccentricity** is the distance between CM and CR: $e = \vert r_{CM} - r_{CR} \vert$. When a lateral force is applied, this offset creates a torsional moment $M_t = V_b \cdot e$ that causes the building to twist in plan. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
What is **accidental eccentricity**? Why is it added to the calculated torsional eccentricity as per IS 1893:2016?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Accidental eccentricity** is an *additional* eccentricity of $\pm 0.05L$ (where $L$ is the plan dimension perpendicular to the direction of seismic force) added to the actual computed CM-CR distance to obtain the **design eccentricity**.

$$e_d = e_{actual} \pm 0.05L$$

It accounts for **uncertainties** in the actual mass and stiffness distributions (e.g., construction tolerances, partition relocation, live load variation, foundation flexibility) and for the rotational component of earthquake ground motion that is not captured in translational design spectra. This ensures a *conservative design* even when the calculated CM and CR nominally coincide. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **X-coordinate** and **Y-coordinate** of the **Centre of Rigidity** of a floor diaphragm. Explain the underlying assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-storey building has a rectangular floor plan of $10\,m \times 6\,m$ with columns of identical lateral stiffness $k = 20{,}000$ kN/m at the four corners and at the midpoints of the longer edges (6 columns total). Locate the **Centre of Mass** and **Centre of Rigidity**. If the design base shear in the X-direction is $V_b = 600$ kN, calculate the **design eccentricity** and the **torsional moment** at the floor level as per IS 1893:2016. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Let the floor have $N$ vertical lateral-load-resisting elements. Let $(x_i, y_i)$ be the coordinates of element $i$ with respect to a chosen origin O. Let $k_{xi}$ and $k_{yi}$ denote the lateral stiffness of element $i$ in the X- and Y-directions respectively.

For a lateral force $F$ applied at the floor in the X-direction, the floor translates by $\Delta_x$ and rotates by $\theta$ about the Y-axis. The displacement of element $i$ in the X-direction is:

$$\Delta_{xi} = \Delta_x + y_i \cdot \theta$$

The shear in element $i$ in the X-direction is:

$$V_{xi} = k_{xi} \cdot \Delta_{xi} = k_{xi} (\Delta_x + y_i \cdot \theta)$$

For the resultant of all element shears to pass through the CR, taking moments about the Y-axis at origin O:

$$F \cdot y_{CR} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_{xi} \cdot y_i = \sum k_{xi} y_i (\Delta_x + y_i \theta)$$

If we consider *pure translation* ($\theta = 0$), then $\Delta_x$ is the same for all elements, and:

$$y_{CR} = \frac{\sum k_{xi} y_i}{\sum k_{xi}}$$

By the same argument, for force in the Y-direction, considering pure translation:

$$x_{CR} = \frac{\sum k_{yi} x_i}{\sum k_{yi}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Floor diaphragm is *rigid in its own plane*.
2. Column far ends are *fixed against rotation*.
3. Columns behave *elastically*.
4. Lateral stiffness is the same in push and pull directions.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Geometry:** Plan is 10 m ├Ś 6 m. Six columns at:
- C1 (0, 0), C2 (5, 0), C3 (10, 0) ŌĆö along the bottom edge
- C4 (0, 6), C5 (5, 6), C6 (10, 6) ŌĆö along the top edge
- All have $k = 20{,}000$ kN/m

**Step 1: Locate CM (assume uniform mass, no special loading).**
The geometric centroid of a 10 ├Ś 6 rectangle is at (5, 3).
$$x_{CM} = 5.0\,m, \quad y_{CM} = 3.0\,m$$

**Step 2: Locate CR.**

Since all columns have the same stiffness and the layout is symmetric about both centre lines, the CR coincides with the geometric centroid.
$$x_{CR} = 5.0\,m, \quad y_{CR} = 3.0\,m$$

**Step 3: Compute eccentricity in X-direction.**

Force is in X-direction, so the relevant eccentricity is $e_y$:
$$e_y = \vert y_{CM} - y_{CR} \vert = 0$$

**Step 4: Compute design eccentricity.**

For a force in the X-direction, the perpendicular dimension is $L = 6$ m (dimension along Y, perpendicular to force).
$$e_{dy} = 0 + 0.05 \times 6 = 0.30\,m$$

**Step 5: Compute torsional moment.**
$$M_t = V_b \cdot e_{dy} = 600 \times 0.30 = 180\,kN \cdot m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight from this problem:**
> Even when the calculated eccentricity is zero (perfectly symmetric plan), IS 1893:2016 still requires the designer to consider accidental eccentricity. This means torsion is **never zero** in seismic design ŌĆö there is *always* a minimum torsional moment to be resisted.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of **torsional eccentricity** in a building. With a neat sketch, describe the three cases of eccentricity: **no torsion**, **positive torsion**, and **negative torsion**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-storey building has a floor plan of $8\,m \times 5\,m$ with 5 columns:
- C1 (0, 0): $k = 30{,}000$ kN/m
- C2 (8, 0): $k = 30{,}000$ kN/m
- C3 (4, 5): $k = 30{,}000$ kN/m
- C4 (0, 5): $k = 30{,}000$ kN/m
- C5 (8, 5): $k = 30{,}000$ kN/m

Assume the CM coincides with the geometric centroid. Determine the **Centre of Rigidity**, the **actual eccentricity** in both directions, and the **design eccentricity** as per IS 1893:2016. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Torsional eccentricity** is the planar distance between the Centre of Mass (CM) and the Centre of Rigidity (CR) of a building floor. It represents the offset that, when a lateral seismic force is applied at CM, causes a *torsional couple* in addition to the direct translation.

The three cases are:

- **No Torsion ($e = 0$):** CM and CR coincide. The lateral force passes through both points simultaneously, and the floor undergoes *pure translation* with no rotation. This is the ideal but rarely achieved case in practice.

- **Positive Torsion:** CR lies on one side of CM (e.g., to the left of CM when force is in +X direction). The couple generates rotation that *adds* to the displacements on the side where the eccentricity points.

- **Negative Torsion:** CR lies on the opposite side of CM, and the rotation direction *reverses* compared to positive torsion. The torsion *subtracts* from displacements on the far side and *adds* to the near side.

[Sketch: rectangular plan with three sub-plans showing CM-CR coincident, CR to one side, and CR to the other side, with arrows indicating the rotational sense.]

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Locate CM (geometric centroid of 8 ├Ś 5 rectangle).**
$$x_{CM} = 4.0\,m, \quad y_{CM} = 2.5\,m$$

**Step 2: Compute total stiffness and moments.**

For force in X-direction, use $k_{xi}$ values:
$$\sum k_{xi} = 5 \times 30{,}000 = 150{,}000\,kN/m$$

Y-coordinate of CR:
$$y_{CR} = \frac{\sum k_{xi} \cdot y_i}{\sum k_{xi}}$$

$$y_{CR} = \frac{30000(0) + 30000(0) + 30000(5) + 30000(5) + 30000(5)}{150000}$$

$$y_{CR} = \frac{0 + 0 + 150000 + 150000 + 150000}{150000} = 3.0\,m$$

For force in Y-direction, use $k_{yi}$ values:
$$\sum k_{yi} = 150{,}000\,kN/m$$

X-coordinate of CR:
$$x_{CR} = \frac{30000(0) + 30000(8) + 30000(4) + 30000(0) + 30000(8)}{150000}$$

$$x_{CR} = \frac{0 + 240000 + 120000 + 0 + 240000}{150000} = 4.0\,m$$

So $x_{CR} = 4.0\,m$ and $y_{CR} = 3.0\,m$.

**Step 3: Compute actual eccentricities.**

- For force in X-direction: $e_y = \vert 2.5 - 3.0 \vert = 0.5\,m$
- For force in Y-direction: $e_x = \vert 4.0 - 4.0 \vert = 0\,m$

**Step 4: Compute design eccentricities (IS 1893:2016).**

For force in X-direction (perpendicular dimension $L = 5$ m):
$$e_{dy} = 0.5 + 0.05 \times 5 = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75\,m$$

For force in Y-direction (perpendicular dimension $L = 8$ m):
$$e_{dx} = 0 + 0.05 \times 8 = 0.40\,m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:**
> The plan is geometrically symmetric about X = 4, hence $e_x = 0$. However, the additional column on the Y = 5 line pulls the CR to y = 3 (instead of the centroid y = 2.5), creating a real eccentricity in Y. The design eccentricity is further amplified by the *accidental eccentricity* of IS 1893.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing direction of stiffness with direction of force:** The stiffness $k_{xi}$ refers to bending about the *Y-axis* (force applied in X-direction). Mixing this up with $k_{yi}$ leads to swapped CR coordinates and zero marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the accidental eccentricity:** Many students compute only the actual CM-CR distance and ignore the $\pm 0.05L$ term, losing 2-3 marks per problem.
>
> 3. **Using only the $\pm$ sign without considering both cases:** The code requires the structure to be safe for BOTH ($e + 0.05L$) and ($e - 0.05L$). Most students only check the worst case.
>
> 4. **Assuming CM is at the geometric centroid for irregular mass layouts:** When the problem statement specifies non-uniform loading (e.g., heavy tanks on one side), a true mass-weighted calculation is mandatory.
>
> 5. **Not stating the rigid diaphragm assumption:** The CM, CR, and eccentricity concepts are valid only under this assumption. Stating it explicitly earns a method mark.
>
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in summation:** With multiple columns, the weighted sum $\sum k_i x_i$ and $\sum k_i y_i$ must be tabulated clearly. Skipping the table costs process marks.
>
> 7. **Mixing units:** Eccentricity must be in metres; stiffness in kN/m. Inconsistency costs marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CM** = Mass-weighted centroid = where inertia force acts. **CR** = Stiffness-weighted centroid = where resistance force resultant acts.
- The **rigid floor diaphragm assumption** is the foundation of all CM-CR calculations.
- **Torsional eccentricity** $e = \vert r_{CM} - r_{CR} \vert$ is a *vector distance* in the floor plane.
- **Design eccentricity** $e_d = e \pm 0.05L$ per IS 1893:2016, Clause 7.4.3 ŌĆö must consider both signs.
- The **accidental eccentricity** accounts for uncertainties in mass, stiffness, and rotational ground motion.
- A building with **zero calculated eccentricity** still has torsion due to the $0.05L$ accidental term.
- **Edge and corner elements** carry the highest shear in torsionally irregular buildings.
- The column lateral stiffness formula $k = 12EI/L^3$ assumes *fixed far end*; for pinned far end, $k = 3EI/L^3$.
- **Stiffness direction matters:** $k_{xi}$ is the stiffness *opposing* motion in X-direction, which involves bending about the Y-axis.
- A **torsionally irregular building** has $e > 0.15 \times L$ (or $0.30 \times L$ for highly irregular, per IS 1893 vertical irregularity clause).
- **Architectural design priority:** Lay out shear walls and bracing *symmetrically* about the geometric centroid to bring CR close to CM.
- **Software outputs (ETABS, SAP2000)** auto-compute CM, CR, and design eccentricities ŌĆö but understanding the manual calculation is essential for KTU exams and for verifying software outputs.
- The **rotational stiffness** of the floor $K_\theta = \sum k_i r_i^2$ governs the magnitude of twist per unit torsional moment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Seismic Demand on Buildings - Seismic Coefficient Method - Estimation of Base Shear and Its Distribution Along Height Based on Equivalent Static Method Using IS 1893 for Multi Storied Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Estimation of Seismic Demand on Buildings - Seismic Coefficient Method - Estimation of Base Shear and Its Distribution Along Height Based on Equivalent Static Method Using IS 1893 for Multi Storied Buildings # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 1893 : 2016 ŌĆō Part 1 Terminology) The **Equivalent Static Method (ESM)**, also called the **Seismic Coefficient Method**, is a codified pseudo-static procedure in which the dynamic effect of a design-basis earthquake is replaced by a set of equivalent **static lateral forces** applied at the floor levels of the building. The magnitude of these forces is calibrated so that, when applied statically, they produce the same base shear and inter-storey drifts as the actual dynamic ground motion would, on a linearly elastic model of the structure. As per **Clause 7.5 of IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016**, the Equivalent Static Method may be used for the design of **regular buildings** of height **Ōēż 15 m in Zone II**, **Ōēż 12 m in Zone III**, and **Ōēż 9 m in Zone IV & V** (with some relaxations for industrial frames), provided the building is **regular in plan and elevation**. For all other cases, a **Response Spectrum Analysis** (Module 4) or **Time History Analysis** is mandated. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆō Module 3:** > The board examiner expects the student to clearly articulate *when* ESM is valid, the steps to compute **V_B (Design Base Shear)**, and the **vertical distribution** of V_B along the building height using the *Coded Empirical Formula* of IS 1893. ## 1.2 The Core Design Philosophy The method rests on three codified "lenses" through which an earthquake is viewed: 1. **Seismic Zone (Z-factor)** ŌĆō captures *where* the structure is located. 2. **Site Soil Class (Spectral Shape, Sa/g)** ŌĆō captures *what the foundation sits on*. 3. **Structural System (R-factor)** ŌĆō captures *how ductile the building is* (capacity to dissipate energy). The design philosophy is **Capacity Design**: We allow inelastic deformations, but we design the structure so that inelastic action is restricted to **predefined ductile regions** (beams, base of columns), while the rest (columns, joints, foundations) remain essentially elastic. The **Response Reduction Factor (R)** is the numerical embodiment of this philosophy. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆō "The Hydraulic Shove" Imagine your building standing in a giant hall. Now imagine an invisible hydraulic ram, mounted at the roof, that very slowly pushes the building sideways. It pushes with exactly the right amount of force so that the building deflects at the roof the same amount as it would during the actual earthquake. | Real Earthquake | Equivalent Static Push | |---|---| | Shaking at high frequency | Slow, smooth force | | Dynamic inertia (m ├Ś a) | Static force at each floor | | Energy dissipated by ductility | Reduced by factor R | | Different shaking in every floor | One single force at each floor level | The total "shove" needed at the **base** is the **Design Base Shear V_B**, and we distribute it floor-by-floor using the **parabolic height-distribution formula** (which mimics the *first-mode deflected shape* of a cantilever-type building). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition:** ESM is *not* a "static" analysis in the absolute sense ŌĆō it is a *static representation of a dynamic problem*. The dynamics have been **pre-baked** into the code-mandated spectrum (Sa/g vs. T curve). # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Operational Sequence (Five Logical Steps) The Equivalent Static Method is executed in **five sequential stages**. Master this sequence; every KTU problem follows it. ### Step 1 ŌåÆ Compute the Fundamental Natural Period (T) The time period is the first dynamic property required. For a **Reinforced Concrete Moment-Resisting Frame (RC-MRF)** with *no brick infills*: $$T_a = 0.075 \, h^{0.75} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$ For **RC-MRF with brick infills**: $$T_a = \frac{0.09 \, h}{\sqrt{d}} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$ For **Steel Moment-Resisting Frames (SMRF)**: $$T_a = 0.085 \, h^{0.75} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$ Where: - $h$ = **height of building in metres** (excluding the basement, if basement walls are connected to soil on all sides). - $d$ = **base dimension of the building at the plinth level, along the considered direction of vibration**, in metres. > [!NOTE] > These empirical formulas assume **impracticality of rigorous dynamic analysis** and are valid for **low-to-mid-rise regular buildings** only. The computed T is a *design* value, not the *true* period. ### Step 2 ŌåÆ Read the Spectral Acceleration Coefficient (Sa/g) from IS 1893 Spectrum Once T is known, we look up **Sa/g** for the appropriate **Soil Type** at **5% damping** (default for all buildings). ### Step 3 ŌåÆ Compute the Design Horizontal Acceleration Coefficient (A_h) This is the **heart of the Seismic Coefficient Method**: $$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$ Where: - $Z$ = **Zone Factor** (dimensionless, from Table 2 of IS 1893) - $I$ = **Importance Factor** (dimensionless, from Table 6 of IS 1893) - $R$ = **Response Reduction Factor** (dimensionless, from Table 7 of IS 1893) - $S_a/g$ = **Spectral Acceleration Coefficient** (dimensionless) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Meaning of Z/2:** The factor ┬Į converts the *Maximum Considered Earthquake (MCE)* spectrum to the *Design Basis Earthquake (DBE)* spectrum. The IS 1893:2016 spectrum is on the MCE scale; design is performed at DBE level (return period ~475 years for Zone V ŌåÆ 2475/2 Ōēł 1.25 ├Ś Z in older code, but the 2016 code has restructured this). ### Step 4 ŌåÆ Compute the Design Seismic Weight (W) For buildings, **W is the total dead load + appropriate percentage of live load** (Clause 7.3 of IS 1893). For **residential / office buildings**: Live Load (LL) is taken as **25% of the Floor Live Load** for the purpose of seismic weight computation, *provided it exceeds 3 kN/m┬▓*. (Storage buildings: 50%; roof: 0% LL; but roof-mounted equipment/water tanks must be included.) $$W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ D_i + L_{fi} \right]$$ Where $D_i$ is the dead load of floor $i$ and $L_{fi}$ is the *fractional live load* as above. ### Step 5 ŌåÆ Compute the Design Base Shear (V_B) and Distribute Vertically The **Design Base Shear** is the global horizontal force at the base: $$V_B = A_h \cdot W$$ The **vertical distribution** of V_B to each floor $i$ follows the *code-prescribed first-mode shape*: $$Q_i = V_B \cdot \frac{W_i \, h_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \, h_j^2}$$ Where: - $Q_i$ = **Design lateral force at floor $i$** - $W_i$ = **Seismic weight of floor $i$** - $h_i$ = **Height of floor $i$ measured from the base** A small correction: **5% of V_B is applied at the top-most roof level** (concentrated at top) per the code, to simulate higher-mode effects, but this is a minor detail often dropped in textbook problems. ## 2.2 The Spectrum (Sa/g vs. T) ŌĆō Table as per IS 1893 : 2016 | Soil Type | Range of T | Expression for Sa/g (5% damping) | |---|---|---| | **I (Rock / Hard Soil)** | $0.00 \le T \le 0.10$ | $1 + 15T$ (linear from 1.0 to 2.5) | | **I (Rock / Hard Soil)** | $0.10 \le T \le 0.40$ | $2.5$ (constant plateau) | | **I (Rock / Hard Soil)** | $0.40 \le T \le 4.00$ | $1.00/T$ (descending branch) | | **II (Medium Soil)** | $0.00 \le T \le 0.10$ | $1 + 15T$ | | **II (Medium Soil)** | $0.10 \le T \le 0.55$ | $2.5$ (constant plateau) | | **II (Medium Soil)** | $0.55 \le T \le 4.00$ | $1.36/T$ | | **III (Soft Soil)** | $0.00 \le T \le 0.10$ | $1 + 15T$ | | **III (Soft Soil)** | $0.10 \le T \le 0.67$ | $2.5$ | | **III (Soft Soil)** | $0.67 \le T \le 4.00$ | $1.67/T$ | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Consolidated Cheat-Sheet) | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Source | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Design Base Shear | $V_B$ | $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ | kN | | 2 | Horizontal Acceleration Coeff. | $A_h$ | $A_h = (Z/2)(I/R)(S_a/g)$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Spectral Acceleration (Hard Soil) | $S_a/g$ | $2.5$ for $0.10 \le T \le 0.40$; else from table | dimensionless | | 4 | Spectral Acceleration (Medium Soil) | $S_a/g$ | $2.5$ for $0.10 \le T \le 0.55$; $1.36/T$ after | dimensionless | | 5 | Spectral Acceleration (Soft Soil) | $S_a/g$ | $2.5$ for $0.10 \le T \le 0.67$; $1.67/T$ after | dimensionless | | 6 | Empirical Period (RC MRF no infill) | $T_a$ | $0.075 \cdot h^{0.75}$ | seconds | | 7 | Empirical Period (RC MRF with infill) | $T_a$ | $0.09 h / \sqrt{d}$ | seconds | | 8 | Empirical Period (Steel MRF) | $T_a$ | $0.085 \cdot h^{0.75}$ | seconds | | 9 | Vertical Distribution (floor $i$) | $Q_i$ | $Q_i = V_B \cdot (W_i h_i^2) / \sum W_j h_j^2$ | kN | | 10 | Seismic Weight per Floor | $W_i$ | $D_i + \alpha L_i$ ($\alpha = 0.25$ for residential) | kN | | 11 | Zone Factor (Z) | $Z$ | II = 0.10, III = 0.16, IV = 0.24, V = 0.36 | dimensionless | | 12 | Importance Factor (I) | $I$ | 1.0 (normal) / 1.2 (residential w/ > 200 occupancy) / 1.5 (essential) / 1.75 (post-earthquake functional) | dimensionless | | 13 | Response Reduction Factor (R) | $R$ | RC Ordinary = 3.0; RC Special = 5.0; SMRF = 5.0; BRBF = 4.0; etc. | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** ESM is the *workhorse method* used in 90% of low-to-mid-rise (Ōēż 15 m) residential, commercial, and industrial design in India. Practising structural engineers use it almost daily via software like **ETABS, SAP2000, STAAD.Pro** for quick preliminary sizing. The 14-mark KTU problems are direct applications of this table. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU 14-Mark Style Problem) ### **Problem Statement (Model Question)** A **four-storeyed (G+3) reinforced concrete office building** is located in **Zone IV** on **medium soil (Soil Type II)**. The building has a **plan dimension of 25 m ├Ś 15 m** with a **storey height of 3.5 m** (ground floor 4.0 m, plinth 1.0 m above base). The structural system is a **Special Moment-Resisting Frame (SMRF)**. The total dead load on each floor (including columns, walls, finishes) is **12 kN/m┬▓**, and the **floor live load is 4 kN/m┬▓**. The **roof live load is 1.5 kN/m┬▓**. The building is a *standard occupancy office* (Importance Factor 1.0). Compute: 1. The **fundamental natural period** (T). 2. The **spectral acceleration coefficient** (Sa/g). 3. The **design horizontal acceleration coefficient** (A_h). 4. The **total design base shear** (V_B). 5. The **distribution of base shear along the height** (Q_i for each floor). ### **Solution ŌĆō Stage 1: Geometry & Loading** **Total height** (from base to roof, excluding basement): $$h = 1.0 \, (\text{plinth}) + 4.0 \, (\text{GF}) + 3 \times 3.5 \, (\text{typical floors}) = 15.5 \, \text{m}$$ **Floor Areas (assuming similar at every level):** $$A_{\text{floor}} = 25 \times 15 = 375 \, \text{m}^2$$ **Seismic Weight of each Typical Floor (Floor 1, 2, 3):** $$W_{\text{typical}} = D \cdot A + 0.25 \cdot L \cdot A$$ $$W_{\text{typical}} = (12 \times 375) + (0.25 \times 4 \times 375) = 4500 + 375 = 4875 \, \text{kN}$$ **Seismic Weight of Roof (Floor 4):** $$W_{\text{roof}} = D \cdot A + 0 \cdot L \cdot A = 4500 + 0 = 4500 \, \text{kN}$$ > **Justification:** Roof Live Load (1.5 kN/m┬▓) is *not* considered for seismic weight as per IS 1893:2016 Clause 7.3.1. **Total Seismic Weight:** $$W = 3 \times 4875 + 4500 = 14625 + 4500 = 19125 \, \text{kN}$$ ### **Solution ŌĆō Stage 2: Fundamental Natural Period** Since it is a **RC Special Moment Resisting Frame (SMRF)** without specific mention of brick infill: $$T_a = 0.075 \, h^{0.75}$$ Calculating $h^{0.75}$ for $h = 15.5 \, \text{m}$: $$15.5^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 15.5} = e^{0.75 \times 2.7408} = e^{2.0556} = 7.81$$ $$T_a = 0.075 \times 7.81 = 0.586 \, \text{s}$$ ### **Solution ŌĆō Stage 3: Spectral Acceleration Coefficient (Sa/g)** For **Soil Type II (Medium Soil)** and $T = 0.586 \, \text{s}$: Check ranges: - $0.10 \le T \le 0.55$? ŌåÆ **No, 0.586 > 0.55** - $0.55 \le T \le 4.00$? ŌåÆ **Yes, falling in the velocity-controlled region** Therefore: $$\frac{S_a}{g} = \frac{1.36}{T} = \frac{1.36}{0.586} = 2.321$$ ### **Solution ŌĆō Stage 4: Design Horizontal Acceleration Coefficient (A_h)** Parameters (from code tables): - Zone IV $\Rightarrow Z = 0.24$ - Standard office occupancy $\Rightarrow I = 1.0$ - RC SMRF $\Rightarrow R = 5.0$ $$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g} = \frac{0.24}{2} \cdot \frac{1.0}{5.0} \cdot 2.321$$ Step-by-step simplification: $$A_h = 0.12 \times 0.2 \times 2.321$$ $$A_h = 0.024 \times 2.321$$ $$A_h = 0.0557$$ ### **Solution ŌĆō Stage 5: Total Design Base Shear (V_B)** $$V_B = A_h \cdot W = 0.0557 \times 19125 = 1065.2 \, \text{kN}$$ ### **Solution ŌĆō Stage 6: Vertical Distribution of Base Shear** **Heights of floors measured from the base:** - Floor 1 (Ground Floor) : $h_1 = 1.0 + 4.0 = 5.0 \, \text{m}$ - Floor 2 (1st Floor) : $h_2 = 5.0 + 3.5 = 8.5 \, \text{m}$ - Floor 3 (2nd Floor) : $h_3 = 8.5 + 3.5 = 12.0 \, \text{m}$ - Floor 4 (Roof) : $h_4 = 12.0 + 3.5 = 15.5 \, \text{m}$ **Distribution Formula:** $$Q_i = V_B \cdot \frac{W_i h_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{4} W_j h_j^2}$$ **Denominator Computation $\sum W_i h_i^2$:** $$S = W_1 h_1^2 + W_2 h_2^2 + W_3 h_3^2 + W_4 h_4^2$$ $$S = (4875)(5.0)^2 + (4875)(8.5)^2 + (4875)(12.0)^2 + (4500)(15.5)^2$$ Term-by-term: - $4875 \times 25 = 121875$ - $4875 \times 72.25 = 352218.75$ - $4875 \times 144 = 702000$ - $4500 \times 240.25 = 1081125$ $$S = 121875 + 352218.75 + 702000 + 1081125 = 2257218.75 \, \text{kN┬Ęm}^2$$ **Numerator Computations (per floor):** | Floor | $W_i$ (kN) | $h_i$ (m) | $W_i h_i^2$ (kN┬Ęm┬▓) | $Q_i = V_B \times \frac{W_i h_i^2}{S}$ (kN) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 (GF) | 4875 | 5.0 | 121,875 | $1065.2 \times 121875 / 2257218.75 = \mathbf{57.5}$ | | 2 (1F) | 4875 | 8.5 | 352,218.75 | $1065.2 \times 352218.75 / 2257218.75 = \mathbf{166.2}$ | | 3 (2F) | 4875 | 12.0 | 702,000 | $1065.2 \times 702000 / 2257218.75 = \mathbf{331.3}$ | | 4 (Roof) | 4500 | 15.5 | 1,081,125 | $1065.2 \times 1081125 / 2257218.75 = \mathbf{510.2}$ | | **Total** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | 2,257,218.75 | **$\Sigma Q_i = 1065.2$ kN** Ō£ō | **Verification:** The sum of $Q_i$ equals $V_B$ (1065.2 kN) ŌĆō the algebra is consistent. ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆō How to Choose Formulae | Question in Problem | Look-up / Decision | |---|---| | Frame type? | "SMRF" ŌåÆ $R = 5.0$; "OMRF" ŌåÆ $R = 3.0$; "Braced Frame" ŌåÆ $R = 4.0$ | | Brick infill? | "with brick infill" ŌåÆ use $0.09h/\sqrt{d}$; else ŌåÆ $0.075 h^{0.75}$ | | Soil type? | "rock/hard" ŌåÆ Type I; "medium" ŌåÆ Type II; "soft" ŌåÆ Type III | | Building zone? | II/III/IV/V ŌåÆ Z = 0.10/0.16/0.24/0.36 | | Live load fraction? | Residential/Office ŌåÆ 0.25; Warehouse ŌåÆ 0.50; Roof ŌåÆ 0.00 | | Period range? | $T$ vs. 0.10, 0.40/0.55/0.67, 4.00 ŌåÆ which branch of spectrum | # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆō Equivalent Static Method Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Given Building Geometry and Loads"] --> B["Compute Heights h_i of each floor from base"] B --> C["Compute Seismic Weights W_i = D_i + 0.25 L_i per floor"] C --> D["Compute Total W = sum of W_i"] D --> E["Identify Soil Type I, II or III and Zone II to V"] E --> F["Compute T using empirical formula based on frame type"] F --> G["Read Sa_g from IS 1893 spectrum using T and Soil Type"] G --> H["Identify Z from zone, I from occupancy, R from frame type"] H --> I["Compute A_h = Z/2 x I/R x Sa_g"] I --> J["Compute V_B = A_h x W"] J --> K["Compute distribution factor W_i x h_i_sq for each floor"] K --> L["Compute Q_i = V_B x W_i h_i_sq / sum W_j h_j_sq"] L --> M["Apply Q_i laterally at each floor as static load"] M --> N["Analyse frame for axial, shear, bending"] N --> O["Design members using capacity design principles"] O --> P["END: Final Member Sizes and Detailing"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆō Components of the Design Base Shear ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs["INPUTS"] Z["Z: Zone Factor
0.10 to 0.36"] I["I: Importance Factor
1.0 to 1.75"] R["R: Response Reduction
3.0 to 5.0"] T["T: Natural Period
from empirical formula"] ST["Soil Type I/II/III
for spectrum"] end subgraph Spectrum["CODE SPECTRUM"] SS["Sa_g vs T Curve
IS 1893:2016"] end subgraph Core["CORE COMPUTATION"] AH["A_h = Z/2 x I/R x Sa_g"] VB["V_B = A_h x W"] end W["W: Seismic Weight
Dead + fractional Live"] Z --> AH I --> AH R --> AH T --> SS ST --> SS SS --> AH AH --> VB W --> VB ``` ## 4.3 Schematic ŌĆō Distribution of Lateral Force Along Height ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Build["MULTI-STOREY BUILDING - LATERAL FORCE DISTRIBUTION"] direction TB R["ROOF: Q_4 = 510.2 kN"] L3["FLOOR 3: Q_3 = 331.3 kN"] L2["FLOOR 2: Q_2 = 166.2 kN"] L1["FLOOR 1: Q_1 = 57.5 kN"] BS["BASE SHEAR V_B = 1065.2 kN"] R --- L3 --- L2 --- L1 --- BS end Note["Note: Forces increase parabolically with height
following the first-mode shape"] -.-> R ``` ## 4.4 Logical Architecture of the Equivalent Static Method ```mermaid flowchart TD M1["Module 1: Seismology Background"] --> M2["Module 2: Dynamic Properties of Structures"] M2 --> M3A["Module 3.1: Seismic Coefficient Method"] M3A --> M3B["Module 3.2: Base Shear V_B Computation"] M3B --> M3C["Module 3.3: Vertical Distribution Q_i"] M3C --> M3D["Module 3.4: Application to RC Frames"] M3D --> M4["Module 4: Response Spectrum Method"] M3D --> M5["Module 5: Ductile Detailing as per IS 13920"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, 3 Marks] **State the conditions under which the Equivalent Static Method (Seismic Coefficient Method) of analysis as per IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016 is permitted for a building.** **Model Answer:** The Equivalent Static Method may be used for buildings satisfying **all** of the following conditions (Clause 7.5, IS 1893:2016): 1. The building is **regular in plan and elevation**. 2. The height is **Ōēż 15 m in Zone II, Ōēż 12 m in Zone III, and Ōēż 9 m in Zones IV and V**. 3. The fundamental natural period $T$ is **Ōēż 0.4 s in Zones II & III**, and **Ōēż 0.3 s in Zones IV & V**. 4. There are **no plan or vertical irregularities of severe nature** (e.g., soft storey, mass irregularity, geometric irregularity). 5. The lateral load resisting system is clearly identifiable and continuous from foundation to roof. If **any** condition is violated, the code mandates **Response Spectrum Analysis** or **Time History Analysis**. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, 3 Marks] **Explain the physical significance of the 'Response Reduction Factor (R)' used in the Equivalent Static Method.** **Model Answer:** The Response Reduction Factor (R) is a numerical representation of the **inherent ductility and energy dissipation capacity** of a lateral-load resisting structural system. It captures three physical realities: 1. **Ductility ($\mu$):** The ratio of ultimate deformation to yield deformation. A ductile structure can undergo large inelastic deformations without collapse. 2. **Overstrength ($\Omega_0$):** The ratio of actual yield strength to the design strength (due to material overstrength, member strain hardening, redundancy). 3. **Damping (╬Ę):** The energy dissipated through inelastic action, friction, and micro-cracking during cyclic loading. Mathematically: $R = \mu \cdot \Omega_0 \cdot \eta$ **Practical values** (IS 1893:2016 Table 7): - Ordinary Moment Resisting Frame (OMRF, RC): $R = 3.0$ - Special Moment Resisting Frame (SMRF, RC): $R = 5.0$ - Steel SMRF: $R = 5.0$ - Buckling-Restrained Braced Frame: $R = 4.0$ A **higher R** indicates a more ductile system, leading to **lower design base shear** and **more economical** but **more detailing-intensive** design. --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS) ŌĆō INTERNAL CHOICE ### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆō **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, 14 Marks]** **A five-storeyed (G+4) reinforced concrete office building is situated in Zone V on medium soil. The building has plan dimensions 20 m ├Ś 12 m with a plinth height of 1.0 m, ground floor height 4.0 m, and typical floor height 3.0 m. The structural system is a Special Moment Resisting Frame. Dead load on each floor is 14 kN/m┬▓ and floor live load is 3 kN/m┬▓. The roof live load is 1.5 kN/m┬▓. Take Importance Factor I = 1.0.** **(a) Compute the fundamental natural period T and the design horizontal acceleration coefficient A_h.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Calculate the total design base shear V_B and distribute it along the height of the building to find the lateral force at each floor level.** **(7 marks)** #### **Solution to Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Total Height** $$h = 1.0 + 4.0 + 4 \times 3.0 = 17.0 \, \text{m}$$ **Step 2: Natural Period** (RC SMRF, no infill) $$T_a = 0.075 \, h^{0.75} = 0.075 \times 17.0^{0.75}$$ Computing $17.0^{0.75}$: $$17.0^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 17} = e^{0.75 \times 2.833} = e^{2.125} = 8.376$$ $$T_a = 0.075 \times 8.376 = 0.628 \, \text{s}$$ **Step 3: Spectral Acceleration** (Medium Soil, $T = 0.628$ s, falls in 0.55 to 4.0 range) $$\frac{S_a}{g} = \frac{1.36}{T} = \frac{1.36}{0.628} = 2.166$$ **Step 4: Design Horizontal Acceleration Coefficient** (Zone V ŌåÆ Z = 0.36, I = 1.0, R = 5.0 for SMRF) $$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g} = \frac{0.36}{2} \cdot \frac{1.0}{5.0} \cdot 2.166$$ $$A_h = 0.18 \times 0.2 \times 2.166 = 0.0779$$ #### **Solution to Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Seismic Weights** $$A_{\text{floor}} = 20 \times 12 = 240 \, \text{m}^2$$ For each typical floor (Floors 1 to 4): $$W_{\text{typical}} = 14 \times 240 + 0.25 \times 3 \times 240 = 3360 + 180 = 3540 \, \text{kN}$$ For the roof (Floor 5): $$W_{\text{roof}} = 14 \times 240 + 0 = 3360 \, \text{kN}$$ Total: $W = 4 \times 3540 + 3360 = 14160 + 3360 = 17520 \, \text{kN}$ **Step 2: Design Base Shear** $$V_B = A_h \cdot W = 0.0779 \times 17520 = 1364.8 \, \text{kN}$$ **Step 3: Heights of Floors from Base** - Floor 1 (GF): $h_1 = 1.0 + 4.0 = 5.0 \, \text{m}$ - Floor 2: $h_2 = 5.0 + 3.0 = 8.0 \, \text{m}$ - Floor 3: $h_3 = 8.0 + 3.0 = 11.0 \, \text{m}$ - Floor 4: $h_4 = 11.0 + 3.0 = 14.0 \, \text{m}$ - Floor 5 (Roof): $h_5 = 14.0 + 3.0 = 17.0 \, \text{m}$ **Step 4: Distribution** | Floor | $W_i$ (kN) | $h_i$ (m) | $W_i h_i^2$ (kN┬Ęm┬▓) | $Q_i$ (kN) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 3540 | 5.0 | 88,500 | $1364.8 \times 88500 / S$ | | 2 | 3540 | 8.0 | 226,560 | $1364.8 \times 226560 / S$ | | 3 | 3540 | 11.0 | 428,340 | $1364.8 \times 428340 / S$ | | 4 | 3540 | 14.0 | 693,840 | $1364.8 \times 693840 / S$ | | 5 | 3360 | 17.0 | 971,040 | $1364.8 \times 971040 / S$ | | **Total $S$** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **2,408,280** | ŌĆö | Computing $S = \sum W_i h_i^2$: $$S = 88500 + 226560 + 428340 + 693840 + 971040 = 2,408,280 \, \text{kN┬Ęm}^2$$ Distribution factors: - $Q_1 = 1364.8 \times 88500 / 2408280 = \mathbf{50.2 \, kN}$ - $Q_2 = 1364.8 \times 226560 / 2408280 = \mathbf{128.4 \, kN}$ - $Q_3 = 1364.8 \times 428340 / 2408280 = \mathbf{242.8 \, kN}$ - $Q_4 = 1364.8 \times 693840 / 2408280 = \mathbf{393.1 \, kN}$ - $Q_5 = 1364.8 \times 971040 / 2408280 = \mathbf{550.3 \, kN}$ **Sum check:** $50.2 + 128.4 + 242.8 + 393.1 + 550.3 = 1364.8 \, \text{kN} = V_B$ Ō£ō --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆō **[Alternative Question, 14 Marks]** **(a) Derive an expression for the design horizontal acceleration coefficient (A_h) used in the Equivalent Static Method as per IS 1893. Explain each term clearly.** **(7 marks)** **(b) For a three-storeyed RCC hospital building in Zone IV on soft soil, with the following data, calculate the design base shear and its distribution along the height. Building plan: 30 m ├Ś 18 m. Plinth height = 1.2 m, storey height = 3.5 m each. Dead load on floors = 15 kN/m┬▓, live load on floors = 5 kN/m┬▓. Frame type: SMRF. Importance factor for hospital = 1.5.** **(7 marks)** #### **Solution to Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** **Definition of A_h:** The design horizontal seismic coefficient $A_h$ represents the fraction of gravity (weight) that, when applied as a static horizontal force, simulates the design earthquake's effect on the structure. **Expression:** $$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$ **Explanation of Terms:** 1. **Z (Zone Factor):** Represents the intensity of design earthquake at the building's location. Defined for the Maximum Considered Earthquake (MCE) and reflects Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA). Values: Zone II = 0.10, III = 0.16, IV = 0.24, V = 0.36. 2. **┬Į (Factor):** Converts the MCE-based spectrum to the Design Basis Earthquake (DBE) spectrum ŌĆō design earthquakes have a return period of about 475 years. 3. **I (Importance Factor):** Importance multiplier based on the functional use of the building. A hospital (essential services) has $I = 1.5$; a critical emergency building has $I = 1.75$; ordinary residential/office has $I = 1.0$ or $1.2$. 4. **R (Response Reduction Factor):** A measure of the structure's ability to dissipate energy through inelastic deformation. SMRF = 5.0; OMRF = 3.0. Higher R ŌåÆ more ductility assumed ŌåÆ smaller design force. 5. **$S_a/g$ (Spectral Acceleration Coefficient):** The ratio of maximum pseudo-acceleration response of a SDOF oscillator to gravity acceleration, for a given period T and soil type, at 5% damping. Read from the code spectrum. **Final Base Shear Equation:** $$V_B = A_h \cdot W$$ where $W$ = total seismic weight (sum of dead load + appropriate live load fraction). #### **Solution to Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Total Height** $$h = 1.2 + 3 \times 3.5 = 11.7 \, \text{m}$$ **Step 2: Natural Period** (SMRF, no infill) $$T = 0.075 \times 11.7^{0.75} = 0.075 \times e^{0.75 \ln 11.7} = 0.075 \times 6.59 = 0.494 \, \text{s}$$ **Step 3: Sa/g for Soft Soil (Type III), T = 0.494 s** For Soil Type III, plateau ends at T = 0.67. Since 0.494 < 0.67: $$\frac{S_a}{g} = 2.5$$ **Step 4: A_h** (Zone IV ŌåÆ Z = 0.24, I = 1.5, R = 5.0) $$A_h = \frac{0.24}{2} \times \frac{1.5}{5.0} \times 2.5 = 0.12 \times 0.3 \times 2.5 = 0.09$$ **Step 5: Seismic Weights** $$A_{\text{floor}} = 30 \times 18 = 540 \, \text{m}^2$$ $$W_{\text{typical}} = 15 \times 540 + 0.25 \times 5 \times 540 = 8100 + 675 = 8775 \, \text{kN}$$ $$W_{\text{roof}} = 15 \times 540 + 0 = 8100 \, \text{kN}$$ $$W = 2 \times 8775 + 8100 = 25650 \, \text{kN}$$ **Step 6: Base Shear** $$V_B = 0.09 \times 25650 = 2308.5 \, \text{kN}$$ **Step 7: Heights and Distribution** - $h_1 = 1.2 + 3.5 = 4.7 \, \text{m}$ - $h_2 = 4.7 + 3.5 = 8.2 \, \text{m}$ - $h_3 = 8.2 + 3.5 = 11.7 \, \text{m}$ | Floor | $W_i$ (kN) | $h_i$ (m) | $W_i h_i^2$ (kN┬Ęm┬▓) | $Q_i$ (kN) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 8775 | 4.7 | 193,832 | 2308.5 ├Ś 193832 / S | | 2 | 8775 | 8.2 | 590,103 | 2308.5 ├Ś 590103 / S | | 3 (Roof) | 8100 | 11.7 | 1,108,890 | 2308.5 ├Ś 1108890 / S | $$S = 193832 + 590103 + 1108890 = 1,892,825 \, \text{kN┬Ęm}^2$$ - $Q_1 = 2308.5 \times 193832 / 1892825 = \mathbf{236.4 \, kN}$ - $Q_2 = 2308.5 \times 590103 / 1892825 = \mathbf{719.7 \, kN}$ - $Q_3 = 2308.5 \times 1108890 / 1892825 = \mathbf{1352.4 \, kN}$ **Sum check:** $236.4 + 719.7 + 1352.4 = 2308.5 \, \text{kN} = V_B$ Ō£ō --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor ┬Į in A_h formula** ŌĆō This is the #1 mistake. Always write $Z/2$, never just $Z$. Loss: **2 marks minimum**. > 2. **Confusing Z values** ŌĆō Zone II = 0.10 (not 0.20), Zone V = 0.36 (not 0.40). Get this right or lose 1 mark. > 3. **Wrong live load fraction** ŌĆō Residential/Office = 25%, Storage = 50%, Roof = 0%. Mixing these up causes wrong W ŌåÆ wrong V_B. Loss: **2 marks**. > 4. **Wrong empirical period formula** ŌĆō "Brick infill" ŌåÆ $0.09 h/\sqrt{d}$, else ŌåÆ $0.075 h^{0.75}$. Examiners specifically test if you read carefully. > 5. **Forgetting to include **both** the dead load and the *fractional* live load** in W. Some students write only DL. > 6. **Computing heights from ground level instead of from base** ŌĆō Heights must be measured from the **base** (plinth level or top of foundation), not from the ground floor slab. > 7. **Wrong spectrum branch selection** ŌĆō Always compare T with the three transition periods (0.10, 0.40/0.55/0.67, 4.0) and write which branch you are using. > 8. **Not checking the sum of $Q_i$ = $V_B$** ŌĆō A small arithmetic error is caught instantly by the examiner; always perform the verification. > 9. **Sign convention for Sa/g** ŌĆō Sa/g is always positive; never use a negative sign. > 10. **Time units** ŌĆō T must be in **seconds**, h in **metres**, W in **kN**, V_B in **kN**. Mixing up units loses the "dimensional consistency" mark. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ### ­¤ÜĆ The "60-Second Rapid Revision" Checklist - [ ] **ESM** = dynamic earthquake ŌåÆ static lateral force at each floor. Valid for **regular, low-rise** buildings. - [ ] **Step 1: T** = $0.075 h^{0.75}$ (RC SMRF) | $0.09 h/\sqrt{d}$ (with infill) | $0.085 h^{0.75}$ (Steel) - [ ] **Step 2:** $S_a/g$ from **5%-damped spectrum**, depends on **T** and **Soil Type** (I/II/III). - [ ] **Step 3:** $A_h = (Z/2) \cdot (I/R) \cdot (S_a/g)$ ŌĆö the master equation. - [ ] **Step 4:** $W = D + 0.25 L$ (residential/office), $D + 0.5 L$ (storage), $D$ only (roof). - [ ] **Step 5:** $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ ŌĆö total base shear. - [ ] **Step 6:** $Q_i = V_B \cdot (W_i h_i^2) / \sum (W_j h_j^2)$ ŌĆö parabolic distribution. - [ ] **Z values:** II=0.10, III=0.16, IV=0.24, V=0.36. - [ ] **R values:** OMRF=3.0, SMRF=5.0, BRBF=4.0. - [ ] **I values:** 1.0 (normal), 1.2 (>200 occupancy), 1.5 (essential), 1.75 (post-earthquake functional). - [ ] **Heights** measured from **base** (top of foundation / plinth), not ground floor slab. - [ ] **Verify** $\sum Q_i = V_B$ at the end. - [ ] **5% damping** is the default for all RC/steel buildings. - [ ] The **/2 factor** in $A_h$ converts **MCE ŌåÆ DBE**. ### ­¤Ä» KTU High-Yield Mnemonics - **"ZIRSA":** **Z**one factor, **I**mportance factor, **R**esponse reduction, **S**pectrum (Sa/g), then **A**cceleration. - **"TWENTY-SEVEN-FIVE":** Plateau value of Sa/g = **2.5** for all three soil types at low periods. - **"P1-S1-P2"** for spectrum shapes: **P**lateau starts at 0.10, **P**lateau ends at 0.40 (I), 0.55 (II), 0.67 (III), then descending branch. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Note for the KTU 2024 Batch:** Always begin your 14-mark answer by **listing the input parameters in a table** (Zone, Soil, I, R, plan area, storey heights, DL, LL), then proceed in the exact five-step sequence. Examiners reward **methodical, structured answers** that follow the code's own procedural flow. Every numerical step must be **justified by a clause reference** (e.g., "As per IS 1893:2016 Cl. 7.3, LL fraction is 0.25"). This single habit can lift your score by **5ŌĆō7 marks** in the final exam.

topics.title: Response Spectrum Method (RSM) - Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET865 - DESIGN OF EARTHQUAKE RESISTANT STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Response Spectrum Method (RSM) - Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Response Spectrum** is a graphical representation of the maximum response (in terms of displacement, velocity, or acceleration) of a family of single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) oscillators, each having a distinct natural period and damping ratio, when subjected to a particular ground motion record at their base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> A **Response Spectrum** is a plot of the peak response quantity (spectral displacement $S_d$, spectral velocity $S_v$, or spectral acceleration $S_a$) of a series of **linearly elastic SDOF systems** with varying natural periods $T_n$ (or frequencies $\omega_n$) and a specified damping ratio $\xi$, all subjected to the same base excitation $\ddot{u}_g(t)$.

The **Response Spectrum Method (RSM)** is a modal superposition-based analysis technique used to estimate the peak response of a multi-storey building subjected to seismic ground motion. It uses the response spectrum as input, thereby avoiding the computational burden of step-by-step time-history integration for design purposes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Earthquake Fingerprint for Buildings"**
>
> Imagine pushing **20 different children on swings**, each swing having a different rope length (i.e., different natural period). You push them all with the **same rhythm of force** (representing one earthquake). Some children swing violently high, others barely move, depending on whether the push frequency matches their natural swing frequency (resonance).
>
> Now imagine you record the **maximum tilt of every swing** and plot it against the **rope length** (natural period). That single plot is the **Response Spectrum**. It is essentially the "**fingerprint**" of that particular earthquake, but expressed in terms of how structures of *different flexibilities* will respond.
>
> The key insight: **One earthquake ŌåÆ many possible structural responses** (one per period). The spectrum captures all of these in a single graph.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (used to normalize response as a fraction of $g$, e.g., $S_a/g$)
- **Standard damping ratios used in design:** $\xi = 2\%$, $5\%$, $10\%$, $20\%$
- **Conventional plotting range:** Natural period $T_n$ from **0.01 s to 10 s** (logarithmic scale)

## 1.4 Why RSM is Essential in Earthquake Engineering

1. **Computational Efficiency** ŌĆō Gives peak response directly without performing time-history integration.
2. **Design Code Alignment** ŌĆō IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016, Eurocode 8, ASCE 7, and IBC all publish **smoothed design response spectra** for routine design.
3. **Captures Resonance** ŌĆō A simple static analysis cannot reveal that a structure with $T_n \approx 0.5$ s on soft soil experiences far greater forces than a stiffer structure on rock.
4. **Provides Statistical Basis** ŌĆō Multiple ground motions can be enveloped into a single **Uniform Hazard Spectrum (UHS)**.
5. **Foundation of Code-Based Lateral Force Distribution** ŌĆō The base shear $V_B = M \cdot S_a(T, \xi)$ comes directly from the spectrum.

## 1.5 Types of Response Spectra (Important Distinction)

| Spectrum Type | Description | Usage |
|---|---|---|
| **Site-Specific Spectrum** | Generated from actual recorded/ simulated ground motion at a site | Special structures, nuclear plants |
| **Code-Based Design Spectrum** | Smoothed, normalized spectrum from IS 1893 / IBC / EC8 | Routine building design |
| **Uniform Hazard Spectrum (UHS)** | Probabilistic envelope spectrum | Performance-based design |
| **Tripartite (Triple-Log) Spectrum** | Combined plot of $S_d$, $S_v$, $S_a$ on a single log-log chart | Historical, research |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 4, the focus is the *conceptual* understanding of RSM, the construction of the spectrum from an accelerogram, and the tripartite representation. Numerical construction of spectra is a higher-level Module 5 topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying SDOF System

Every response spectrum is built upon the **linear elastic Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system. Let us revisit its fundamentals.

### 2.1.1 Components of an SDOF System
- **Mass $m$** (concentrated at top)
- **Stiffness $k$** (lateral resistance)
- **Damping $c$** (energy dissipation)
- **Lateral displacement $u(t)$** (relative to ground)

### 2.1.2 Equation of Motion under Ground Excitation

When the base of the SDOF system is subjected to ground acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t)$, the absolute acceleration of the mass is $\ddot{u}_a(t) = \ddot{u}(t) + \ddot{u}_g(t)$. Applying D'Alembert's principle:

$$m\ddot{u}(t) + c\dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = -m\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = relative displacement of mass with respect to ground
- $\dot{u}(t)$ = relative velocity
- $\ddot{u}(t)$ = relative acceleration
- The right-hand side $-m\ddot{u}_g(t)$ is the **effective earthquake force** (inertia force from ground motion)

### 2.1.3 Normalised Form

Dividing by $m$ and using $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\xi\omega_n = c/m$:

$$\ddot{u}(t) + 2\xi\omega_n \dot{u}(t) + \omega_n^2 u(t) = -\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

## 2.2 The Three Spectral Quantities

For a given ground motion $\ddot{u}_g(t)$, a given $\omega_n$ and $\xi$, the solution $u(t)$ of the above equation has a peak value $|u|_{\max} = S_d$. From this single number, **two derived spectral quantities** are defined:

### 2.2.1 Spectral Displacement $S_d$
$$S_d(T_n, \xi) = \vert u(t) \vert_{\max}$$

### 2.2.2 Pseudo-Spectral Velocity $S_v$
$$S_v = \omega_n \cdot S_d = \frac{2\pi}{T_n} S_d$$

### 2.2.3 Pseudo-Spectral Acceleration $S_a$
$$S_a = \omega_n^2 \cdot S_d = \left(\frac{2\pi}{T_n}\right)^2 S_d$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Pseudo"?** The word *pseudo* is used because $S_v$ and $S_a$ are computed from the displacement peak using the natural frequency relations. For lightly damped systems ($\xi < 20\%$), the pseudo values are nearly identical to the *true* relative velocity and absolute acceleration peaks. This approximation is universally used in design codes.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Response Spectrum is Constructed

1. **Obtain the accelerogram** $\ddot{u}_g(t)$ of an earthquake at a site.
2. **Select a damping ratio** $\xi$ (commonly $5\%$ for buildings).
3. **For a fixed $T_n$ (or $\omega_n$)**, solve the SDOF equation of motion (typically via Duhamel's integral) to obtain $u(t)$.
4. **Record $|u|_{\max}$** ŌåÆ this is $S_d$ for that $T_n$.
5. **Compute $S_v = \omega_n S_d$** and **$S_a = \omega_n^2 S_d$**.
6. **Repeat for many values of $T_n$** (say 50ŌĆō100 periods from 0.01 s to 10 s).
7. **Plot** $S_d$, $S_v$, or $S_a$ versus $T_n$ on a log-log scale ŌåÆ the response spectrum is born.

## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Tripartite Plot

Because $S_d$, $S_v$, $S_a$ are related by a factor of $\omega$ and $\omega^2$, plotting all three on a single log-log chart is possible by rotating the axes by $45^\circ$ in the appropriate direction. This allows reading off all three quantities at a glance for any chosen $T_n$.

## 2.5 Pseudo-Acceleration as Effective Force

The peak restoring (elastic) force in the SDOF system is:

$$F_{\max} = k \cdot S_d = (m \omega_n^2) \cdot S_d = m \cdot S_a$$

Therefore **$S_a$ is directly proportional to the peak base shear** experienced by the system. This is the *single most important insight* in code-based seismic design.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equation of motion (SDOF under ground motion) | $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = -m\ddot{u}_g$ | N | Absolute motion on LHS, ground forcing on RHS |
| 2 | Natural (circular) frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | $f_n = \omega_n / 2\pi$ |
| 3 | Natural period | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ | s | Building fundamental period |
| 4 | Damping ratio | $\xi = c / (2 m \omega_n)$ | dimensionless | $\xi = 5\%$ for RC buildings |
| 5 | Spectral displacement | $S_d = \vert u(t) \vert_{\max}$ | m | Direct output of Duhamel's integral |
| 6 | Pseudo-spectral velocity | $S_v = \omega_n S_d$ | m/s | Used in tripartite plot |
| 7 | Pseudo-spectral acceleration | $S_a = \omega_n^2 S_d$ | m/s┬▓ | Directly gives base shear |
| 8 | Spectral relationships | $S_a = \omega_n S_v = \omega_n^2 S_d$ | ŌĆō | Holds for any $T_n$ |
| 9 | Peak base shear | $V_B = M \cdot S_a$ | kN | $M$ = total seismic mass |
| 10 | Base shear coefficient | $C_s = S_a / g$ | ŌĆō | Multiplied by $W$ to get $V_B$ |
| 11 | Period-frequency conversion | $T_n = 1 / f_n = 2\pi / \omega_n$ | s | Always check units |
| 12 | Log-tripartite slope | $\log S_v$ vs $\log S_d$ ŌåÆ slope $+1$ | ŌĆō | Geometric rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Table Cells:** In all rows above, the absolute-value notation has been replaced with explicit words ("max", "direct output") to comply with the markdown table pipe-symbol restriction. For displayed mathematics outside the table, $\vert \cdot \vert$ is used.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of RSM |
|---|---|
| **Building Design (Routine)** | Code-based spectrum ŌåÆ compute design base shear $V_B$ |
| **Bridge Engineering** | Multi-span irregular bridges use site-specific spectra |
| **Nuclear Power Plants** | Floor response spectra derived from RSM at each elevation |
| **Performance-Based Design** | Pushover spectra combined with elastic spectra |
| **Seismic Isolation** | Long-period isolated buildings exploit the descending branch of the spectrum |
| **Machine Foundations** | Resonance avoidance by shifting $T_n$ away from spectral peak |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equation of Motion for SDOF under Ground Motion

### Step 1: Free-Body Setup
Consider an SDOF system of mass $m$ supported by a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of coefficient $c$. The base of the system is excited horizontally by ground acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t)$.

Define:
- $u_g(t)$ = ground (base) displacement
- $u(t)$ = relative displacement of mass w.r.t. base
- $u_a(t) = u(t) + u_g(t)$ = absolute displacement of mass

### Step 2: Equilibrium of Forces on the Mass
Three forces act on the mass:
1. **Inertia force** (D'Alembert): $F_I = m \ddot{u}_a = m(\ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g)$
2. **Damping force**: $F_D = c \dot{u}$
3. **Spring force**: $F_S = k u$

For dynamic equilibrium $F_I + F_D + F_S = 0$:

$$m\ddot{u} + m\ddot{u}_g + c\dot{u} + k u = 0$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to Standard Form
Move the ground-acceleration term to the right-hand side (it is the *forcing* function, not a reaction):

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = -m\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

### Step 4: Normalise by Mass
Dividing throughout by $m$ and using $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\xi\omega_n = c/m$:

$$\ddot{u}(t) + 2\xi\omega_n \dot{u}(t) + \omega_n^2 u(t) = -\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The minus sign indicates that when the ground accelerates to the right, the mass tends to lag behind (inertia), producing a relative displacement opposite to the base motion.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pseudo-Spectral Acceleration from Spectral Displacement

### Step 1: Peak Restoring Force
The peak elastic restoring force in the SDOF oscillator is given by Hooke's law:

$$F_{\text{peak}} = k \cdot \vert u(t) \vert_{\max} = k \cdot S_d$$

### Step 2: Substitute Stiffness in Terms of Mass and Frequency
Since $k = m\omega_n^2$:

$$F_{\text{peak}} = m \omega_n^2 \cdot S_d$$

### Step 3: Define Pseudo-Spectral Acceleration
By definition, the peak base shear / mass is the **pseudo-spectral acceleration**:

$$\frac{F_{\text{peak}}}{m} = \omega_n^2 \cdot S_d \equiv S_a$$

Therefore:

$$S_a = \omega_n^2 \cdot S_d = \left(\frac{2\pi}{T_n}\right)^2 S_d$$

### Step 4: Derive Pseudo-Spectral Velocity Similarly
The peak kinetic-equivalent quantity from displacement:

$$S_v = \omega_n \cdot S_d = \frac{2\pi}{T_n} S_d$$

### Step 5: Show Chain Relationship
Eliminating $S_d$ between the two:

$$S_a = \omega_n^2 \cdot \frac{S_v}{\omega_n} = \omega_n \cdot S_v$$

Hence the **canonical triad** holds:

$$S_a = \omega_n S_v = \omega_n^2 S_d = \left(\frac{2\pi}{T_n}\right)^2 S_d$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Base Shear from Spectral Acceleration

### Step 1: Total Seismic Mass
The total seismic mass of a multi-storey building is:

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i$$

where $m_i$ is the lumped mass at floor $i$.

### Step 2: Modal Base Shear
For the first (fundamental) mode, the equivalent static base shear is:

$$V_B = M \cdot S_a(T_1, \xi)$$

### Step 3: Base Shear Coefficient
Dividing by total seismic weight $W = Mg$:

$$C_s = \frac{V_B}{W} = \frac{S_a(T_1, \xi)}{g}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This coefficient $C_s$ is the dimensionless quantity published in design codes (e.g., IS 1893:2016 Clause 6.4.2). The code's $S_a/g$ curve is essentially a **normalized design response spectrum**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Relation Between Period and Stiffness (for completeness)

For a cantilever-like multi-storey building approximated as an SDOF:

$$T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{M}{K}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$T_n^2 = 4\pi^2 \frac{M}{K} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K = \frac{4\pi^2 M}{T_n^2}$$

Therefore **the stiffer the building (larger $K$), the shorter the period $T_n$**, and vice-versa. This explains why:
- **Rigid short-period structures** sit on the **plateau** of the spectrum (high $S_a$).
- **Flexible long-period structures** sit on the **descending branch** (lower $S_a$).

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Starting Point | End Result | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| EOM under ground motion | D'Alembert's principle | $\ddot{u} + 2\xi\omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = -\ddot{u}_g$ | Ground forcing enters as effective inertia |
| Pseudo-acceleration | Hooke's law $F = ku$ | $S_a = \omega_n^2 S_d$ | $S_a$ = peak force per unit mass |
| Pseudo-velocity | Spectral displacement | $S_v = \omega_n S_d$ | Equivalent kinetic-energy measure |
| Base shear | Newton's 2nd law | $V_B = M \cdot S_a$ | Foundation of code-based design |
| PeriodŌĆōstiffness | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{M/K}$ | $K \propto 1/T_n^2$ | Stiff structures ŌåÆ short $T_n$ ŌåÆ high $S_a$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of SDOF System with Ground Excitation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GroundBase[GROUND ACCELERATION ug double dot] -->|Excitation| BaseBlock[BASE OF STRUCTURE]
    BaseBlock --> MassNode["MASS m"]
    MassNode --- DamperLink["DAMPER c"]
    MassNode --- SpringLink["SPRING k"]
    DamperLink --> FixedWall[FIXED SUPPORT]
    SpringLink --> FixedWall
    MassNode -.->|Relative disp u| OutputDisp["OUTPUT u of t"]
    OutputDisp --> SpecCalc[SPECTRAL QUANTITIES]
    SpecCalc --> SdNode["Sd DISPLACEMENT"]
    SpecCalc --> SvNode["Sv VELOCITY"]
    SpecCalc --> SaNode["Sa ACCELERATION"]
```

> **Reading Aid:** The mass $m$ is connected to a fixed support through a spring ($k$) and a damper ($c$) acting in parallel. The base is excited by the ground motion $\ddot{u}_g(t)$. The mass responds with a relative displacement $u(t)$, whose peak value yields the spectral quantities $S_d$, $S_v$, $S_a$.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Construction of a Response Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Step 1: Input Accelerogram ug double dot of t] --> StepB[Step 2: Choose Damping xi - typically 5 percent]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3: Select a trial Period Tn]
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4: Solve SDOF Equation by Duhamel Integral]
    StepD --> StepE[Step 5: Obtain time history u of t]
    StepE --> StepF[Step 6: Record peak value Sd equals max of u of t]
    StepF --> StepG[Step 7: Compute Sv and Sa using omega n relations]
    StepG --> StepH[Step 8: Repeat for next Tn]
    StepH --> StepC
    StepH --> StepI[Step 9: Plot Sd or Sa versus Tn on log log axis]
    StepI --> StepJ[Step 10: RESPONSE SPECTRUM READY]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Layout: Shape of an Elastic Response Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PeriodAxis[PERIOD Tn increasing right] --> PlateauZone[PLATEAU REGION]
    PlateauZone --> DescendingZone[DESCENDING BRANCH]
    DescendingZone --> LongPeriodZone[LONG PERIOD TAIL]
    PlateauZone ---|Controlled by PGA| Region1["Constant Sa equals ZONE 2 IS 1893"]
    DescendingZone ---|Sa proportional to 1 by T| Region2["Sa inversely with T"]
    LongPeriodZone ---|Constant Sd for very long T| Region3["Sa proportional to 1 by T squared"]
```

> **Reading Aid:** An elastic response spectrum has three characteristic regions:
> 1. **Short-period plateau** (constant $S_a$ region, controlled by peak ground acceleration PGA).
> 2. **Descending branch** ($S_a \propto 1/T$ for $T > T_c$ in many codes).
> 3. **Long-period tail** where displacement $S_d$ becomes constant and $S_a \propto 1/T^2$).
>
> This shape reveals that **stiffer is NOT always better for earthquake safety** ŌĆö a very short-period structure sits on the plateau and experiences the *highest* spectral acceleration.

## 4.4 Tripartite Plot: How the Three Quantities Coexist

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TriOrigin[TRIPARTITE ORIGIN] --> TriAxis1["VERTICAL AXIS RIGHT Sd"]
    TriOrigin --> TriAxis2["DIAGONAL UP RIGHT Sv"]
    TriOrigin --> TriAxis3["DIAGONAL DOWN RIGHT Sa"]
    TriAxis1 ---|Slope 1 line| R1["Sv equals omega n Sd"]
    TriAxis2 ---|Slope 1 line| R2["Sa equals omega n Sv"]
    TriAxis1 -.->|Slope 2 line| R3["Sa equals omega n squared Sd"]
    TriAxis3 --- R2
    TriAxis3 --- R3
```

> **Reading Aid:** In a tripartite plot, a single curve represents all three spectra simultaneously. To read $S_d$ at a given $T_n$, project horizontally to the right axis. To read $S_v$, project at $45^\circ$ up-right. To read $S_a$, project at $45^\circ$ down-right. The geometric elegance comes from the relationship $S_a = \omega_n S_v = \omega_n^2 S_d$, which gives perfect slopes of 1, 1, and 2 in log-log space.

## 4.5 End-to-End Block Diagram: Application of RSM in Building Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Geometry and Material] --> B[Step 2: Compute Seismic Mass M]
    B --> C[Step 3: Estimate Fundamental Period T1 by code formula or modal analysis]
    C --> D[Step 4: Read Sa from IS 1893 Design Spectrum for T1 and 5 percent damping]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute Base Shear VB equals M times Sa]
    E --> F[Step 6: Distribute VB vertically to floor levels per code formula]
    F --> G[Step 7: Apply Equivalent Static Lateral Loads on structural model]
    G --> H[Step 8: Perform Static Analysis for forces and displacements]
    H --> I[Step 9: Design structural members for combined gravity and seismic effects]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
